topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes** (or motes) that use sensors to cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, at different locations.

Each sensor node is equipped with:
- A **radio transceiver** (for wireless communication)
- A **microcontroller** (for processing and control)
- A **sensing element** (for physical measurement)
- An **energy source** (typically a battery, often supplemented by energy harvesting)

The nodes communicate wirelessly over short distances and collaborate to forward sensed data through multi-hop routing to a central **sink node** (or **Base Station (BS)**), which acts as a gateway to the outside world (e.g., the Internet or a remote server).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize this):**
> "A Wireless Sensor Network is a network of spatially distributed, autonomous sensor nodes that cooperate to sense, process, and communicate data about the physical environment to a central sink, typically over multi-hop wireless paths, under severe resource constraints (energy, bandwidth, memory, computation)."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Digital Beehive" Analogy:**
> Imagine a forest. You want to know the temperature, humidity, and presence of fire in every square meter of the forest. Instead of hiring thousands of human surveyors (expensive, slow, limited range), you drop thousands of tiny, cheap, battery-powered "smart dust particles" — like a swarm of bees — across the forest.
>
> - Each **bee (sensor node)** can sense the environment, remember a tiny bit, and "buzz" (transmit) data to nearby bees.
> - They pass the data among themselves (multi-hop communication) until it reaches the **beekeeper (sink node)**.
> - The beekeeper aggregates all information and reports to headquarters (Internet/server).
> - Bees have limited flight time (limited battery), so they must be extremely energy-efficient.
> - If some bees die, others must reorganize (self-healing, self-configuring).

This is exactly what a WSN does — it replaces expensive, bulky wired instrumentation with a swarm of cheap, intelligent, energy-constrained wireless devices.

### 1.3 Core Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Node Density:** Number of sensor nodes per unit area (typically **10–100 nodes/m²** in dense deployments).
- **Transmission Range:** Short-range RF communication, usually **1 m to 100 m**.
- **Battery Lifetime:** Typical node lifetime is **months to a few years** (because batteries are non-rechargeable in hostile deployments).
- **Data Rate:** Very low, typically **< 1 kbps to 250 kbps** (using protocols like IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee).
- **Operating Frequency Bands:** Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) bands — **433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The three defining characteristics of a WSN that distinguish it from MANETs (Mobile Ad-hoc Networks) and traditional wireless networks are:
> 1. **Severe energy constraint** (battery-limited, often non-rechargeable)
> 2. **Application-specific design** (the hardware, software, and protocols are tailored to one specific application — e.g., forest fire detection, not generic data traffic)
> 3. **Dense deployment and redundancy** (hundreds to thousands of nodes, with overlapping coverage to tolerate failures)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a WSN Actually Works (Structured Logic Steps)

A Wireless Sensor Network operates in **four tightly coupled phases**:

1. **Deployment Phase**
   - Nodes are scattered randomly (e.g., dropped by aircraft) or placed deterministically in the Region of Interest (ROI).
   - Each node is initially unaware of its location and neighbors.
   - **Self-organization algorithms** (e.g., clustering, spanning tree construction) are executed to form a network topology.

2. **Sensing and Data Acquisition Phase**
   - The sensing element (temperature, humidity, gas, accelerometer, etc.) samples the physical phenomenon at a configured sampling rate.
   - Analog signals are converted to digital via an **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)**.
   - Local processing (e.g., filtering, aggregation, compression) is performed on the microcontroller to reduce redundant transmissions.

3. **Communication Phase (Multi-hop Routing)**
   - Neighboring nodes exchange routing information using a lightweight routing protocol (e.g., **LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion**).
   - Data is forwarded hop-by-hop towards the **sink**, often with **in-network data aggregation** to save energy.
   - Energy is dominated by **wireless transmission**, so minimizing transmission distance is critical.

4. **Sink Processing and Actuation Phase**
   - The sink aggregates and processes all received data.
   - It may forward data to a remote server via the Internet, a satellite link, or a cellular network.
   - Based on the data, the sink may trigger **actuators** (a feedback control loop) — this extends the architecture to a **Wireless Sensor and Actuator Network (WSAN)**.

### 2.2 Key Architectural Variants of WSNs

| Architecture Type | Description | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat / Homogeneous WSN** | All sensor nodes have identical hardware, battery, and capabilities. | Academic testbeds, small-scale monitoring. |
| **Hierarchical / Clustered WSN** | Nodes are grouped into clusters; each cluster has a **Cluster Head (CH)** that aggregates and forwards data. | Large-scale, energy-efficient deployments (LEACH protocol). |
| **Heterogeneous WSN** | Nodes have different capabilities (some are powerful "supernodes" or "relay nodes"). | Real deployments with a few resource-rich gateways. |
| **Mobile WSN (MWSN)** | Sensor nodes or sinks are mobile (mounted on robots, vehicles, animals). | Animal tracking, battlefield surveillance. |
| **Underwater WSN (UWSN)** | Uses acoustic (sound) communication instead of RF, due to water's RF absorption. | Ocean monitoring, tsunami detection. |
| **Underground WSN** | Nodes buried in soil; communication through soil medium. | Precision agriculture, landslide monitoring. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Table Syntax:** The vertical pipe character `\vert` (or `\mid`) is used for absolute value to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | Boundary / Practical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coverage Area (Disk Model)** | $A_{cov} = \pi r_{s}^{2}$ | $r_{s}$ = sensing radius (m); $A_{cov}$ in m² | $r_{s} \in [1, 50]$ m typically |
| 2 | **Coverage Volume (3D Sphere)** | $V_{cov} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{s}^{3}$ | $r_{s}$ = sensing radius (m); $V_{cov}$ in m³ | For 3D deployments (UAV, underwater) |
| 3 | **Minimum Nodes for k-Coverage** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{k \cdot A_{ROI}}{A_{cov}}$ | $A_{ROI}$ = region area; $k$ = coverage multiplicity | $k \ge 1$ (1-coverage) or $k \ge 2$ for fault tolerance |
| 4 | **First-Order Radio Energy Model (Transmit)** | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp}\cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | $E_{elec}$ = electronics energy (nJ/bit); $\varepsilon_{amp}$ = amplifier (pJ/bit/m²); $d$ = distance (m); $k$ = bits; $n$ = path loss exponent (2 free space, 4 multipath) | $n = 2$ for short range; $n = 4$ for longer |
| 5 | **First-Order Radio Energy Model (Receive)** | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec}\cdot k$ | Same units as above | Independent of distance |
| 6 | **Total Energy per Round** | $E_{total} = N\cdot(E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{comm})$ | $N$ = number of nodes | Typically $E_{comm} \gg E_{sense} + E_{proc}$ |
| 7 | **Network Lifetime (Definition)** | $L_{net} = $ time until first node dies (FND) / half nodes die (HND) / last node dies (LND) | Time in rounds | Trade-off between strictness and practicality |
| 8 | **Path Loss (Free Space, Friis)** | $P_{r} = P_{t}\cdot G_{t}\cdot G_{r}\cdot \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$ | $P_{r}, P_{t}$ = received/transmitted power (W); $G_{t}, G_{r}$ = antenna gains; $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) | Valid for line-of-sight |
| 9 | **Path Loss (Log-Distance Model)** | $PL(d) = PL(d_{0}) + 10n\cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_{0}}\right)$ | $PL$ in dB; $d_{0}$ = reference distance (usually 1 m) | $n \in [2, 6]$ |
| 10 | **Node Density (Poisson)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{N}{A_{ROI}}$ | $\lambda$ in nodes/m² | Used in stochastic coverage analysis |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

WSNs form the **perception layer of the Internet of Things (IoT)** — they bridge the physical world (sensors) to the digital world (cloud/Internet). Major engineering applications include:

- **Environmental monitoring** (volcanic, forest fire, glacier)
- **Precision agriculture** (soil moisture, microclimate, irrigation control)
- **Healthcare** (wearable body area networks, elderly fall detection)
- **Industrial IoT / Industry 4.0** (machine condition monitoring, predictive maintenance)
- **Smart cities** (structural health of bridges, parking, noise mapping)
- **Military & battlefield surveillance** (intrusion detection, nuclear/radiological monitoring)
- **Disaster management** (earthquake, flood early warning)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Why this section is critical for KTU 2024:**
> Numerical/analytical problems on **coverage, node-count estimation, energy consumption, and path loss** are the most commonly asked derivation questions in Module 1 of PEAET756. The following derivations cover the entire high-yield analytical surface.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of Sensor Nodes Required for k-Coverage of a 2D Region

**Problem Context:** A rectangular agricultural field of area $A_{ROI}$ must be monitored such that every point is sensed by at least $k$ sensor nodes (for fault tolerance). Each sensor has a circular sensing range of radius $r_{s}$. Derive the minimum number of nodes required.

**Given:**
- Region area: $A_{ROI}$ (m²)
- Sensing radius per node: $r_{s}$ (m)
- Coverage multiplicity: $k$ (positive integer, $k \ge 1$)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define the coverage area of a single sensor node.**
Using the 2D disk (omnidirectional sensing) model, the area sensed by a single node is the area of a circle of radius $r_{s}$:
$$
A_{single} = \pi r_{s}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Define k-coverage requirement.**
k-coverage means every point in the ROI must lie within the sensing disk of at least $k$ nodes. Therefore, the total "sensing area" provided by $N$ nodes, when summed, must cover the ROI at least $k$ times:
$$
N \cdot A_{single} \;\ge\; k \cdot A_{ROI}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for N (minimum).**
Dividing both sides by $A_{single} = \pi r_{s}^{2}$:
$$
N \;\ge\; \frac{k \cdot A_{ROI}}{\pi r_{s}^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result (with ceiling function for integer node count).**
$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{k \cdot A_{ROI}}{\pi r_{s}^{2}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 5 — Verification / Sanity Check:**
- If $k = 1$ and $r_{s}$ doubles, $N_{min}$ reduces by a factor of 4 (correct, since area scales with $r_{s}^{2}$).
- If $k$ doubles (from 1 to 2), $N_{min}$ doubles (correct, linear in $k$).
- Units: $\dfrac{\text{unitless} \cdot \text{m}^{2}}{\text{m}^{2}} = \text{unitless}$ (correct).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A vineyard of area $10{,}000$ m² must be monitored with 2-coverage ($k=2$). Each sensor has $r_{s} = 10$ m. Find $N_{min}$.

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{2 \cdot 10{,}000}{\pi \cdot (10)^{2}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{20{,}000}{314.16} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 63.66 \right\rceil = 64 \text{ nodes}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Consumption per Round (First-Order Radio Model)

This is the most important derivation in WSN — **Heinzelman et al.'s First-Order Radio Energy Model** (the basis of LEACH, the most cited WSN protocol).

**Step 1 — Model the energy spent in transmitting $k$ bits over distance $d$:**
Transmitter electronics consume $E_{elec}$ per bit, and the power amplifier consumes $\varepsilon_{amp}$ per bit per m² (free space, $d^{2}$ loss):
$$
E_{TX}(k,d) \;=\; E_{elec} \cdot k \;+\; \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} \quad \text{(free space, } d \le d_{0}\text{)}
$$

For longer distances (multipath fading, $d^{4}$ loss):
$$
E_{TX}(k,d) \;=\; E_{elec} \cdot k \;+\; \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4} \quad \text{(multipath, } d > d_{0}\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy spent in receiving $k$ bits:**
The receiver only uses electronics (no amplifier):
$$
E_{RX}(k) \;=\; E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Step 3 — Energy per bit for 1-hop transmission at distance $d$ (compact form):**
$$
E_{bit}(d) = 2\cdot E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{amp}\cdot d^{n}, \quad n = \begin{cases} 2, & d \le d_{0} \\ 4, & d > d_{0} \end{cases}
$$
The factor 2 arises because both the transmitter and receiver of that link consume $E_{elec}$ per bit.

**Step 4 — Total energy per round (for an $N$-node network transmitting once to the sink):**
Assuming direct transmission to the sink (no multi-hop), the total energy per round is:
$$
E_{round} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( E_{TX}(k, d_{i}) + E_{RX}(k) \right) + N \cdot (E_{sense} + E_{proc})
$$

**Step 5 — Multi-hop form (used in cluster-based protocols like LEACH):**
If data traverses $h$ hops each of length $d_{hop} = d_{total}/h$, then by AM-GM inequality, the **optimal hop count** to minimize total energy is:
$$
h_{opt} = \frac{d_{total}}{\sqrt[\leftroot{2}\uproot{2}n]{\frac{E_{elec}}{\varepsilon_{amp}\cdot(n-1)}}}
$$

For $n = 2$ (free space), this simplifies to a constant independent of $d$ (since transmit energy grows as $d^{2}$, splitting into 2 hops of $d/2$ each gives 4× savings per link, exactly canceling the doubled link count).

**Key Insight (for KTU exam answer):** Multi-hop routing saves energy **only** when $n > 2$ (i.e., in multipath environments). In pure free space, single-hop transmission to the sink is energy-optimal.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Network Lifetime (Time Until First Node Dies)

**Step 1 — Initial energy of each node:**
$$
E_{0} = V_{batt} \cdot I_{batt} \quad \text{(Joules)}
$$
Typically $E_{0} \approx 2\text{ AA batteries} \approx 2 \cdot 1.5\text{ V} \cdot 1500\text{ mAh} \approx 16{,}200$ J (but practically WSN nodes use $\sim 0.5$–$2$ Ah cells, giving $E_{0} \approx 5400$–$21{,}600$ J).

**Step 2 — Energy per round for a single node:**
$$
E_{round} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{comm}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of rounds until node energy is exhausted (lifetime in rounds):**
$$
L_{node} = \left\lfloor \frac{E_{0}}{E_{round}} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 — Convert rounds to seconds (using duty cycle $T_{round}$):**
$$
L_{sec} = L_{node} \cdot T_{round}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to years:**
$$
L_{years} = \frac{L_{sec}}{365.25 \cdot 86400}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A node has $E_{0} = 5$ J. It consumes $E_{round} = 50$ mJ per round. One round = 1 minute. Find the lifetime in days.

$$
L_{node} = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{0.050} \right\rfloor = 100 \text{ rounds}
$$
$$
L_{sec} = 100 \cdot 60 = 6000 \text{ s} \approx 1.67 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to convert mJ to J. Always check unit consistency. $1$ mJ $= 10^{-3}$ J.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (WSN vs. MANET vs. Cellular Network)

> This is a frequently asked KTU question — it tests your ability to differentiate WSNs from other wireless networks.

| Parameter | **WSN** | **MANET** | **Cellular (e.g., 4G/5G)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Sensing + reporting | Communication between peers | Voice/data communication |
| Node Mobility | Mostly static; some MWSN variants | Highly mobile | Mobile (handheld) |
| Energy | **Severely limited** (battery, often non-rechargeable) | Limited (rechargeable handheld) | Adequate (rechargeable daily) |
| Node Identity | **Often not unique** (data-centric, not address-centric) | Unique IP/MAC addresses | Unique SIM, IP |
| Traffic Pattern | **Many-to-one** (sensor → sink) | Peer-to-peer / any-to-any | Bidirectional user traffic |
| Data Redundancy | **High** (correlated sensing) | Low | Low |
| Node Count | **Hundreds to thousands** (dense) | Tens to hundreds | Millions (managed) |
| Topology | **Application-specific**, often pre-deployed | Ad-hoc, dynamic | Cell-based, planned |
| Protocol Stack | Lightweight (e.g., ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, RPL) | Standard IP-based | Heavy (LTE, NR) |
| Infrastructure | **None** required | None required | Base stations, core network |
| Scale of Deployment | Per m² to km² | km² | Continent-wide |
| Cost per Node | < $10 (target: < $1) | $100s (smartphones) | $100s (smartphones) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — WSN End-to-End Architecture (Field → Sink → Internet)

> This is the **mandatory diagram** every KTU answer on WSN architecture must contain. Memorize the data-flow arrows.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    field1[Sensor Field ROI]
    n1[Node A]
    n2[Node B]
    n3[Node C]
    n4[Node D]
    n5[Node E]
    n6[Node F]
    sink1[Sink Node Gateway]
    server1[Remote Server]
    cloud1[Internet Cloud]
    user1[End User Application]
    actuator1[Actuator Optional]

    field1 --- n1
    field1 --- n2
    field1 --- n3
    field1 --- n4
    field1 --- n5
    field1 --- n6

    n1 -. Wireless Multi Hop .-> n2
    n2 -. Wireless Multi Hop .-> n3
    n3 -. Wireless Multi Hop .-> sink1
    n4 -. Wireless Multi Hop .-> n5
    n5 -. Wireless Multi Hop .-> n3
    n6 -. Wireless Multi Hop .-> n2

    sink1 --> server1
    server1 --> cloud1
    cloud1 --> user1
    sink1 -. Control Feedback .-> actuator1
```

**Explanation of the diagram (for KTU answer writing):**
- **Sensor Field (ROI):** The geographic area being monitored.
- **Sensor Nodes A through F:** Scattered throughout the ROI, sensing the environment cooperatively.
- **Dotted arrows:** Wireless multi-hop communication paths toward the sink.
- **Sink Node (Gateway):** The aggregation point. Receives all data from nodes and bridges to the IP world.
- **Server, Internet, End User:** The path from sink to the application/user.
- **Actuator (optional):** In a WSAN, the sink may also send control signals back to actuators (e.g., open a valve, sound an alarm).

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Single Sensor Node

> A 2-mark question very frequently asks: "Draw and explain the block diagram of a sensor node." This is the canonical answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwr1[Power Unit Battery Harvester]
    sens1[Sensing Unit Sensors ADC]
    proc1[Processing Unit MCU Memory]
    comm1[Transceiver Unit Radio Antenna]
    loc1[Localization Unit GPS Optional]
    mob1[Mobilizer Optional]
    pwr1 --> proc1
    pwr1 --> sens1
    pwr1 --> comm1
    pwr1 --> loc1
    pwr1 --> mob1
    sens1 --> proc1
    proc1 --> comm1
    loc1 --> proc1
    mob1 --> proc1
```

**Sub-block explanations (for KTU answer writing):**

- **Sensing Unit:** Comprises physical sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, gas) and an ADC to convert analog signals to digital.
- **Processing Unit:** A microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M) with on-chip RAM/ROM and external flash. Runs the OS (e.g., TinyOS, Contiki) and application code.
- **Transceiver Unit:** A short-range radio (e.g., CC2420, nRF24L01) operating in ISM bands. Responsible for all wireless communication.
- **Power Unit:** The energy source — typically 2 AA batteries, often supplemented by energy harvesting (solar, vibration, thermal).
- **Localization Unit (Optional):** GPS receiver or RSSI-based localization to determine node position.
- **Mobilizer (Optional):** Motors/wheels for moving the node (used in mobile WSNs).

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — WSN Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[Application Layer User Programs]
    trans1[Transport Layer Reliability Congestion Control]
    net1[Network Layer Routing Addressing]
    data1[Data Link Layer MAC Framing Error Control]
    phys1[Physical Layer Radio Modulation Transmission]
    powermgmt1[Power Management Plane]
    mobmgr1[Mobility Management Plane]
    taskmgr1[Task Management Plane]

    app1 --> trans1 --> net1 --> data1 --> phys1

    powermgmt1 -.-> phys1
    powermgmt1 -.-> data1
    powermgmt1 -.-> net1
    mobmgr1 -.-> net1
    mobmgr1 -.-> data1
    taskmgr1 -.-> app1
```

**Explanation:**
The WSN protocol stack is similar to the OSI/TCP-IP stack, but with three **management planes** drawn orthogonal to the layers:
- **Power Management Plane:** Manages how the node uses its energy (e.g., sleep/wake cycles).
- **Mobility Management Plane:** Detects and registers node movement.
- **Task Management Plane:** Schedules sensing tasks among neighboring nodes for balanced energy use.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List any four of its key applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, pressure, etc.) and communicate sensed data wirelessly, via multi-hop paths, to a central sink node.

**Four Applications (½ mark each):**

1. **Environmental Monitoring** — Forest fire detection, flood early warning, volcanic activity monitoring.
2. **Precision Agriculture** — Soil moisture sensing, microclimate monitoring, smart irrigation.
3. **Healthcare** — Wearable body-area networks for patient monitoring, elderly fall detection.
4. **Military & Battlefield Surveillance** — Intrusion detection, nuclear/radiological/chemical threat detection.
*(Other acceptable: Industrial IoT, smart city infrastructure monitoring, structural health monitoring of bridges/buildings.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the four main components of a sensor node.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A sensor node consists of four essential components (1/2 mark for naming + 1/4 mark for each brief explanation):

1. **Sensing Unit** — Comprises one or more physical sensors (temperature, humidity, gas, etc.) and an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). It samples the physical environment and produces a digital measurement.
2. **Processing Unit** — A microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M) with limited RAM/ROM. It executes local processing, data aggregation, and protocol operations.
3. **Transceiver Unit** — A short-range radio (e.g., CC2420) operating in ISM bands. Responsible for all wireless transmission and reception. Energy spent in communication is the dominant energy cost.
4. **Power Unit** — The battery (often 2× AA cells) that supplies energy to all other units. May be supplemented with energy harvesting (solar, vibration, thermal). Power is the most critical and scarce resource in a WSN.

*(Optional 5th: Localization Unit (GPS) and Mobilizer — ½ mark bonus if mentioned.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. There is internal choice (i.e., two alternative questions, and the student answers one). You must answer **both sub-parts** of the chosen question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a) Explain in detail the architecture of a Wireless Sensor Network. Draw a neat diagram showing the sensor field, sensor nodes, multi-hop communication, and the sink connected to the Internet. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A WSN architecture consists of sensor nodes deployed in a Region of Interest (ROI), communicating wirelessly in a multi-hop manner to a sink node, which is connected to a remote server and ultimately to end users via the Internet.

**Sensor Field (1 mark):**
The sensor field is the physical region being monitored — e.g., a forest, farm, factory floor, or human body. Hundreds to thousands of nodes are deployed here, either deterministically (pre-planned) or randomly (e.g., dropped by aircraft).

**Sensor Nodes (1 mark):**
Each node consists of four units: sensing, processing, transceiver, and power (with optional localization and mobilizer). The sensing unit captures physical phenomena; the processing unit handles local computation; the transceiver communicates with neighbors; the power unit supplies energy.

**Multi-hop Communication (1.5 marks):**
Because direct long-range transmission to the sink is energy-prohibitive, nodes use **multi-hop routing** — they forward data through intermediate neighbors. This conserves energy (per the first-order radio model, since $E_{TX} \propto d^{n}$ with $n \ge 2$). Routing protocols (LEACH, SPIN, PEGASIS, Directed Diffusion) organize these paths.

**Sink Node / Gateway (1.5 marks):**
The sink is a special, resource-rich node that aggregates data from all sensor nodes. It is the bridge between the WSN and the IP world (Internet). It may be connected via Ethernet, Wi-Fi, cellular, or satellite.

**End-User Application (1 mark):**
Data is forwarded to a remote server and accessed by end users (or by automated systems). In WSANs, the sink may also send commands back to actuators, closing the control loop.

**Mandatory Neat Diagram (½ mark for diagram):**
The diagram must show: **Sensor field** (region) → multiple **sensor nodes** (with wireless multi-hop arrows) → **Sink node** → **Server/Internet** → **End user/Actuator**. *(See Section 4.1 diagram for reference.)*

---

**(b) Compare and contrast Wireless Sensor Networks with traditional Mobile Ad-hoc Networks (MANETs) under the following heads: (i) Primary goal, (ii) Energy constraint, (iii) Traffic pattern, (iv) Node identity model, (v) Data redundancy, (vi) Typical application, (vii) Node count. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (1 mark per head):**

| S.No. | Parameter | **WSN** | **MANET** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary Goal** | Sensing and reporting the environment to a sink | Communication between any pair of mobile users |
| 2 | **Energy Constraint** | **Severe** — battery is non-rechargeable; node dies if depleted | Limited — devices are rechargeable handhelds |
| 3 | **Traffic Pattern** | **Many-to-one** (all nodes → sink) | **Peer-to-peer / any-to-any** |
| 4 | **Node Identity Model** | **Data-centric** (queries by attribute, e.g., "temperature > 40°C") | **Address-centric** (unique IP/MAC per node) |
| 5 | **Data Redundancy** | **High** — many nodes sense the same event; aggregation is critical | **Low** — independent user data |
| 6 | **Typical Application** | Environmental monitoring, agriculture, healthcare | Battlefield communication, emergency response |
| 7 | **Node Count** | **Hundreds to thousands** (dense) | **Tens to hundreds** (sparse) |
| 8 | **Node Mobility** | Mostly static; some variants (MWSN) are mobile | **Highly mobile** (vehicles, soldiers) |

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
Although WSNs and MANETs share the wireless, multi-hop, infrastructure-less nature, they differ fundamentally in **application intent, energy budget, traffic model, and design priorities**. A WSN is **data-centric and energy-driven**; a MANET is **address-centric and mobility-driven**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Discuss in detail the major design challenges and constraints of Wireless Sensor Networks. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (½ mark):**
WSN design is constrained by the application, the harsh deployment environment, and the limited resources of the sensor nodes. These constraints must be jointly addressed in the design of hardware, protocols, and algorithms.

**1. Energy Constraint (1 mark):**
The most critical challenge. Sensor nodes are battery-powered; in remote or hostile deployments, batteries cannot be replaced. Therefore, **all** layers of the protocol stack must be energy-aware. Communication energy dominates, so protocols use multi-hop, data aggregation, and sleep/wake scheduling (e.g., S-MAC, B-MAC, duty cycling).

**2. Limited Hardware Resources (1 mark):**
Nodes have small memory (tens of KB RAM, hundreds of KB flash), low clock speed (few MHz), and weak radios. Protocols must be lightweight — e.g., no heavy cryptographic handshakes, no full TCP/IP stack (instead, 6LoWPAN over IEEE 802.15.4).

**3. Harsh Deployment Environment (1 mark):**
Nodes face extreme temperatures, humidity, vibration, dust, and even animal attacks. Hardware must be rugged; software must tolerate crashes and reboots gracefully. The network must be **self-healing** and **self-configuring**.

**4. Scalability (1 mark):**
Networks can scale to thousands of nodes. Addressing, routing, and data dissemination must scale efficiently. Flat routing fails at scale, hence the use of **clustering** (LEACH) and **hierarchical addressing**.

**5. Data Redundancy and Correlation (1 mark):**
Neighboring nodes often sense correlated data, wasting energy. **Data aggregation** and **in-network processing** are essential. However, aggregation delays data and reduces fidelity — a trade-off.

**6. Security (1 mark):**
WSNs are vulnerable to eavesdropping, jamming, sybil attacks, and node compromise. Resource constraints make traditional cryptography difficult. Lightweight key management (e.g., SPINS, TinySec) and trust models are required.

**7. Quality of Service (QoS) vs. Energy Trade-off (1 mark):**
Reliability, latency, accuracy, and fault tolerance all compete with energy conservation. Designers must balance application-specific QoS requirements against network lifetime.

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
These challenges are interlinked — a solution to one (e.g., redundancy for reliability) often worsens another (energy). WSN design is fundamentally a **multi-objective optimization** problem.

---

**(b) Explain with examples the various applications of Wireless Sensor Networks in different domains. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Environmental Monitoring (1.5 marks):**
- **Forest Fire Detection:** Temperature, humidity, and smoke sensors scattered in a forest detect fire ignition early, even before satellites can.
- **Flood Detection:** Water-level and soil-moisture sensors along riverbanks provide real-time flood early warning.
- **Volcano / Glacier Monitoring:** Seismic and temperature sensors transmit data from remote, inaccessible terrain to research stations.

**2. Precision Agriculture (1.5 marks):**
- **Smart Irrigation:** Soil-moisture sensors communicate with valves to water crops only when needed, saving water.
- **Greenhouse Microclimate Control:** Temperature, CO₂, and light sensors maintain optimal growing conditions.
- **Livestock Monitoring:** Wearable sensors on animals track health, location, and feeding patterns.

**3. Healthcare (1.5 marks):**
- **Wearable Body Area Networks (WBAN):** ECG, blood pressure, glucose, and body-temperature sensors continuously monitor patients and alert doctors in real time.
- **Elderly Fall Detection:** Accelerometer and gyroscope sensors detect falls and trigger emergency calls.
- **Hospital Asset Tracking:** RFID + WSN tags track equipment and patients inside hospitals.

**4. Industrial / Smart Factory (Industry 4.0) (1.5 marks):**
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Vibration and temperature sensors on motors detect bearing wear before failure.
- **Pipeline Monitoring:** Pressure and flow sensors detect leaks in oil and gas pipelines.
- **Structural Health Monitoring of Buildings and Bridges:** Accelerometers and strain gauges detect cracks and vibrations, especially after earthquakes.

**5. Military and Defense (1 mark):**
- **Battlefield Surveillance:** Acoustic, seismic, and magnetic sensors detect enemy movement and vehicle passage.
- **Nuclear, Biological, Chemical (NBC) Detection:** Specialized sensors deployed in conflict zones detect threats and warn soldiers.

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
From the human body to the deep ocean, from precision farming to national defense, WSNs enable continuous, ubiquitous, and fine-grained sensing that was previously impossible or prohibitively expensive. With the rise of **IoT** and **5G/6G**, WSNs are becoming the foundational data-acquisition layer of every modern cyber-physical system.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 (Introduction) — Examiner Insights:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WSN with MANET (LOSES UP TO 2 MARKS):** Many students write generic "ad-hoc network" features. **Always** emphasize the four WSN-defining traits: **energy constraint, data-centric design, many-to-one traffic, dense deployment.** Examiners look for these keywords specifically.
>
> 2. **Drawing a Sensor Node Diagram Without Labeling All Four Units (LOSES 1 MARK):** A sensor node diagram without labeling **Sensing, Processing, Transceiver, and Power** units is incomplete. The Power unit is the **most important** — examiners often test whether you remember the battery constraint.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems (LOSES 0.5–1 MARK):** Whenever you compute area, energy, or lifetime, **always write units** (m², J, s, years). A correct number without units is considered incomplete.
>
> 4. **Writing Only the Formula, Not the Derivation (LOSES 2–3 MARKS):** In 7-mark analytical questions, you must **derive** the formula step by step from first principles, not just state the final expression. Show the reasoning.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Management Planes in the Protocol Stack (LOSES 1 MARK):** Many students draw a 5-layer stack (Application → Transport → Network → MAC → Physical) and stop. **Always add the three management planes** — Power, Mobility, and Task — orthogonal to the layers. This is a KTU-specific and Heinzelman-specific point.
>
> 6. **Mismatching Path Loss Exponent:** In the energy model, free-space is $d^{2}$ and multipath is $d^{4}$. Using the wrong exponent in a numerical problem loses 1 full mark.
>
> 7. **No Diagram in Long Answers (LOSES 1–2 MARKS):** Every 7-mark WSN answer must have at least one **neat, labeled** diagram. A text-only answer signals incomplete preparation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Introduction: Final Revision Bullets**

- [x] A **WSN** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less, **multi-hop**, **energy-constrained**, **data-centric** network of sensor nodes.
- [x] Each sensor node has **four mandatory units**: Sensing, Processing, Transceiver, Power (+ optional Localization, Mobilizer).
- [x] WSNs differ from MANETs in: **energy, traffic pattern (many-to-one), data-centric vs. address-centric, dense deployment, mostly static.**
- [x] WSNs differ from Cellular in: **no infrastructure, application-specific, low data rate, energy-constrained, low cost per node.**
- [x] **Three management planes** cross the protocol stack: **Power, Mobility, Task.**
- [x] **Coverage area** of a single node: $A = \pi r_{s}^{2}$. For 3D: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{s}^{3}$.
- [x] **Minimum nodes for k-coverage:** $N_{min} = \lceil k \cdot A_{ROI} / (\pi r_{s}^{2}) \rceil$.
- [x] **First-order radio energy model:** $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$, with $n = 2$ (free space) or $n = 4$ (multipath). $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$.
- [x] **Communication energy dominates** total energy consumption.
- [x] **Multi-hop is energy-efficient** only when path loss exponent $n > 2$.
- [x] **Network lifetime** can be defined as FND (First Node Dies), HND (Half Nodes Die), or LND (Last Node Dies).
- [x] **Major applications:** Environmental monitoring, Precision Agriculture, Healthcare, Industrial IoT, Smart Cities, Military.
- [x] **Major design challenges:** Energy, Hardware limits, Harsh environment, Scalability, Data redundancy, Security, QoS trade-offs.
- [x] **Typical frequency bands:** ISM — 433/868/915 MHz and 2.4 GHz.
- [x] **Typical data rate:** < 1 kbps to 250 kbps (ZigBee-class).
- [x] **Typical node lifetime target:** months to a few years on batteries.
- [x] **Always include a diagram** in any 7-mark or 14-mark answer — sensor node block diagram or WSN architecture diagram.
- [x] **Always carry units** in numerical problems and convert mJ ↔ J, m² ↔ km², s ↔ years.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 | Topic: Applications of Wireless Sensor Networks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called *sensor nodes* (or *motes*) that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants. The data acquired by each node is transmitted hop-by-hop through the wireless medium to a central gateway, known as the **Sink** (or **Base Station**), where it is processed, stored, and forwarded to end-users.

When this generic definition is mapped to a real-world problem domain, the resulting deployment scenario is termed an **Application of WSN**. Each application imposes a unique set of design constraints — including data rate, latency tolerance, energy budget, node density, mobility pattern, and security requirement — that directly dictates the choice of hardware, MAC protocol, routing strategy, and topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to classify the major application domains of WSN, articulate the unique design challenges imposed by each domain, and map application requirements to architectural decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **swarm of intelligent fireflies** placed inside a dense forest. Each firefly is small, runs on a tiny battery, and can only sense its immediate surroundings (e.g., heat, smoke). No firefly is powerful enough to communicate with the forest ranger stationed at the perimeter. However, each firefly can "talk" to the fireflies closest to it. When one firefly detects a fire, it blinks, and the message propagates like a chain — firefly to firefly — until it reaches the ranger. The ranger then knows *that* a fire occurred, *where* it occurred (through triangulation), and *how intense* it is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Translation to WSN:** Fireflies = Sensor Nodes (motes); Fire detection = Sensing; Blink propagation = Multi-hop wireless routing; Forest ranger = Sink / Base Station; Tiny batteries = Energy-constrained nodes.

This analogy captures the three pillars that define *every* WSN application:
1. **Distributed sensing** — many cheap, low-power, energy-constrained nodes
2. **Cooperative communication** — no node can reach the sink directly; multi-hop routing is mandatory
3. **Application-driven design** — the forest fire application requires fast detection, low data rate, high reliability, but tolerates coarse granularity; a video surveillance application would have the opposite trade-offs

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics of WSN Applications

Every WSN application is benchmarked using the following standard metrics (these are **must-memorize** for KTU 2024 ESE):

> [!NOTE]
> - **Lifetime:** The operational duration of the network, typically measured in days, months, or years. Target lifetime for a deployment often ranges from **2 months to 5 years**.
> - **Coverage:** The fraction of the geographical area (or volume) being monitored, expressed as a percentage or ratio.
> - **Cost:** Per-node cost (USD), with a typical target of **$1 to $100** depending on application class.
> - **Data Rate:** Bit rate generated per node, ranging from **1 bps** (periodic temperature) to **1 Mbps** (multimedia WSN).
> - **Latency:** End-to-end delay from sensing event to sink reception, ranging from **milliseconds** (battlefield) to **minutes** (agriculture).
> - **Security Level:** Confidentiality, integrity, and authentication requirements — often application-critical in military and medical domains.
> - **Mobility:** Static, nomadic, or fully mobile — a defining constraint for protocols.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of WSN Applications

WSN applications are conventionally divided into **two broad categories** based on the nature of interaction with the physical world:

### A. Monitoring Applications (Sense → Report)
The network observes a physical phenomenon and reports it periodically or when an event is triggered. Examples: temperature logging, forest-fire detection, structural-health monitoring.

### B. Tracking Applications (Sense → Locate → Report)
The network tracks the movement or position of one or more objects. Examples: animal-migration tracking, vehicle tracking, battlefield target tracking.

## 2.2 Major Application Domains (Detailed Breakdown)

### Domain 1: Military Applications
- **Why WSN fits:** Rapid deployment, self-organization, fault tolerance, dense coverage for enemy intrusion detection.
- **Examples:** battlefield surveillance, target tracking, nuclear/biological/chemical attack detection, smart munitions.
- **Design Drivers:** **High security**, low-latency delivery, robustness against node capture, mobility support.
- **Typical Data Rate:** Low to moderate (audio, vibration).
- **Real-world Deployment Reference:** The seminal **DARPA SensIT program** funded research in this domain in the late 1990s.

### Domain 2: Environmental Applications
- **Why WSN fits:** Need for in-situ, long-duration, large-area observation with minimal human intervention.
- **Examples:** forest-fire detection, flood detection, precision agriculture, animal-habitat monitoring, glacier monitoring, pollution monitoring (air, water, soil).
- **Design Drivers:** **Long lifetime** (years), weather-resilient packaging, solar/wind energy harvesting, large-scale coverage (hundreds of square kilometres).
- **Real-world Reference:** **Great Duck Island Project (2002)** — used Mica motes to monitor seabird nesting habitat.

### Domain 3: Health / Medical Applications
- **Why WSN fits:** Enables continuous, unobtrusive, real-time patient monitoring inside hospitals or at home.
- **Examples:** patient monitoring (heart-rate, blood-pressure, oxygen), drug administration tracking, telemonitoring of elderly, body-area networks (BANs), smart hospital asset tracking.
- **Design Drivers:** **Wearable form factor**, ultra-low power (battery or harvested), high reliability, **HIPAA-style data privacy**, biocompatible materials.
- **Latency Bound:** Hard real-time for critical-care monitoring (**sub-second**).

### Domain 4: Home / Industrial Automation
- **Why WSN fits:** Avoids retrofitting wired infrastructure; enables retrofit deployment.
- **Examples:** smart lighting, HVAC control, intrusion alarms, appliance control, smart metering, industrial process control, machine-health monitoring.
- **Design Drivers:** Low cost, interoperability, **IoT gateway integration**, small packet sizes.

### Domain 5: Structural / Civil Engineering Monitoring
- **Why WSN fits:** Embeds into concrete, steel, and composite structures for lifelong health checks.
- **Examples:** bridge vibration monitoring, building tilt and crack monitoring, dam safety, pipeline integrity, aircraft structural health.
- **Design Drivers:** **Harsh-environment packaging**, very long lifetime (10+ years), high-precision accelerometers, time-synchronised sampling.

### Domain 6: Transportation & Smart Cities
- **Why WSN fits:** Distributed sensing of road conditions, traffic flow, parking availability.
- **Examples:** traffic monitoring, vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication, parking-lot occupancy detection, road-icing detection, intelligent transportation systems (ITS).
- **Design Drivers:** **Mobility handling**, high node density in urban canyons, RF interference management.

### Domain 7: Agricultural / Precision Farming
- **Why WSN fits:** Enables site-specific irrigation, fertilization, and pest control at micro-plot level.
- **Examples:** soil moisture monitoring, micro-climate sensing, livestock tracking, greenhouse automation.
- **Design Drivers:** Solar harvesting, very long lifetime, low cost per hectare.

### Domain 8: Underwater, Underground, Multimedia & Mobile WSNs
These are **special-purpose application classes** with extreme design constraints:
- **Underwater WSN (UWSN):** Acoustic communication, low bandwidth (~kbps), long propagation delay, high error rate. Used for oceanographic data collection, tsunami warning, offshore oil-rig monitoring.
- **Underground WSN (UGSN):** RF signals through soil experience severe attenuation; magnetometer-based or seismic sensors are used. Used for earthquake and landslide prediction, mine safety.
- **Multimedia WSN (WMSN):** Image, audio, and video transmission. Requires high bandwidth (**hundreds of kbps to Mbps**), QoS-aware routing, in-network compression.
- **Mobile WSN (MWSN):** Nodes and/or sinks move continuously. Demands adaptive routing, localization under mobility, and energy-efficient mobility management.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Comparison Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, this comparative matrix is the single most-asked application-topic artifact. Memorize the application → constraint mapping.

| Application Domain | Primary Sensor Type | Lifetime Target | Mobility | Security Level | Energy Source | Data Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Military / Battlefield | Acoustic, Seismic, Magnetic, Image | Weeks – Months | Static & Mobile | **Very High** | Battery + Limited Harvesting | Low – Medium |
| Environmental (Forest, Flood) | Temperature, Humidity, Gas, Rain | **2 – 5 Years** | Mostly Static | Medium | Battery + Solar | Very Low |
| Health / Medical | ECG, EEG, Accelerometer, Glucose | Days – Months | **Body-Mobile** | **Very High** | Battery + Body-Harvesting | Low – Medium |
| Home / Industrial | PIR, Light, Temperature, Pressure | 5 – 10 Years | Static | Medium | Battery + Mains | Low |
| Structural Health | Accelerometer, Strain Gauge, Tilt | **10+ Years** | Static | Medium | Battery + Vibration-Harvesting | Low – Medium |
| Transportation / Smart City | Magnetic, Camera, Acoustic, CO₂ | 3 – 7 Years | **Mobile (Vehicles)** | Medium – High | Battery + Solar | Medium – High |
| Agriculture | Soil Moisture, pH, Temperature, Humidity | **2 – 5 Years** | Static | Low | **Solar Dominant** | Very Low |
| Underwater (UWSN) | Pressure, Acoustic, Chemical | Months – Years | Mobile (Drift) | Medium | Battery (Replaceable) | Low (kbps) |
| Underground (UGSN) | Seismic, Magnetometer, Moisture | 1 – 3 Years | Static | Medium | Battery | Very Low |
| Multimedia (WMSN) | **Camera, Microphone** | Months | Mostly Static | High | Battery + Mains | **High (Mbps)** |
| Mobile WSN (MWSN) | Varies | Months | **Fully Mobile** | High | Battery + Harvesting | Varies |

## 2.4 Mapping Applications to WSN Architecture

Each application domain selects an architecture from the three primary WSN topologies:

1. **Star Topology** — single-hop; used in home automation, body-area networks, simple industrial control.
2. **Mesh Topology** — multi-hop, self-healing; used in environmental monitoring, military.
3. **Hybrid (Cluster) Topology** — clustered with cluster-heads aggregating data; used in agriculture, large-scale environmental, smart cities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off Equation (logical form):** For a given application $A$ with required coverage area $A_{cov}$ and per-node sensing radius $r_s$, the *minimum number of nodes* required for full k-coverage is:
>
> $$N_{min} \;\geq\; \frac{A_{cov}}{k \cdot \pi r_s^{\,2}}$$
>
> where $k$ is the desired **coverage degree** ($k = 1$ for simple monitoring, $k \geq 2$ for fault-tolerant military deployments).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Node Count for Application-Driven Coverage

### Problem Statement
A precision-agriculture deployment is required over a rectangular field of dimensions **500 m × 300 m**. Each soil-moisture sensor has a circular sensing radius $r_s = 25$ m. Determine the minimum number of nodes required for (a) single-coverage ($k = 1$) and (b) double-coverage ($k = 2$) to provide fault tolerance against single-node failure.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the deployment area.**
The field is rectangular, so the area is:
$$A_{cov} = L \times W = 500 \text{ m} \times 300 \text{ m} = 150{,}000 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the per-node sensing footprint.**
The sensing region of one node is a circle of radius $r_s = 25$ m, with area:
$$A_{node} = \pi r_s^{\,2} = \pi \times (25)^2 = 625\pi \text{ m}^2 \approx 1963.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the application-driven k-coverage formula.**
For $k$-coverage:
$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{A_{cov}}{k \cdot A_{node}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{150{,}000}{k \cdot 1963.5} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for k = 1 (single coverage, basic monitoring).**
$$N_{min}(k=1) = \left\lceil \frac{150{,}000}{1 \times 1963.5} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 76.4 \right\rceil = 77 \text{ nodes}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate for k = 2 (double coverage, fault tolerance).**
$$N_{min}(k=2) = \left\lceil \frac{150{,}000}{2 \times 1963.5} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 38.2 \right\rceil = 39 \text{ nodes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that doubling the coverage degree does **not** double the node count — the formula yields roughly 39 nodes for 2-coverage vs 77 for 1-coverage. This is because each node now contributes to *two* independent sensing regions. KTU examiners love this conceptual trap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Application-Driven Energy Budget for Network Lifetime

### Problem Statement
A forest-fire-detection WSN consists of **N = 100 nodes** deployed in a cluster topology with **5 cluster heads (CHs)**. Each non-CH node generates a fire event of **1 packet (256 bits)** per minute. Each non-CH node dissipates:
- $E_{tx} = 50$ nJ/bit for transmission
- $E_{rx} = 50$ nJ/bit for reception
- $E_{agg} = 5$ nJ/bit for data aggregation (at CHs only)
- Each node is powered by two AA batteries totalling $E_{bat} = 2 \times 2500$ mAh at $V = 3$ V.

Estimate the network lifetime in days.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert battery energy to Joules.**
A single AA battery stores approximately $E_{cell} = 2500$ mAh $\times 3$ V $= 7500$ mWh. For two cells:
$$E_{bat} = 2 \times 7500 \text{ mWh} = 15000 \text{ mWh} = 15 \text{ Wh}$$

Converting to Joules (1 Wh = 3600 J):
$$E_{bat} = 15 \times 3600 = 54{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Per-node energy consumption per reporting cycle.**
Each non-CH node transmits **256 bits** per minute, so per minute:
$$E_{node,\,min} = E_{tx} \cdot L = 50 \text{ nJ/bit} \times 256 \text{ bits} = 12{,}800 \text{ nJ} = 12.8 \text{ }\mu\text{J}$$

**Step 3 — Lifetime of one non-CH node in minutes.**
$$T_{node} = \frac{E_{bat}}{E_{node,\,min}} = \frac{54{,}000 \text{ J}}{12.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J/min}} = 4.21875 \times 10^{9} \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days.**
$$T_{node} = \frac{4.21875 \times 10^{9}}{60 \times 24} \approx 2{,}929{,}687.5 \text{ days} \approx 8{,}027 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This figure is unrealistic for a real deployment because the simplified model omits idle listening, retransmissions, sensing energy, and clustering overhead. In practice, MAC idle listening alone can consume $50{-}100 \times$ the active transmission energy. KTU examiners will accept the above calculation for a *first-order analytical model*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application-Requirement Decision Table

The following matrix maps an application's **operational requirement** to the **architectural and protocol choice** that satisfies it. This is the highest-yield KTU exam artifact for the Applications topic.

| Application Requirement | Resulting Architecture / Protocol Choice | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Long lifetime (years) | **Clustered topology + Sleep scheduling (S-MAC, B-MAC)** | Duty-cycling cuts idle-listening by $10{-}100 \times$ |
| High data rate (multimedia) | **Single-hop or 2-hop + QoS-aware routing (MMSPEED, ReInForM)** | Multi-hop routing adds jitter; QoS is needed for video |
| Mobile nodes / sinks | **Geographic routing (GPSR) + predictive mobility models** | Topological routing fails; position-based routing scales |
| Harsh RF environment (underwater, underground) | **Acoustic / Seismic transceivers + delay-tolerant networking (DTN)** | RF attenuates severely in soil/water |
| Event-driven (low average load) | **Asynchronous wakeup + event-triggered reporting** | Periodic polling wastes energy when no event occurs |
| Security-critical (military, medical) | **Key pre-distribution (e.g., EG, q-composite) + SPINS protocol** | Asymmetric crypto is too expensive for sensor nodes |
| Coverage-critical (military, environment) | **k-coverage deployment with redundant nodes** | Single coverage fails when a node dies |
| Localization-required (tracking apps) | **RSSI / TDoA / AoA localization + beacon nodes** | Position is the primary data of interest |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Multi-Hop Energy Trade-off (Application-Driven)

### Problem Statement
Show that for a linear chain of $n$ equally spaced nodes each separated by distance $d$, transmitting a packet of length $L$ bits from source to sink, the **multi-hop transmission** dissipates less energy than direct transmission under the standard first-order radio model, and derive the critical distance $d_0$ at which both schemes are equivalent.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the first-order radio model.**
$$E_{tx}(k, d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^2, & d < d_0 \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4, & d \geq d_0 \end{cases}$$
$$E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

**Step 2 — Energy for direct transmission across total distance $D = n \cdot d$.**
$$E_{direct} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot D^4 \quad (D \text{ assumed large})$$

**Step 3 — Energy for multi-hop transmission across $n$ hops of length $d$ each.**
$$E_{multi} = n \cdot \left( L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4 \right) + (n-1) \cdot L \cdot E_{elec}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**
$$\frac{E_{multi}}{E_{direct}} = \frac{ n \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^4 + (2n-1) \cdot E_{elec} }{ \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot (nd)^4 } \cdot 1 \approx \frac{1}{n^{3}} \quad \text{as } d \to \infty$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
For large $d$, multi-hop energy scales as $1/n^3$ relative to direct transmission. This is the **fundamental justification** for multi-hop routing in energy-constrained WSN applications. Environmental and structural-health applications, which demand multi-year lifetimes, **must** adopt multi-hop or clustered architectures.

**Step 6 — Critical distance $d_0$.**
Set $E_{tx,\,fs}(d_0) = E_{tx,\,mp}(d_0)$:
$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference values:** $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² and $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, giving $d_0 \approx 87$ m. Below $d_0$ free-space model is used; above it, the multi-path model applies.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Application-Specific Data Aggregation Gain

### Problem Statement
In a clustered WSN with $N$ nodes per cluster reporting 1 packet each of $L$ bits, the cluster head aggregates the incoming data by a *data-aggregation ratio* $\rho$ (defined as the fraction of incoming data preserved after aggregation; $0 \leq \rho \leq 1$). Compute the energy saved per reporting cycle at the cluster head relative to the no-aggregation case.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Energy without aggregation (CH forwards all N packets raw).**
$$E_{no\,agg} = N \cdot L \cdot E_{tx} + (N-1) \cdot L \cdot E_{rx}$$

**Step 2 — Energy with aggregation (CH receives N packets, emits $\rho \cdot N$ packets).**
$$E_{agg} = \rho N \cdot L \cdot E_{tx} + (N-1) \cdot L \cdot E_{rx}$$

**Step 3 — Energy saved.**
$$\Delta E = E_{no\,agg} - E_{agg} = (1 - \rho) \cdot N \cdot L \cdot E_{tx}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage saving.**
$$\eta = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{no\,agg}} \times 100\% = \frac{(1-\rho) N L E_{tx}}{N L E_{tx} + (N-1) L E_{rx}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration.**
For $N = 50$, $L = 256$ bits, $E_{tx} = E_{rx} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\rho = 0.1$ (i.e., 90% data reduction):
$$\Delta E = (1 - 0.1) \times 50 \times 256 \times 50 \text{ nJ} = 576{,}000 \text{ nJ} = 576 \text{ }\mu\text{J per cycle}$$
$$\eta = \frac{0.9 \times 50 \times 256 \times 50}{50 \times 256 \times 50 + 49 \times 256 \times 50} = \frac{576{,}000}{1{,}203{,}200} \approx 47.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters per application:** Data aggregation is **critical** for environment-monitoring and agriculture applications (where readings are spatially correlated and $\rho$ is very small, e.g., 0.05), but **less effective** for event-detection applications (e.g., military) where each node's report is unique and $\rho \approx 1$. KTU examiners will test whether you understand this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
