# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section mirrors the *exact* KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern: Part A short questions (3 marks each) and Part B long essay questions (14 marks each) with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Wireless Personal Area Network (WPAN). List any two WPAN standards with their typical operating ranges.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A WPAN is a short-range wireless network that interconnects personal devices within a personal operating space of approximately **10 m**, as standardized under **IEEE 802.15**.
>
> **Two Standards (2 marks):**
> 1. **IEEE 802.15.1 (Bluetooth):** Range up to **10 m** (Class 2) or **100 m** (Class 1), data rate 1–3 Mbps.
> 2. **IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee/6LoWPAN):** Range up to **100 m** outdoor, data rate 250 kbps in 2.4 GHz ISM band, optimized for ultra-low-power WSNs.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between BSS and IBSS in IEEE 802.11 WLAN architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | BSS (Basic Service Set) | IBSS (Independent BSS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Access Point (AP)** | Mandatory central controller | No AP — peer-to-peer |
| **Topology** | Star (infrastructure) | Mesh (ad-hoc) |
| **Range Extension** | Can chain via DS to form ESS | Limited to direct STAs |
| **Typical Use** | Office Wi-Fi, campus | Temporary meetings, WSN MANETs |

> **One-line takeaway:** BSS is *infrastructure-mode Wi-Fi*; IBSS is *ad-hoc Wi-Fi* — both are foundational to understanding WSN multi-hop routing (1 extra mark for the WSN link).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You are required to attempt **one full question** (either Question A *or* Question B). The choice below is presented exactly as it would appear on the KTU question paper.

---

### ✅ CHOICE 1 — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an IEEE 802.11 WLAN with neat diagrams. Discuss the roles of BSS, ESS, and DS in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee) device hierarchy. Explain why IEEE 802.15.4 is preferred over IEEE 802.11 for low-rate WSN motes. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Define WLAN and standard:** A WLAN is a radio-based LAN governed by **IEEE 802.11** (Wi-Fi). The 802.11 standard defines the **PHY** (radio modulation) and **MAC** (CSMA/CA) sub-layers. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — BSS (Basic Service Set):** A BSS is the *fundamental building block* of an 802.11 network. It consists of:
- One **Access Point (AP)** acting as a central hub.
- A set of **stations (STAs)** that associate with the AP.
- A unique **BSSID** (the AP's MAC address) identifying the cell.
- Coverage area: typically 30 m indoor / 300 m outdoor.
*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — DS and ESS:** When multiple BSSs must cover a large building, they are interconnected through a **Distribution System (DS)** — usually a wired Ethernet backbone. The union of BSSs + DS forms an **Extended Service Set (ESS)**. The ESS appears to upper layers as a *single logical LAN*, enabling seamless roaming between APs. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — IBSS (Ad-hoc Mode):** In the absence of an AP, two or more STAs can form an **Independent BSS (IBSS)** for direct peer-to-peer communication. IBSS is the conceptual ancestor of WSN multi-hop ad-hoc routing. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the canonical KTU diagram showing BSS, ESS, DS, and IBSS. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — IEEE 802.15.4 device taxonomy:** Two device classes are defined: *(2 marks)*
- **Full-Function Device (FFD):** Implements the *complete* protocol stack. Can act as coordinator, router, or end device. Always-on (typically mains-powered).
- **Reduced-Function Device (RFD):** Implements a *minimal* protocol. Can only be a leaf node; can only talk to one FFD parent.

**Step 2 — Logical hierarchy:** *(2 marks)*
- **PAN Coordinator:** A special FFD, exactly one per PAN, that issues *beacons* and assigns *GTS slots*.
- **Router (FFD):** Forwards frames, may sleep between beacons (synchronized).
- **End Device (RFD):** Sleeps > 99 % of the time, wakes only to transmit or receive.

**Step 3 — Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes (the PAN Coordinator → Router → End Device tree). *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Why 802.15.4 > 802.11 for WSN motes:** *(2 marks)*

| Criterion | 802.15.4 | 802.11 |
|---|---|---|
| Tx current | ~25 mA | ~250 mA |
| Sleep current | ~5 µA | ~10 mA (still high) |
| Battery life on 2xAA | 1–3 years | < 1 day |
| Frame overhead | Tiny (~30 B) | Large (≥ 34 B) |
| Boot time | ~1 ms | > 1 s |
| Optimized for | Energy, cost | Throughput, mobility |

**Conclusion:** 802.15.4 achieves *years* of battery life via aggressive duty cycling, which is infeasible with 802.11.

---

### ✅ CHOICE 2 — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) equation. A Wi-Fi system operates at 5.8 GHz. Compute the path loss at a distance of 200 m. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the average current consumption and battery lifetime of a ZigBee mote with the following parameters and hence justify the role of duty cycling in WSNs. *(7 marks)*

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Battery capacity | 2 × AA alkaline = **2500 mAh** |
| $I_{tx}$ | 28 mA |
| $I_{rx}$ | 18 mA |
| $I_{sl}$ | 6 µA |
| Active cycle | 8 ms tx + 12 ms rx per 1 s |
| Sleep remainder | 980 ms |

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — State Friis' Law:** *(1 mark)*

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi d)^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Derive FSPL:** Path loss is the ratio $L = P_t / P_r$ (with $G_t = G_r = 1$). After substituting $\lambda = c / f$ and converting to dB with the constant $\frac{4\pi}{c}$ evaluated for $f$ in MHz and $d$ in km: *(3 marks)*

$$\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(f_{\text{MHz}}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(d_{\text{km}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution:** Given $f = 5800$ MHz and $d = 0.2$ km: *(2 marks)*

$$\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5800) + 20\log_{10}(0.2)$$

$$20\log_{10}(5800) = 20 \times 3.7634 = 75.27 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(0.2) = 20 \times (-0.6990) = -13.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 75.27 - 13.98 = 93.73 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:** The path loss at 200 m on a 5.8 GHz Wi-Fi link is **≈ 93.7 dB**. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute duty cycles:** *(1 mark)*

$$\delta_1 = \frac{8}{1000} = 0.008, \quad \delta_2 = \frac{12}{1000} = 0.012, \quad 1 - \delta_1 - \delta_2 = 0.980$$

**Step 2 — Average current:** Convert $I_{sl}$ to mA: $6\,\mu\text{A} = 0.006$ mA. *(2 marks)*

$$I_{\text{avg}} = (28)(0.008) + (18)(0.012) + (0.006)(0.980)$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 0.224 + 0.216 + 0.00588 = 0.44588 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Battery lifetime in hours:** *(2 marks)*

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{2500}{0.44588} \approx 5607 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days:** *(1 mark)*

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{5607}{24} \approx 233.6 \text{ days} \approx 7.7 \text{ months}$$

**Step 5 — Justification of duty cycling (1 mark):** Had the node been *always on* in transmit mode, $I_{\text{avg}} = 28$ mA and the lifetime would collapse to $2500/28 \approx 89$ hours. **Duty cycling extends lifetime by a factor of ~63×** — proving why WSN MAC protocols (S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 GTS) are designed around aggressive sleeping.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "WLAN = WSN":** A WSN is *not* a Wi-Fi network. The 802.11 family is the *gateway tier*, not the *mote tier*. (-2 marks typical deduction.)
> 2. **Forgetting units in FSPL:** The constant 32.44 is valid *only* when $f$ is in **MHz** and $d$ is in **km**. Plugging GHz/km or MHz/m silently gives wrong answers. (-2 marks.)
> 3. **Mixing $I_{sl}$ units:** $I_{sl}$ is in **µA**, but the formula uses **mA**. Students frequently forget to convert and get lifetimes off by 1000×. (-2 marks.)
> 4. **Missing the WSN context:** A 14-mark answer that talks only about Wi-Fi and never ties it back to WSN architecture is considered *incomplete* by KTU examiners. (-2 marks.)
> 5. **No diagram in (a)-type questions:** A sketch (even hand-drawn) of BSS/ESS/IBSS is *compulsory* for full marks on 7-mark design questions. (-3 marks if absent.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these before entering the exam hall:**
>
> ☐ **IEEE 802.11** = WLAN, Wi-Fi, high-rate, mains-powered, *gateway* tier of WSNs.
>
> ☐ **IEEE 802.15.4** = WPAN, ZigBee, low-rate, battery-powered, *mote* tier of WSNs. **This is the central standard of the course.**
>
> ☐ **FSPL formula** = $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})$. **Units matter.**
>
> ☐ **Duty cycling** = *the* technique that gives WSNs years of battery life. Always compute $I_{\text{avg}}$ using $\delta_1, \delta_2$.
>
> ☐ **BSS** = AP + STAs; **ESS** = BSSs + DS; **IBSS** = ad-hoc, no AP.
>
> ☐ **FFD vs. RFD** = FFD is the router/coordinator (always-on); RFD is the leaf (sleeps 99 %+ of the time).
>
> ☐ **PAN Coordinator** is unique per PAN and *never sleeps*.
>
> ☐ **ZigBee 16-bit short address** ⇒ up to **65 536** nodes per PAN.
>
> ☐ **Coexistence:** Both 802.11 and 802.15.4 share the 2.4 GHz ISM band — this is why interference mitigation is studied later in Module 2.
>
> ☐ **Path-loss exponent $n$** is 2 (free space) → 4 (obstructed indoor). Use the log-distance model when FSPL is too optimistic.
>
> ☐ **Beacons, GTS, CSMA/CA** are the three pillars of IEEE 802.15.4 MAC — learn the order of operations for any MAC-level question.
>
> ☐ **Cite the standard number** in every answer. KTU examiners reward IEEE standard citations generously (+1 mark per occurrence).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of WLANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of WLANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a WLAN

A **Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN)** is a flexible data communication system that uses **radio frequency (RF)**, **infrared (IR)**, or **microwave** electromagnetic wave propagation as the physical layer medium to interconnect network nodes within a limited geographical area, typically within a building, campus, or small radius, without the need for wired infrastructure between end devices.

Formally, the **IEEE 802.11** standard (commercially branded as **Wi-Fi**) defines a WLAN as a set of stations (STAs) that communicate over a shared wireless medium governed by the **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sublayer of the Data Link Layer and a defined **Physical Layer (PHY)** specification operating in unlicensed industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) radio bands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The fundamentals section of WLANs in KTU's WSN course establishes the architectural and protocol-level baseline required to understand how wireless sensor nodes interact with infrastructure-based networks. Sensor nodes often transmit their collected data to an AP/gateway that is part of a WLAN.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Invisible Postman"

Imagine a busy office floor where no one is allowed to stand up and walk to a colleague's desk to deliver a memo. Instead, every employee (the **wireless station**) has a personal voice that carries across the room. To avoid everyone shouting at once (which would create chaos), they have a strict rule: **"Listen first, speak only when the room is silent, and wait for acknowledgement."**

This is precisely how a WLAN works:
- The **"room"** is the shared radio spectrum (e.g., the 2.4 GHz band).
- The **"voice"** is the radio signal carrying data frames.
- The **"listen before speaking"** rule is the **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** protocol.
- The **"wait for acknowledgement"** is the **ACK frame** exchange at the MAC layer.
- The **"head of the office"** is the **Access Point (AP)** that coordinates the chatter, akin to a referee in a tennis match.

Just as you wouldn't expect a memo shouted across a stadium to reach one person clearly, WLAN signals are confined to a **bounded coverage zone** (the **Basic Service Set**).

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of a WLAN

| Metric | Standard Value/Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Frequency Bands | **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz** (unlicensed ISM) | Determines range, penetration, and capacity |
| Typical Coverage Radius | **30 m to 100 m** (indoor); up to **300 m** (outdoor) | Defines the size of a Basic Service Set (BSS) |
| Maximum Theoretical Throughput | **11 Mbps (802.11b) → 9.6 Gbps (802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6E)** | Bandwidth ceiling per radio link |
| Channel Bandwidths | **20 MHz, 40 MHz, 80 MHz, 160 MHz, 320 MHz** | Wider channels = more data per OFDM symbol |
| Modulation Schemes | DSSS, OFDM, OFDMA, 1024-QAM | Higher order QAM = denser bits per Hertz |
| Half-Duplex Nature | **All commercial 802.11 radios are half-duplex** | A station cannot transmit and receive simultaneously on the same channel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Wi-Fi Alliance vs IEEE 802.11**
> The **IEEE 802.11** standard specifies the technical PHY and MAC rules. The **Wi-Fi Alliance** is the industry consortium that certifies interoperability of products using the IEEE 802.11 family. Thus, "Wi-Fi" is the commercial certification mark; "IEEE 802.11" is the underlying engineering standard.

---

## 1.4 Why WLANs Matter in WSN (Module Context)

In **Wireless Sensor Networks**, sensor motes (e.g., TelosB, MicaZ, Waspmote) often have constrained transmit power and a low data rate. They typically form an *ad-hoc mesh* (covered later in this module), but ultimately their aggregated data is "sunk" to a **base station or gateway** that connects to a **WLAN/Wi-Fi access point** for onward transmission to the Internet or a local server. Thus, every WSN engineer must understand how the WLAN side works to design efficient gateways, handoff procedures, and MAC-bridging protocols.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WLAN Architectural Topologies (Building Blocks)

A WLAN is constructed from well-defined building blocks, standardized in **IEEE 802.11-2020 (Clause 4 — Frame Formats & Architecture)**.

### 2.1.1 Station (STA)
Any device containing a wireless network interface card (NIC) and conforming to the 802.11 MAC/PHY. Examples: laptops, smartphones, sensor motes with Wi-Fi chips.

### 2.1.2 Basic Service Set (BSS) — The Cell
A **BSS** is the fundamental building block of an 802.11 network — it is the cell of coverage. Two variants exist:

- **Independent BSS (IBSS) / Ad-hoc Mode**: Stations communicate directly (peer-to-peer) without an AP. Used rarely; common in older device-pairing and in some WSN bootstrap stages.
- **Infrastructure BSS**: An AP acts as a central relay. All client traffic passes *through* the AP, even between two clients (a deliberate design choice for collision domain control and security policy enforcement).

### 2.1.3 Distribution System (DS)
The DS is the *backbone* that interconnects multiple BSSs via their APs. In practice, the DS is an **Ethernet wired LAN** or a **wireless mesh** (now standardized in IEEE 802.11s). The DS enables mobility across APs.

### 2.1.4 Extended Service Set (ESS)
An **ESS** is the union of one or more BSSs interconnected by a common DS. The ESS appears to upper-layer protocols (e.g., IP) as a single logical network, allowing seamless roaming.

### 2.1.5 Service Set Identifier (SSID)
A 32-byte, case-sensitive alphanumeric string (commonly 0–32 octets) that names the network. The SSID is broadcast in **Beacon frames** by the AP every **100 ms** by default (the **Beacon Interval**).

---

## 2.2 The IEEE 802.11 Protocol Family — Evolution Timeline

| Standard | Year | Band | Max Rate | PHY Technique | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 802.11 (legacy) | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | 1–2 Mbps | DSSS, FHSS | The original Wi-Fi |
| 802.11a | 1999 | 5 GHz | 54 Mbps | OFDM | First OFDM PHY, less crowded 5 GHz |
| 802.11b | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | 11 Mbps | DSSS / CCK | Higher rate at 2.4 GHz |
| 802.11g | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | 54 Mbps | OFDM | Backward compatible with 802.11b |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 600 Mbps | OFDM + MIMO | MIMO, 40 MHz channels, frame aggregation |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 2013 | 5 GHz | 6.93 Gbps | OFDM + MU-MIMO | Downlink MU-MIMO, 160 MHz, 256-QAM |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E) | 2019 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 9.6 Gbps | **OFDMA + BSS Coloring** | High-density, IoT, deterministic scheduling |
| 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7) | 2024 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 46 Gbps | EHT-MU-MIMO, 320 MHz, 4096-QAM | Multi-link operation (MLO) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A frequent 14-mark question asks you to "Compare and contrast 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax." Memorize the *band*, *maximum data rate*, *PHY technique*, and *one unique feature* of each — these are the four columns examiners almost always require.

---

## 2.3 The 802.11 MAC Layer — CSMA/CA, DCF, and PCF

Because radio is a *shared, half-duplex, broadcast medium*, **collision detection (CSMA/CD — used in Ethernet)** is impractical. A transmitting station **cannot hear its own signal** while transmitting, and the hidden/exposed terminal problems are severe in RF environments.

### 2.3.1 Distributed Coordination Function (DCF) — The Default

DCF is the cornerstone of Wi-Fi MAC. It uses **CSMA/CA with binary exponential backoff**.

**Step-by-step logic of DCF:**

1. **Carrier Sense**: Before transmitting, the STA senses the channel for a **DIFS (Distributed Inter-Frame Space) = 34 µs** at 802.11b (varies by PHY). If idle, it proceeds.
2. **Backoff Counter**: Even when idle, the STA waits an additional random backoff of $CW$ slots, where $CW$ is the **Contention Window**.
3. **Transmit**: After the countdown reaches zero, the frame is sent.
4. **ACK**: The receiver waits a **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space = 10 µs)** and transmits an acknowledgement. The SIFS is shorter than DIFS, ensuring ACK wins the next contention.
5. **Collision Recovery**: If no ACK arrives, the sender assumes collision and doubles $CW$ (Binary Exponential Backoff) up to $CW_{max}$.

### 2.3.2 RTS/CTS Handshake (Optional but Vital)
To defeat the **Hidden Node Problem**, the sender may first send a **Request-To-Send (RTS)** frame. The AP replies with **Clear-To-Send (CTS)**. All stations hearing CTS (or RTS) defer for the **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** duration. RTS frames are 20 bytes; CTS are 14 bytes — much smaller than data, so collisions are cheap.

### 2.3.3 Point Coordination Function (PCF) — Legacy Centralized
PCF was an optional, AP-polled, contention-free service. It is **practically obsolete** in modern Wi-Fi but is often asked in KTU exams. Replaced by **HCF (Hybrid Coordination Function)** with HCCA/EDCA in 802.11e (QoS amendments).

---

## 2.4 The 802.11 Physical Layer Concepts

### 2.4.1 Spread Spectrum (802.11b era)
Two main flavors were used:
- **DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum)**: Each data bit is XORed with an 11-chip **Barker sequence**. Spreads bandwidth from ~20 MHz nominal. Provides processing gain and immunity to narrowband interference.
- **FHSS (Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum)**: Carrier hops among 79 channels (1 MHz wide) in pseudo-random order, 2.5 hops/sec (US) or slower elsewhere. Largely deprecated.

### 2.4.2 OFDM (802.11a/g/n/ac/ax)
**Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing** divides the channel into many orthogonal subcarriers (52 usable in 802.11a/g; up to 4096 in 802.11ax). Each subcarrier carries a low-rate QAM symbol. Advantages: resilience to multipath, spectral efficiency, MIMO compatibility.

### 2.4.3 MIMO and MU-MIMO
**MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output)** uses multiple antennas at transmitter and receiver to create spatial streams. With **$N_T \times N_R$** antennas, the maximum number of spatial streams is $\min(N_T, N_R)$. The Shannon-inspired capacity gain scales roughly as:

$$ C \approx B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \text{SNR} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_S} \vert \mathbf{H}_i \vert^2 \right) \text{ bits/s}$$

where $N_S$ is the number of spatial streams and $\mathbf{H}$ is the MIMO channel matrix. **MU-MIMO (802.11ac/ax)** allows the AP to send to **multiple clients simultaneously** in the same OFDM symbol — a true "multi-user" gain.

### 2.4.4 OFDMA (802.11ax)
A revolutionary change: instead of one client occupying the full channel for one time period, OFDMA subdivides the channel into **Resource Units (RUs)** in frequency and time. Up to **9 clients** (in 20 MHz) can be scheduled in one TXOP, ideal for IoT and high-density deployments — directly relevant to WSNs.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEEPER FORMULAS — Memorize These for Module 1**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon-Hartley: $C$=capacity (bps), $B$=bandwidth (Hz) | Theoretical Wi-Fi throughput ceiling |
| 2 | $\text{DIFS} = \text{SIFS} + 2 \cdot \text{SlotTime}$ | Inter-frame spaces (SIFS ≈ 10 µs, Slot = 9 µs) | DCF timing |
| 3 | $CW_{new} = \min(2 \cdot (CW_{old}+1)-1, CW_{max})$ | Contention window backoff | DCF collision recovery |
| 4 | $\text{MIMO Capacity} \approx N_S \cdot B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}/N_S)$ | $N_S$ = spatial streams | 802.11n/ac/ax throughput |
| 5 | $N_{subcarriers} = 52 \text{ (legacy)},\; 256 \text{ (802.11ac)},\; 2048 \text{ (802.11ax)}$ | FFT size for OFDM | PHY performance analysis |
| 6 | $EIRP = P_T + G_T - L_C$ | $P_T$ tx power, $G_T$ tx gain, $L_C$ cable loss | Link budget & FCC compliance |
| 7 | $\text{Path Loss (FSPL)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz) | Free-space path loss | Coverage planning |
| 8 | $t_{prop} = d / c$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | Propagation delay | Slot timing justification |
| 9 | $N_{users\ per\ OFDMA\ frame} \le \lfloor N_{subcarriers} / N_{RU} \rfloor$ | RU allocation | 802.11ax efficiency |
| 10 | $P_{tx,\max} = 20 \text{ dBm (100 mW) — EIRP for 2.4 GHz in most countries}$ | Regulatory limit | Compliance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict LaTeX Rule Applied:** All absolute-value and conditional notations use `\vert` and `\mid` rather than the pipe `|` to keep the table cleanly parseable. The Shannon equation is shown in its simplest form; MIMO versions use the same alphabet but with matrix norms.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | WLAN Feature Used |
|---|---|
| Smart home IoT | Wi-Fi 6 OFDMA + BSS Coloring for dense node coexistence |
| Industrial WSN gateways | 802.11n/ax APs aggregating Zigbee/BLE/LoRa data |
| Hospital asset tracking | Roaming across ESS without dropping UDP streams |
| Campus-wide WSN backhaul | 802.11s mesh as the DS |
| Vehicular V2X gateways | 802.11p (DSRC) — derived directly from 802.11 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shannon Capacity of a Single Wi-Fi Channel

We derive the **theoretical maximum throughput** achievable on a single non-interfering Wi-Fi channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal-to-noise ratio $\text{SNR}$ (linear, not dB).

**Starting point — Shannon-Hartley Theorem (1948):**

$$ C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \text{[bits/second]} $$

**Line-by-line reasoning:**

1. The theorem postulates a channel corrupted by **additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN)** of power $N$ (watts), and a received signal of power $S$ (watts). $\text{SNR} = S/N$.
2. The number of *distinguishable symbols* the channel can support per second is proportional to the bandwidth $B$ (in Hz). Nyquist sampling gives $2B$ samples per second.
3. Shannon showed the *maximum reliable bits per sample* is $\log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
4. Multiplying gives capacity per second.

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

A **2.4 GHz Wi-Fi channel** of width $B = 20$ MHz operating in a typical office environment has $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB $= 10^{30/10} = 1000$ (linear).

$$ C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) $$

$$ \log_2(1001) = \frac{\ln(1001)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{6.9088}{0.6931} \approx 9.966 \text{ bits/sample} $$

$$ C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 9.966 = 1.993 \times 10^8 \text{ bps} = 199.3 \text{ Mbps} $$

The achieved throughput of 802.11g is 54 Mbps; Shannon tells us the **theoretical ceiling** is **~199 Mbps** in this SNR regime, leaving room for 802.11n (with MIMO) to push further. This is the *gap* between Shannon capacity and real PHY rate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Contention Window Evolution (Binary Exponential Backoff)

The DCF algorithm updates the **Contention Window (CW)** after every failed transmission attempt.

**Step 1 — Initial state:** After a successful transmission, the CW resets to $CW_{min}$ (e.g., **31 slots** for 802.11b/g/n, 15 slots for 802.11ax).

**Step 2 — After 1st failure:** $CW = 2 \cdot (CW_{min} + 1) - 1$.

**Step 3 — After $i$-th failure (general form):**

$$ CW_i = \min\!\left( 2^i \cdot (CW_{min} + 1) - 1,\; CW_{max} \right) $$

**Step 4 — Compute for 802.11g (CW_min = 31, CW_max = 1023):**

| Attempt $i$ | $CW_i$ (slots) |
|---|---|
| 0 (success) | 31 |
| 1 | 63 |
| 2 | 127 |
| 3 | 255 |
| 4 | 511 |
| 5 | 1023 |
| 6+ | 1023 (capped) |

**Backoff Time** before $i$-th retransmission:

$$ T_{backoff,i} = \text{rand}(0, CW_i) \cdot \text{SlotTime} $$

For 802.11g, **SlotTime = 9 µs**, so the worst-case backoff on the 5th retry is $1023 \times 9 = 9207\ \mu s \approx 9.2$ ms.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Coverage Radius via Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

The maximum range of a WLAN cell depends on the **link budget**. A derivation for isotropic antennas in free space:

$$ FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \quad \text{(d in km, f in MHz)} $$

**Derivation outline (Friis Transmission Equation):**

$$ P_R = P_T \cdot G_T \cdot G_R \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 $$

Convert to dB and use $\lambda = c / f$:

$$ P_R\text{[dBm]} = P_T\text{[dBm]} + G_T\text{[dBi]} + G_R\text{[dBi]} - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44 $$

Rearranging the path loss component yields the FSPL formula.

**Numerical Example:**

AP transmit power $P_T = 20$ dBm, antenna gains $G_T = G_R = 2$ dBi, RX sensitivity $S = -90$ dBm. Frequency $f = 2.4$ GHz.

Maximum allowed path loss:

$$ L_{max} = P_T + G_T + G_R - S = 20 + 2 + 2 - (-90) = 114 \text{ dB} $$

$$ 114 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44 $$

$$ 20\log_{10}(d) = 114 - 32.44 - 20\log_{10}(2400) $$

$$ 20\log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 = 67.60 \text{ dB} $$

$$ 20\log_{10}(d) = 114 - 32.44 - 67.60 = 13.96 \text{ dB} $$

$$ d = 10^{13.96/20} = 10^{0.698} = 4.98 \text{ km (free-space)} $$

Indoors, walls and multipath typically reduce this to **30–50 m**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: OFDM Symbol Duration and Subcarrier Spacing

For OFDM PHY in 802.11a/g (legacy):

- Channel bandwidth: $B = 20$ MHz
- FFT size: $N_{FFT} = 64$
- Number of data subcarriers: $N_D = 48$
- Number of pilot subcarriers: $N_P = 4$
- **Subcarrier spacing:**

$$ \Delta f = \frac{B}{N_{FFT}} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{64} = 312.5 \text{ kHz} $$

- **Useful OFDM symbol duration:**

$$ T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{312.5 \times 10^3} = 3.2\ \mu s $$

- **Cyclic prefix (guard interval) duration:** $T_{CP} = 0.8\ \mu s$ (1/4 of $T_u$).

- **Total OFDM symbol duration:** $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP} = 4.0\ \mu s$.

This explains the per-symbol rate:

$$ R_{symbol} = \frac{1}{T_{sym}} = 250{,}000 \text{ symbols/sec} $$

With 64-QAM (6 bits per subcarrier, 48 data subcarriers), per-symbol payload = $48 \times 6 = 288$ bits. Coding rate 3/4 yields $288 \times 3/4 = 216$ bits/symbol. Multiplying:

$$ R_{max} = 216 \text{ bits} \times 250{,}000 \text{ sym/s} = 54 \text{ Mbps} $$

This is exactly the maximum 802.11a/g rate — derived from first principles.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DCF vs PCF vs HCF

| Parameter | DCF (802.11 baseline) | PCF (802.11 legacy optional) | HCF/EDCA (802.11e) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access Method | CSMA/CA, contention-based | Polled, contention-free | Hybrid |
| QoS Support | None (best-effort) | Limited | 4 Access Categories (Voice, Video, Best Effort, Background) |
| Coordinator | None (distributed) | AP acts as Point Coordinator (PC) | Hybrid Coordinator (HC) in AP |
| Throughput Fairness | First-come-first-served | Time-bounded service | Prioritized contention |
| Use Today | Always active baseline | Obsolete | Default in 802.11n/ac/ax |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common 14-marker asks "Explain CSMA/CA and compare DCF with PCF." Always draw the **timing diagram** (SIFS, DIFS, backoff slots) — the *spacing* of inter-frame spaces is what distinguishes DCF and PCF timing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — IEEE 802.11 Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Station STA1] -->|Wi-Fi Radio| nodeB[Access Point AP1]
    nodeC[Station STA2] -->|Wi-Fi Radio| nodeB
    nodeD[Station STA3] -->|Wi-Fi Radio| nodeB
    nodeE[Station STA4] -->|Wi-Fi Radio| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Ethernet Backbone| nodeF[Distribution System DS]
    nodeF -->|Wired or 802.11s Mesh| nodeG[Access Point AP2]
    nodeG -->|Wi-Fi Radio| nodeH[Station STA5]
    nodeG -->|Wi-Fi Radio| nodeI[Station STA6]
    nodeG -->|Wi-Fi Radio| nodeJ[Station STA7]
    nodeB -.Beacon Frames.-> nodeA
    nodeB -.Beacon Frames.-> nodeC
    nodeB -.Beacon Frames.-> nodeD
    nodeB -.Beacon Frames.-> nodeE
    nodeG -.Beacon Frames.-> nodeH
    nodeG -.Beacon Frames.-> nodeI
    nodeG -.Beacon Frames.-> nodeJ
    nodeA -.Association Auth.-.-> nodeB
    nodeG --- nodeB
    nodeK[External Internet Router] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeK
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The two APs (AP1, AP2) form **two BSSs** (BSS1, BSS2). Together with the DS, they constitute an **ESS**.
- The **Distribution System** (DS) is typically Ethernet but in modern mesh deployments is an 802.11s wireless mesh.
- Stations in BSS1 share the same channel — they contend for medium access via DCF.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DCF Channel Access Timeline (Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA as Sender Station
    participant MED as Wireless Medium
    participant RX as Receiver AP
    STA->>STA: Sense channel for DIFS = 34 microseconds
    STA->>STA: Choose random backoff counter from 0 to CW
    STA->>STA: Decrement counter while channel is idle
    Note over STA,MED: Counter reached zero
    STA->>MED: Transmit RTS frame
    MED->>RX: Forward RTS
    RX->>MED: Transmit CTS frame after SIFS
    MED->>STA: Forward CTS
    STA->>MED: Transmit DATA frame after SIFS
    MED->>RX: Forward DATA
    RX->>MED: Transmit ACK frame after SIFS
    MED->>STA: Forward ACK
    Note over STA,MED: Frame successfully delivered
    STA->>STA: Reset CW to CWmin
```

**Key observations:**
- DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space) is **longer** than SIFS. This guarantees that an ACK, sent after SIFS, is always ready to transmit before any new contender can finish its DIFS wait.
- The whole exchange uses **SIFS** (Short Inter-Frame Space) for "I am already in dialogue" responses.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — OFDM Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Input Data Bits] --> nodeB[Convolutional Encoder Rate 1/2 2/3 3/4]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Interleaver]
    nodeC --> nodeD[BPSK QPSK 16QAM 64QAM Mapper]
    nodeD --> nodeE[IFFT 64 point]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Insert Cyclic Prefix 0.8 microseconds]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Digital to Analog Converter]
    nodeG --> nodeH[RF Front End Antenna]
    nodeH -->|Radio Wave 2.4 or 5 GHz| nodeI[Wireless Channel]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Receiver Antenna RF Front End]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[ADC]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Remove Cyclic Prefix]
    nodeL --> nodeM[FFT 64 point]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Channel Estimation Equalization]
    nodeN --> nodeO[QAM Demapper]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Deinterleaver]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Viterbi Decoder]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Output Data Bits]
```

**Block-by-block flow:**

1. **Encoder**: Adds redundancy for forward error correction (FEC).
2. **Interleaver**: Spreads burst errors across multiple codewords.
3. **Mapper**: Maps groups of bits to complex QAM symbols.
4. **IFFT**: Generates the orthogonal subcarriers — this is the OFDM magic.
5. **Cyclic Prefix (CP)**: A copy of the end of the symbol prepended, to absorb multipath echoes.
6. **DAC + RF**: Sends the analog waveform to the antenna.

The reverse chain is followed at the receiver.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Hidden Node Problem & RTS/CTS Solution

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Station A] -->|Signal to B| nodeB[AP Node B]
    nodeC[Station C] -.->|Cannot hear A so it is HIDDEN| nodeB
    nodeA -.->|Wants to send to B| nodeB
    nodeC -.->|Wants to send to B at same time| nodeB
    nodeB2[Collision at B] --- nodeA
    nodeB2 --- nodeC
    nodeA -->|Solution: Send RTS first| nodeB
    nodeB -->|CTS heard by all nodes| nodeA
    nodeB -->|CTS heard by all nodes| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Defers for NAV duration| nodeB
    nodeA -->|Transmits DATA safely| nodeB
```

**Engineering insight:**
The hidden node problem occurs because two stations (A and C) are both in range of the AP (B) but **out of range of each other**. Without RTS/CTS, A and C can transmit simultaneously and collide at the AP. By having *all* nodes hear the CTS, C learns to back off.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — BSS, DS, and ESS Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[BSS1 - Basic Service Set 1] --> nodeB[AP1]
    nodeC[BSS2 - Basic Service Set 2] --> nodeD[AP2]
    nodeE[BSS3 - Basic Service Set 3] --> nodeF[AP3]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Distribution System DS - Typically Ethernet]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Extended Service Set ESS - One Logical Network]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Portal to External Network - Router]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Internet or Corporate WAN]
    nodeB -.Same SSID.-> nodeD
    nodeD -.Same SSID.-> nodeF
    nodeF -.Same SSID.-> nodeB
```

**Interpretation for KTU exams:**
- The **SSID** is the *network name* of the ESS, common across all APs in the ESS.
- A **roaming** client sees multiple BSSs but logically remains in one ESS.
- The **DS** carries frames between APs using 4 MAC addresses (the 802.11 frame format has 4 address fields for this exact reason).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term BSS in the context of IEEE 802.11. Distinguish between an IBSS and an Infrastructure BSS.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Basic Service Set (BSS)** is the fundamental building block of an IEEE 802.11 WLAN, defining the cell of wireless coverage where stations can directly communicate. Each BSS is identified by a unique **BSSID**, which is the MAC address of the Access Point (in infrastructure mode) or a randomly generated 46-bit value (in ad-hoc mode).

| Feature | IBSS (Independent / Ad-hoc) | Infrastructure BSS |
|---|---|---|
| Access Point | None | Required |
| Topology | Peer-to-peer (mesh of stations) | Star (AP at center) |
| Direct STA-to-STA frames | Allowed | Must go through AP |
| Use case | Quick pairing, WSN bootstrap | Enterprise / home Wi-Fi |
| Power efficiency | Lower (each STA relays) | Higher (STA talks to AP only) |

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for distinction.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the function of the DCF Inter-Frame Space (DIFS) in CSMA/CA? Why is SIFS shorter than DIFS?**

**Model Answer:**

**DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** is the fixed time a station must sense the medium as idle *after* the previous frame before it can begin its backoff countdown or transmit. Its primary function is to give **higher priority to acknowledgment and other control frames** by ensuring the channel remains quiet long enough for those frames to win the next contention.

**SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)** is the gap between receiving a frame and responding to it (e.g., ACK, CTS). It is *shorter* than DIFS so that the *recipient* of a frame can always begin its reply before any other station can finish its DIFS wait, guaranteeing uninterrupted frame exchange sequences.

The relationship: $\text{DIFS} = \text{SIFS} + 2 \cdot \text{SlotTime}$.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for DIFS role, 1.5 for SIFS-DIFS relationship and priority reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. They typically have sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks, OR are holistic 14-mark descriptive problems. The model answers below use the sub-part convention.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the IEEE 802.11 MAC layer**, including the **DCF** and **PCF** coordination functions. Compare their key features in a table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wi-Fi access point transmits at **$P_T = 100$ mW** with a transmit antenna gain of **$G_T = 5$ dBi** and a receiver antenna gain of **$G_R = 2$ dBi** on the **2.4 GHz** ISM band. The mobile station has a receiver sensitivity of **$-85$ dBm**. Ignoring cable losses, calculate the **maximum free-space line-of-sight range** of this WLAN link. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Architecture of IEEE 802.11 MAC Layer:**

The 802.11 MAC sublayer is divided into two main coordination functions sitting on top of the PHY:

1. **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** — The default, contention-based access mechanism. Uses CSMA/CA + Binary Exponential Backoff. Every station competes equally for the medium.
2. **Point Coordination Function (PCF)** — An optional, contention-free, AP-polled mechanism. The AP acts as a Point Coordinator and polls stations in a round-robin manner during contention-free periods.
3. **Hybrid Coordination Function (HCF)** — Added in 802.11e, combines contention-based (EDCA) and controlled-access (HCCA) for QoS.

**Block diagram (drawn in exam as below):**

```
+----------------------------------------+
|  Logical Link Control (802.2)          |
+----------------------------------------+
|  MAC Sublayer                          |
|  +-----------+   +-----------+         |
|  |   DCF     |   |   PCF     |         |
|  | CSMA/CA   |   | Polled    |         |
|  | Backoff   |   | CF Period |         |
|  +-----------+   +-----------+         |
+----------------------------------------+
|  PHY Layer (DSSS / OFDM / MIMO)        |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | DCF | PCF |
|---|---|---|
| Access Type | Contention-based | Contention-free (polled) |
| Coordinator | None (distributed) | Point Coordinator (in AP) |
| Timings | DIFS, SIFS, Backoff | PIFS, SIFS, CF polls |
| QoS | Best-effort | Time-bounded (limited) |
| Implementation | Mandatory | Optional |
| Use in modern Wi-Fi | Always active | Obsolete |

*(7 marks — 2 for architecture diagram, 3 for DCF/PCF explanation, 2 for comparison table.)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $P_T = 100$ mW $= 10\log_{10}(100) = 20$ dBm
- $G_T = 5$ dBi, $G_R = 2$ dBi
- $f = 2.4$ GHz $= 2400$ MHz
- Receiver sensitivity $S = -85$ dBm

**Step 1 — Maximum allowed path loss (link budget):**

$$ L_{max} = P_T + G_T + G_R - S $$

$$ L_{max} = 20 + 5 + 2 - (-85) = 112 \text{ dB} $$

**Step 2 — Free-space path loss formula:**

$$ FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \quad \text{(d in km, f in MHz)} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$:**

$$ 112 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44 $$

$$ 20\log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 = 67.60 \text{ dB} $$

$$ 20\log_{10}(d) = 112 - 32.44 - 67.60 = 11.96 \text{ dB} $$

$$ d = 10^{11.96/20} = 10^{0.598} \approx 3.96 \text{ km} $$

**Answer:** The maximum free-space line-of-sight range is approximately **3.96 km**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world reduction:** In urban/indoor environments, multipath, walls, and foliage reduce this to a few hundred meters. The 3.96 km figure is an *upper bound under ideal line-of-sight free-space conditions*.

*(7 marks — 1 for link budget, 2 for FSPL formula setup, 3 for algebraic evaluation, 1 for final answer with units.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **OFDM physical layer** of IEEE 802.11g. With appropriate derivations, show that the **maximum physical layer data rate** of 802.11g is **54 Mbps**, given 64 subcarriers, 64-QAM, coding rate 3/4, and OFDM symbol duration of 4 µs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Hidden Node Problem** in WLANs? How does the **RTS/CTS** mechanism solve it? Use a timing diagram in your explanation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**OFDM in 802.11g:**

OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) splits the 20 MHz channel into 64 orthogonal subcarriers. Of these, 48 carry data, 4 are pilots, and 12 are nulls (guard bands).

**Step 1 — Subcarrier spacing:**

$$ \Delta f = \frac{20 \text{ MHz}}{64} = 0.3125 \text{ MHz} = 312.5 \text{ kHz} $$

**Step 2 — Useful OFDM symbol duration:**

$$ T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = 3.2\ \mu s $$

**Step 3 — Cyclic prefix (guard interval):**

$$ T_{CP} = 0.8\ \mu s \quad \text{(1/4 of } T_u \text{)} $$

**Step 4 — Total OFDM symbol duration:**

$$ T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP} = 3.2 + 0.8 = 4.0\ \mu s $$

**Step 5 — Number of OFDM symbols per second:**

$$ N_{sym/sec} = \frac{1}{T_{sym}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 250{,}000 \text{ symbols/sec} $$

**Step 6 — Bits per OFDM symbol:**
- 48 data subcarriers × 6 bits (64-QAM) = 288 bits/symbol
- Coding rate 3/4 → 288 × 3/4 = **216 bits/symbol**

**Step 7 — Maximum data rate:**

$$ R_{max} = 216 \text{ bits/sym} \times 250{,}000 \text{ sym/sec} = 54{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps} = \mathbf{54\ Mbps} $$

**Hence derived: The 802.11g peak PHY rate is 54 Mbps.** ✓

*(7 marks — 2 for OFDM explanation, 2 for symbol timing derivation, 3 for rate calculation.)*

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Hidden Node Problem:**

The **Hidden Node Problem** occurs when two stations, say **A** and **C**, are both within range of the Access Point **B**, but **A and C cannot sense each other's transmissions** because they are out of each other's radio range (due to distance, walls, or RF obstacles).

Both A and C may sense the medium as idle and transmit simultaneously to B, causing a **collision at B**, even though neither A nor C "saw" the other's transmission.

**Example scenario:** Station A and Station C are in different rooms, both connected to AP in the corridor. A→B and C→B collide at the AP.

**RTS/CTS Solution:**

The IEEE 802.11 standard includes an optional **RTS/CTS handshake** that solves this:

1. Station A sends a short **RTS (Request-To-Send)** frame to AP B (20 bytes).
2. AP B replies with a **CTS (Clear-To-Send)** frame (14 bytes), broadcast to all stations in range.
3. All stations (including C) hearing the CTS set their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** and **defer transmission** for the duration announced.
4. Station A transmits its DATA frame, knowing the medium is reserved.
5. AP B replies with ACK.

**Timing diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
   A          Channel          B (AP)        C
   |            |               |            |
   |--RTS------>|               |            |
   |            |-------------->|            |
   |            |<-----CTS------|            |
   |            |               |---CTS----->| (C sets NAV)
   |--DATA----->|-------------->|            | (deferred)
   |            |<----ACK-------|            |
```

**Result:** Since C hears the CTS, it stays silent. The hidden node conflict is resolved.

*(7 marks — 2 for problem statement, 3 for RTS/CTS mechanism, 2 for timing diagram.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (read before writing your exam):**
>
> 1. **Don't write "CSMA/CD"** for Wi-Fi — it is **CSMA/CA**. Half-duplex radios cannot detect collisions while transmitting, so Collision Detection is impossible.
> 2. **Don't confuse SSID with BSSID.** SSID is the *network name* (string). BSSID is the *MAC address* of the AP (48-bit hex).
> 3. **Don't say "WLANs use 2.4 GHz only."** Modern Wi-Fi uses 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz. IEEE 802.11a is 5 GHz; 802.11b/g are 2.4 GHz; 802.11n is dual-band.
> 4. **Don't forget the units** in path-loss problems. KTU examiners often award partial marks only for correct final units (m or km).
> 5. **Distinguish "Wi-Fi" and "IEEE 802.11".** Wi-Fi is a *certification mark* by the Wi-Fi Alliance. The technical standard is IEEE 802.11. Many students lose 1–2 marks for conflating them.
> 6. **Hidden node ≠ Exposed node.** Don't mix them up in short notes.
> 7. **In Shannon capacity problems**, always convert SNR from dB to *linear* using $\text{SNR}_{lin} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{dB}/10}$ before substituting.
> 8. **Don't claim 802.11g is OFDM-only at 2.4 GHz** without mentioning its *backward compatibility* with 802.11b DSSS — that compatibility is the whole reason it was a market success.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Fundamentals of WLANs" (Module 1)**

- 🔑 **WLAN = IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)**, operating in **unlicensed ISM bands (2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz)**.
- 🔑 **Architecture = STA → BSS → DS → ESS → Portal**, with SSID as the network name and BSSID = AP MAC.
- 🔑 **Two BSS types**: **IBSS (ad-hoc, no AP)** vs **Infrastructure BSS (with AP)**.
- 🔑 **MAC is CSMA/CA + DCF** (mandatory) with optional **RTS/CTS**; PCF is essentially obsolete.
- 🔑 **DIFS = SIFS + 2 × SlotTime**; SIFS grants priority to ACK/CTS.
- 🔑 **Binary Exponential Backoff**: $CW_i = \min(2^i(CW_{min}+1) - 1, CW_{max})$.
- 🔑 **PHY evolved**: DSSS (802.11b) → OFDM (802.11a/g) → MIMO (802.11n) → MU-MIMO (802.11ac) → OFDMA (802.11ax) → MLO (802.11be).
- 🔑 **Shannon limit**: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — the *theoretical* Wi-Fi ceiling.
- 🔑 **OFDM math**: 64 subcarriers, $\Delta f = 312.5$ kHz, $T_u = 3.2\ \mu s$, $T_{CP} = 0.8\ \mu s$, $T_{sym} = 4\ \mu s$.
- 🔑 **802.11g max rate = 54 Mbps** (48 data subcarriers × 6 bits × 3/4 coding × 250k sym/s).
- 🔑 **Hidden Node solved by RTS/CTS**; all stations hearing CTS set their NAV.
- 🔑 **FSPL** $= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz).
- 🔑 **Link budget**: $L_{max} = P_T + G_T + G_R - S_{RX}$ (all in dB/dBm).
- 🔑 **WSN context**: Sensor motes often uplink data to a **WLAN gateway/AP** for onward Internet transmission.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic: Fundamentals of WLANs**
