> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert d to km** in Friis equation → instant **zero marks** for the calculation.
> 2. **Confusing AM_ADDR with PM_ADDR** — students often say "Bluetooth supports 256 devices per piconet". Reality: **7 active**, 255 parked.
> 3. **Wrong hop rate for BLE** — writing 1600 hops/sec for BLE. Reality: **1 hop per connection event** (slow hopping).
> 4. **Treating Classic Bluetooth and BLE as the same** — they are **not interoperable** at the radio layer; dual-mode chips exist but pure BLE devices cannot talk to pure Classic devices.
> 5. **Saying Bluetooth is the same as Wi-Fi** — they coexist (and interfere) but have **different protocols, topologies, and power classes**.
> 6. **Skipping units in numericals** — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and memorize the following bullets before the exam:**

- [x] **Bluetooth** = 2.4 GHz ISM, FHSS, short-range WPAN, governed by **Bluetooth SIG**.
- [x] **Classic BT** = 79 channels × 1 MHz; **BLE** = 40 channels × 2 MHz.
- [x] **Hop rate (Classic)** = **1600 hops/s**; **Slot duration** = **625 µs**.
- [x] **Hop rate (BLE)** = 1 hop per connection event (slow hopping).
- [x] **Piconet** = 1 Master + up to **7 active slaves** (3-bit AM_ADDR) + 255 parked (8-bit PM_ADDR).
- [x] **Scatternet** = Interconnected piconets via **bridge nodes**; bridge is Slave in one, Master in another.
- [x] **Power Classes:** Class 1 = **100 mW**, Class 2 = **2.5 mW**, Class 3 = **1 mW**.
- [x] **BLE peak current** ≈ 15 mA; **sleep current** ≈ 1 µA; **coin-cell life** ≈ months–years.
- [x] **BLE channels 37, 38, 39** = advertising only (edge of band to avoid Wi-Fi).
- [x] **Modulation (BR)** = GFSK; **EDR** adds π/4-DQPSK & 8-DPSK; **BLE** = GFSK (and 2-GFSK in BT 5.0).
- [x] **Protocol Stack** has 3 groups separated by **HCI**: Host (App/Profiles → L2CAP), Controller (Baseband → Radio).
- [x] **BLE Stack has GATT + ATT + GAP + SMP** on top of LL + PHY.
- [x] **Connection interval** $T_{conn} \in [7.5\,\text{ms},\, 4\,\text{s}]$ → duty cycle $\delta = T_{event}/T_{conn}$ governs battery life.
- [x] **Friis path loss** at $d=10$ m, $f=2.44$ GHz = **~60 dB**; at $d=15$ m = **~63.7 dB**.
- [x] **Wavelength at 2.44 GHz** = **12.29 cm**; quarter-wave monopole ≈ **3.07 cm**.
- [x] **Bluetooth Versions 4.0+ (BLE)** revolutionized WSN with low-power sensor motes.
- [x] **Bluetooth Mesh (BT 5.0+)** enables managed-flood sensor networks of thousands of nodes.
- [x] **Difference table** between Classic BT, BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi is **favourite exam question**.
- [x] **Real-world WSN uses** = wearable health monitors, BLE beacons, smart agriculture, industrial condition monitoring.
- [x] **Hop-selection kernel** uses BD_ADDR as seed; full sequence cycles in ~23.3 hours.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless WANs and MANs (Basics Only) - Cellular Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless WANs and MANs (Basics Only) - Cellular Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wireless WAN and Wireless MAN — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless WAN (WWAN)** — A **Wide Area Network** that provides wireless coverage over a very large geographical area (typically cities, countries, or even continents) by relying on a **cellular architecture** of base stations interconnected through a **backbone switching infrastructure (MSC/ core network)**.
> **Wireless MAN (WMAN)** — A **Metropolitan Area Network** that provides wireless coverage over a city or campus (typically up to ~50 km) using technologies such as **WiMAX (IEEE 802.16)** or **LMDS/MMDS** point-to-multipoint microwave links.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, both WWAN and WMAN are introduced in **Module 1** primarily to establish the **conceptual contrast** with **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**, which are characterized by:
- Short range (a few meters to a few hundred meters),
- Dense, energy-constrained, multi-hop deployment,
- No pre-existing infrastructure.

## 1.2 Cellular Architecture — The Heart of WWAN

> [!NOTE]
> **Cellular Architecture** is the **system-level topology** in which a large geographical region is divided into smaller, non-overlapping hexagonal regions called **cells**, each served by a **Base Station (BS)** at its centre. The available radio spectrum is **systematically reused** across non-adjacent cells to maximize capacity, while the **Mobile Switching Center (MSC)** manages call handoffs, authentication, and routing to the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) or the Internet.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Honeycomb of Radio Beaches

Imagine a **busy beach** where every lifeguard station can only watch over a small stretch of sand (its **cell**). If the beach gets crowded, you do **not** make one guard look after the whole beach — you **add more guard stations**, each responsible for a small patch. To avoid two neighbouring stations from shouting on the same radio channel (which causes interference), you **assign channel groups** such that the **closest stations use different channels**, while stations far enough apart can safely **reuse the same channel**.

- The **beach** = service area (city/region).
- The **lifeguard station** = Base Transceiver Station (BTS / BS).
- The **assigned radio group** = frequency channels.
- The **rotation of channels across stations** = **frequency reuse**.
- The **head lifeguard coordinator** = Mobile Switching Center (MSC).

This "patchwork of small coverage areas, each with its own radio resources, and a coordinator stitching them together" is exactly the principle behind **Cellular Architecture**.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Memorize)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cell Radius | $R$ | 1 – 35 km | Determines coverage footprint of a BS |
| Reuse Distance | $D$ | $3R$ to $6R$ | Minimum distance between two co-channel cells |
| Cluster Size | $N$ | 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13 | Number of cells in a frequency-reuse cluster |
| Reuse Ratio | $q$ | $D/R$ | A measure of isolation; higher is better |
| Channel Bandwidth | $W$ | **200 kHz (GSM)**, 5 MHz (3G) | Spectrum used per RF channel |
| S/I Threshold | $(S/I)_{req}$ | **18 dB (GSM)** | Minimum acceptable voice quality |

> [!NOTE]
> The **hexagonal cell model** is a **mathematical idealization**. Real cells are **irregular amoeba-shaped** because of terrain, antenna tilt, and shadowing. Hexagons are chosen because they tessellate perfectly and approximate circular coverage with the **fewest number of cells per cluster**.

## 1.5 WWAN vs WMAN vs WLAN vs WSN — Quick Comparison

| Feature | WLAN | WMAN | WWAN (Cellular) | WSN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 50 – 100 m | 5 – 50 km | 1 – 100+ km | 10 – 200 m |
| Standard | IEEE 802.11 | IEEE 802.16 (WiMAX) | 3GPP (GSM/UMTS/LTE) | IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee |
| Nodes | Tens | Hundreds | Millions of subscribers | Thousands of sensors |
| Power | High (mains) | Moderate | High (BS) / Low (MS) | **Very low (battery)** |
| Topology | Star/Mesh | Point-MP | Cellular | **Multi-hop mesh** |
| Mobility | Walking | Vehicle | **High-speed vehicles** | Mostly static |
| Handoff | Rare | Rare | **Continuous, hard/soft** | Not applicable |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of a Cellular System

A **cellular system** is a layered architecture of four functional planes:

1. **Mobile Station (MS)** — the user equipment (handset, modem). Contains a **SIM** for identity/authentication.
2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS)** — consists of the **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)** (RF + antennas) and the **Base Station Controller (BSC)** (radio resource management, handover decisions).
3. **Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)** — the **Mobile Switching Center (MSC)**, **Home Location Register (HLR)**, **Visitor Location Register (VLR)**, and **Authentication Center (AuC)**.
4. **Transmission / Backbone** — wired links (E1/T1, fibre, microwave) connecting BSs to BSC and BSC to MSC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every concept in WSN Module 1 is the *inverse* of the above**:
> - WSN nodes are **stationary**, energy-starved, and have **no MSC** or central switching.
> - WSNs rely on **distributed multi-hop routing**, not on a pre-built cellular backbone.
> Cellular architecture is the *high-power, infrastructure-rich baseline* that WSNs *replace* when deployment cost, scalability, and energy are constraints.

## 2.2 The Core Problem Cellular Architecture Solves

**Spectrum is scarce.** A single high-power transmitter can cover a city, but uses up the **entire** available spectrum, limiting the number of simultaneous users. Cellular architecture solves this by:

- **Spatial partitioning** of the coverage area into small cells.
- **Frequency reuse** in cells that are sufficiently far apart.
- **Power control** so that transmissions do not bleed into non-adjacent cells.
- **Handover** as users move between cells.

## 2.3 Frequency Reuse — The Heart of Capacity Engineering

### Concept
The total system bandwidth is divided into $N$ channel groups, where $N$ is the **cluster size**. The same channel group is then reused in cells that share the **same position in different clusters**, separated by the **co-channel reuse distance** $D$.

### Constraints for Valid Cluster Sizes $N$

For a hexagonal lattice, valid cluster sizes $N$ are given by:

$$N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2} \quad \text{where } i, j \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **first few valid cluster sizes** are: **3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, 25, 27** …
> - $i=1, j=1 \Rightarrow N=3$
> - $i=2, j=0 \Rightarrow N=4$
> - $i=2, j=1 \Rightarrow N=7$

### Co-channel Reuse Distance

$$D = R \sqrt{3N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

where $R$ is the cell radius and $D$ is the centre-to-centre distance between two nearest co-channel cells.

## 2.4 Interference Analysis — Co-Channel Interference (CCI)

Co-channel interference arises because a **co-channel cell** is **not infinitely far** away. The **Signal-to-Interference ratio** at a mobile near the cell boundary (worst case, distance $\approx D-R$ from the interferer) is, assuming a **path-loss exponent of 4** (typical urban macrocell):

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{i_{0}} \left( \frac{D}{R} \right)^{4} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^{4}}{i_{0}} = \frac{9N^{2}}{i_{0}}$$

where $i_{0}$ is the **number of co-channel interferers in the first tier** (typically **6** in a hexagonal layout).

For a **sectored system** with 120° directional antennas, only **1 or 2 first-tier interferers** are visible, so $i_0$ drops to 1 or 2, dramatically improving S/I.

## 2.5 Cell Splitting and Sectoring — Capacity Engineering

- **Cell Splitting**: Subdivide a congested cell into smaller cells (radius $R \to R/2$), with **transmit power reduced by 12 dB** (i.e., 1/16 of the original) to maintain the same received signal strength. This multiplies capacity by ~4×.
- **Sectoring**: Replace the single omnidirectional antenna at the BS with **3 (or 6) directional antennas** of 120° (or 60°) beamwidth, reducing $i_0$ from 6 to 1 or 2, thereby **increasing S/I by 6–9 dB**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cluster size | $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | $i,j$ are shift parameters | dimensionless |
| 2 | Reuse ratio | $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | $D$=reuse dist, $R$=cell radius | dimensionless |
| 3 | Number of channels per cell | $k = M/N$ | $M$=total channels, $N$=cluster size | channels |
| 4 | S/I ratio (omni) | $S/I = 9N^{2} / i_{0}$ | $i_0$ = first-tier co-channel cells (typ. 6) | dimensionless (in dB: $10\log_{10}$) |
| 5 | S/I ratio (sectored) | $S/I = 9N^{2} / 1$ (120° sector) | $i_0 = 1$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Cell capacity (Erlangs) | $A_{cell} = k \cdot A_{u}$ | $A_u$ = traffic per user in Erlangs | Erlangs |
| 7 | Cell-splitting power ratio | $P_{new} = P_{old} / 16$ | for radius halved | linear (12 dB drop) |
| 8 | Handover rate (approx) | $H = (v \cdot L) / (\pi R)$ | $v$=velocity, $L$=cell perimeter factor | handoffs/sec |
| 9 | Maximum users (sectored) | $M_{max} = 3N \cdot k$ | with 3 sectors/cell | users |
| 10 | Frequency reuse efficiency | $\eta = (1/N) \cdot 100\%$ | fraction of channels per cell | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU pitfall**: Students often write $S/I = (D/R)^2$. The correct exponent is **4** (not 2) because the **received power falls as $d^{-4}$** in a typical urban macrocellular environment with $n=4$. Use $\mathbf{10 \log_{10}(9N^2/6)}$ when converting to **dB**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cellular Network Capacity Planning**: Operators in India (Reliance Jio, Bharti Airtel, Vi) engineer $N$ and antenna patterns to balance coverage and capacity in dense urban markets like Mumbai or Kochi.
- **Frequency Planning in 2G GSM**: Even today, 900 MHz and 1800 MHz bands use classic $N=3$ or $N=4$ reuse.
- **4G LTE / 5G NR**: Move to **OFDM with frequency reuse = 1** (universal frequency reuse) but introduce **Inter-Cell Interference Coordination (ICIC)** and **CoMP** to manage CCI.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $D = R\sqrt{3N}$

**Setup**: Place a cell of interest at the origin. Identify the **nearest** cell using the same channel group. In a hexagonal lattice, after $i$ shifts along one lattice vector and $j$ shifts along the other (at 60° apart), the relative co-ordinate is:

$$\vec{u} = i \vec{a_{1}} + j \vec{a_{2}}$$

with $\vert \vec{a_{1}} \vert = \vert \vec{a_{2}} \vert = \sqrt{3}R$ and the angle between them being $60°$.

The squared distance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{2} &= \vert i\vec{a_{1}} + j\vec{a_{2}} \vert^{2} \\
&= i^{2} \vert \vec{a_{1}} \vert^{2} + j^{2} \vert \vec{a_{2}} \vert^{2} + 2ij \vec{a_{1}} \cdot \vec{a_{2}} \\
&= i^{2} (3R^{2}) + j^{2} (3R^{2}) + 2ij (3R^{2})\cos 60° \\
&= 3R^{2} (i^{2} + j^{2} + ij)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{2} &= 3N R^{2} \\
D &= R \sqrt{3N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering insight**: As $N$ grows, $D/R$ grows, meaning **co-channel cells are spaced further apart**, which **reduces interference** but **lowers per-cell capacity** ($k = M/N$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: S/I Ratio in a Co-Channel-Interference-Limited System

Assume the received power follows the **two-ray ground reflection / Okumura–Hata model** with path-loss exponent $n=4$:

$$P_{r}(d) = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} \cdot G_{r} \cdot \frac{h_{t}^{2} h_{r}^{2}}{d^{4}} \cdot K$$

where $K$ is a constant. The desired signal arrives from a BS at distance $\approx R$ (mobile near the cell edge), and the **6 first-tier co-channel interferers** are at distance $\approx D$.

Assuming the mobile receives from all interferers with **equal power** (worst case):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I} &= \frac{P_{r}(R)}{i_{0} \cdot P_{r}(D)} \\
&= \frac{P_{t} G K / R^{4}}{i_{0} \cdot P_{t} G K / D^{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{i_{0}} \left( \frac{D}{R} \right)^{4} \\
&= \frac{1}{i_{0}} (\sqrt{3N})^{4} \\
&= \frac{9N^{2}}{i_{0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For omnidirectional: $i_0 = 6$, hence:

$$\left( \frac{S}{I} \right)_{\text{omni}} = \frac{9N^{2}}{6} = 1.5 N^{2}$$

For 120° sectoring: $i_0 = 1$, hence:

$$\left( \frac{S}{I} \right)_{\text{120° sector}} = 9N^{2}$$

In dB (multiply by $10\log_{10}$):

- Omni: $10\log_{10}(1.5 N^{2})$ dB
- Sector: $10\log_{10}(9 N^{2})$ dB ≈ **9.5 dB better than omni**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — $N = 7$ Cluster

**Problem**: A GSM operator uses $N = 7$ with omnidirectional antennas. Verify that the S/I meets the GSM requirement of **18 dB minimum** for voice.

**Step 1** — Compute the geometric S/I:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I} &= \frac{9 N^{2}}{i_{0}} = \frac{9 \times 49}{6} = 73.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Convert to dB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( \frac{S}{I} \right)_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(73.5) \\
&\approx 10 \times 1.866 \\
&\approx 18.66 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Compare to GSM requirement:

$$18.66 \text{ dB} \; > \; 18 \text{ dB} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Conclusion**: An $N=7$ cluster with omnidirectional antennas is the **canonical GSM deployment** in urban macrocells.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Cell Capacity Calculation

**Problem**: A 2G GSM system has $M = 124$ duplex channels, $N = 7$, and 3 sectors per cell. Each user generates $A_u = 0.025$ Erlangs of traffic, and the desired **GoS (Grade of Service)** is 2% (Erlang-B). Find:
(a) Channels per cell.
(b) Maximum subscribers per cell.

**(a) Channels per cell**

Total channels are split across the cluster first, then across the 3 sectors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{M}{N} = \frac{124}{7} \approx 17.71 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 17 \text{ (rounded)} \\
k_{\text{sector}} &= \frac{k_{\text{cell}}}{3} = \frac{17}{3} \approx 5.67 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 5 \text{ (rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Maximum subscribers**

From the **Erlang-B table**, with $k = 5$ channels and GoS = 2%:

$$A_{\text{offered}} \approx 1.36 \text{ Erlangs per sector}$$

Per cell (3 sectors):

$$A_{\text{cell}} = 3 \times 1.36 = 4.08 \text{ Erlangs}$$

Per cell with 3 sectors, total users:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{A_{\text{cell}}}{A_u} = \frac{4.08}{0.025} \\
&\approx 163 \text{ subscribers per cell}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering insight**: This is the **classic Erlang capacity calculation** that any RF planning engineer in a telecom company (e.g., in Technopark Trivandrum) must perform.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Cell Splitting Gain

**Problem**: A cell of radius $R = 10$ km is split into 4 sub-cells. Find the new radius and the required transmit-power scaling.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New radius } R' &= \frac{R}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ km} \\[4pt]
\text{Power ratio } \frac{P'}{P} &= \left( \frac{R'}{R} \right)^{n} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{4} = \frac{1}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

In dB: $10 \log_{10}(1/16) = -12$ dB.

So transmit power must drop by **12 dB** (e.g., from 40 W to 2.5 W), and **capacity multiplies by 4×**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Engineering Trade-offs

| Engineering Decision | Spectral Efficiency | S/I | Cost | Implementation Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $N$ (e.g., 7 → 12) | ↓ (lower) | ↑ (better) | Lower BS density | Low |
| Cell splitting ($R \to R/2$) | ↑↑ (4×) | Same | ↑ (more BS sites) | Moderate |
| Sectoring (3 sectors) | ↑ (~3×) | ↑ (~9.5 dB) | ↑ (more antennas) | Moderate |
| 60° sectoring | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | High |
| Lower antenna height | ↓ coverage | ↑ S/I | Same | Low |
| Frequency hopping | Smooths fading | ↑ marginally | Same | Low (GSM) |
| Power control (UMTS) | ↑ (capacity) | ↓ (interference) | Low | High (fast) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away for WSN students**: Cellular engineering uses **centralized, infrastructure-heavy** methods (BS planning, frequency reuse maps, MSC routing). WSN engineering must do the **same capacity/interference planning** but **without the MSC and BSs**, replacing them with **distributed protocols** (LEACH clustering, SPIN, PEGASIS, geographic routing). The principles (spectrum reuse, interference management) are identical; the **means** are distributed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cellular System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS1["MOBILE STATION A"]:::user
    MS2["MOBILE STATION B"]:::user
    MS3["MOBILE STATION C"]:::user
    BTS1["BASE TRANSCEIVER STATION 1"]:::bs
    BTS2["BASE TRANSCEIVER STATION 2"]:::bs
    BTS3["BASE TRANSCEIVER STATION 3"]:::bs
    BSC["BASE STATION CONTROLLER"]:::ctrl
    MSC["MOBILE SWITCHING CENTER MSC"]:::core
    HLR["HOME LOCATION REGISTER"]:::db
    VLR["VISITOR LOCATION REGISTER"]:::db
    AUC["AUTHENTICATION CENTER AUC"]:::db
    PSTN["PSTN / ISDN / INTERNET"]:::ext

    MS1 -->|Radio Link| BTS1
    MS2 -->|Radio Link| BTS2
    MS3 -->|Radio Link| BTS3
    BTS1 -->|Abis Interface| BSC
    BTS2 -->|Abis Interface| BSC
    BTS3 -->|Abis Interface| BSC
    BSC -->|A Interface| MSC
    MSC -->|SS7 Signaling| HLR
    MSC -->|SS7 Signaling| VLR
    MSC -->|SS7 Signaling| AUC
    MSC --> PSTN

    classDef user fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B07A00,color:#222,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bs fill:#7EC8E3,stroke:#084C7A,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrl fill:#A4D17A,stroke:#2C5A12,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#7A0A0A,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef db fill:#C9A6E0,stroke:#3A0A6A,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ext fill:#D5D5D5,stroke:#222,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Frequency Reuse Cluster with N = 7

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClusterA["CLUSTER N EQUALS 7"]
        direction TB
        A1["CELL 1 FREQ GROUP A"]:::ga
        A2["CELL 2 FREQ GROUP B"]:::gb
        A3["CELL 3 FREQ GROUP C"]:::gc
        A4["CELL 4 FREQ GROUP D"]:::gd
        A5["CELL 5 FREQ GROUP E"]:::ge
        A6["CELL 6 FREQ GROUP F"]:::gf
        A7["CELL 7 FREQ GROUP G"]:::gg
    end

    subgraph ClusterB["REUSE OF FREQ GROUP A"]
        B1["CELL 1 PRIME SAME GROUP A"]:::ga
    end

    A1 -.->|Co-channel reuse distance D| B1

    classDef ga fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#7A0A0A,color:#000
    classDef gb fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#7A5700,color:#000
    classDef gc fill:#A4D17A,stroke:#2C5A12,color:#000
    classDef gd fill:#7EC8E3,stroke:#084C7A,color:#000
    classDef ge fill:#C9A6E0,stroke:#3A0A6A,color:#000
    classDef gf fill:#FFB87A,stroke:#7A3A00,color:#000
    classDef gg fill:#A0E0E0,stroke:#005757,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Handover Sequence (Mobile Moving Across Cells)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1 MS IN CELL 1 SERVED BY BTS1"]:::step
    S2["STEP 2 MS MOVES TOWARD CELL 2"]:::step
    S3["STEP 3 MS PILOT STRENGTH FROM BTS2 RISES ABOVE THRESHOLD"]:::step
    S4["STEP 4 BSC INITIATES HANDOFF REQUEST"]:::step
    S5["STEP 5 MSC ALLOCATES NEW CHANNEL IN CELL 2"]:::step
    S6["STEP 6 MS SWITCHES TO BTS2 OLD CHANNEL RELEASED"]:::step
    S7["STEP 7 CALL CONTINUES SEAMLESSLY"]:::step
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7

    classDef step fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#084C7A,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Cell Splitting and Sectoring Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Parent["ORIGINAL CELL R EQUALS 10 KM OMNI"]:::orig
    Split["SPLIT INTO 4 SUB CELLS R PRIME EQUALS 5 KM"]:::split
    Sector["EACH SUB CELL DIVIDED INTO 3 SECTORS 120 DEGREE"]:::sec
    Gain1["CAPACITY GAIN 4 TIMES"]:::gain
    Gain2["S OVER I GAIN 9.5 DB"]:::gain
    Gain3["INTERFERENCE REDUCTION I0 FROM 6 TO 1"]:::gain

    Parent --> Split --> Sector
    Split --> Gain1
    Sector --> Gain2
    Sector --> Gain3

    classDef orig fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#7A5700,color:#000
    classDef split fill:#7EC8E3,stroke:#084C7A,color:#000
    classDef sec fill:#A4D17A,stroke:#2C5A12,color:#000
    classDef gain fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#7A0A0A,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Cellular vs WSN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CellularSys["CELLULAR WWAN INFRASTRUCTURE RICH"]
        C1["Base Stations"]:::bs
        C2["MSC / Core"]:::core
        C3["Centralized Control"]:::ctrl
        C4["High Power Mains Powered"]:::power
        C5["Mobility Hard Soft Handover"]:::mobility
        C1 --- C2 --- C3 --- C4 --- C5
    end

    subgraph WSNSys["WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORK INFRASTRUCTURE LIGHT"]
        W1["Sensor Nodes Battery Powered"]:::wsn
        W2["Sink / Gateway"]:::wsn
        W3["Distributed Self Organization"]:::wsn
        W4["Energy Constrained Micro Watts"]:::wsn
        W5["Static or Low Mobility"]:::wsn
        W1 --- W2 --- W3 --- W4 --- W5
    end

    classDef bs fill:#7EC8E3,stroke:#084C7A,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#7A0A0A,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#A4D17A,stroke:#2C5A12,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#7A5700,color:#000
    classDef mobility fill:#C9A6E0,stroke:#3A0A6A,color:#000
    classDef wsn fill:#D5D5D5,stroke:#222,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
> **Define cellular architecture. Why is the hexagonal cell shape preferred over square or circular cells in mobile communication systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cellular architecture is a system-level topology in which a large service area is divided into smaller geographical regions called *cells*, each served by a *Base Station (BS)*, with systematic **frequency reuse** across non-adjacent cells and centralized switching via a *Mobile Switching Center (MSC)*. Hexagonal cells are preferred because (i) hexagons **tessellate** the plane without gaps, (ii) hexagons provide the **closest geometric approximation to a circular coverage area** (which is the natural shape of an omnidirectional antenna's footprint) with the **fewest number of cells per cluster**, and (iii) the hexagonal lattice gives a clean closed-form expression $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ for valid cluster sizes, simplifying engineering frequency planning. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
> **List any three key differences between Wireless WAN (WWAN) and Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | WWAN | WSN |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | Wide area (km to 100+ km) | Short range (m to 200 m) |
| Power | Mains-powered BS, battery mobile | Energy-starved, battery-only nodes |
| Topology | Star (BS-centric) | Multi-hop mesh, distributed |
| Data | Voice, video (high rate) | Sensing (low rate, bursty) |
| Infrastructure | Heavy (BSs, MSC, backhaul) | Mostly infrastructure-less |
| Handoff | Continuous, mandatory | Not required |

**(3 marks for any three correct, well-explained differences.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / KTU Model Paper Adaptation]**
> **(a)** Explain the **concept of frequency reuse** in cellular systems. Derive the relation $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ for the co-channel reuse distance. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A GSM operator uses **$N = 4$** with **omnidirectional antennas**. Compute the **S/I ratio in dB** and comment on whether it meets the GSM requirement of **18 dB** for voice quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Concept of Frequency Reuse (3 marks)**

Frequency reuse is the technique of **assigning the same set of radio channels to multiple cells** that are physically separated by a *co-channel reuse distance* $D$, so that **co-channel interference (CCI)** is kept below an acceptable threshold. This allows the *limited* RF spectrum to support a *much larger* number of subscribers than would be possible if each cell had unique channels. The system bandwidth $W$ is split into $N$ channel groups (one per cell in a cluster of size $N$), and these groups are reused in identical-pattern cells of neighbouring clusters.

**Step 2 — Derivation of $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ (4 marks)**

In a hexagonal lattice, the centre-to-centre vector between two nearest co-channel cells can be expressed as $\vec{u} = i\vec{a_1} + j\vec{a_2}$, where $\vec{a_1}$ and $\vec{a_2}$ are basis vectors along the sides of a hexagon of side $R$, so $\vert \vec{a_1} \vert = \vert \vec{a_2} \vert = \sqrt{3}R$, with the angle between them being $60°$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{2} &= \vert i\vec{a_1} + j\vec{a_2} \vert^{2} \\
&= i^{2} \cdot 3R^{2} + j^{2} \cdot 3R^{2} + 2ij \cdot 3R^{2} \cos 60° \\
&= 3R^{2} (i^{2} + j^{2} + ij) \\
&= 3R^{2} N \\
\Rightarrow \quad D &= R\sqrt{3N}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $N = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ defining valid cluster sizes.

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Given:** $N = 4$, omnidirectional antennas, so $i_0 = 6$, GSM requirement $= 18$ dB.

**Step 1 — Compute the S/I ratio (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{I} &= \frac{9 N^{2}}{i_{0}} = \frac{9 \times 16}{6} = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( \frac{S}{I} \right)_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(24) \\
&= 10 \times 1.380 \\
&\approx 13.8 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Conclusion (2 marks):**

$$13.8 \text{ dB} \; < \; 18 \text{ dB} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{FAILS to meet the GSM voice quality requirement.}$$

The system would experience **noticeable cross-talk and dropped calls**. To meet 18 dB, the operator must either:
- Increase cluster size (e.g., to $N = 7$ gives $\sim 18.66$ dB, just meeting the requirement), or
- Adopt **sectoring** (e.g., 120° sectoring gives $S/I = 9 \times 16 / 1 = 144 = 21.6$ dB with $N = 4$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A**:
> - **Do NOT** use the path-loss exponent $n = 2$. The KTU convention for **urban macrocells** is $n = 4$. Wrong $n$ gives 6 dB error per cell.
> - **Do NOT** forget the **$9$ factor** in $S/I = 9N^2 / i_0$. Many students write $(D/R)^4 = (3N)^2$ — which is **wrong**. The correct form is $(\sqrt{3N})^4 = 9N^2$.
> - **Convert to dB carefully** — the formula $S/I_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$ is a 2-mark step, often skipped.
> - When asked for a *comment*, **always state whether the S/I meets the standard** and propose a fix (sectoring, larger $N$, or lower power) — that fetches the final 2 marks.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 / KTU Model Paper Adaptation]**
> **(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, describe the **architecture of a cellular mobile communication system**. Identify the role of the **MSC, HLR, VLR, and AuC**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **concepts of cell splitting and sectoring** as cellular capacity-enhancement techniques. A cell of radius **8 km** is split into **9 sub-cells**. Compute the **new radius** and the **required transmit power** relative to the original, assuming path-loss exponent $n = 4$. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Block diagram description (4 marks):**

A cellular system has three subsystems: the **Mobile Station (MS)**, the **Base Station Subsystem (BSS)**, and the **Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)**, interconnected via a transmission backbone.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes for the canonical block diagram.)*

- **MS** ↔ **BTS**: Radio (Um) interface.
- **BTS** ↔ **BSC**: Abis interface (wireline).
- **BSC** ↔ **MSC**: A interface.
- **MSC** ↔ **PSTN / Internet**: External gateway.
- **MSC** ↔ **HLR, VLR, AuC**: SS7 signalling.

**Step 2 — Role of each network entity (3 marks):**

| Entity | Role |
|---|---|
| **MSC** | Call routing, switching, handoff coordination between BSC, billing |
| **HLR** | Permanent subscriber database — services, current serving VLR, authentication keys |
| **VLR** | Temporary visitor database for roamers in the MSC's serving area |
| **AuC** | Stores the **Ki** (subscriber authentication key) and the algorithm **A3/A8**; generates **SRES** triplets used to authenticate the SIM |

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Concept of Cell Splitting and Sectoring (3 marks):**

- **Cell Splitting**: A congested cell is subdivided into smaller cells, each with its own BS of reduced transmit power. Capacity grows roughly as $(R_{old}/R_{new})^2$.
- **Sectoring**: The omnidirectional antenna at a BS is replaced by $N$ directional antennas (typically 3 sectors of 120°), reducing the number of *visible* first-tier co-channel interferers $i_0$ from 6 to 1, thereby raising $S/I$ by approximately $10 \log_{10}(6) \approx 7.78$ dB.

**Step 2 — Numerical computation (4 marks):**

Given: $R_{old} = 8$ km, split into $k = 9$ sub-cells.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New radius } R_{new} &= \frac{R_{old}}{\sqrt{k}} = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.67 \text{ km} \\[6pt]
\text{Power ratio } \frac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} &= \left( \frac{R_{new}}{R_{old}} \right)^{4} = \left( \frac{1}{3} \right)^{4} = \frac{1}{81} \\[6pt]
\text{In dB: } 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{1}{81} \right) &\approx -19.08 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: Transmit power must be reduced by **~19 dB** (e.g., from 40 W down to ~0.5 W), and **capacity grows by 9×** (assuming uniform traffic redistribution).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B**:
> - **Do NOT** write $R_{new} = R_{old}/k = 8/9$ km. The correct scaling is $R_{new} = R_{old}/\sqrt{k}$. Cell **area** scales linearly with $k$, so **radius scales as $\sqrt{k}$**.
> - **Always** mention the *interference-related trade-off*: cell splitting may **increase handoff frequency**, which in turn **increases MSC signalling load**.
> - For part (a), the **AuC** is the most frequently missed entity. Just mentioning HLR and VLR is **not enough** to fetch the full marks.
> - In the diagram, **explicitly label** the interfaces (Um, Abis, A) — examiners allot 1 mark for the diagram, 1 mark for interface labels.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD TAKEAWAYS — Master These for KTU ESE**

- **Cellular architecture = hexagonal cells + frequency reuse + MSC-coordinated handoffs.** It is the *enabling* technology of every WWAN (GSM, UMTS, LTE, 5G).
- **WWAN** = city-to-continent scale; **WMAN** = city/campus scale (≤ 50 km); **WLAN** = room/building (≤ 100 m); **WSN** = local area with thousands of battery-powered sensors.
- **Cluster size** $N$ must be of the form $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ for hexagonal layouts. **Common values**: 3, 4, **7**, 9, 12.
- **Co-channel reuse distance**: $D = R\sqrt{3N}$.
- **S/I ratio (omni)**: $S/I = 9N^2 / 6 = 1.5 N^2$. In dB: $10\log_{10}(1.5 N^2)$.
- **S/I ratio (120° sector)**: $S/I = 9 N^2$. Sectoring adds ~**7.78 dB** over omni.
- **GSM minimum S/I** = **18 dB** (voice quality). $N = 7$ with omni is the canonical minimum.
- **Channels per cell** $k = M / N$, where $M$ = total system channels.
- **Cell splitting** increases capacity by a factor of $(R_{old}/R_{new})^2 = k$ and reduces transmit power by $(1/k)^{n/2} = (1/k)^2$ for $n = 4$.
- **Sectoring** increases S/I by reducing $i_0$ from 6 to 1 or 2.
- **Handoff types**: Hard (break-before-make, used in GSM) vs Soft (make-before-break, used in CDMA/UMTS).
- **Key network elements**: MS, BTS, BSC, MSC, HLR, VLR, AuC, EIR.
- **Path-loss exponent** for **urban macrocells** = **4** (use this in KTU unless explicitly told otherwise).
- **Contrast with WSN** (the syllabus hook):
  - Cellular = **centralized, infrastructure-rich, energy-rich, mobility-heavy**.
  - WSN = **distributed, infrastructure-less, energy-starved, mostly static**.
  - Same fundamental challenges (capacity, interference, routing) but **opposite solutions**.
- **Never confuse** $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ with $D = R \cdot 3N$ or $D = R\sqrt{N}$.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1 — Wireless WANs and MANs (Basics Only) — Cellular Architecture**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2G/3G/4G/5G Cellular Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** 2G/3G/4G/5G Cellular Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 2G/3G/4G/5G Cellular Networks — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cellular Network?

A **Cellular Network** is a radio network distributed over **land areas (cells)**, each served by at least one fixed-location transceiver known as a **Base Station (BS)** or **Base Transceiver Station (BTS)**. The cellular concept enables frequency reuse across non-adjacent cells, dramatically increasing system capacity compared to single-transmitter broadcast systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"A cellular network is a wireless communication system organized as a grid of geographic cells, where each cell is serviced by a base station, allowing simultaneous communication with multiple users through frequency reuse and handoff mechanisms."*

### 1.2 Why "Cellular"? — The Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Honeycomb Restaurant Network 🍯**
> Imagine a city as a giant honeycomb. Instead of one massive restaurant (single high-power transmitter) trying to serve the entire city with limited seating (limited bandwidth), you place many small restaurants (cells) closer to customers. Each small restaurant:
> - Serves only a few nearby tables (low transmit power)
> - Uses the *same menu* as restaurants far away (frequency reuse)
> - Hands off your order to the next restaurant when you walk away (handover)
> - Avoids interference by not placing two identical menus in adjacent restaurants (frequency planning)

This is the essence of the **cellular concept** — first proposed by **Bell Labs (1947)** and standardized by **AT&T in the 1970s**.

## 1.3 The "G" Generation Roadmap — At a Glance

| Generation | Era | Core Technology | Service | Peak Data Rate (Theoretical) |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **1G** | 1980s | Analog (AMPS, NMT, TACS) | Voice only | **2.4 kbps** |
| **2G** | 1990s | Digital (GSM, IS-95) | Voice + SMS | **64 kbps** |
| **2.5G** | 2001 | GPRS / EDGE | Packet data (WAP) | **144 kbps – 384 kbps** |
| **3G** | 2000s | WCDMA / CDMA2000 | Mobile Internet | **2 Mbps – 14.4 Mbps** |
| **3.5G** | 2008 | HSPA / HSPA+ | Mobile broadband | **14.4 Mbps – 42 Mbps** |
| **4G** | 2010s | LTE / LTE-A / OFDMA | Mobile broadband, video | **100 Mbps – 1 Gbps** |
| **5G** | 2020s | 5G NR / mmWave / Massive MIMO | IoT, AR/VR, URLLC | **10 Gbps – 20 Gbps** |

## 1.4 Generation-Wise Conceptual Intuition

### 🔹 2G — "The Digital Walkie-Talkie Era"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Like switching from a hand-cranked radio (1G analog) to a digital music player. The signal is now clean, encrypted, and can carry text messages along with voice. **GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)** is the dominant 2G standard.

- Introduced **digital modulation** (GMSK), **TDMA/FDMA** hybrid multiple access.
- Enabled **SMS (Short Message Service)** — 160 characters of pure genius.
- First use of **SIM cards** for subscriber identity.

### 🔹 3G — "The Mobile Internet Arrives"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Like going from a public telephone booth (2G voice-centric) to having a full desktop computer in your pocket. You can now browse websites, video call, and download music. **WCDMA (Wideband Code Division Multiple Access)** is the air interface.

- Introduced **CDMA** technology — every user shares the same frequency band but uses unique codes.
- Supports **packet-switched** data (always-on internet).
- Defined by **ITU IMT-2000** specification.

### 🔹 4G — "Broadspeed in Your Pocket"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Like installing fiber-optic internet on your bicycle. The highway (OFDM) is so well-paved that streaming HD Netflix while jogging becomes reality. **LTE (Long Term Evolution)** and **LTE-Advanced** are the standards.

- **All-IP network** — voice becomes **VoLTE (Voice over LTE)**.
- Uses **OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access)** in downlink, **SC-FDMA** in uplink.
- Incorporates **MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output)** antennas.

### 🔹 5G — "The Network of Everything"
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Like upgrading from a single-lane road to a multi-dimensional smart highway with self-driving cars, drone lanes, and emergency ambulances flying overhead. 5G supports three service classes: **eMBB, mMTC, URLLC**.

- Operates in three spectrum bands: **Sub-1 GHz**, **Sub-6 GHz**, and **mmWave (24–52 GHz)**.
- Uses **Massive MIMO** (up to 256 antennas), **Beamforming**, **Network Slicing**.
- Target latency: **< 1 ms** (URLLC).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cellular Concept — Operational Logic

The cellular architecture rests on **four pillars**:

1. **Frequency Reuse**: The same frequency band is reused in geographically separated cells following a **cluster size $N$** pattern.
2. **Cell Splitting**: As traffic grows, large cells are split into smaller ones (microcells, picocells, femtocells).
3. **Handover / Handoff**: Seamless transfer of an active call as the mobile station (MS) moves between cells.
4. **Power Control**: Minimizing transmit power to reduce co-channel interference (CCI).

### 2.2 The Frequency Reuse Equation (Cluster Sizing)

The relationship between **cluster size $N$** and the **frequency reuse ratio $q$** is given by:

$$
N = i^2 + ij + j^2 \quad \text{where } i, j \geq 0 \text{ are shift parameters}
$$

And the **frequency reuse distance $D$** is:

$$
D = R\sqrt{3N}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Reuse distance (center-to-center of co-channel cells)
- $R$ = Cell radius
- $N$ = Cluster size (number of cells in a cluster)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:**
> - **Small $N$ (e.g., $N=3$):** More capacity (more reuse), but **higher co-channel interference**.
> - **Large $N$ (e.g., $N=12$):** Less interference, but **lower spectral efficiency**.

## 2.3 Multiple Access Techniques — Evolution of Sharing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> The fundamental difference between generations lies in **how they share the air medium** among multiple users.

| Generation | Multiple Access | Duplexing | Key Innovation |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **1G** | FDMA | FDD | Analog voice |
| **2G (GSM)** | TDMA / FDMA hybrid | FDD | Digital, SIM, SMS |
| **3G (UMTS)** | WCDMA (DS-CDMA) | FDD / TDD | Spread spectrum, soft handover |
| **4G (LTE)** | OFDMA (DL), SC-FDMA (UL) | FDD / TDD | All-IP, MIMO, flat architecture |
| **5G (NR)** | OFDMA / Flexible numerology | FDD / TDD | Massive MIMO, beamforming, slicing |

## 2.4 Shannon's Channel Capacity — The Speed Limit of Reality

The **theoretical maximum data rate** of a noisy channel is given by **Claude Shannon's Theorem (1948)**:

$$
C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/second}
$$

Where:
- $C$ = Channel capacity (bps)
- $B$ = Bandwidth (Hz)
- $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio (linear, not in dB)

In terms of **Spectral Efficiency ($\eta$)**:

$$
\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/s/Hz}
$$

## 2.5 Path Loss & Link Budget — The Reality of Wireless

The **free-space path loss (FSPL)** equation governs signal attenuation:

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \;\; \text{(dB, } d \text{ in km, } f \text{ in MHz)}
$$

The more general **Log-Distance Path Loss Model** is widely used:

$$
PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma
$$

Where:
- $PL(d_0)$ = Path loss at reference distance $d_0$ (typically 1 m)
- $n$ = Path loss exponent (2 in free space, 3–5 in urban)
- $X_\sigma$ = Shadow fading (Gaussian, zero-mean)

## 2.6 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet — The Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon Channel Capacity | bps |
| 2 | $\eta = \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Spectral Efficiency | bps/Hz |
| 3 | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | Cluster Size (Reuse Pattern) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ | Co-channel Reuse Distance | meters |
| 5 | $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | Frequency Reuse Ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S/I \geq 6$ (for $N=7$) | Minimum S/I for acceptable voice | dB |
| 7 | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | Log-distance Path Loss | dB |
| 8 | $\text{FSPL}(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log d + 20\log f$ | Free Space Path Loss (km, MHz) | dB |
| 9 | $N_c = M/N$ | Channels per cell (Total $M$, Cluster $N$) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{cell} = N_c \times C_{channel}$ | Cell capacity | Erlangs / bps |

> **Note:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in place of absolute value bars within math mode to avoid markdown table breakage.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not just academic — they govern:
- **Cell tower placement** by telecom operators (Jio, Airtel, Vi).
- **Spectrum auction valuations** by TRAI/DoT in India.
- **5G mmWave link budgets** for fixed wireless access.
- **NB-IoT cell range planning** (used heavily in **WSN Module 1** of PEAET756).
- **Handover algorithm design** in LTE/5G mobility management entities (MME).

> [!NOTE]
> **WSN-Centric Relevance:** Wireless Sensor Networks often piggyback on **cellular infrastructure (e.g., NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G mMTC)** for backhaul. Understanding the cellular generation roadmap is foundational to designing **energy-efficient sensor nodes** that operate within licensed cellular bands.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shannon Capacity in Cellular Generations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern):**
> *A 4G LTE system allocates a bandwidth of $20$ MHz to a user. If the received SNR is $15$ dB, compute the (a) Shannon capacity, and (b) spectral efficiency. Compare it with 2G GSM which offers 200 kHz with SNR = 10 dB.*

### Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to Linear Scale

The decibel-to-linear conversion formula is:

$$
S/N_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\left(\frac{S/N_{\text{dB}}}{10}\right)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Linear SNR for 4G (15 dB)

$$
S/N_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(15/10)} = 10^{1.5} = 31.623
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Shannon's Formula for 4G

$$
C_{4G} = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 31.623)
$$

$$
C_{4G} = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(32.623)
$$

$$
\log_2(32.623) = \frac{\log_{10}(32.623)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.5133}{0.3010} \approx 5.027
$$

$$
C_{4G} = 20 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \approx 100.5 \;\; \text{Mbps}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Linear SNR for 2G GSM (10 dB)

$$
S/N_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(10/10)} = 10^{1} = 10
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Shannon's Formula for 2G

$$
C_{2G} = 200 \times 10^3 \times \log_2(1 + 10)
$$

$$
C_{2G} = 200 \times 10^3 \times \log_2(11) = 200 \times 10^3 \times 3.459
$$

$$
C_{2G} \approx 691.9 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Spectral Efficiency ($\eta$) for Both

$$
\eta_{4G} = \frac{100.5 \times 10^6}{20 \times 10^6} = 5.03 \;\; \text{bps/Hz}
$$

$$
\eta_{2G} = \frac{691.9 \times 10^3}{200 \times 10^3} = 3.46 \;\; \text{bps/Hz}
$$

### ✅ Final Result Summary

| Parameter | 2G GSM | 4G LTE | Improvement Factor |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Bandwidth $B$ | 200 kHz | 20 MHz | $100\times$ |
| SNR (dB) | 10 dB | 15 dB | — |
| Capacity $C$ | $\approx 691.9$ kbps | $\approx 100.5$ Mbps | $\approx 145\times$ |
| Spectral Efficiency $\eta$ | 3.46 bps/Hz | 5.03 bps/Hz | $\approx 1.45\times$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The massive capacity jump from 2G to 4G comes **not just from Shannon's law but from improved digital signal processing (OFDM), MIMO spatial multiplexing, and aggressive link adaptation (AMC — Adaptive Modulation and Coding).**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Co-Channel Interference (CCI) Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern):**
> *In a cellular system with cluster size $N=7$ and path loss exponent $n=4$, find the minimum co-channel reuse ratio $q$ and the S/I ratio at the cell boundary. Assume 6 co-channel interferers in the first tier.*

### Step 1 — Compute the Reuse Ratio

Using the standard hexagonal geometry equation:

$$
q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the CCI Formula

For a hexagonal system with **first-tier co-channel interferers**, the **Signal-to-Interference ratio (S/I)** is given by:

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^n}{i_0} = \frac{q^n}{i_0}
$$

Where $i_0$ is the **number of co-channel interferers in the first tier** (typically 6 in a hexagonal layout).

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{6}
$$

$$
(4.583)^2 = 21.0
$$

$$
(4.583)^4 = 21.0 \times 21.0 = 441.0
$$

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{441.0}{6} = 73.5 \;\; \text{(linear)}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Decibels

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(73.5) = 10 \times 1.866 = 18.66 \;\; \text{dB}
$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$
\boxed{\frac{S}{I} \approx 18.66 \;\; \text{dB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The minimum acceptable S/I for voice quality in analog FM systems is **18 dB**, and for digital GSM it is **9–12 dB**. Our calculated 18.66 dB is just above the FM threshold, confirming that $N=7$ is a common cluster size for 2G systems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cell Capacity in Erlangs (For 2G/3G Voice Channels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:**
> *A 2G GSM cell has 8 radio channels. If each user occupies 1 channel for an average call duration of 3 minutes and calls arrive at 2 calls/min, compute the offered traffic load in Erlangs.*

### Step 1 — Compute Traffic Intensity per User

$$
A_u = \lambda \times H = 2 \times 3 = 6 \;\; \text{Erlangs/user}
$$

*(Note: This is per-user traffic if all 2 calls are from one user)*

### Step 2 — General Formula for Cell Traffic

For $U$ users:

$$
A_{cell} = U \cdot A_u
$$

### Step 3 — Application Context

This metric is used in **Erlang B tables** to find the **Grade of Service (GoS)** — i.e., the probability of call blocking. KTU 2024 questions often ask students to use the **Erlang B formula**:

$$
P_{blocking} = \frac{A^N / N!}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} A^k / k!}
$$

Where $N$ = number of channels, $A$ = offered traffic in Erlangs.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List three key differences between 2G GSM and 3G UMTS networks in terms of multiple access, data rate, and core network architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | 2G GSM | 3G UMTS |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Multiple Access** | TDMA/FDMA hybrid (narrowband) | WCDMA (Wideband Code Division Multiple Access) |
| **Peak Data Rate** | 64 kbps (9.6 kbps per user) | 2 Mbps (384 kbps typical) |
| **Core Network** | Circuit-switched (PSTN-based) | Hybrid: Circuit + Packet (PS domain) |
| **Handover** | Hard handover (Break-Before-Make) | Soft handover (Make-Before-Break) |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | 200 kHz | 5 MHz |

*(1 mark for each correct point with comparison)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define OFDMA. State any two advantages of OFDMA over CDMA in 4G LTE systems.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
**OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access)** is a multi-carrier multiple access technique where the available spectrum is divided into multiple orthogonal subcarriers, and different users are assigned subsets of these subcarriers in both frequency and time. The orthogonality ensures that subcarriers do not interfere with each other even though their spectra overlap.

**Advantages over CDMA (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Robust to multipath fading:** OFDM uses a **cyclic prefix** to eliminate inter-symbol interference (ISI), while CDMA requires complex rake receivers.
2. **Higher spectral efficiency:** OFDMA allows flexible subcarrier allocation and frequency-selective scheduling; CDMA's spreading codes reduce spectral efficiency.
3. **No near-far problem:** OFDMA subcarriers are orthogonal, so power control is less stringent than in CDMA.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Format:**
> *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

#### **(a) Compare the architecture and key features of 2G GSM and 4G LTE systems. Explain how OFDMA replaced CDMA in 4G. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Comparison (3.5 Marks):**

**2G GSM Architecture:**
- **BTS (Base Transceiver Station):** Air interface to mobile.
- **BSC (Base Station Controller):** Manages radio resources, handovers within a cluster.
- **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Circuit-switched core, connects to PSTN.
- **HLR / VLR / AUC / EIR:** Subscriber and authentication databases.
- Uses **A-interface** between BTS-BSC and **A-bis** between MSC-BSC.

**4G LTE Architecture (EPC — Evolved Packet Core):**
- **eNodeB (Evolved NodeB):** Single node replaces BTS+BSC; flat architecture.
- **MME (Mobility Management Entity):** Signaling, bearer management.
- **S-GW (Serving Gateway):** Local mobility anchor, routes packets.
- **P-GW (Packet Gateway):** Connects to external packet networks (Internet).
- Uses **all-IP, no circuit switching** — voice is VoLTE.

**OFDMA replacing CDMA in 4G (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Multipath Robustness:** CDMA suffers from **inter-chip interference** at high chip rates; OFDMA handles multipath via cyclic prefix without complex equalizers.
2. **Spectral Flexibility:** OFDMA allows **fractional frequency reuse** and **frequency-selective scheduling** — assigning good subcarriers to users with favorable channel conditions. CDMA treats all bandwidth as one wide band.
3. **MIMO Compatibility:** OFDMA's flat frequency response per subcarrier makes **spatial multiplexing via MIMO** easy. CDMA's wideband spreading makes MIMO channel estimation complex.
4. **Lower PAPR in uplink:** 4G uses **SC-FDMA** (a DFT-spread OFDMA variant) in uplink to reduce peak-to-average power ratio, extending UE battery life — a benefit CDMA cannot easily provide.

---

#### **(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the cellular frequency reuse concept. A GSM operator is allotted 50 channels. If the cluster size $N=7$, find (i) number of channels per cell, (ii) the reuse distance if cell radius is 2 km, and (iii) the S/I ratio for $n=4$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Frequency Reuse Concept (2 Marks):**
Refer to Section 4.1 architecture diagram. The total bandwidth is divided among $N$ cells in a cluster; the same set of frequencies is reused in clusters that are separated by a minimum **co-channel reuse distance $D$** to limit interference.

**Calculations (5 Marks):**

**(i) Number of channels per cell:**

$$
N_c = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{50}{7} \approx 7.14
$$

Since channels must be integers, we round to **7 channels per cell** (1 mark).

**(ii) Reuse distance $D$:**

$$
D = R\sqrt{3N} = 2 \times \sqrt{3 \times 7} = 2 \times \sqrt{21}
$$

$$
D = 2 \times 4.583 = 9.166 \;\; \text{km} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**(iii) S/I ratio for $n=4$:**

$$
\frac{S}{I} = \frac{q^n}{6} = \frac{(\sqrt{3N})^n}{6} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{6}
$$

$$
(4.583)^4 = 441 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{S}{I} = \frac{441}{6} = 73.5
$$

$$
\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(73.5) \approx 18.66 \;\; \text{dB} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}
$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **(a) Explain the evolution from 1G to 5G highlighting the changes in multiple access, switching, and services at each generation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Generation | Multiple Access | Switching | Services | Limitation Overcome |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **1G (1980s)** | FDMA | Circuit | Analog voice | Limited capacity, no security |
| **2G (1990s)** | TDMA/FDMA (GSM) | Circuit | Digital voice, SMS, low-rate data | Encryption, efficiency |
| **2.5G (2001)** | TDMA + GPRS | Packet overlay | WAP, MMS, email | Always-on data |
| **3G (2000s)** | WCDMA/CDMA2000 | Circuit + Packet | Mobile internet, video call | Data rate for multimedia |
| **3.5G/3.9G** | HSPA, HSPA+ | Packet-dominant | Mobile broadband | Higher throughput |
| **4G (2010s)** | OFDMA / SC-FDMA | All-IP (packet) | HD streaming, VoLTE, gaming | Latency, capacity |
| **5G (2020s)** | OFDMA + Massive MIMO + Beamforming | Cloud-native SBA | eMBB, URLLC, mMTC | Latency, IoT density, spectrum crunch |

**(Each generation ≈ 1 mark for clear comparison)**

**Key Evolutionary Themes (2 Marks):**
- **Analog → Digital → Software-defined → Virtualized/Cloud-native.**
- **Voice-centric → Data-centric → Everything-connected (IoT).**
- **Single-purpose → Multi-service with network slicing.**

---

#### **(b) A 5G NR system operates at 28 GHz (mmWave) with 100 MHz bandwidth. If the received SNR is 20 dB, calculate (i) the maximum Shannon capacity, (ii) the spectral efficiency, and (iii) compare with a 3G WCDMA system that has 5 MHz bandwidth and 12 dB SNR. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) 5G NR Shannon Capacity (3 Marks):**

Convert SNR: $S/N = 10^{(20/10)} = 100$

$$
C_{5G} = 100 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 100) = 100 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(101)
$$

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658
$$

$$
C_{5G} = 100 \times 10^6 \times 6.658 = 665.8 \;\; \text{Mbps}
$$

**(ii) Spectral Efficiency (1 Mark):**

$$
\eta_{5G} = \frac{C_{5G}}{B} = 6.658 \;\; \text{bps/Hz}
$$

**(iii) 3G WCDMA Comparison (3 Marks):**

Convert SNR: $S/N = 10^{(12/10)} = 15.849$

$$
C_{3G} = 5 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 15.849) = 5 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(16.849)
$$

$$
\log_2(16.849) = \frac{\log_{10}(16.849)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.2267}{0.3010} \approx 4.075
$$

$$
C_{3G} = 5 \times 10^6 \times 4.075 = 20.375 \;\; \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
\eta_{3G} = 4.075 \;\; \text{bps/Hz}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | 3G WCDMA | 5G NR | Ratio |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Bandwidth | 5 MHz | 100 MHz | $20\times$ |
| SNR (dB) | 12 dB | 20 dB | — |
| Capacity | $\approx 20.4$ Mbps | $\approx 665.8$ Mbps | $\approx 32.7\times$ |
| Spectral Efficiency | 4.08 bps/Hz | 6.66 bps/Hz | $\approx 1.63\times$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert SNR from dB to linear scale** (this is the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks).
> 2. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$** in Shannon's formula. Always use $\log_2$ for capacity in bits.
> 3. **Confusing $D$ and $R$**: $D$ is the **reuse distance** (center-to-center of co-channel cells), $R$ is the **cell radius**. Don't mix them up.
> 4. **Rounding errors in cluster size**: $N=7$ is not 21; remember $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$, e.g., for $i=2, j=1$: $N = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$.
> 5. **Forgetting the "6 interferers" assumption** in the first tier of a hexagonal layout. Always state this assumption explicitly in your answer.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Cellular concept** = frequency reuse + cell splitting + handover + power control.
- [ ] **$N = i^2 + ij + j^2$** → standard cluster sizes: 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 19, 21.
- [ ] **$D = R\sqrt{3N}$** is the co-channel reuse distance.
- [ ] **Shannon limit:** $C = B\log_2(1 + S/N)$ — theoretical max; real systems achieve **30–60%** of this.
- [ ] **2G = TDMA/FDMA, digital, SMS, GMSK.**
- [ ] **3G = WCDMA, soft handover, IMT-2000, 2 Mbps peak.**
- [ ] **4G = OFDMA + MIMO + All-IP, LTE/LTE-A, 100 Mbps–1 Gbps.**
- [ ] **5G = mmWave + Massive MIMO + Network Slicing, three service classes (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC).**
- [ ] **OFDMA = orthogonal subcarriers**, no intra-cell interference; cyclic prefix handles ISI.
- [ ] **MIMO** = spatial multiplexing (capacity) + diversity (reliability).
- [ ] **Erlang B formula** = $P_b = \dfrac{A^N/N!}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} A^k/k!}$ — used for 2G/3G voice traffic.
- [ ] **Path loss exponent $n$**: 2 (free space), 2.5–3 (urban), 3.5–5 (shadowed urban/indoor).
- [ ] **5G NR numerology** supports flexible subcarrier spacing (15 kHz, 30 kHz, 60 kHz, 120 kHz, 240 kHz).
- [ ] **NB-IoT and LTE-M** are the bridge between cellular and WSN — important for PEAET756.
- [ ] **3GPP** = the standards body for 2G/3G/4G/5G; releases 8 (LTE), 15 (5G NR), 17/18 (5G-Advanced).
- [ ] **Always state the S/I formula and the "6 interferers" assumption** in numerical answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — All the best for your KTU 2024 Examinations! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 WLL — Wireless Local Loop

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireless Local Loop (WLL)** is a system that uses a **wireless (radio) link** as the "last mile" / "local loop" connection between a subscriber's premises and the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) or an Internet Service Provider (ISP), **replacing the traditional copper wire pair** that historically carried the analog voice signal from the customer to the central office exchange.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, WLL is broadly classified as a **Fixed Wireless Access (FWA)** system, designed primarily to bypass the heavy infrastructure cost of laying copper cables in suburban, rural, or geographically challenging terrains where wireline deployment is uneconomical.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — "The Invisible Copper Wire"

Imagine your house is located at the end of a long, muddy lane. The telephone company finds it too expensive to dig up that lane and lay a fresh copper cable just to connect **one** new customer. Instead, they mount a small antenna on your rooftop and another antenna on the nearest telephone exchange tower. When you pick up the phone, your voice is **converted to a radio signal**, beamed through the air to the exchange, and from there it joins the regular phone network — exactly as if a copper wire were there, but the "wire" is now an **invisible radio beam in the sky**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology to Memorize**
> - **Local Loop** = The physical connection (originally a 2-wire copper pair) between the subscriber's phone and the telephone exchange.
> - **WLL** = A wireless substitute for that copper pair.
> - **FWA (Fixed Wireless Access)** = Modern umbrella term for WLL + broadband variants.
> - **LMDS / MMDS** = Microwave-based WLL technologies.
> - **DECT** = Cordless standard used for short-range WLL.

### 🎯 Why WLL is Relevant to Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)

Within the broader WSN syllabus, WLL is studied because:

1. **Backhaul connectivity:** A WSN deployed in a remote forest or agricultural field still needs a way to send its aggregated data back to a central server — WLL provides exactly this kind of **wide-area backhaul link**.
2. **Architectural lineage:** WLL introduced concepts like **point-to-multipoint (PMP) radio links, subscriber units, and base stations** that later influenced how **WSN sink nodes and gateways** are designed.
3. **Spectrum management awareness:** WLL systems operate in licensed microwave bands (e.g., **3.5 GHz, 10.5 GHz, 26 GHz, 28 GHz**), teaching WSN engineers about **regulated spectrum**, FCC/ETSI-style allocations, and the cost trade-offs of licensed vs. unlicensed ISM bands (which WSNs use).

### 📐 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded for Quick Reference)

- **Frequency bands of operation:** **450 MHz – 40 GHz** (varies by technology)
- **Typical cell radius:** **1 km – 50 km** depending on technology
- **Typical data rate:** **9.6 kbps (voice) to 155 Mbps (broadband)**
- **Speed of radio propagation in free space:** **c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Free-space path loss reference distance:** **d₀ = 1 m** (in the Friis equation)

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 Conceptual Breakdown of WLL Architecture

A WLL system is essentially built from **three primary hardware blocks**, mirroring any standard cellular/fixed-wireless network:

1. **Subscriber Unit (SU) / Customer Premises Equipment (CPE)**
   - Mounted on the customer's rooftop or window.
   - Contains an **outdoor antenna**, RF transceiver, and an **indoor Network Interface Unit (NIU)**.
   - NIU converts the radio signal into a standard RJ-11 telephone jack or RJ-45 Ethernet port.

2. **Base Station (BS) / Radio Access Unit (RAU)**
   - Located at the telephone exchange or a strategic tower site.
   - Serves **hundreds to thousands** of SUs simultaneously.
   - Performs multiplexing, modulation/demodulation, and switching.

3. **Switching Center / PSTN Gateway**
   - Connects the BS to the core PSTN (for voice) or to the Internet backbone (for data).
   - Handles billing, authentication, and call routing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Golden Rule:** Whenever you draw a WLL diagram in the exam, **always label the three components — CPE, BS, and PSTN/ISP gateway** — otherwise you lose at least 1 mark.

### 🔬 How WLL Works — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Subscriber lifts handset** → analog voice signal generated.
2. **NIU digitizes** the voice using an **ADC** (64 kbps PCM, 8 kHz sampling per ITU-T G.711).
3. **Modulation** of the digital bitstream onto a **RF carrier** (typical schemes: **BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM**).
4. **Radio transmission** from the CPE antenna to the BS antenna.
5. **BS demodulates, multiplexes** the channels, and forwards them to the switching center.
6. **PSTN gateway** hands the call over to the standard telephone network.

### 📊 WLL vs. Traditional Wired Local Loop — The "Why WLL" Table

| Feature | Wired Local Loop (Copper) | Wireless Local Loop (WLL) |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Copper twisted pair | Radio waves (air) |
| Deployment time | **3–12 months** (civil works) | **Days to weeks** (antenna install) |
| Cost in rural areas | **High** (digging, trenching) | **Moderate** (tower + antennas) |
| Maintenance | Susceptible to corrosion, water ingress | Susceptible to rain fade, multipath |
| Scalability | Slow (must lay new cables) | Fast (add new SU with new antenna) |
| Security | Physical tapping difficult | **Encrypted over-the-air** but vulnerable to interception |
| Capacity | **4 kHz voice per pair** | **Multi-Mbps possible** with modern schemes |

### 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside the table below to keep markdown table syntax valid.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FSPL \;=\; 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-Space Path Loss (dB) | d in km, f in MHz |
| 2 | $P_r \;=\; P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Friis transmission equation | Power in Watts, d in metres, λ in metres |
| 3 | $SNR_{dB} \;=\; P_r(dB) - N_{floor}(dB)$ | Receiver SNR | dB |
| 4 | $C \;=\; B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + SNR\right)$ | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | Bits/sec, Hz, dimensionless SNR |
| 5 | $t_{prop} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{c}$ | One-way propagation delay | seconds, d in metres, c = 3×10⁸ m/s |
| 6 | $N_{cells} \;=\; \dfrac{A_{region}}{A_{cell}}$ | Number of base stations needed for coverage | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_{b}/N_{0} \;=\; SNR \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_b}$ | Energy per bit to noise spectral density | dB |

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility of WLL

- **Rural telephony in India (BSNL's CDMA-WLL, 2003-onwards):** Used extensively before mobile phones saturated rural markets.
- **Disaster recovery:** When earthquakes or cyclones destroy copper infrastructure, WLL systems can be **truck-mounted and operational within hours**.
- **5G Fixed Wireless Access (FWA):** Modern 5G NR operates at **24–47 GHz (mmWave)** — a direct descendant of WLL.
- **IoT backhaul:** Modern WSN deployments (smart agriculture, pipeline monitoring) use WLL-style **point-to-point microwave links** to forward data to cloud servers.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔁 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) for a WLL Link

The Friis transmission equation gives the **received power** under ideal free-space conditions:

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the path loss component.**

The fraction of power lost between the transmit and receive antennas is the **path loss** $L_p$:

$$
L_p = \frac{1}{G_t \cdot G_r} \cdot \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the linear ratio to decibels.**

Taking $10 \log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$
L_{p,\;dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$.**

$$
L_{p,\;dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi d \cdot f}{c} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the constant term.**

$$
4\pi / c \;\approx\; 4\pi / (3 \times 10^{8}) \;\approx\; 4.19 \times 10^{-8}
$$

Taking $20 \log_{10}(4.19 \times 10^{-8}) \approx -147.55$ dB.

**Step 5 — Rewrite with practical engineering units.**

When d is in **kilometres** and f is in **MHz**, the constant becomes $-32.44$ dB, yielding the **standard FSPL formula**:

$$
L_{p,\;dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic explained:** The constant changes because $4\pi/c$ in seconds becomes $4\pi f/(c \cdot 10^6)$ in MHz and $d$ becomes $d \cdot 10^3$ in km; after simplification the new constant is $\;20 \log_{10}(4\pi \cdot 10^3 \cdot 10^6 / (3 \times 10^8)) \approx 32.44\;$ dB.

### 🔁 Derivation 2 — Cell Radius of a WLL Base Station

A WLL base station must deliver a minimum received power $P_{r,min}$ to satisfy the receiver sensitivity. Given transmit power $P_t$, antenna gains $G_t$ and $G_r$, and link frequency $f$, the **maximum cell radius** $R_{max}$ is:

**Step 1 — Start with the Friis equation and solve for d.**

$$
d = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{r,min}} }
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$.**

$$
R_{max} = \frac{c}{4\pi f} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_{r,min}} }
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example for a typical 3.5 GHz WLL link.**

Let:
- $P_t$ = **1 W = 0 dBW**
- $G_t$ = $G_r$ = **18 dBi** (typical sector/parabolic antenna)
- $P_{r,min}$ = **−90 dBm = 10⁻¹² W**
- $f$ = **3.5 GHz = 3.5 × 10⁹ Hz**

$$
R_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4\pi (3.5 \times 10^{9})} \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{(1)(10^{1.8})(10^{1.8})}{10^{-12}} }
$$

$$
R_{max} \approx 0.00682 \times \sqrt{ 6.31 \times 10^{13} } \approx 0.00682 \times 7.94 \times 10^{6} \approx 54.2 \text{ km}
$$

In a real deployment this is **reduced by 20–40%** due to rain fade, foliage loss, and multipath.

### 🔁 Derivation 3 — Shannon Capacity of a WLL Channel

Given bandwidth B and SNR, the **maximum error-free data rate** is:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_{2}(1 + SNR) \quad \text{[bits/sec]}
$$

**Worked numerical example:**
- $B$ = **5 MHz** (typical WLL channel)
- $SNR$ = **20 (linear) = 13 dB**

$$
C = 5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 20) = 5 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(21)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(21) = \frac{\ln 21}{\ln 2} \approx \frac{3.045}{0.693} \approx 4.392
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 21.96 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

This matches the data rate of **WiMAX-style WLL systems** in practice.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define Wireless Local Loop. List any two advantages of WLL over wired local loop.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Wireless Local Loop (WLL)** is a system that uses **radio communication** as a substitute for the traditional copper-wire connection between a subscriber's premises and the telephone exchange, thereby providing the "last-mile" link wirelessly.
>
> **Two advantages:**
> 1. **Faster deployment** — antennas and base stations can be installed in days, whereas trenching copper can take months.
> 2. **Lower cost in rural or difficult terrain** — no civil works required to lay cables across rivers, mountains, or forests.
> 3. (Bonus) **Easy scalability** — adding a new subscriber only needs a CPE installation, not new cable laying.

### ✍️ Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of a Wireless Local Loop (WLL) system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the functions of the **Subscriber Unit (SU), Base Station (BS)**, and **PSTN Gateway**. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A WLL base station transmits with $P_t = 2$ W at a carrier frequency of **3.5 GHz**. Both transmit and receive antennas have a gain of **20 dBi**. The receiver sensitivity is **−92 dBm**. Calculate the **maximum cell radius** in free space. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The WLL architecture consists of three primary blocks:

1. **Subscriber Unit (SU) / CPE**
   - **Outdoor antenna** mounted on rooftop or window.
   - **RF transceiver** — handles modulation, upconversion, amplification.
   - **Network Interface Unit (NIU)** — converts radio signal to standard RJ-11 voice port or RJ-45 Ethernet.
   - Functions: provide the user's access to the WLL system; digitize voice; perform error-correction coding.

2. **Base Station (BS)**
   - Mounted on a tall tower or building.
   - Contains **sectorized antennas** (typically 60° or 90° sectors) covering 360° around the tower.
   - Performs **channel allocation, modulation/demodulation, multiplexing, and handover management** (for mobility cases).
   - Capacity: serves **hundreds to thousands** of subscribers per sector.

3. **PSTN Gateway / Switching Center**
   - Acts as the interface between the radio access network and the **public switched telephone network** (for voice) or **ISP backbone** (for data).
   - Performs **billing, authentication, call routing, and inter-BS handover** if required.

The block-diagram flow is:
**Subscriber Phone → NIU → CPE Antenna → (radio) → BS Antenna → BS Baseband → PSTN Gateway → PSTN / ISP core.**

**Functions summarized:**
- **SU:** digitize, modulate, transmit.
- **BS:** aggregate traffic, manage channels, control handoffs.
- **PSTN GW:** connect to core network, handle billing, ensure QoS.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given:
- $P_t = 2 \text{ W} = 3 \text{ dBW} = 33 \text{ dBm}$
- $f = 3.5 \text{ GHz} = 3500 \text{ MHz}$
- $G_t = G_r = 20 \text{ dBi}$
- $P_{r,min} = -92 \text{ dBm}$

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum tolerable path loss.**

$$
L_{max} \;=\; P_t(dBm) + G_t + G_r - P_{r,min}(dBm)
$$

$$
L_{max} \;=\; 33 + 20 + 20 - (-92) \;=\; 165 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the FSPL formula and solve for d.**

$$
L_{max} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

$$
165 = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(3500) + 32.44
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(3500) = 20 \times 3.5441 = 70.88 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 165 - 70.88 - 32.44 = 61.68
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 3.084 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{km} = 10^{3.084}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{max} \approx 1213 \text{ km}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the practical correction factor.**

In real deployment with rain fade, foliage, and multipath, the **effective radius is 30–40% of the free-space value**, giving:

$$
d_{practical} \approx 0.35 \times 1213 \approx \textbf{425 km}
$$

(This is unrealistically large because we ignored atmospheric losses; realistic WLL cell radius at 3.5 GHz is **30–50 km**.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Compare the various **WLL technologies** (DECT, PHS, MMDS, LMDS) in terms of frequency band, cell radius, capacity, and application. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain the role of WLL as a **backhaul technology** in modern Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) deployments, with a suitable example. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Technology | Frequency Band | Cell Radius | Capacity per user | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DECT** | **1.88 – 1.90 GHz** | 50 – 300 m | 32 kbps (voice) | Indoor cordless, dense urban WLL |
| **PHS** | **1.89 – 1.92 GHz** (Japan) | 100 – 500 m | 32 kbps | Walk-around telephony, micro-cellular WLL |
| **MMDS** | **2.1 – 2.7 GHz (3.5 GHz extended)** | 30 – 50 km | 64 kbps – 2 Mbps | Rural broadband, broadcast TV |
| **LMDS** | **26 – 28 GHz (mmWave)** | 2 – 5 km | 2 – 155 Mbps | Urban broadband, enterprise links |

**Explanation:**
- **DECT & PHS** are **low-power, short-range** technologies originally designed for indoor cordless phones, later adapted for dense urban WLL.
- **MMDS** uses **microwave frequencies** that propagate well over long distances, making it suitable for **rural and suburban WLL**.
- **LMDS** uses **mmWave** to deliver very high data rates but suffers from rain attenuation, restricting it to **short urban cells**.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

A Wireless Sensor Network is typically composed of:
- **Hundreds of low-power sensor nodes** (temperature, humidity, soil moisture, etc.)
- **One or more sink nodes** that aggregate the data
- **A backhaul link** that carries the aggregated data from the sink to the remote cloud server

In a **smart agriculture WSN**:
- Sensor nodes are scattered across a 5 km² farm, forming a **multi-hop mesh**.
- A **sink node** is placed at the farm office.
- The sink node does **not** connect to a wired ISP (no fiber/copper in remote villages).
- Instead, it uses a **WLL point-to-point link** (e.g., MMDS or 5.8 GHz unlicensed band) to a **base station at the nearest town** 20 km away.
- This WLL link acts as the **backhaul**, ferrying the aggregated farm data (soil moisture, weather, irrigation status) to the cloud every few minutes.

**Why WLL is preferred for WSN backhaul:**
1. **No wired infrastructure** in remote deployment zones.
2. **Adequate bandwidth** for low-rate sensor data (kbps range).
3. **Long range** (km) covers the typical sink-to-aggregation-server distance.
4. **Low cost** compared to satellite backhaul.

**Other WSN backhaul scenarios using WLL:**
- **Forest fire detection networks** in mountainous regions.
- **Pipeline monitoring** along hundreds of km of oil/gas pipeline.
- **Disaster-zone environmental monitoring** where all wired links are destroyed.

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw all three blocks** (SU, BS, PSTN) in the WLL diagram → loses 2–3 marks easily.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in numericals:** Mixing dBm and dBW without conversion → loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing LMDS and MMDS:** Remember: **L = Local (short), M = Multichannel (long)**. Easy to flip in exam stress.
> 4. **Not writing the FSPL constant 32.44 in the correct units** (d in km, f in MHz) → entire numerical marked wrong.
> 5. **Writing "WLL is just Wi-Fi"** in the exam — it is **NOT**; WLL is licensed, fixed, and operator-managed, whereas Wi-Fi is unlicensed, short-range, and best-effort.

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **WLL = Wireless Local Loop = radio replacement for the last-mile copper wire** between subscriber and telephone exchange.
- **Three components:** Subscriber Unit (CPE), Base Station, PSTN/ISP Gateway.
- **Frequency bands:** DECT (1.9 GHz), PHS (1.9 GHz), MMDS (2.5/3.5 GHz), LMDS (26/28 GHz).
- **Standard FSPL formula:** $L_p = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ — memorize with units.
- **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ — fundamental link-budget tool.
- **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ — used to estimate maximum WLL throughput.
- **WLL relevance to WSN:** Provides the **backhaul link** from the WSN sink node to the remote server when wired connectivity is unavailable.
- **Two WLL categories:** Low capacity (<64 kbps — DECT/PHS) and High capacity (>2 Mbps — MMDS/LMDS).
- **Advantages:** Rapid deployment, low civil-works cost, scalability.
- **Disadvantages:** Rain fade (especially at mmWave), line-of-sight requirement, spectrum licensing cost.
- **Modern descendant:** 5G Fixed Wireless Access (FWA) at 24–47 GHz.
- **Real-world Indian context:** BSNL's CDMA-WLL deployment (2003 onwards) brought telephony to remote villages without copper infrastructure.
- **Exam tip:** Always end your WLL architecture answer with a sentence on **why WLL is still relevant in the WSN/5G era** — examiners reward the "modern context" sentence.
