| 8 | $P_c = \Pr[G \text{ is connected}]$ | Connectivity probability | dimensionless |
| 9 | $D = \sum_{l=1}^{L} (d_{prop,l} + d_{proc,l} + d_{queue,l})$ | End-to-end delay | s |
| 10 | $\text{PDR} = \dfrac{N_{rcvd}}{N_{sent}} \times 100\%$ | Packet Delivery Ratio | % |
| 11 | $\text{Th} = \dfrac{\text{Total bits delivered}}{\text{Time}}$ | Throughput | bps |
| 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful work}}{\text{Energy spent}}$ | Energy efficiency ratio | bit/J |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In examination answer sheets, always use the **symbolic notation** (e.g., $E_{tx}$, $T_{FND}$) as shown in textbooks. KTU examiners award marks for *correct symbol usage*, not just plain English.

### 2.4 Multi-Objective Optimization Formulation

A general WSN optimization problem is expressed as:

$$\min_{x \in \mathcal{X}} \; F(x) = [f_1(x), f_2(x), ..., f_m(x)]^T$$

subject to:

$$g_j(x) \le 0, \quad j = 1, ..., p$$
$$h_k(x) = 0, \quad k = 1, ..., q$$

Where:
- $x$ = design variables (e.g., transmission power, routing paths, sleep schedules)
- $f_i(x)$ = objective functions (e.g., $E_{tot}$, $-T_{FND}$, $-C$)
- $g_j(x), h_k(x)$ = inequality and equality constraints (e.g., connectivity, coverage, energy availability)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** This multi-objective framework is used in **smart agriculture** (maximize coverage × lifetime under energy constraints), **disaster monitoring** (minimize latency × maximize reliability), and **structural health monitoring** (maximize data fidelity × minimize energy).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Radio Energy Model

The **First-Order Radio Model** (Heinzelman et al.) is the most cited energy model in WSN literature. It expresses transmission and reception energy for *k* bits over distance *d*.

**Step 1 — Start with the general energy equation for transmission:**

$$E_{tx}(k, d) = E_{tx\_elec}(k) + E_{tx\_amp}(k, d)$$

**Step 2 — The electronic circuitry consumes a fixed energy per bit ($E_{elec}$):**

$$E_{tx\_elec}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

**Step 3 — The amplifier energy depends on the path-loss exponent $n$:**

$$E_{tx\_amp}(k, d) = \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Combine Steps 2 and 3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tx}(k, d) &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} \\
&= k \cdot (E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — For reception, only electronic circuitry is used (no amplifier):**

$$E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

**Step 6 — The path-loss exponent $n$ depends on the propagation environment:**

| Environment | Path-Loss Exponent $n$ |
|---|---|
| Free space | **2** |
| Open outdoor (line-of-sight) | **2 to 2.5** |
| Urban cellular | **2.7 to 3.5** |
| Indoor with obstacles | **3 to 4** |
| Heavy indoor (factory) | **4 to 6** |

**Step 7 — Typical parameter values (CC2420 radio):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_{elec}$ | **50 nJ/bit** |
| $\epsilon_{amp}$ (free space) | **10 pJ/bit/m²** |
| $\epsilon_{amp}$ (multipath) | **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** |
| $d_0$ (crossover distance) | **87 m** |

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Detection Probability for K-Coverage

A point in the sensing field is **k-covered** if it lies within the sensing range of at least *k* sensor nodes. Derive the probability that a point is detected by *k* sensors.

**Step 1 — Define the single-sensor detection probability:**

Let $p_s$ be the probability that a single sensor detects an event at a given point.

**Step 2 — Compute the probability that a single sensor does NOT detect the event:**

$$P_{miss,1} = 1 - p_s$$

**Step 3 — For k independent sensors, the probability that NONE of them detects the event is:**

$$P_{miss,k} = (1 - p_s)^{k}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore, the probability that at least one of the k sensors detects the event is:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 1 - P_{miss,k} \\
&= 1 - (1 - p_s)^{k}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:** If $p_s = 0.7$ and $k = 3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 1 - (1 - 0.7)^{3} \\
&= 1 - (0.3)^{3} \\
&= 1 - 0.027 \\
&= 0.973
\end{aligned}$$

This means **97.3% detection probability** with 3 sensors each having 70% individual detection probability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Network Lifetime (T_FND) from Energy Decay

Consider a network of $N$ nodes. Each node $i$ has initial energy $E_0$ and dissipates energy at a rate $r_i(t)$ Joules/second.

**Step 1 — Energy remaining in node $i$ at time $t$:**

$$E_{res,i}(t) = E_0 - \int_{0}^{t} r_i(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — The death time of node $i$ is when $E_{res,i}(t) = 0$:**

$$t_{die,i} = E_0 \cdot \left( \int_{0}^{t_{die,i}} r_i(\tau) \, d\tau \right)^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Time until the FIRST node dies (T_FND):**

$$T_{FND} = \min_{i \in \{1,...,N\}} t_{die,i}$$

**Step 4 — Time until HALF the nodes die (T_HND):**

$$T_{HND} = \min \left\{ t \;\bigg|\; \left\vert \{i : E_{res,i}(t) > 0\} \right\vert \le \frac{N}{2} \right\}$$

**Step 5 — For uniform energy consumption (all nodes have $r_i = r$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{die,i} &= \frac{E_0}{r} \quad \text{(same for all } i \text{)} \\
T_{FND} &= T_{HND} = T_{LND} = \frac{E_0}{r}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Example calculation:** $E_0 = 5\text{ J}$, $r = 0.01\text{ J/s}$:

$$T_{FND} = \frac{5}{0.01} = 500 \text{ seconds} \approx 8.33 \text{ minutes}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Pareto Optimality in Multi-Objective WSN

When multiple optimization goals conflict, the concept of **Pareto Optimality** is used.

**Step 1 — Define a solution vector $\mathbf{x}$ that minimizes $F(\mathbf{x}) = [f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x})]$.**

**Step 2 — Solution $\mathbf{x}^*$ is **Pareto optimal** if there is NO other solution $\mathbf{x}$ such that:**

$$f_i(\mathbf{x}) \le f_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \quad \forall i, \quad \text{with strict inequality for at least one } i$$

**Step 3 — The set of all Pareto optimal solutions forms the **Pareto Front**.**

**Step 4 — Application to WSN:** A common bi-objective is **minimize energy AND maximize lifetime** — but reducing energy generally increases lifetime, so they are often aligned. A more interesting conflict is **minimize latency vs. maximize lifetime** (multi-hop reduces latency but increases total energy for intermediate nodes).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Optimization Goal | Primary FoM | Trade-off With | Typical Improvement Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Minimization** | E_b (J/bit) | Coverage, Latency | Sleep/wake scheduling, data aggregation |
| **Lifetime Maximization** | T_FND | Coverage, Throughput | Clustering (LEACH), energy harvesting |
| **Coverage Maximization** | C (%) | Energy, Cost | Node density, mobility, deployment |
| **Connectivity** | P_c | Energy (transmission range) | Topology control, relay placement |
| **Latency Minimization** | D (s) | Energy, Lifetime | Multi-hop shortest path, prioritization |
| **Reliability** | PDR (%) | Energy, Throughput | Retransmissions, redundancy |
| **Security** | Resistance to attack | Energy, Latency | Encryption, authentication |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Evaluation Pipeline for a WSN Design


---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **Figure of Merit** in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks. List any **four** commonly used figures of merit.

**Model Answer:**

A **Figure of Merit (FoM)** is a **quantitative metric** used to evaluate the performance of a WSN against its design objectives. It converts qualitative goals (e.g., "long lifetime") into measurable quantities (e.g., "180 days").

**Four commonly used Figures of Merit:**
1. **Network Lifetime** ($T_{FND}, T_{HND}, T_{LND}$) — measured in days/hours.
2. **Energy per bit** ($E_b$) — measured in Joules/bit.
3. **Coverage Ratio** ($C$) — measured in %.
4. **End-to-End Delay** ($D$) — measured in seconds.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **Optimization Goals** and **Figures of Merit** in WSN with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Optimization Goal | Figure of Merit |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Qualitative design objective | Quantitative performance metric |
| **Question answered** | *What* do we want to achieve? | *How well* did we achieve it? |
| **Type** | Verb (e.g., maximize, minimize) | Number with units |
| **Example goal** | Maximize network lifetime | $T_{FND} = 365$ days |
| **Example goal** | Minimize latency | $D = 50$ ms |
| **Example goal** | Maximize coverage | $C = 95\%$ |

**Example:** The goal *"maximize network lifetime"* is measured by the FoM *"$T_{FND}$ — time until the first node runs out of energy"*.

---

---

### 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] List and explain the **major optimization goals** in Wireless Sensor Networks. Discuss the inherent **trade-offs** among them.

**Model Answer:**

WSN optimization is a **multi-objective problem** constrained by energy, bandwidth, and processing limits. The major goals are:

**(i) Energy Minimization** — Sensor nodes are battery-powered and often deployed in inaccessible regions. Energy consumption includes $E_{tx}, E_{rx}, E_{sense}, E_{proc}, E_{idle}$. Goal: minimize $E_{tot}$.

**(ii) Network Lifetime Maximization** — Lifetime is defined using T_FND, T_HND, or T_LND. Maximizing lifetime requires energy-efficient protocols (LEACH, PEGASIS).

**(iii) Coverage Maximization** — Ensures the sensing field is adequately monitored. Measured by **Area Coverage Ratio** $C = A_{covered} / A_{total}$ and **K-coverage index**.

**(iv) Connectivity Maintenance** — The network graph must remain connected for data delivery. Measured by **Connectivity Probability** $P_c$.

**(v) Latency / Delay Minimization** — Time-critical applications (e.g., fire detection) need low end-to-end delay $D$.

**(vi) Reliability / Data Fidelity** — Measured by **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** and **Detection Probability** $P_d$.

**(vii) Security** — Resistance to eavesdropping, DoS, Sybil attacks.

**Trade-offs:**
- **Energy vs. Coverage:** Higher coverage requires more active nodes → more energy.
- **Energy vs. Latency:** Multi-hop routing reduces per-hop energy but increases delay.
- **Lifetime vs. Throughput:** Aggressive data transmission drains batteries quickly.
- **Security vs. Energy:** Encryption consumes significant energy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question A(a):** Students often list goals but **fail to mention trade-offs**. KTU examiners allocate at least **2 marks specifically for the trade-off discussion**. Always state which goals conflict and why.

---

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] Derive the **First-Order Radio Energy Model** for transmitting *k* bits over distance *d*. Given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², and $d = 100$ m, compute $E_{tx}$ for $k = 2000$ bits in free space.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Start with the transmission energy model:**

$$E_{tx}(k, d) = E_{tx\_elec} + E_{tx\_amp}$$

**Step 2 — Electronic energy for transmission:**

$$E_{tx\_elec} = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

**Step 3 — Amplifier energy in free space (path-loss exponent $n = 2$):**

$$E_{tx\_amp} = \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$E_{tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit, $\epsilon_{amp} = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m², $d = 100$ m, $k = 2000$ bits.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tx} &= (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 2000 + (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 2000 \cdot (100)^{2} \\
&= 100 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2000 \cdot 10000 \\
&= 100 \times 10^{-6} + 0.2 \\
&= 0.0001 + 0.2 \\
&= 0.2001 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $E_{tx} \approx 0.2001$ J ≈ **200.1 mJ**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question A(b):** Common errors include:
> 1. **Forgetting units** — $E_{elec}$ is in nJ (nano), $\epsilon_{amp}$ in pJ (pico). Convert correctly to Joules.
> 2. **Wrong path-loss exponent** — Free space uses $n = 2$, multipath uses $n = 4$.
> 3. **Not showing the substitution step** — Examiners award step-marks; always write each substitution explicitly.

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **three definitions of network lifetime** in WSN. Which one is most commonly used, and why?

**Model Answer:**

A WSN's lifetime ends when it can no longer fulfill its designated function. There are three commonly cited definitions:

**(i) Time until First Node Dies ($T_{FND}$):**
$$T_{FND} = \min_{i \in \{1,...,N\}} t_{die,i}$$
This is the **most conservative** definition. It assumes that as soon as one node dies, the network's mission may be compromised (e.g., a coverage hole appears, a routing path breaks). Suitable for **mission-critical applications** like fire detection in a nuclear plant.

**(ii) Time until Half Nodes Die ($T_{HND}$):**
$$T_{HND} = \min \left\{ t \;\bigg|\; \left\vert \{i : E_{res,i}(t) > 0\} \right\vert \le \frac{N}{2} \right\}$$
This is the **most realistic** definition for general applications. By the time half the nodes have died, redundancy has already been exploited, and the network is clearly degrading. It is widely used in **research benchmarks**.

**(iii) Time until Last Node Dies ($T_{LND}$):**
$$T_{LND} = \max_{i \in \{1,...,N\}} t_{die,i}$$
This is the **most pessimistic** definition. The network is considered alive as long as **any single node** still operates. Suitable for **non-critical monitoring** (e.g., periodic environmental logging).

**Most commonly used:** $T_{FND}$ is the most commonly reported metric in research papers (e.g., LEACH, PEGASIS evaluations) because it provides a **lower bound** on lifetime and is easy to measure.

---

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] A WSN has $N = 100$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in a 100 m × 100 m field. Each node has a sensing range of 20 m. Compute the **average number of sensors covering an arbitrary point** in the field and the **detection probability** if each sensor has $p_s = 0.8$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the area of the field and the sensing footprint of one node:**

$$A_{field} = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{sense} = \pi r^2 = \pi \times 20^2 = 400\pi \approx 1256.64 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average number of sensors covering an arbitrary point:**

$$\begin{aligned}
k_{avg} &= N \cdot \frac{A_{sense}}{A_{field}} \\
&= 100 \cdot \frac{400\pi}{10{,}000} \\
&= 100 \cdot 0.12566 \\
&= 12.566
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — The detection probability with $k_{avg} \approx 12.57$ sensors and $p_s = 0.8$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_d &= 1 - (1 - p_s)^{k_{avg}} \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.8)^{12.566} \\
&= 1 - (0.2)^{12.566}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(0.2)^{12.566}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.2)^{12.566} &= e^{12.566 \cdot \ln(0.2)} \\
&= e^{12.566 \cdot (-1.6094)} \\
&= e^{-20.226} \\
&\approx 1.63 \times 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final detection probability:**

$$P_d \approx 1 - 1.63 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.9999999984$$

**Result:** $k_{avg} \approx 12.57$ sensors cover an arbitrary point, and $P_d \approx 99.9999998\%$ — essentially **certain detection**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(b):** Common mistakes:
> 1. **Using diameter instead of radius** in $A_{sense} = \pi r^2$.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the binomial model** — students sometimes just use $P_d = p_s$ instead of the k-sensor formula.
> 3. **Numerical errors in powers** — $(0.2)^{12.566}$ requires logarithms; use $\ln$ tables or scientific calculators.

---

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 2: Optimization Goals and Figures of Merit**

- ✅ **Optimization Goals** are the *qualitative design objectives* (maximize lifetime, minimize energy, maximize coverage, etc.).
- ✅ **Figures of Merit (FoM)** are the *quantitative performance metrics* that measure goal achievement.
- ✅ **Three Lifetime Definitions:**
  - $T_{FND}$ = Time until **First Node Dies** (conservative, most cited).
  - $T_{HND}$ = Time until **Half Nodes Die** (realistic).
  - $T_{LND}$ = Time until **Last Node Dies** (pessimistic).
- ✅ **First-Order Radio Model:** $E_{tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$.
- ✅ **Reception Energy:** $E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ (no amplifier term).
- ✅ **Free space:** $n = 2$; **Multipath:** $n = 4$.
- ✅ **Detection Probability (K-coverage):** $P_d = 1 - (1 - p_s)^{k}$.
- ✅ **Coverage Ratio:** $C = A_{covered} / A_{total}$.
- ✅ **Average k-coverage:** $k_{avg} = N \cdot A_{sense} / A_{field}$.
- ✅ **PDR:** $\text{PDR} = N_{rcvd} / N_{sent} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **End-to-End Delay:** $D = \sum (d_{prop} + d_{proc} + d_{queue})$.
- ✅ **Throughput:** $\text{Th} = \text{Total bits} / \text{Time}$ (in bps).
- ✅ **Energy per bit:** $E_b = E_{tot} / N_{bits}$ (in J/bit) — the **gold-standard** FoM for energy efficiency.
- ✅ **Pareto Optimality** is used when goals conflict; no solution can improve one goal without worsening another.
- ✅ **Major Trade-offs to Remember:**
  - Energy ↔ Coverage, Latency, Reliability, Security.
  - Latency ↔ Lifetime.
- ✅ **Multi-objective optimization** is expressed as $\min F(x) = [f_1(x), f_2(x), ..., f_m(x)]$ subject to constraints.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Three Lifetime Definitions:**
> **"F**irst, **H**alf, **L**ast" → "**F**ault-critical, **H**uman-realistic, **L**ogging-lax"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Principles of WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Principles of WSNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) Design Principles** refer to the set of fundamental engineering constraints, trade-offs, and architectural guidelines that govern the conception, hardware selection, and operational deployment of distributed networks of resource-constrained sensor nodes. Unlike traditional ad-hoc or cellular networks, WSN design is dominated by four pillars: **energy efficiency**, **application-specific customization**, **data-centric routing**, and **dense fault-tolerant deployment**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the design of a WSN is not a one-size-fits-all approach; it is strictly an **application-driven synthesis** where every hardware choice, protocol, and routing strategy must be co-designed to satisfy the unique mission of the network (e.g., precision agriculture, battlefield surveillance, structural health monitoring, or underwater habitat mapping).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The design of WSNs is fundamentally different from the design of traditional wireless networks (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, Bluetooth). WSNs are **data-centric**, **infrastructure-less**, **self-organizing**, **energy-constrained**, and **application-specific**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Smart Farm:**
Imagine a vast farm where a farmer wants to know the soil moisture, temperature, and humidity at 10,000 different points. Instead of laying physical wires, he scatters thousands of tiny, battery-powered "smart pebbles" across the field. Each pebble has a sensor (to feel the soil), a tiny brain (to compute), a small radio (to whisper to neighbors), and a battery (its life support). The pebbles form their own mesh, gossiping data from neighbor to neighbor until the information reaches a central gateway. The farmer's *design challenge* is to make these pebbles **last 5 years on a single AA battery**, **survive rain and dust**, and **not cost more than a cup of tea each**.

**Analogy 2 — The Hospital Ward:**
Think of WSN design like designing a network of tiny, wireless patient monitors in a hospital. The monitors (sensor nodes) have limited battery, limited memory, and must relay patient vitals (heart rate, oxygen) reliably to a central nursing station (sink/base station). The design must ensure **no patient data is lost** (reliability), **battery lasts a full shift** (energy efficiency), and **the network scales when more patients are admitted** (scalability).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Radio Frequency Bands (Industrial, Scientific, Medical — ISM):**
  - 433 MHz (Europe, Asia, range ~30-100 m)
  - 868 MHz (Europe, range ~30-100 m)
  - **915 MHz** (North America, range ~30-100 m)
  - **2.4 GHz** (Global standard, IEEE 802.15.4/ZigBee, range ~10-30 m indoor, ~100 m outdoor)
- **Baud Rate Range:** 10 kbps to 250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4)
- **Typical Node Supply Voltage:** **1.8 V to 3.6 V DC** (2× AA or coin cell)
- **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) Exponent:** Typically between **2 (free space)** and **4 (urban/indoor)**
- **Energy of a Single Bit Transmission (E_b):** Often modeled in the range of **50 nJ/bit to 100 nJ/bit**
- **Standard Mote Form Factor:** MicaZ, TelosB, IRIS (Crossbow/MEMSIC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Traditional networks are designed for **high throughput** and **low latency**. WSNs are designed for **low energy**, **long lifetime**, and **fault tolerance**. If a student confuses these priorities in the exam, marks will be lost.

## 1.4 Hierarchical View of WSN Design Considerations

| Design Layer | Primary Concern | Typical Range/Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Layer** | Energy, memory, processing | Joules, KB, MHz |
| **Physical Layer** | Modulation, transmission range | dBm, BER |
| **MAC Layer** | Duty cycle, collision avoidance | Sleep/wake ratio |
| **Network Layer** | Routing, data aggregation | Hop count, paths |
| **Application Layer** | QoS, query processing, event detection | Latency, accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 7 Primary Design Goals of a WSN (KTU High-Yield)

According to the seminal work of Akyildiz, Vuran, and the KTU 2024 syllabus, the design of any WSN must satisfy the following seven concurrent objectives. These are **highly testable** in KTU exams:

### 2.1.1 Energy Efficiency
- The single most important constraint. Energy budget is finite (battery or energy harvesting).
- **Goal:** Maximize **network lifetime** (time until first node / last node dies).
- **Metric:** Joules consumed per bit transmitted/received.

### 2.1.2 Scalability
- A WSN may contain **hundreds to millions of nodes**.
- Protocols must scale logarithmically, not linearly.
- **Metric:** Performance degradation as N → 10,000 nodes.

### 2.1.3 Fault Tolerance & Reliability
- Nodes can fail due to **battery depletion, physical damage, RF interference, or environmental hazards**.
- Network must continue operating even if **20–30% of nodes die**.
- Achieved via **redundancy, multi-path routing, and self-healing mesh topology**.

### 2.1.4 Production Cost
- Each node must be **inexpensive** (target: <$1 per node in large deployments).
- Use of COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) components.
- Cost = Hardware + Software + Deployment + Maintenance.

### 2.1.5 Hardware Constraints
- Limited **CPU** (e.g., 8 MHz ATmega128), limited **RAM** (e.g., 4–10 KB), limited **ROM/Flash** (e.g., 48–128 KB).
- Limited **sensing modalities** (temperature, humidity, vibration, gas, motion, light).
- Limited **communication range** (a few meters to ~100 m).

### 2.1.6 Operating Environment
- WSNs are often deployed in **hostile, inaccessible, or harsh** locations: inside machinery, under water, in battlefields, inside human bodies, or underground.
- Must withstand **temperature extremes, humidity, vibration, dust, EMI, corrosion**.

### 2.1.7 Transmission Media
Three primary physical media are used, each with distinct trade-offs:

| Medium | Pros | Cons | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Radio Frequency (RF)** | Omni-directional, long range, no line-of-sight | High energy, interference, eavesdropping | Most WSNs (ZigBee, BLE, LoRa) |
| **Optical (Laser/IR)** | Low energy, high bandwidth, secure | Requires line-of-sight (LoS), alignment critical | Smart dust, indoor VLC |
| **Acoustic (Ultrasound)** | Works underwater, LoS not strictly required | Very low bandwidth, high latency, affected by noise | Underwater WSNs (UWSN) |
| **Magnetic Induction** | Works in metal-rich environments | Very short range, extremely low data rate | Underground WSNs, tunnels, mines |

## 2.2 Hardware Architecture of a Sensor Node (4 Subsystems)

A typical sensor node (mote) consists of **four tightly integrated subsystems**:

1. **Sensing Subsystem:** ADC + physical sensors (temperature, humidity, light, accelerometer, gas, magnetometer, camera, acoustic). Consumes moderate power.
2. **Processing Subsystem:** Microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ATmega128) + memory (SRAM, Flash). Often the lowest power consumer.
3. **Communication Subsystem:** Radio transceiver (CC2420, CC1000, XBee, LoRa). Typically the **highest power consumer**.
4. **Power Supply Subsystem:** Battery (Alkaline, Li-Ion, Li-SOCl2) + optional energy harvester (solar, vibration, thermal, RF).

```
        ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
        │         SENSOR NODE (MOTE)         │
        ├─────────────┬───────────┬───────────┤
        │  SENSING    │ PROCESSING│  POWER    │
        │  SUBSYSTEM  │ SUBSYSTEM │  SUBSYSTEM│
        │ (ADC+Sensors)│ (μC+RAM)  │ (Battery) │
        └──────┬──────┴─────┬─────┴─────┬─────┘
               │            │            │
               └────────────┴────────────┘
                          │
                  ┌───────┴────────┐
                  │ COMMUNICATION  │
                  │   SUBSYSTEM    │
                  │ (Radio xcvr)   │
                  └────────────────┘
```

## 2.3 Sources of Energy Consumption (Energy Budget)

Energy in a sensor node is consumed primarily by three operations:

| Operation | Typical Energy Ratio | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Transmission (Tx)** | 60–80% | Highest energy: dominated by RF amplifier power |
| **Reception (Rx)** | 20–40% | Radio must be in active mode |
| **Sensing + Processing** | 5–10% | Often negligible compared to radio |
| **Idle Listening** | Equal to Rx | A major WSN energy pitfall (why MAC duty cycling exists) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Fact:** The radio subsystem (Tx + Rx) typically consumes **80% to 90%** of the total node energy. This is why **energy-efficient MAC and routing protocols** are the most researched areas in WSNs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 ESE and internal exams. All quantities use SI units unless otherwise specified.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | First-Order Radio Energy Model (Transmission) | Joules |
| 2 | $E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Energy to receive a k-bit packet | Joules |
| 3 | $E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$ | Energy per bit for Tx/Rx electronics (typical) | J/bit |
| 4 | $\epsilon_{amp} = 100 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$ | Transmit amplifier energy (free space, n=2) | J/bit/m² |
| 5 | $\epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$ | Transmit amplifier energy (multi-path, n=4) | J/bit/m⁴ |
| 6 | $E_{total} = N \cdot (E_{tx} + E_{rx}) + N \cdot E_{sense}$ | Total network energy for N nodes | Joules |
| 7 | $L_{net} = \frac{E_{initial}}{E_{round}}$ | Network lifetime in rounds (LEACH model) | Rounds |
| 8 | $T_{duty} = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | MAC duty cycle ratio | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $PS_{avrg} = \frac{V^2}{R}$ | Average power dissipation (resistive) | Watts |
| 10 | $T_{lifetime} = \frac{C_{bat} \cdot V}{P_{avrg}}$ | Battery lifetime (Peukert's approximation) | Seconds/Hours |
| 11 | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} / G_{ant}^2$ | Friis Transmission Equation (simplified) | Watts |
| 12 | $FSPL = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | Free-Space Path Loss (d, f in km, MHz) | dB |
| 13 | $SNR = P_{signal} / P_{noise}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Dimensionless (dB) |
| 14 | $BER = Q(\sqrt{2 \cdot SNR})$ | Bit Error Rate (BPSK in AWGN) | Dimensionless |
| 15 | $N_{max} = \lfloor A / (\pi r^2) \rfloor$ | Max nodes in area A with sensing range r | Nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** The vertical bar `|` is forbidden inside markdown tables. For absolute value, **always** use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$. For conditional probability, use $\vert$ as well.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design principles of WSNs underpin some of the most critical modern systems:
- **Precision Agriculture** — Soil moisture and crop health monitoring across 100-acre farms.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** — Bridges (Golden Gate Bridge has a 200-node WSN) and buildings.
- **Healthcare** — Wireless body area networks (WBAN) for continuous patient monitoring.
- **Environmental Monitoring** — Volcano monitoring (Pinatubo), forest fire detection, glacier movement.
- **Military/Defense** — Battlefield surveillance, intrusion detection, sniper localization.
- **Smart Grids & Smart Cities** — Real-time monitoring of power lines, water pipelines, and traffic.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Predictive maintenance of motors, turbines, and rotating machinery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman Model)

The **First-Order Radio Energy Model** (proposed by Heinzelman, Chandrakasan, and Balakrishnan, 2000) is the de-facto energy model used in KTU syllabus and LEACH protocol analysis. We derive it step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions of the Model

- Radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ to run the transmitter/receiver circuitry (per bit).
- Transmit amplifier dissipates $\epsilon_{amp}$ for free-space ($d^{2}$) or $\epsilon_{mp}$ for multi-path ($d^{4}$) propagation.
- Symmetric channel: Energy to transmit from A → B = Energy from B → A.
- All nodes transmit with equal power control (can be relaxed for advanced models).

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Transmission Energy $E_{tx}$

**Step 1: Define the energy components.**
The total energy to transmit a k-bit message over distance d consists of two parts:
1. Energy to run the **transmitter electronics** = $E_{elec} \cdot k$
2. Energy to run the **transmit power amplifier** = $\epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$

**Step 2: Sum the two energy components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{tx}(k, d) &= \underbrace{E_{elec} \cdot k}_{\text{electronics}} + \underbrace{\epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}}_{\text{amplifier}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the path-loss exponent.**

- For **free-space propagation** ($n = 2$): $\quad \epsilon_{amp} = \epsilon_{fs} = 100 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$
- For **multi-path propagation** ($n = 4$): $\quad \epsilon_{amp} = \epsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$

**Step 4: Apply the crossover distance threshold.**

The transmitter uses the free-space model if $d < d_{crossover}$, otherwise multi-path. The crossover distance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{crossover} &= \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{100}{0.0013}} \\
&= \sqrt{76923.08} \\
&\approx 87.7 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Write the final piecewise transmission energy model.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{tx}(k, d) &= \begin{cases}
E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} & \text{if } d < d_{crossover} \\[4pt]
E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4} & \text{if } d \geq d_{crossover}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Reception Energy $E_{rx}$

**Step 1:** Reception only requires running the receiver electronics, not the amplifier.

**Step 2:** The energy consumed is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{rx}(k) &= E_{elec} \cdot k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Note that receiving is often as expensive (or nearly so) as transmitting short messages because the receiver electronics must run at full power to maintain synchronization and detect weak signals.

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Total Energy for a Single Round (LEACH-style Cluster)

**Step 1:** Consider a single cluster head (CH) that receives data from N cluster members and aggregates/transmits it to the base station.

**Step 2:** Energy consumed by each member to send k bits to its CH at distance $d_{toCH}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{member} &= E_{tx}(k, d_{toCH}) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Energy consumed by CH to receive N such packets (k bits each):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{CH, rx} &= N \cdot E_{rx}(k) = N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Energy consumed by CH to aggregate and transmit the fused data over distance $d_{toBS}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{CH, tx} &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Total energy per round for the cluster:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{round} &= N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + N \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_{toCH}^{2} \\
&\quad + N \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WSN Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increases with → | Trade-off / Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission range (d) | ↑ | ↑ Energy consumption (d² or d⁴) |
| Transmit power (Pt) | ↑ | ↑ Energy, ↑ Interference to neighbors |
| Number of nodes (N) | ↑ | ↑ Network coverage, ↑ Energy, ↑ Collisions |
| Sampling rate (fs) | ↑ | ↑ Sensing energy, ↑ Data volume |
| Duty cycle (DC) | ↑ | ↑ Latency, ↑ Energy |
| Number of hops to sink | ↑ | ↑ Latency, ↓ Per-hop energy, ↑ Reliability |
| Data aggregation ratio | ↑ | ↓ Energy, ↓ Fidelity, ↑ Complexity |
| Cluster size (k) | ↑ | ↑ Intra-cluster collisions, ↑ Throughput |
| Redundancy factor (r) | ↑ | ↑ Fault tolerance, ↑ Energy |
| Security level | ↑ | ↑ Processing, ↑ Energy, ↑ Latency |

## 3.3 Numerical Example (High-Yield KTU Problem Type)

**Problem:** Consider a WSN where 100 sensor nodes are uniformly distributed in a 100 m × 100 m field. Each node generates a 2000-bit data packet and transmits it directly to a base station located at the center. Using the free-space energy model, calculate:

(a) The total energy consumed by all nodes in a single round.
(b) The average energy per node.

**Given:**
- $N = 100$, $k = 2000$ bits
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 100$ pJ/bit/m²
- Assume average distance from each node to BS: $d_{avg} = \sqrt{\frac{Area}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{10000}{2\pi}} \approx 39.89$ m

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Energy per node to transmit k bits over d_avg:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{tx}(k, d_{avg}) &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_{avg}^{2} \\
&= (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 2000 + (100 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 2000 \cdot (39.89)^{2} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-4} + (2 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 1591.2 \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-4} + 3.18 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 4.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Total energy for N = 100 nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= N \cdot E_{tx} = 100 \cdot 4.18 \times 10^{-4} = 4.18 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} = 41.8 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{avg} &= \frac{E_{total}}{N} = \frac{41.8 \text{ mJ}}{100} = 0.418 \text{ mJ per node}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to compare single-hop vs multi-hop, observe that single-hop saves coordination energy but explodes amplifier energy (d⁴ term). Multi-hop distributes energy load evenly and is generally preferred for d > 87.7 m.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Detailed WSN Node Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Battery 2xAA 3V]:::pwr
    A2[Solar Harvester 5V]:::pwr
    A3[DC-DC Converter 3.3V]:::pwr

    B1[Temperature Sensor]:::sens
    B2[Humidity Sensor]:::sens
    B3[Light Sensor LDR]:::sens
    B4[Gas Sensor MQ-2]:::sens
    B5[ADC MCP3008 10-bit]:::sens

    C1[MCU MSP430 16-bit]:::proc
    C2[SRAM 10KB]:::proc
    C3[Flash 48KB]:::proc
    C4[ROM TinyOS]:::proc
    C5[Aggregation Engine]:::proc

    D1[Radio CC2420 2.4GHz]:::comm
    D2[Antenna Chip 2.4GHz]:::comm
    D3[MAC Engine IEEE 802.15.4]:::comm
    D4[Crypto Engine AES-128]:::comm

    E1[Sink Gateway]:::sink
    E2[Internet Cloud]:::sink
    E3[Server Analytics]:::sink

    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> C1
    A3 --> D1

    B1 --> B5
    B2 --> B5
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1

    C1 <--> C2
    C1 <--> C3
    C1 <--> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C5 --> D3

    D3 <--> D1
    D1 <--> D2
    D1 <--> D4

    D2 -->|RF Wireless| E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3

    classDef pwr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000

    classDef sink fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: 7 Design Goals Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[WSN Design Principles]:::root

    G1[Energy Efficiency]:::g1
    G2[Scalability]:::g2
    G3[Fault Tolerance]:::g3
    G4[Production Cost]:::g4
    G5[Hardware Constraints]:::g5
    G6[Operating Environment]:::g6
    G7[Transmission Media]:::g7

    G1A[Duty Cycling]
    G1B[Data Aggregation]
    G1C[Multi-hop Routing]
    G1D[Energy Harvesting]

    G2A[Hierarchical Protocols]
    G2B[Clustering LEACH]
    G2C[Geographic Routing]

    G3A[Redundancy]
    G3B[Multi-path Routing]
    G3C[Self-healing Mesh]

    G7A[RF Radio]
    G7B[Optical IR]
    G7C[Acoustic Ultrasound]
    G7D[Magnetic Induction]

    ROOT --> G1
    ROOT --> G2
    ROOT --> G3
    ROOT --> G4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Node Operational States & Energy Profile

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sleep
    Sleep --> Wake: Timer Interrupt
    Wake --> Sense: ADC Read
    Sense --> Process: Aggregation
    Process --> Transmit: Buffer Ready
    Transmit --> Receive: Wait ACK
    Receive --> Sleep: Send Done
    Wake --> Sleep: No Event
    Transmit --> Sleep: Collision Retries Exhausted

    note right of Sleep: Lowest power ~1 uA
    note right of Transmit: Highest power ~25 mA
    note right of Receive: High power ~20 mA
    note right of Process: Low power ~1.5 mA
```
