## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Friis Free-Space Path Loss (Full Derivation)

**Starting Point:** Electromagnetic wave propagation in free space follows the **inverse-square law** for an isotropic radiator.

**Step 1 — Define Power Flux Density at Distance d:**

The transmitting antenna radiates power Pt uniformly over a sphere of radius d. The power flux density (PFD) is:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

*Reason:* The surface area of a sphere of radius d is 4πd². The product Gt accounts for the antenna's directional gain.

**Step 2 — Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna:**

The receiving antenna of effective aperture Ae captures the power:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t A_e}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Effective Aperture Relation:**

The effective aperture of an antenna is related to its gain by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

*Reason:* This is derived from antenna reciprocity and is a standard electromagnetic result.

**Step 4 — Combine and Simplify:**

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Express in dB:**

Convert to dB form for engineering convenience:

$$P_r\,[\text{dBm}] = P_t\,[\text{dBm}] + G_t\,[\text{dBi}] + G_r\,[\text{dBi}] - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

The term in the logarithm is the **path loss in free space**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Practical Path Loss Formula in dB

**Step 1 — Substitute λ = c / f into the Friis equation:**

$$PL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Take the logarithm (base 10) and multiply by 10:**

$$PL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^{2}\right]$$

$$PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi f}{c}\right) + 20\log_{10}(d)$$

**Step 3 — Insert numerical values: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, and use d in km, f in MHz:**

When d is in km, d = 1000 × d_km, so 20 log10(d) = 20 log10(1000) + 20 log10(d_km) = 60 + 20 log10(d_km).

When f is in MHz, f = 10⁶ × f_MHz, so 20 log10(f) = 20 log10(10⁶) + 20 log10(f_MHz) = 120 + 20 log10(f_MHz).

Substituting and simplifying:

$$PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 32.44$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{PL_{dB}(d) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Receiver Sensitivity and Noise Floor

**Step 1 — Thermal Noise Power in a Bandwidth B:**

By Nyquist's theorem, the thermal noise power available from a resistor at temperature T in bandwidth B is:

$$N = k T B$$

Where **k = Boltzmann constant = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** and T = 290 K (room temperature).

**Step 2 — Convert to dBm (per 1 Hz):**

$$N_0 = 10\log_{10}(kT \cdot 1) = 10\log_{10}(1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290)$$

$$N_0 = 10\log_{10}(4.00 \times 10^{-21}) = -203.98 \text{ dBW/Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Converting to dBm/Hz:**

$$N_0 = -203.98 + 30 = -173.98 \approx -174 \text{ dBm/Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Total Noise in Bandwidth B (dBm):**

$$N = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$$

**Step 5 — Add Noise Figure and Required SNR for Target BER:**

For a target BER of 10⁻⁵ using BPSK, Q(√(2Eb/N0)) = 10⁻⁵ gives Eb/N0 ≈ 9.6 dB.

$$\boxed{P_{rx,min} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B) + NF + SNR_{min}\,[\text{dB}]}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy per Bit of a WSN Radio

**Step 1 — Transmit Energy for a Packet of N bits:**

$$E_{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{packet} = P_{tx} \cdot \frac{N_{bits}}{R_b}$$

*Reason:* The packet duration equals the number of bits divided by the bit rate Rb.

**Step 2 — Substitute into Energy-per-Bit formula:**

$$E_b^{tx} = \frac{E_{tx}}{N_{bits}} = \frac{P_{tx}}{R_b}$$

**Step 3 — Including receiver energy (asymmetric link):**

$$E_b^{total} = \frac{P_{tx} + P_{rx}}{R_b}$$

*Note:* This shows a counter-intuitive result — **higher bit rate Rb ⇒ lower energy per bit for the same packet**, which motivates high-data-rate WSN radios.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Log-Normal Shadowing (Empirical Path Loss)

**Step 1 — Start with the free-space path loss at a reference distance d₀:**

$$PL(d_0) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d_0}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Scale for distance d using path-loss exponent n (n = 2 in free space):**

$$PL_{avg}(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Add random shadowing term Xσ (Gaussian, zero-mean, σ dB):**

$$\boxed{PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma}$$

Typical values: **n = 2 (free space), 3 (urban), 4–5 (shadowed indoor); σ = 4 to 10 dB**.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Physical Layer in Wireless Sensor Networks. List any four of its primary responsibilities.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Physical Layer (PHY) is Layer 1 of the WSN protocol stack and is responsible for the **actual transmission and reception of raw bit streams** over the wireless medium.

**Four primary responsibilities (½ mark each):**
1. **Frequency selection and carrier generation** — Synthesizing the RF carrier using a local oscillator.
2. **Modulation and demodulation** — Converting digital bits into analog waveforms (e.g., O-QPSK) and vice versa.
3. **Channel coding / decoding** — Adding redundancy for error detection and correction (e.g., BCH, Hamming).
4. **Bit synchronization and signal detection** — Recovering the clock and detecting the start of frames at the receiver.

*(Acceptable: antenna interface, transmit/receive switching, encryption at PHY.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is meant by "transceiver duty cycle" in WSNs? Why is reducing duty cycle critical for energy-constrained sensor nodes?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Duty Cycle (1.5 marks):** Duty cycle δ is defined as the ratio of the time the radio is in the active (Tx/Rx) state to the total time (active + sleep) of one operational cycle:

$$\delta = \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

**Why critical (1.5 marks):** The active state consumes **3 to 4 orders of magnitude more power** than the sleep state (e.g., CC2420: 18.8 mA Rx vs. 1 μA sleep). Since the battery capacity is fixed, **reducing the on-time directly extends the node's lifetime** — often from days to years. This is the **single most important design lever** in energy-constrained WSN design.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **five major design considerations** for the physical layer of a Wireless Sensor Network. Discuss the trade-offs involved in selecting the **carrier frequency band** and **modulation scheme**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN node uses a transmitter with **Pt = 10 mW** at **f = 2.4 GHz**, and isotropic antennas (Gt = Gr = 1). The receiver sensitivity is **−90 dBm**. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum free-space communication range `d_max`. **(4 Marks)**
   (ii) The received power at **d = 50 m**. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Design Considerations (7 Marks):**

The five major design considerations for the WSN Physical Layer are:

1. **Energy Efficiency (2 marks)** — Sensor nodes are battery-powered and often deployed in inaccessible locations. The PHY must minimize energy per bit by using:
   - Low-duty-cycle operation with aggressive sleep modes.
   - Power-efficient modulation (e.g., O-QPSK instead of high-order QAM).
   - Optimized transmit power (not always maximum).

2. **Cost and Hardware Complexity (1 mark)** — For mass deployment (1000s of nodes), per-node cost must be low. This drives the choice of single-chip transceivers (e.g., CC2420) over discrete designs.

3. **Robustness to Channel Impairments (1.5 marks)** — Multipath fading, shadowing, and interference must be combated. Solutions: DSSS spreading (IEEE 802.15.4), forward error correction, and link-layer retransmissions.

4. **Carrier Frequency Band Selection (1.5 marks):**

| Frequency | Pros | Cons | WSN Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 433/868/915 MHz | Long range, less path loss, better wall penetration | Lower data rates, larger antennas | Outdoor agriculture, industrial |
| 2.4 GHz | High data rate, small antenna, global ISM | Higher path loss, crowded (Wi-Fi interference) | Indoor WSN, IEEE 802.15.4 |
| UWB (3.1–10.6 GHz) | Low power, ranging capability | Regulatory limits, complex DSP | Localization, BAN |

5. **Modulation Scheme Selection (1 mark):**
   - **OOK/ASK:** Simplest, but poor noise immunity.
   - **FSK:** Robust to amplitude noise, used in CC1000.
   - **O-QPSK with DSSS:** Best compromise — used in **IEEE 802.15.4** for 2.4 GHz WSN. Provides processing gain against interference.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Pt = 10 mW = 10 dBm
- f = 2.4 GHz
- Gt = Gr = 1 (isotropic, 0 dBi)
- Prx,min = −90 dBm

**(i) Maximum Range (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert frequency and power to dB:
Pt(dBm) = 10 log10(10 mW / 1 mW) = 10 dBm

**Step 2:** Use the practical path-loss formula in dB:

$$PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$$

**Step 3:** For maximum range, the received power must equal the sensitivity:

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL_{max}$$

$$-90 = 10 + 0 + 0 - PL_{max}$$

$$PL_{max} = 100 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for d:

$$100 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$$

$$100 = 32.44 + 67.60 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$$

$$20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 100 - 100.04 = -0.04$$

$$\log_{10}(d_{km}) = -0.002 \implies d_{km} = 10^{-0.002} = 0.9954 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{d_{max} \approx 995 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Received Power at d = 50 m (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate path loss at 50 m = 0.05 km:

$$PL_{dB}(50\,m) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 20\log_{10}(0.05)$$

$$PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 67.60 + (-26.02) = 74.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2:** Compute received power:

$$P_r(dBm) = 10 + 0 + 0 - 74.02 = -64.02 \text{ dBm}$$

$$\boxed{P_r \approx -64 \text{ dBm}}$$

**Verification:** Since −64 dBm > −90 dBm (sensitivity), communication is feasible at 50 m. ✔

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a WSN transceiver** (CC2420 type). Explain the function of the **DSSS spreading** and **O-QPSK modulation** in the IEEE 802.15.4 standard. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A WSN node is powered by a **3 V, 1000 mAh** battery. In each operational cycle of 1 second:
   - It transmits for **5 ms** at **60 mW**
   - It receives for **10 ms** at **55 mW**
   - It is in idle mode for **15 ms** at **1.5 mW**
   - It is in sleep for the remaining **970 ms** at **0.003 mW**

Calculate:
   (i) The total energy consumed per cycle. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) The average current drawn. **(2 Marks)**
   (iii) The estimated battery lifetime in years. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — CC2420 Transceiver Architecture (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the mermaid architecture diagram.)

The CC2420 is a single-chip 2.4 GHz IEEE 802.15.4 compliant transceiver. Its main blocks are:

- **Crystal Oscillator (16 MHz)** — Provides the reference frequency.
- **Frequency Synthesizer** — Generates the 2.4 GHz LO from the 16 MHz reference using a PLL.
- **Power Amplifier (PA)** — Amplifies the modulated signal to the antenna.
- **Low Noise Amplifier (LNA)** — Amplifies weak received signals with minimum added noise.
- **Mixers (Up/Down Conversion)** — Convert between baseband and RF.
- **Channel Filters and ADC/DAC** — Bandlimit and digitize the signal.
- **DSSS Spreader / De-spreader** — Spreads each 4-bit symbol into a 32-chip PN sequence.
- **O-QPSK Modulator / Demodulator** — Maps the spread chips onto I/Q carriers with half-symbol offset.

**DSSS Spreading (2 marks):** Each 4-bit symbol is mapped to one of **16 nearly-orthogonal 32-chip PN sequences** (a chip rate of 2 Mchips/s gives a symbol rate of 62.5 ksym/s → 250 kbps). This provides a **processing gain of 9 dB** (10 log10(32/4)) against narrowband interference and improves multipath robustness.

**O-QPSK Modulation (2 marks):** The chips are split into I and Q rails, with the Q-rail **delayed by half a chip period (Tc/2)** to prevent simultaneous I/Q transitions. This reduces the envelope variation → **more linear power amplifier operation** → higher efficiency. O-QPSK retains the same BER-vs-Eb/N0 performance as BPSK/QPSK in AWGN.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- V = 3 V, C = 1000 mAh
- Cycle T = 1 s
- t_tx = 5 ms, P_tx = 60 mW
- t_rx = 10 ms, P_rx = 55 mW
- t_idle = 15 ms, P_idle = 1.5 mW
- t_sleep = 970 ms, P_sleep = 0.003 mW

**(i) Total Energy per Cycle (3 marks):**

Energy in each state is E = P × t.

$$E_{tx} = 60 \text{ mW} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.30 \text{ mJ}$$

$$E_{rx} = 55 \text{ mW} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.55 \text{ mJ}$$

$$E_{idle} = 1.5 \text{ mW} \times 15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.0225 \text{ mJ}$$

$$E_{sleep} = 0.003 \text{ mW} \times 970 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.00291 \text{ mJ}$$

**Total:**

$$E_{cycle} = 0.30 + 0.55 + 0.0225 + 0.00291 = 0.87541 \text{ mJ}$$

$$\boxed{E_{cycle} \approx 0.8754 \text{ mJ per cycle}}$$

**(ii) Average Current Drawn (2 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total energy per second (= average power):

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T} = \frac{0.87541 \text{ mJ}}{1 \text{ s}} = 0.87541 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to average current using P = V × I:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{P_{avg}}{V} = \frac{0.87541 \text{ mW}}{3 \text{ V}} = 0.2918 \text{ mA}$$

$$\boxed{I_{avg} \approx 0.292 \text{ mA}}$$

**(iii) Battery Lifetime (2 marks):**

**Step 1:** Lifetime in hours:

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{0.2918 \text{ mA}} = 3427.0 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to years:

$$T_{life} = \frac{3427.0}{24 \times 365} = \frac{3427.0}{8760} = 0.391 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{T_{life} \approx 0.391 \text{ years} \approx 143 \text{ days}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert mW × ms to mJ (or equivalently, use W × s → J). Always check that time is in **seconds** and power in **watts** before computing energy in joules.
> 2. **dBm vs dBW Confusion:** 10 mW = **10 dBm**, not 10 dBW. The offset is 30 dB.
> 3. **Isotropic Antenna Assumption:** If Gt = Gr = 1 (i.e., 0 dBi) is not given, students must include the antenna gains in the link budget.
> 4. **Battery Capacity Misuse:** Battery lifetime = Capacity / Current → both must be in the same units (mAh / mA = h, NOT mA / mAh).
> 5. **Duty Cycle Miscomputation:** Some students compute δ as t_on / t_off, which is wrong. The correct formula is δ = t_on / (t_on + t_off).
> 6. **DSSS Processing Gain:** When asked "why DSSS in 802.15.4?", students must mention **both interference rejection AND low-power spectral spreading** — losing 1 mark if they mention only one.
> 7. **O-QPSK vs QPSK:** Offsetting the Q-rail by Tc/2 is what makes it O-QPSK. Forgetting to mention the offset = losing 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Physical Layer** = Layer 1; responsible for modulation, coding, frequency selection, and signal detection.
- ☐ **ISM Bands used in WSN:** 433 MHz, 868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz (all license-free).
- ☐ **Modulation in IEEE 802.15.4:** O-QPSK with DSSS (16-ary orthogonal, 32 chips/symbol, 250 kbps at 2.4 GHz).
- ☐ **Friis Free-Space Equation:** Pr = Pt·Gt·Gr·(λ / 4πd)² — remember λ = c/f.
- ☐ **Practical Path Loss (dB):** PL = 32.44 + 20 log10(f_MHz) + 20 log10(d_km).
- ☐ **Log-Normal Shadowing:** PL(d) = PL(d₀) + 10n log10(d/d₀) + Xσ; n=2 (free space), 3–5 (indoor).
- ☐ **Receiver Sensitivity (dBm):** −174 + 10 log10(B) + NF + SNR_min.
- ☐ **BER of BPSK/O-QPSK in AWGN:** Pb = Q(√(2Eb/N0)); for 10⁻⁵ BER, need Eb/N0 ≈ 9.6 dB.
- ☐ **Duty Cycle:** δ = t_on / (t_on + t_off); lowering δ is the **most effective energy saver**.
- ☐ **Hamming Bound for t-error correction:** d_min ≥ 2t + 1.
- ☐ **Shannon Capacity:** C = B·log2(1 + S/N).
- ☐ **Battery Life:** T = Capacity / I_avg; check units carefully.
- ☐ **CC2420 (most-asked transceiver):** 2.4 GHz, O-QPSK/DSSS, 250 kbps, Rx = 18.8 mA, Sleep = 1 μA.
- ☐ **CC1000:** Sub-GHz FSK radio, longer range, lower data rate.
- ☐ **Active power >> Sleep power** by 3–4 orders of magnitude — design for short active bursts.
- ☐ **Higher carrier frequency → smaller antenna but higher path loss.**
- ☐ **DSSS provides processing gain** = 10 log10(chips / data bits) — for 802.15.4, this is 9 dB.
- ☐ **Path loss exponent n:** n=2 (free space), n=3 (urban), n=4–5 (shadowed indoor / foliage).
- ☐ **Energy per bit = Pt / Rb** — higher bit rate paradoxically reduces energy per bit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Ad Hoc Networks and Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs) — Formal Definition

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mobile Ad Hoc Networks and Wireless Sensor Networks

A **Mobile Ad Hoc Network (MANET)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less wireless network in which mobile nodes (laptops, smartphones, vehicles, drones) communicate with each other over multi-hop wireless links. Each node simultaneously acts as a **host** (source/sink of traffic) and a **router** (forwarding packets on behalf of others). Because there is no fixed base station, access point, or backbone, the network topology changes dynamically as nodes move.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** A MANET is "a collection of autonomous mobile nodes that form a temporary, dynamic network without centralized administration, employing multi-hop routing over shared wireless media."

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Walkie-Talkie Rescue Squad"

Imagine a group of rescue workers spread across a disaster-hit valley. There is no cellular tower, no Wi-Fi router. Each rescuer holds a walkie-talkie. To send a message from Worker A to Worker D (out of range), the message hops through Workers B and C, who are closer. Every rescuer is both a sender **and** a relay. As they walk, the route changes continuously. **This is a MANET.**

---

## 1.2 Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) — Formal Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a specialized wireless network comprising a large number of spatially distributed, resource-constrained **sensor nodes** (also called *motes*) that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, pressure, vibration, pollutants) and forward sensed data, typically via multi-hop communication, to a central **sink node** (or **Base Station**, BS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Difference from MANET:** WSN nodes are *primarily* sensor-equipped and *energy-constrained*, with the traffic pattern dominated by **many-to-one** (sensors → sink) flows. MANET nodes are *primarily* communication endpoints with peer-to-peer traffic.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Farm"

Picture a vast farm with thousands of tiny solar-powered soil sensors sprinkled across the field. Each sensor measures moisture and temperature. A farmer's gateway (sink) is at the farm's edge. Sensors do not directly call the gateway (too far). Instead, sensor-to-sensor, the moisture reading "hops" toward the gateway. The farmer receives a real-time moisture map of the entire farm. **This is a WSN.**

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | MANET | WSN |
|---|---|---|
| **Node Mobility** | High & continuous | Mostly **stationary** (or limited mobility) |
| **Node Density** | Low–Medium (**~10–100 nodes**) | Very High (**~1000s of nodes**) |
| **Power Source** | Rechargeable battery / mains | **Non-rechargeable / harvested** (e.g., 2×AA) |
| **Traffic Pattern** | Peer-to-peer (P2P) | **Many-to-one** (convergecast) |
| **Node Identity** | Critical (addressing) | **Often not** (data-centric) |
| **Primary Constraint** | Routing & mobility | **Energy efficiency** |
| **Scale** | Tens | Thousands to millions |
| **Data Rate** | Higher (kbps–Mbps) | Lower (bps–kbps) |
| **Standard Std.** | IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi), IEEE 802.15.1 | **IEEE 802.15.4** (ZigBee, 6LoWPAN) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Frequency Bands (bold per KTU convention):** MANETs typically use **2.4 GHz** and **5 GHz** ISM bands (IEEE 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax). WSNs use **2.4 GHz** (worldwide), **868 MHz** (Europe), and **915 MHz** (USA) ISM bands per **IEEE 802.15.4**.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Applications

**MANET Applications:**
- Military battlefield communication (Tactical MANETs)
- Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (**VANETs** — V2V, V2I)
- Disaster recovery & emergency response
- Conference / classroom ad hoc collaboration
- Search-and-rescue robotics swarms

**WSN Applications:**
- **Environmental monitoring** (forest fire detection, precision agriculture)
- **Healthcare** (body-area networks, patient monitoring)
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — structural health, pipeline monitoring
- **Smart cities** — air quality, traffic, lighting
- **Military surveillance** (sensor field for intrusion detection)
- **Underwater / habitat monitoring**

---

## 1.5 Challenges Common to Both (but Differing in Magnitude)

1. **Limited Bandwidth** — shared wireless medium, contention
2. **Error-Prone Links** — fading, interference, multipath
3. **Multi-Hop Routing** — route maintenance overhead
4. **Security** — eavesdropping, jamming, Sybil/wormhole attacks
5. **Scalability** — particularly severe in WSN (1000s of nodes)
6. **Quality of Service (QoS)** — delay, jitter, reliability constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MANET — Operational & Protocol Architecture

### 2.1.1 Why a Traditional Protocol Stack Fails for MANETs

The classical TCP/IP protocol stack assumes:
- Stable end-to-end paths
- Low bit-error rates on wired links
- Routers with plentiful power

In a MANET, **all three assumptions break**:
- Paths change every few seconds due to mobility
- Wireless BER is high (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵)
- Every node is a battery-powered router

### 2.1.2 The Five-Layer MANET Protocol Stack (KTU Standard)

| Layer | MANET-Specific Function | Key Protocols/Concepts |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | User traffic, service discovery | Custom apps, HTTP/MQTT over wireless |
| **Transport Layer** | Reliable delivery over lossy wireless | **TCP with ECN/Freeze**, UDP variants |
| **Network Layer** | Multi-hop routing, addressing | **AODV, DSR, OLSR, TORA, ZRP** |
| **Data Link Layer (MAC)** | Channel access, hidden/exposed terminal | **IEEE 802.11 DCF (CSMA/CA), MACA, MACAW** |
| **Physical Layer** | Modulation, transmission, sensing | OFDM, DSSS, dynamic spectrum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** MANET routing protocols are classified into three families:
> 1. **Proactive (Table-Driven)** — e.g., **OLSR, DSDV** — maintain fresh routes always
> 2. **Reactive (On-Demand)** — e.g., **AODV, DSR** — discover routes only when needed
> 3. **Hybrid** — e.g., **ZRP, TORA** — combine both

### 2.1.3 Hidden & Exposed Terminal Problems

- **Hidden Terminal:** Node A and Node C both want to send to Node B (in range of B but **out of range of each other**). They cannot sense each other's transmission → **collision at B**. Solved by **RTS/CTS handshake** (MACA, IEEE 802.11).
- **Exposed Terminal:** Node B is sending to A; Node C wants to send to D, but C senses B's carrier and unnecessarily defers. Wastes channel. RTS/CTS partially solves it.

---

## 2.2 WSN — Operational & Protocol Architecture

### 2.2.1 The WSN Protocol Stack (KTU Standard — 5 Layers + Cross-Layer)

| Layer | WSN Function | WSN-Specific Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | Sensing application logic | Custom (e.g., temperature monitoring) |
| **Transport Layer** | Reliability for event delivery | PSFQ, RMST, CODA |
| **Network Layer** | Multi-hop routing, data aggregation | **LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion** |
| **Data Link Layer** | MAC, error control, framing | **S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4** |
| **Physical Layer** | Radio, modulation, energy | O-QPSK (802.15.4), duty-cycled radios |
| *(Cross-cutting)* | Power, mobility, task management planes | All layers |

### 2.2.2 Why WSN Routing ≠ MANET Routing

In MANETs, the **identity of the destination** matters most. In WSNs, the **attribute of the data** matters most (e.g., "any node sensing temperature > 50°C in region X"). Hence:

- **MANET routing → address-centric**
- **WSN routing → data-centric** (SPIN, Directed Diffusion, Rumor Routing)

### 2.2.3 Energy Consumption — First-Order Radio Model (Heinzelman Model)

This is the most cited energy model in WSN literature and is **board-favorite** in KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Formula (Heinzelman, 2000):** For a transmitter to send a *k*-bit packet over distance *d*:
> - **Transmit energy:** $E_{Tx}(k,d) = E_{Tx\text{-}elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$
> - **Receive energy:** $E_{Rx}(k) = E_{Rx\text{-}elec} \cdot k$

**Path-loss exponent *n* depends on the propagation environment:**

| Environment | *n* (path-loss exponent) |
|---|---|
| Free space | 2 |
| Urban (LOS) | 2.5 – 3.0 |
| Urban (NLOS, shadowed) | 3.0 – 5.0 |
| Indoor LOS | 1.6 – 1.8 |
| Obstructed in-building | 4.0 – 6.0 |
| Factory, heavy obstructions | 2.0 – 3.0 |

Typical KTU-assumed values: $E_{Tx\text{-}elec} = E_{Rx\text{-}elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$, $E_{amp} = 100 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$ (free-space) or $0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$ (multipath).

### 2.2.4 Network Lifetime (KTU Board-Favorite)

> **Definition:** Network lifetime is the time until **the first sensor node** (or a specified fraction *α*) depletes its energy. Mathematically:
> $$L_{net} = \frac{E_{initial} - E_{threshold}}{P_{avg}}$$
> where $E_{initial}$ is the initial battery energy, $E_{threshold}$ is the residual energy below which the node is considered dead, and $P_{avg}$ is the average power consumption per node.

**LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** increases lifetime via **rotated cluster-head (CH) election** every round. Probability of becoming CH per round:
$$P_{CH} = \frac{k}{N}$$
where *k* is the desired number of CHs and *N* is the total number of nodes.

---

## 2.3 Side-by-Side Architectural Comparison

| Feature | MANET | WSN |
|---|---|---|
| **Node purpose** | Communication end-point | Sensing + relay |
| **Mobility model** | Random Waypoint, Gauss-Markov | Mostly static; rarely RWP |
| **Addressing** | IP-based / unique IDs | Data-centric (attribute-based) |
| **Data flow** | P2P, multicast | Many-to-one, one-to-many (query) |
| **Aggregation** | Rare (application layer) | **In-network aggregation** (network layer) |
| **Energy design** | Battery aware | **Energy first-class citizen** |
| **Routing metric** | Shortest hop, min delay | **Minimum energy, max lifetime** |
| **MAC layer** | Contention-based (CSMA/CA) | **Duty-cycled** TDMA/CSMA hybrids (S-MAC) |
| **Failure model** | Node may leave/join | Nodes can **die** permanently (battery) |
| **Clock sync** | Not critical | Often **required** (TDMA, localization) |
| **Localization** | Optional | Often **essential** (per-sensor location) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize this table. At least one formula from this table appears in nearly every KTU WSN question paper.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tx energy (Heinzelman) | $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | Joules; *n* = 2 (free) or 4 (multipath) |
| 2 | Rx energy (Heinzelman) | $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Joules |
| 3 | Total energy per round (LEACH) | $E_{round} = N \cdot \left(2 E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{DA} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{2} \right) / k$ | Joules per round |
| 4 | Network lifetime | $L_{net} = (E_{init} - E_{th}) / P_{avg}$ | seconds |
| 5 | LEACH CH probability | $P_{CH} = k / N$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Friis free-space path loss | $P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} G_{r} \left( \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d} \right)^{2}$ | d in meters, $\lambda$ in meters |
| 7 | Two-ray ground model | $P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot G_{t} G_{r} \cdot h_{t}^{2} h_{r}^{2} / d^{4}$ | valid for $d \gg h_{t}, h_{r}$ |
| 8 | Optimal hop distance (min energy) | $d_{opt} = \sqrt[n]{E_{elec} / ((n-1) E_{amp})}$ | meters |
| 9 | Optimal # of clusters (LEACH) | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N / (2 \pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Throughput of IEEE 802.15.4 | $T_{max} \approx 250 \text{ kbps}$ (O-QPSK, 2.4 GHz) | bits/sec |
| 11 | Delay in multi-hop | $D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} \left( D_{queue,i} + D_{tx,i} + D_{prop,i} \right)$ | seconds |
| 12 | Energy per useful bit (MTE) | $E_{bit} = E_{total} / N_{useful\_bits}$ | Joules/bit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ (forgetting the constant *k* multiplier inside the amplifier term). The *k*-bit packet is transmitted AND amplified — *k* multiplies **both** terms. **Loses 1–2 marks** in valuation.

---

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Heinzelman model** underpins **LEACH**, the most-cited WSN protocol — directly used in IoT chip design (e.g., TI CC2650 firmware).
- **Network lifetime** is the **primary SLA** in industrial WSN deployments (e.g., 5-year battery life for smart meters).
- **Friis / Two-ray models** govern **transceiver power budget** in hardware (e.g., ZigBee PCB antenna design).
- **Optimal cluster count $k_{opt}$** guides **firmware tuning** in products like Bosch Sensortec.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Transmission Energy in Multi-Hop Routing

### Problem Setup
A source node must send a *k*-bit packet to a sink located at distance *D* using *h* equal hops, each of length $d = D/h$. Use the Heinzelman model with path-loss exponent $n$.

### Step 1: Energy per hop (transmit + receive at relay)

The energy consumed **per hop** (transmitter of that hop + receiver of that hop) is:

$$
E_{hop} = E_{Tx}(k, d) + E_{Rx}(k)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Heinzelman model

$$
E_{hop} = \left( E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} \right) + E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
E_{hop} = 2 E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

The factor of **2** arises because the relay both receives the *k* bits and re-transmits them.

### Step 3: Total energy over all *h* hops

$$
E_{total} = h \cdot E_{hop} = h \cdot \left( 2 E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} \right)
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $d = D/h$ to express in terms of *h*

$$
E_{total}(h) = h \cdot 2 E_{elec} \cdot k + h \cdot E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \left( \frac{D}{h} \right)^{n}
$$

Expanding the right-hand term:

$$
E_{total}(h) = 2 E_{elec} \cdot k \cdot h + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot D^{n} \cdot h^{1-n}
$$

### Step 5: Differentiate w.r.t. *h* and set to zero

$$
\frac{d E_{total}}{d h} = 2 E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot D^{n} \cdot (1-n) \cdot h^{-n} = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
2 E_{elec} = E_{amp} \cdot D^{n} \cdot (n-1) \cdot h^{-n}
$$

### Step 6: Solve for the optimal hop count *h\**

$$
h^{n} = \frac{E_{amp} \cdot D^{n} \cdot (n-1)}{2 E_{elec}}
$$

$$
h^{*} = \left[ \frac{E_{amp} \cdot (n-1) \cdot D^{n}}{2 E_{elec}} \right]^{1/n}
$$

### Step 7: Compute the optimal per-hop distance

$$
d^{*} = \frac{D}{h^{*}} = \left[ \frac{2 E_{elec}}{E_{amp} (n-1)} \right]^{1/n}
$$

### Step 8: Final compact form (KTU Board Form)

$$
\boxed{\, d_{opt} = \left[ \frac{2 E_{elec}}{(n-1) \cdot E_{amp}} \right]^{1/n} \,}
$$

This is the **distance** that minimizes total transmission energy per bit, irrespective of the total source-to-sink distance *D*. Remarkably, *D* drops out of the per-hop optimum — the optimal hop length depends only on hardware and propagation parameters.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Past-Year Style)

### Problem
Consider a WSN with 100 nodes uniformly distributed in a 100 m × 100 m field. Sink is at the center. Use:
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $E_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² (free space, *n* = 2)
- $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit (data aggregation)
- Packet size $k = 4000$ bits
- Probability of being cluster head $P_{CH} = 0.05$

**Compute:**
1. Expected number of cluster heads per round.
2. Average distance from a CH to the sink.
3. Energy dissipated by a non-CH node per frame.
4. Energy dissipated by a CH per frame.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Expected CH count per round:**

$$
N_{CH} = N \cdot P_{CH} = 100 \cdot 0.05 = 5 \text{ CHs/round}
$$

**Step 2 — Average CH-to-BS distance (uniform distribution in 100 × 100 m):**

For a uniform distribution in a square of side *M* with sink at the center, the expected distance is:

$$
E[d_{toBS}] = \int_{0}^{M/2} \int_{0}^{M/2} \frac{2}{M^{2}} \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \, dx\, dy \approx 0.3829 \cdot M
$$

Thus:

$$
E[d_{toBS}] \approx 0.3829 \cdot 100 = 38.29 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Non-CH node energy per frame:**

A non-CH sends its data to its nearest CH (assume average cluster radius $r \approx M / \sqrt{\pi k}$):

$$
r \approx \frac{100}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot 5}} \approx \frac{100}{3.963} \approx 25.23 \text{ m}
$$

Using the LEACH non-CH energy formula (transmit only, free space):

$$
E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot r^{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
E_{nonCH} = 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (25.23)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{nonCH} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 4.0 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 636.55
$$

$$
E_{nonCH} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.546 \times 10^{-4} = 4.546 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J/frame}
$$

**Step 4 — CH energy per frame:**

A CH must (i) receive from all cluster members, (ii) aggregate, (iii) transmit to BS:

$$
E_{CH} = k \cdot E_{elec} \left( \frac{N}{k_{CH}} - 1 \right) + k \cdot E_{DA} \cdot \frac{N}{k_{CH}} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{2}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
E_{CH} = 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (20 - 1) + 4000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 20 + 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (38.29)^{2}
$$

Evaluating term by term:

- Receive: $4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 19 = 3.8 \times 10^{-3}$ J
- Aggregate: $4000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 20 = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- CH own transmit: $4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- CH→BS amp: $4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1466.1 = 5.864 \times 10^{-4}$ J

Summing:

$$
E_{CH} = 3.8 \times 10^{-3} + 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.864 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
E_{CH} \approx 4.987 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J/frame}
$$

This is roughly **10× higher** than a non-CH node — the very reason LEACH **rotates** the CH role every round.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MANET vs WSN Routing

| Routing Aspect | MANET Solution | WSN Solution | Reason for Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Route metric | Shortest hop / min delay | **Min energy / max lifetime** | WSN nodes are energy-limited |
| Route stability | Tolerates fast change (reactive protocols) | **Aims for stable paths** (clustered) | WSN has many static nodes; re-routing is expensive |
| Data importance | **Every packet matters** | **Aggregated event matters** | WSN tolerates loss, exploits redundancy |
| Hierarchy | Flat (or hybrid) | **Hierarchical (clusters)** | Scalability to 1000s of nodes |
| Mobility | First-class design input | Often ignored or secondary | WSN nodes mostly static |
| Security | Encryption, key management | **Lightweight crypto** (RC5, Skipjack) | Limited CPU/power |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Optimal Number of Clusters in LEACH

### Step 1: Total energy per round (per CH, including its members)

The Heinzelman 2000 paper derives:

$$
E_{round} = k \left( 2 N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + E_{amp} \left( k \cdot d_{toBS}^{2} + N d_{toCH}^{2} \right) \right)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute expected distances

For uniform distribution:

- $E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \dfrac{M^{2}}{2 \pi k}$ (cluster area argument)
- $E[d_{toBS}^{2}]$ is geometry-dependent

### Step 3: Differentiate w.r.t. *k* (number of CHs)

After derivation (see Heinzelman 2000):

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{ \frac{N}{2 \pi} } \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}} } \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

- **More nodes (↑N)** → more CHs optimum
- **Larger field (↑M)** → more CHs
- **Greater BS distance (↑d_toBS)** → fewer CHs (each CH must cover more area)
- **Higher multipath loss (↓$\varepsilon_{mp}$)** → fewer CHs (cluster communication expensive)

---

## 3.5 Power Delay Profile (PDP) — Why WSNs Use Short Ranges

For a Rayleigh-fading channel, the **coherence bandwidth** is inversely proportional to **RMS delay spread** $\sigma_{\tau}$:

$$
B_{c} \approx \frac{1}{5 \sigma_{\tau}}
$$

Indoor channels have $\sigma_{\tau} \approx 50$–250 ns → $B_{c} \approx 0.8$–4 MHz. WSNs use narrowband signaling (**O-QPSK at 250 kbps**) to remain **flat-fading**, simplifying the receiver. This is why **IEEE 802.15.4 chipsets** are cheap and low-power compared to Wi-Fi.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MANET vs WSN — Network Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MANET["MANET - Mobile Ad Hoc Network"]
        direction LR
        MN1["Node A<br/>Mobile Laptop"]
        MN2["Node B<br/>Smartphone"]
        MN3["Node C<br/>Drone"]
        MN4["Node D<br/>Vehicle"]
        MN5["Node E<br/>Tablet"]
        MN1 <-->|"Wireless hop"| MN2
        MN2 <-->|"Wireless hop"| MN3
        MN3 <-->|"Wireless hop"| MN4
        MN4 <-->|"Wireless hop"| MN5
        MN1 <-->|"Wireless hop"| MN3
        MN2 <-->|"Wireless hop"| MN4
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WSN["WSN - Wireless Sensor Network"]
        direction TB
        S1["Sensor Node 1<br/>Temperature"]
        S2["Sensor Node 2<br/>Humidity"]
        S3["Sensor Node 3<br/>Pressure"]
        S4["Sensor Node 4<br/>Vibration"]
        S5["Sensor Node 5<br/>Gas"]
        S6["Sensor Node 6<br/>Light"]
        CH1["Cluster Head 1<br/>Aggregator"]
        CH2["Cluster Head 2<br/>Aggregator"]
        BS["Sink / Base Station<br/>Gateway"]
        S1 --> CH1
        S2 --> CH1
        S3 --> CH1
        S4 --> CH2
        S5 --> CH2
        S6 --> CH2
        CH1 --> BS
        CH2 --> BS
    end
```

---

## 4.2 WSN Sensor Node — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sensing Unit<br/>Sensor + ADC"]
    B["Processing Unit<br/>Microcontroller + Memory"]
    C["Transceiver Unit<br/>Radio TX/RX"]
    D["Power Unit<br/>Battery + Harvester"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    D -.->|supplies| A
    D -.->|supplies| B
    D -.->|supplies| C
    B -.->|control| A
    B -.->|control| C
```

> **Block functions (KTU standard):**
> - **Sensing Unit:** physical transducer + ADC, samples environment
> - **Processing Unit:** stores/buffers data, runs routing & MAC protocols
> - **Transceiver Unit:** radio (typically O-QPSK at 2.4 GHz), the **most power-hungry** block
> - **Power Unit:** battery (2×AA ≈ 2.2 Ah @ 3 V) or energy harvester (solar/vibration/RF)

---

## 4.3 MANET Protocol Stack (5-Layer + Cross-Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Application Layer<br/>HTTP, FTP, Custom Apps"]
    L2["Transport Layer<br/>TCP with ECN, UDP"]
    L3["Network Layer<br/>AODV, DSR, OLSR, ZRP"]
    L4["Data Link Layer (MAC)<br/>IEEE 802.11 DCF, MACA"]
    L5["Physical Layer<br/>OFDM, DSSS, FHSS"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L3 -.->|Cross-layer info| L4
    L3 -.->|Cross-layer info| L5
    L4 -.->|Cross-layer info| L5
```

---

## 4.4 WSN Protocol Stack (5 Layers + 3 Management Planes)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WSNSTACK["WSN Protocol Stack"]
        direction TB
        APP["Application Layer<br/>User Sensing App"]
        TR["Transport Layer<br/>PSFQ, RMST, CODA"]
        NET["Network Layer<br/>LEACH, SPIN, Directed Diffusion"]
        MAC["Data Link Layer<br/>S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4"]
        PHY["Physical Layer<br/>O-QPSK, Duty-Cycled Radio"]
        PWR["Power Management Plane"]
        MOB["Mobility Management Plane"]
        TSK["Task Management Plane"]
        APP --> TR --> NET --> MAC --> PHY
        PWR -.->|touches all| APP
        PWR -.->|touches all| NET
        PWR -.->|touches all| MAC
        PWR -.->|touches all| PHY
        MOB -.->|touches all| NET
        TSK -.->|touches all| APP
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three management planes (**Power, Mobility, Task**) are the *defining difference* between WSN protocol stack and MANET protocol stack per the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 4.5 Hidden & Exposed Terminal Scenarios

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HT["Hidden Terminal Problem"]
        direction LR
        A1["A - Transmitter"] -->|data| B1["B - Receiver"]
        C1["C - Hidden Terminal<br/>Cannot sense A"]
        A1 -.->|"out of range"| C1
        C1 -->|"collides at B!"| B1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ET["Exposed Terminal Problem"]
        direction LR
        B2["B - Transmitter"] -->|data to A| A2["A - Receiver"]
        C2["C - Exposed Terminal<br/>Defers unnecessarily"]
        C2 -.->|"wants to send to D"| D2["D - Receiver"]
        B2 -.->|"carrier sensed by C"| C2
    end
```

**Solution:** RTS (Request-To-Send) / CTS (Clear-To-Send) handshake — used in IEEE 802.11 DCF.

---

## 4.6 LEACH Operational Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Round Start<br/>t = 0"] --> S2["Cluster Setup Phase<br/>CH Election by random threshold"]
    S2 --> S3["CHs broadcast ADV msg<br/>Non-CHs pick nearest CH"]
    S3 --> S4["Non-CHs send join-REQ<br/>CHs create TDMA schedule"]
    S4 --> S5["Steady-State Phase<br/>Sensing + Data Transmission"]
    S5 --> S6{"Round r mod 1/p completed?"}
    S6 -->|"No"| S5
    S6 -->|"Yes"| S7["Re-elect CHs<br/>New Round Begins"]
    S7 --> S1
```

---

## 4.7 Energy Consumption Breakdown in a Sensor Node (KTU Exam Figure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOT["Total Node Energy Budget E_total"]
    TOT --> ETX["E_Tx<br/>~ 60-70 percent"]
    TOT --> ERX["E_Rx<br/>~ 15-20 percent"]
    TOT --> EIDLE["E_Idle Listening<br/>~ 10-15 percent"]
    TOT --> ESLEEP["E_Sleep<br/>~ 1-2 percent"]
    TOT --> EPROC["E_Processing<br/>~ 3-5 percent"]
    ETX --> ETX1["Amplifier dominates in long range"]
    ERX --> ERX1["Idle listening while waiting"]
    EIDLE --> EIDLE1["Duty cycling key to reduction"]
    ESLEEP --> ESLEEP1["Should be maximum"]
    EPROC --> EPROC1["CPU in active mode"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Transmit energy dominates, but **idle listening** is the silent killer. The biggest gains in WSN come from aggressive **duty cycling** (e.g., S-MAC achieves 99% sleep duty).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between MANET and WSN in terms of mobility, energy, traffic pattern, and addressing scheme.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | MANET | WSN |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility** | High and continuous (Random Waypoint) | Mostly static, limited mobility |
| **Energy** | Rechargeable, not the primary constraint | **Non-rechargeable; primary design constraint** |
| **Traffic** | Peer-to-peer (P2P), multicast | **Many-to-one** (convergecast to sink) |
| **Addressing** | Unique IP/ID per node, address-centric | **Data-centric** (queries by attribute) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Explain the "hidden terminal" problem in MANETs. How is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **hidden terminal problem** occurs when two nodes *A* and *C* are out of radio range of each other but both wish to transmit to a common node *B*. Since *A* and *C* cannot sense each other's carrier, they transmit simultaneously, causing a **collision at B** (the receiver).

**Example:** In an IEEE 802.11 wireless LAN, three stations are arranged linearly as A–B–C with *A* and *C* out of range.

**Mitigation:** **RTS/CTS handshake** (used in **MACA**, **IEEE 802.11 DCF**). The sender transmits a small RTS frame; if the medium is free, the receiver replies with CTS. All nodes within range of either frame defer for the announced duration, avoiding collision.

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

#### **Part (a)** — Compare the architecture of MANET and WSN with suitable diagrams. List four key differences. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
- A **MANET** is a self-organizing, infrastructure-less network of mobile nodes that act as both endpoints and routers, communicating over multi-hop wireless links.
- A **WSN** is a network of spatially distributed sensor nodes that cooperatively sense physical phenomena and forward data (typically via multi-hop) to a sink.

**Architectural Comparison:**

**MANET Architecture:** Flat, with peer-to-peer links. Every node has identical hardware/software capability (typically laptop/phone class). Routes change every few seconds due to mobility. Routing is **address-centric**.

**WSN Architecture:** Often **hierarchical** — sensors → cluster heads → sink. Node classes are heterogeneous (sensor vs aggregator). Topology is mostly static. Routing is **data-centric**.

**Key Differences (4 required, list any four — 1 mark each for elaboration):**

1. **Energy design:** WSN is **energy-constrained** (battery-powered, often non-rechargeable). Energy is the *primary* design metric. MANET tolerates higher power consumption.
2. **Node purpose:** MANET nodes are *endpoints*; WSN nodes primarily *sense* and forward.
3. **Traffic pattern:** MANET = P2P/multicast. WSN = many-to-one (convergecast).
4. **Addressing:** MANET = address-centric routing. WSN = data-centric (attribute-based queries).
5. **Scale:** MANET = tens of nodes. WSN = thousands.
6. **Data aggregation:** Rare in MANET; **in-network aggregation** is a key WSN feature.
7. **Mobility model:** MANETs assume continuous mobility; WSNs often static.
8. **Hardware cost per node:** MANET — order of $100s. WSN — order of $1–10.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 above).** Refer to those.

---

#### **Part (b)** — Explain the Heinzelman energy model for WSN. Compute the total energy dissipated in transmitting a 4000-bit packet over 50 m using free-space path loss with $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $E_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Heinzelman Energy Model (1st-order radio model):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula to reproduce:**
> $E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$

For a *k*-bit packet transmitted over distance *d* with path-loss exponent *n*:
- $E_{elec}$: per-bit energy consumed by transmitter electronics (DAC, filters, mixer)
- $E_{amp}$: per-bit energy consumed by the transmit amplifier
- $n = 2$ for free-space, $n = 4$ for multipath

For **receiving** *k* bits (radio electronics only):
$$E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

**Numerical computation:**

Given: $k = 4000$ bits, $d = 50$ m, $n = 2$, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit, $E_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² $= 10 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m².

$$
E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}
$$

$$
E_{Tx} = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 4000 + (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4000 \cdot (50)^{2}
$$

First term:

$$
E_{elec} \cdot k = 50 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 4000 = 200{,}000 \times 10^{-9} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

Second term:

$$
E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 4000 \cdot 2500 = 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

Total:

$$
\boxed{E_{Tx} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.3 \text{ mJ}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

#### **Part (a)** — Explain the five-layer WSN protocol stack with the three management planes. Why is each management plane necessary? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **WSN protocol stack** is a five-layer architecture augmented by three cross-cutting management planes. The layers (top to bottom) are:

1. **Application Layer** (1 mark) — Hosts the user sensing application. Performs sensor data formatting, time-stamping, and may include software for collaboration among nodes.
2. **Transport Layer** (1 mark) — Ensures reliable event delivery. Examples: **PSFQ (Pump-Slowly, Fetch-Quickly)**, **RMST**, **CODA**. Maintains fairness among flows.
3. **Network Layer** (1 mark) — Performs multi-hop routing of sensor data. Protocols: **LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion**. Often includes **in-network data aggregation**.
4. **Data Link (MAC) Layer** (1 mark) — Manages channel access, framing, error control. Critically implements **duty cycling** to save energy. Protocols: **S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4**.
5. **Physical Layer** (1 mark) — Modulation, radio, frequency selection. Typical: O-QPSK at 2.4 GHz, **-10 dBm** transmit power.

**Three Management Planes (2 marks):**

1. **Power Management Plane** — Governs how a node uses its power. Decides when to **sleep, wake, transmit**. Without it, idle listening would drain the battery in days.
2. **Mobility Management Plane** — Detects and registers node movement. Updates route tables and maintains connectivity when a node relocates (e.g., a mobile sensor on a moving vehicle).
3. **Task Management Plane** — Balances and schedules sensing tasks across the network. Avoids redundant sensing — assigns which nodes sense what, when. Critical for **energy efficiency and coverage**.

> **Why each is necessary:** The three management planes are essential because WSN nodes have **limited power, possible mobility, and must coordinate tasks to avoid redundancy**. Each plane interacts with all five layers — this is the **cross-cutting** nature that distinguishes the WSN stack from a generic protocol stack.

---

#### **Part (b)** — A WSN is deployed to monitor a square field of side 100 m with the sink at the center. There are 100 nodes, each with initial energy 2 J. LEACH is used with $P_{CH} = 0.05$, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit, packet size 4000 bits. Estimate:
- (i) Number of CHs per round
- (ii) Average CH-to-BS distance
- (iii) Approximate total energy per round

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of CHs per round:**

$$
k_{CH} = N \cdot P_{CH} = 100 \cdot 0.05 = 5
$$

**(ii) Average CH-to-BS distance:**

For uniform distribution in a square of side *M* with sink at center:

$$
E[d_{toBS}] \approx 0.3829 \cdot M = 0.3829 \cdot 100 = 38.29 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Total energy per round:**

Each round, every node transmits once to its CH (or CH transmits once to BS). Energy per non-CH node (sending to nearest CH, average distance $r \approx M / \sqrt{\pi k_{CH}} = 25.23$ m):

$$
E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot r^{2}
$$

$$
E_{nonCH} = 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (25.23)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{nonCH} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 4.0 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 636.55 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.546 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
E_{nonCH} = 4.546 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

Total non-CH energy per round:

$$
E_{nonCH,total} = (N - k_{CH}) \cdot E_{nonCH} = 95 \cdot 4.546 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.0432 \text{ J}
$$

Energy per CH (receive from members, aggregate, transmit to BS):

$$
E_{CH} = k \cdot E_{elec} \cdot \left(\frac{N}{k_{CH}} - 1\right) + k \cdot E_{DA} \cdot \frac{N}{k_{CH}} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{2}
$$

$$
E_{CH} = 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 19 + 4000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 20 + 4000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} + 4000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1466.1
$$

$$
E_{CH} = 3.8 \times 10^{-3} + 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.864 \times 10^{-4} \approx 4.987 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

Total CH energy per round:

$$
E_{CH,total} = k_{CH} \cdot E_{CH} = 5 \cdot 4.987 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.0249 \text{ J}
$$

**Total energy per round:**

$$
\boxed{E_{round} \approx 0.0432 + 0.0249 \approx 0.0681 \text{ J}}
$$

Approximate number of rounds until first node dies (assume 2 J / 0.0681 J ≈ 29.4 J per round per network, so each node averaging 2 J / 100 = 0.02 J budget — i.e., first-node death at ≈ 0.02/avg ≈ hundreds of rounds; detailed per-node simulation required for exact value).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks (Dec 2023 / May 2024 paper patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_{Tx}$ and $E_{Rx}$:** $E_{Tx}$ contains **two** terms (electronics + amplifier); $E_{Rx}$ contains **only** electronics. Many students incorrectly add an amplifier term to receive energy. **−1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the *k*-bit multiplier:** The amplifier term is $E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^n$, not $E_{amp} \cdot d^n$ alone. **−1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Wrong path-loss exponent:** Free-space is $n = 2$, multipath is $n = 4$. The question always specifies; do not assume. **−1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** $E_{elec}$ is typically in nJ/bit, $E_{amp}$ in pJ/bit/m². Converting carelessly leads to $10^{3}$–$10^{6}$ errors. **−2 marks.**
>
> 5. **In LEACH, "CH-to-BS" is typically multipath ($n=4$):** Many students apply free-space ($n=2$) for that long hop, under-estimating CH energy. **−1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Skipping diagrams in (a)-parts:** KTU explicitly awards 1–2 marks for proper labelled diagrams in any 7-mark question. A text-only answer forfeits that. **−1 to −2 marks.**
>
> 7. **Confusing "MANET routing protocols" with "WSN routing protocols":** AODV/DSR are for MANETs; LEACH/SPIN are for WSNs. Cross-listing confuses the examiner. **−1 mark.**

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision (read this aloud 5 times before the exam):**

- ✅ **MANET =** Mobile + Ad Hoc + Network — no infra, multi-hop, mobile nodes
- ✅ **WSN =** many small, energy-limited sensor nodes + sink; data-centric
- ✅ **Three families of MANET routing:** Proactive (OLSR, DSDV), Reactive (AODV, DSR), Hybrid (ZRP, TORA)
- ✅ **Three WSN management planes:** Power, Mobility, Task
- ✅ **Heinzelman Tx formula:** $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} k + E_{amp} k d^n$
- ✅ **Heinzelman Rx formula:** $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} k$ *(no amplifier term!)*
- ✅ **Path-loss exponents:** $n=2$ (free space), $n=4$ (multipath)
- ✅ **LEACH CH probability:** $P_{CH} = k/N$
- ✅ **LEACH rotates CH every round** to balance energy — *not* assigned permanently
- ✅ **Optimal hop distance:** $d_{opt} = \left[\dfrac{2 E_{elec}}{(n-1) E_{amp}}\right]^{1/n}$
- ✅ **Hidden terminal solved by RTS/CTS** (MACA, IEEE 802.11)
- ✅ **Exposed terminal is harder to solve** — partial mitigation only
- ✅ **WSN MAC = duty-cycled** (S-MAC, B-MAC); MANET MAC = CSMA/CA contention
- ✅ **In-network data aggregation is a WSN-only feature** that reduces transmissions
- ✅ **Frequency bands:** 2.4 GHz ISM (worldwide), 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US)
- ✅ **IEEE 802.15.4** is the WSN standard; **IEEE 802.11** is the MANET/Wi-Fi standard
- ✅ **First-order radio model assumes** constant $E_{elec}$ for both Tx and Rx electronics
- ✅ **Network lifetime** is *the* WSN design metric — not throughput, not delay
- ✅ **Standard values to memorize:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{amp}^{fs} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², $E_{amp}^{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴

> 🎓 **Final Exam Mantra:** *Energy model — write the formula before substituting. Always carry units. Always draw a diagram. Always state the path-loss exponent.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Buses and Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Field Buses and Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fieldbus? (Formal KTU Definition)

A **Fieldbus** is a digital, serial, two-way communication system that interconnects field-level devices (sensors, actuators, controllers, and valves) in an industrial automation environment, replacing the traditional point-to-point analog wiring (4–20 mA current loops) with a single shared digital bus. Formally, per the **IEC 61158 / IEC 61784** standards adopted by KTU syllabus, a Fieldbus is defined as:

> "A digital, bidirectional, multidrop, serial-bus communication network used to connect field-level instrumentation and control devices in process and manufacturing automation, providing deterministic, real-time data exchange between distributed control system (DCS) elements."

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term "Fieldbus" is positioned as the **layer-1/layer-2 industrial backbone** that bridges the **physical process (sensors/actuators)** with the **supervisory control layer (PLC / SCADA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, the term "Fieldbus" is NOT limited to wired protocols. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly extends it to **wireless fieldbus extensions** such as **WirelessHART (IEC 62591)** and **ISA100.11a (IEC 62734)**, which is why this topic directly precedes WSN architectures in Module 3.

### Key Properties of a Fieldbus (Examiner-Weighted Bullet Points)

- **Digital Communication** — Replaces analog 4–20 mA signalling with discrete binary encoding.
- **Bidirectional** — Data flows both from sensor→controller and controller→actuator.
- **Multidrop / Multi-device** — Up to **32** (RS-485) or **256** (modern Fieldbus) devices on a single twisted pair.
- **Deterministic** — Guaranteed worst-case response time (typically **1 ms to 100 ms**).
- **Distributed Intelligence** — Devices have local processing; not just dumb transducers.
- **Open / Interoperable** — Conforms to international standards so multi-vendor devices can coexist.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Fieldbus = A Multilingual Group Chat"

Imagine a **large chemical plant** with **200 temperature sensors, 80 valves, and 15 controllers**. In the **1980s (pre-Fieldbus era)**, every sensor had a dedicated copper wire running all the way to the control room — like every person in a company having their own private telephone line to the CEO. Costly, messy, impossible to scale.

A **Fieldbus** is like a **WhatsApp group chat** for the entire plant:
- All devices "join" the same digital highway (the bus).
- Each device has a unique "username" (network address).
- Messages are short, structured packets (telegrams).
- The group is **moderated** — only one device talks at a time (token passing, TDMA, CSMA/CD — depending on protocol).
- A **plant operator (master/controller)** can broadcast a command ("Close Valve 7!"), and Valve 7 immediately obeys.

Now, a **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is the **mobile-data version of that group chat** — same concept, but the "wires" are replaced by **radio waves**, making the chat spontaneous, mobile, and battery-powered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fieldbus vs. WSN — The One-Line Distinction**
> - **Fieldbus** → Wired (or wired-with-wireless-extensions) industrial deterministic bus.
> - **WSN** → Fully wireless, multi-hop, self-organizing, energy-constrained network of sensor nodes.

---

## 1.3 Why Fieldbuses Exist: The 4–20 mA Legacy Problem

The traditional **4–20 mA current loop**, standardized in the **1950s**, had serious limitations:

| Problem | Description |
|---|---|
| **One-wire-per-signal** | 1 sensor = 2 copper wires over hundreds of meters → expensive cabling |
| **Analog noise** | EMI, ground loops degrade signal over long distances |
| **Unidirectional** | Sensor can only *send*; cannot receive configuration or diagnostics |
| **No self-diagnosis** | Device health, calibration data, error codes are inaccessible |
| **No multi-variable** | One loop = one process variable; a Coriolis flow meter needs 4–5 loops |

The **Fieldbus** solves ALL of these in one architectural shift. This is the single most important "Why?" examiners test.

---

## 1.4 The KTU Hierarchy: Where Does the Fieldbus Sit?

A modern industrial automation pyramid (the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model**) has 5 levels. The Fieldbus occupies **Levels 0, 1, and 2**:

| Level | Name | Devices | Network |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 4** | Enterprise | ERP, MES | Ethernet / WAN |
| **Level 3** | Operations | SCADA, HMI | Industrial Ethernet |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | PLCs, DCS Controllers | Fieldbus / Industrial Ethernet |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | Sensors, Actuators, Transmitters | **Fieldbus** ← Core domain |
| **Level 0** | Process | Motors, Valves, RTDs, Thermocouples | Wired to Level 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** When the examiner asks "At which level of the automation hierarchy does the Fieldbus operate?" the model answer is: **Level 0 (Process) and Level 1 (Basic Control), bridging into Level 2 (Supervisory).** Mentioning **IEC 61158** as the defining standard earns bonus marks.

---

## 1.5 Standard Fieldbus Protocols (KTU 2024 Mandated List)

The KTU Module-3 syllabus expects familiarity with these specific protocols:

### 1.5.1 PROFIBUS (Process Field Bus) — IEC 61158
- Developed by **Siemens / PROFIBUS International (PI)**.
- Two variants: **PROFIBUS-DP** (Decentralised Periphery, for factory automation) and **PROFIBUS-PA** (Process Automation, for hazardous areas).
- Physical layer: **RS-485** (DP) or **IEC 61158-2 Manchester-encoded bus** (PA).
- Speed: **9.6 kbps to 12 Mbps**; max 32 stations per segment.
- Token passing among masters, polled slave communication.

### 1.5.2 FOUNDATION Fieldbus (FF) — IEC 61158
- Designed specifically for **process industries** (oil & gas, chemical, pharma).
- Two types: **H1 (31.25 kbps, intrinsically safe)** and **HSE (High Speed Ethernet, 100 Mbps)**.
- Carries not just I/O data but also **function blocks** for distributed control.

### 1.5.3 CAN (Controller Area Network) — ISO 11898
- Originally for **automotive** (Bosch, 1986), now ubiquitous in industrial control.
- Speed: **125 kbps to 1 Mbps**; max 8-byte payload per frame.
- CSMA/CA + message prioritization (11-bit or 29-bit ID).
- Sub-protocols: **CANopen (CiA 301)**, **DeviceNet**.

### 1.5.4 Modbus (RTU / TCP) — Modicon / IEC tradition
- The **grandfather** of industrial protocols (1979).
- Master-slave, simple register-based communication.
- Modbus RTU over RS-485 (serial) and Modbus TCP over Ethernet.

### 1.5.5 Wireless Extensions: WirelessHART & ISA100.11a
- **WirelessHART (IEC 62591, 2007)** — 2.4 GHz DSSS, TDMA + channel hopping, AES-128.
- **ISA100.11a (IEC 62734, 2014)** — More flexible, supports multiple protocols over IPv6/6LoWPAN.
- These are the **direct bridge** from Fieldbus thinking to WSN design.

---

## 1.6 Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) — Definition in KTU Context

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring infrastructure of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, vibration, pollutants) and forward their measurements through **multi-hop wireless communication** to a central sink (base station / gateway).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Formal Definition (Verbatim from KTU-Mod-3 Syllabus):**
> "A WSN consists of a large number of low-cost, low-power, multifunctional sensor nodes that are densely deployed either inside a phenomenon or very close to it, and are connected through a wireless medium forming a self-organizing network."

### WSN vs. Traditional Fieldbus — The KTU Comparison Framework

| Parameter | Wired Fieldbus | Wireless Sensor Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Medium** | Copper (twisted pair / fiber) | Radio (RF) |
| **Power** | Mains-powered | **Battery / energy-harvesting** (key constraint) |
| **Topology** | Bus, tree, star (planned) | **Ad-hoc, multi-hop, mesh** (self-forming) |
| **Mobility** | Fixed nodes | **Mobile nodes possible** |
| **Determinism** | High (ms-level guaranteed) | Best-effort (statistical guarantees) |
| **Scalability** | Limited by bus length & loading | High (thousands of nodes) |
| **Standards** | PROFIBUS, FF, CAN, Modbus | IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, WirelessHART |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fieldbus Architecture — The OSI Mapping

A true Fieldbus does NOT implement the full 7-layer OSI stack (that would be too slow). Instead, it implements a **3-layer "Fieldbus-OSI" profile** for speed and determinism:

| OSI Layer | Fieldbus Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 — Application | **User Layer / Function Blocks** | Process variable mapping, control loops |
| Layer 2 — Data Link | **Data Link Layer (DLL)** | Bus access, telegram framing, error detection |
| Layer 1 — Physical | **Physical Layer (PHY)** | Voltage, current, encoding, cabling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The reason Fieldbuses deliberately *skip* Layers 3–6 is that **routing, session management, and presentation services are unnecessary in a deterministic, short-distance industrial network**. Mentioning this earns examiner credit.

---

## 2.2 Fieldbus Physical Layer — Signal Encoding Choices

There are three dominant encoding schemes, each with deterministic properties:

### 2.2.1 Manchester Encoding (Used in FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1, PROFIBUS-PA)
- Each bit interval contains a **mid-bit transition**: 0 = H→L, 1 = L→H.
- Self-clocking — no separate clock line needed.
- **No DC component** — ideal for AC-coupled transformer isolation in hazardous areas.

### 2.2.2 NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) — Used in RS-485 (PROFIBUS-DP, Modbus RTU)
- Bit value held constant for full bit period.
- Requires a separate clock or known baud rate.
- DC-balanced only over long periods.

### 2.2.3 Bit Stuffing + NRZ — Used in CAN
- After 5 consecutive identical bits, a stuff bit of opposite polarity is inserted.
- Guarantees enough edges for clock recovery.

---

## 2.3 Data Link Layer — Bus Access Methods

The bus-access method determines **determinism** — a critical KTU 2024 concept.

| Method | Protocol Example | Determinism |
|---|---|---|
| **Token Passing** | PROFIBUS, Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) | High — guaranteed token rotation time |
| **Master-Slave Polling** | Modbus RTU, AS-i | Very high — master controls all timing |
| **CSMA/CD** | Ethernet, early CAN | Low — collisions possible |
| **CSMA/CA + Priority Arbitration** | CAN, WirelessHART | High — non-destructive bitwise arbitration |
| **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)** | WirelessHART, ISA100.11a | Very high — strict time-slot allocation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Favourite Trick:** "Why is CAN considered deterministic despite using CSMA?" — Answer: Because CAN uses **non-destructive bitwise arbitration** on a wired bus; the dominant bit (0) always wins, and only one message survives, so there is **no collision-induced retransmission delay**.

---

## 2.4 Fieldbus Timing Analysis — The Cycle Time Formula

For a deterministic Fieldbus, the **worst-case bus cycle time** is a critical design parameter:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( t_{\text{frame},i} + t_{\text{prop},i} \right) + t_{\text{overhead}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{frame},i}$ = transmission time of message $i$ (telegram length / baud rate)
- $t_{\text{prop},i}$ = propagation delay (cable length / signal velocity ≈ 5 ns/m for copper)
- $t_{\text{overhead}}$ = inter-frame gap, token rotation, retry margin
- $N$ = number of devices on the bus

**Numerical Example (PROFIBUS-DP at 1.5 Mbps, 32 devices, 32-byte telegrams, 100 m cable):**

$$
t_{\text{frame}} = \frac{32 \times 8 \text{ bits} + \text{overhead}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{256 + 64}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} \approx 213\ \mu s
$$

$$
t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.5\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} \approx 32 \times (213 + 0.5)\ \mu s + 100\ \mu s \approx 6.94\ ms
$$

This 6.94 ms is well within the 10 ms refresh typical of PROFIBUS-DP fast cycles.

---

## 2.5 WSN Architecture — The Protocol Stack (KTU Module-3 Core)

The WSN protocol stack is **NOT** a copy of the OSI stack; it is a **5-layer energy-aware** profile:

| Layer | WSN Implementation | Key Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Sensing + actuation logic | Custom, MQTT, CoAP |
| **Transport** | Reliability where needed | UDP-based (no TCP overhead) |
| **Network** | Multi-hop routing | LEACH, PEGASIS, AODV, RPL |
| **Data Link (MAC)** | Channel access, duty cycling | S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 |
| **Physical (PHY)** | RF modulation, encoding | IEEE 802.15.4 (2.4 GHz O-QPSK) |

---

## 2.6 WSN Energy Model — The First-Order Radio Model (Heinzelman)

The KTU Module-3 syllabus almost always tests the **Heinzelman first-order radio energy model** in Part B questions. Memorize it:

**Transmit energy** for a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$:

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) = 
\begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{\text{elec}} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d < d_{0} \\[4pt]
k \cdot E_{\text{elec}} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d \ge d_{0}
\end{cases}
$$

**Receive energy:**

$$
E_{RX}(k) = k \cdot E_{\text{elec}}
$$

**Threshold distance** $d_0$ (where free-space equals multipath):

$$
d_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

**Total energy for an $N$-hop transmission of $k$ bits over distance $D$:**

$$
E_{\text{total}}(N) = N \cdot E_{TX}\left(\frac{k}{N}, d_{\text{hop}}\right) + (N-1) \cdot E_{RX}\left(\frac{k}{N}\right)
$$

where $d_{\text{hop}} = D/N$.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (One-Page Revision Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — these 10 equations cover ~80% of KTU Module-3 numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \sum (t_{\text{frame}} + t_{\text{prop}}) + t_{\text{ovh}}$ | Worst-case bus cycle time | seconds |
| 2 | $t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bits}}}{R_{\text{baud}}}$ | Frame transmission time | seconds |
| 3 | $t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{cable}}}{v_{\text{signal}}} \approx \dfrac{L}{2 \times 10^{8}}$ | Propagation delay | seconds |
| 4 | $E_{TX}(k,d) = k E_{\text{elec}} + k \varepsilon_{fs} d^{2}$ | Free-space transmit energy | Joules |
| 5 | $E_{TX}(k,d) = k E_{\text{elec}} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d^{4}$ | Multipath transmit energy | Joules |
| 6 | $E_{RX}(k) = k E_{\text{elec}}$ | Receive energy | Joules |
| 7 | $d_{0} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | meters |
| 8 | $E_{\text{total}}(N) = N E_{TX}(k/N, D/N) + (N-1) E_{RX}(k/N)$ | Multi-hop total energy | Joules |
| 9 | $N_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs} D^{2}}{E_{\text{elec}}}}$ | Optimal hop count (free-space) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$ | Duty cycle (energy saving) | dimensionless |

### Typical KTU-Standard Parameter Values

| Symbol | Value (typical) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{\text{elec}}$ | **50** | nJ/bit |
| $\varepsilon_{fs}$ | **10** | pJ/bit/m² |
| $\varepsilon_{mp}$ | **0.0013** | pJ/bit/m⁴ |
| $d_0$ | **≈ 87.7** | m |
| Initial node energy $E_0$ | **0.5 – 1.0** | J |
| Data packet size $k$ | **2000 – 4000** | bits |

---

## 2.8 Fieldbus-to-WSN Migration: Why Industries Are Adopting Wireless

Three real-world engineering drivers (frequent in KTU essay questions):

1. **Retrofit cost** — Installing 10 km of cable in an existing plant may cost 10× the cost of wireless motes.
2. **Rotating equipment** — Cranes, robotic arms, conveyor belts cannot have wires.
3. **Hazardous areas** — In oil refineries, removing one cable gland reduces ignition risk dramatically.

However, the **engineering trade-offs** are:
- **Latency:** Wireless ≈ 100 ms – 10 s vs. Wired ≈ 1 – 10 ms.
- **Reliability:** Wired > 99.999% vs. Wireless ≈ 99.9% (Packet Error Rate ~10⁻³).
- **Security:** Wired is physically secure; wireless needs AES-128/256.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conclusion:** Fieldbuses remain the gold standard for **deterministic, mission-critical, fast control loops** (motor control, safety interlocks, fast PID). WSNs dominate where **cost, mobility, density, and ease of deployment** outweigh raw determinism (environmental monitoring, asset tracking, structural health).
