- If a wireless segment is lost, BS detects it via duplicate local ACKs and triggers a local retransmission from SS — the destination is never involved.
- When the wired connection returns an ACK from DS, BS uses it to advance its own send window and may also spoof a cumulative ACK to SS.

*(Diagram as shown in Section 4.1 should be redrawn in the exam for full marks.)*

**(b) Comparative Analysis (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Standard TCP | Split TCP | Snoop TCP |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Throughput in Lossy WSN** | Very low (misinterprets loss) | **High** (local recovery) | High (snoop cache) |
| **BS Computational Complexity** | None | **High** (full TCP state) | Medium (snoop agent) |
| **End-to-End Semantics** | **Preserved** | **Violated** (BS terminates conn.) | **Preserved** (transparent) |
| **ACK Mechanism** | End-to-end | Local at BS | Snooped and delayed if needed |
| **Security/Privacy** | Secure | BS can read payload | Payload not examined |
| **Handoff Support** | Poor | **Excellent** (BS handles reconnection) | Good |
| **Deployment in WSN** | Not recommended | **Recommended for static BS** | Recommended for mobile scenarios |

---

**Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Choice B]**

*Q. (a)* With a suitable sequence diagram, explain the connection establishment, data transfer, and local recovery mechanism in Split TCP. *(7 marks)*

*Q. (b)* A sensor node communicates to a destination server via a Split TCP base station. The wireless link has a one-way delay of 15 ms, transmission time of 2 ms per segment, and packet loss probability of 0.04. The wired link has a one-way delay of 80 ms and negligible loss. Calculate the throughput of the standard TCP and Split TCP, and comment on the gain. Assume MSS = 1200 bytes. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sequence Diagram and Mechanism (7 marks)**

*(The student should reproduce a sequence diagram similar to the one in Section 4.2, showing SS, BS, and DS interactions.)*

**Mechanism Phases:**

1. **Connection Establishment:**
   - SS sends `SYN` → BS replies with `SYN+ACK` → SS sends `ACK` (Wireless TCP established).
   - BS sends `SYN` → DS replies with `SYN+ACK` → BS sends `ACK` (Wired TCP established).
   - Two independent TCP sessions now exist.

2. **Data Transfer:**
   - SS transmits data segments. BS stores them in its proxy buffer and instantly returns a local ACK.
   - BS relays the buffered data over the wired TCP session to DS.
   - DS returns its own ACK, which the BS uses to slide its wired send window.

3. **Local Recovery:**
   - If SS sends segment N and the BS does not receive it, the BS will not send a local ACK for N.
   - SS times out and retransmits segment N (or the BS triggers fast retransmit via duplicate ACKs).
   - Crucially, the wired connection to DS is **not affected** — the DS sees a smooth, in-order byte stream.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $t_{p}^{w} = 15$ ms, $t_{tx} = 2$ ms, $p_{w} = 0.04$
- $t_{p}^{wd} = 80$ ms, $p_{wd} \approx 0$
- $MSS = 1200$ bytes $= 9600$ bits

**Step 1: Compute RTTs**

Wireless RTT (with negligible BS processing time $t_{proc}^{BS} \approx 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTT_{w} &= 2 \cdot t_{p}^{w} + 2 \cdot t_{tx} \\
&= 2(15) + 2(2) \\
&= 34 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wired RTT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTT_{wd} &= 2 \cdot t_{p}^{wd} + 2 \cdot t_{tx} \\
&= 2(80) + 2(2) \\
&= 164 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: End-to-End RTT (Standard TCP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTT_{e2e} &= 2 \cdot t_{p}^{w} + 2 \cdot t_{p}^{wd} + 2 \cdot t_{tx} \\
&= 2(15) + 2(80) + 2(2) \\
&= 194 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Loss Probabilities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{e2e} &= 1 - (1 - 0.04)(1 - 0) = 0.04 \\
p_{split} &\approx 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Standard TCP Throughput (using Padhye model)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{std} &= \frac{MSS}{RTT_{e2e} \cdot \sqrt{p_{e2e}}} \\
&= \frac{9600}{0.194 \cdot \sqrt{0.04}} \\
&= \frac{9600}{0.194 \cdot 0.2} \\
&= \frac{9600}{0.0388} \\
&\approx 247{,}422 \text{ bps} \approx 247.4 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Split TCP Throughput (limited by wireless hop)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{split} &= \frac{MSS}{RTT_{w} \cdot \sqrt{p_{w}}} \\
&= \frac{9600}{0.034 \cdot 0.2} \\
&= \frac{9600}{0.0068} \\
&\approx 1{,}411{,}764 \text{ bps} \approx 1411.8 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Throughput Gain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{\eta_{split}}{\eta_{std}} = \frac{1411.8}{247.4} \approx 5.71
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Split TCP delivers approximately **5.71 times** the throughput of standard TCP by isolating the high wireless loss probability from the end-to-end congestion control loop. The sensor node's congestion window remains stable, allowing continuous transmission.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Snooping TCP" with "Split TCP":** In Snooping TCP, the BS is a **transparent** agent that only caches and snoops ACKs — the end-to-end TCP connection is preserved. In Split TCP, the BS **terminates** the connection and starts a new one. This distinction is the **most common 2-mark trap** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Always mention the violation of end-to-end semantics** when describing Split TCP. Examiners award a full mark for this explicit acknowledgment.
> 3. **In numerical problems,** students frequently forget to convert MSS from bytes to bits (multiply by 8). Always show the unit conversion explicitly.
> 4. **Don't state that Split TCP "increases the congestion window size"** — it merely prevents the *unnecessary collapse* of the window. The window still grows according to standard TCP rules.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Idea:** Split TCP divides an end-to-end TCP connection at the **Base Station**, which acts as a **local TCP proxy** to isolate wireless losses.
- **Key Mechanism:** **Local ACK generation** and **local retransmission** at the Base Station.
- **Primary Advantage:** **Drastically improved throughput** in lossy WSN environments (typically 3× to 6× gain) by preventing the misinterpretation of wireless loss as congestion.
- **Critical Disadvantage:** **Violates end-to-end TCP semantics**, as the BS terminates and re-originates the connection.
- **BS Complexity:** The base station must maintain **two full TCP state machines** simultaneously.
- **Energy Impact:** **Reduces sensor node energy consumption** by avoiding long-distance retransmissions, but increases energy usage at the BS.
- **Variants in the Family:** I-TCP (Indirect TCP), M-TCP (Mobile TCP), Snooping TCP — all are split-based or split-inspired approaches.
- **Comparison Triggers:** Be ready to compare with **Standard TCP**, **Snooping TCP**, and **Mobile TCP** in tabular form.
- **Formula to Memorize:** $\eta \approx \dfrac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ (Padhye simplified TCP throughput model).
- **KTU Buzzwords for Full Marks:** *Proxy*, *Local ACK*, *Local Retransmission*, *End-to-End Semantics Violation*, *ACK Spoofing*, *Connection Splitting*, *Bandwidth-Delay Product*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimising Web Over Wireless
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimising Web Over Wireless

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optimising Web Over Wireless** in the context of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) refers to the set of techniques, architectural adaptations, and protocol-level modifications used to make traditional web-centric communication (HTTP/TCP/IP stack) efficient, lightweight, and energy-aware when deployed over resource-constrained wireless sensor nodes. The objective is to bridge the **Internet-of-Things (IoT)** paradigm with the **Web-of-Things (WoT)**, allowing sensor data to be accessed, queried, and controlled through standard web interfaces without exhausting the limited energy, bandwidth, and memory budgets of sensor motes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this is positioned under Module 3 as part of the **WSN-to-Internet integration** layer, where the emphasis is on:
- Reducing the **protocol overhead** (header-to-payload ratio).
- Replacing verbose **text-based protocols (HTTP/HTML)** with binary equivalents (**CoAP, MQTT-SN, 6LoWPAN**).
- Using **gateways/proxies** to translate between IPv6-internet and IEEE 802.15.4 networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Optimising Web Over Wireless involves tailoring the application, transport, and network layer protocols of the TCP/IP suite so that web services (data publishing, querying, control) can be delivered reliably across lossy, bandwidth-limited, and energy-constrained wireless links."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Postman and the Mountain Village"**
>
> Imagine the modern Internet as a **super-highway system with large delivery trucks (HTTP packets, often several KB)** carrying parcels. A WSN is like a **remote mountain village connected by a narrow, winding footpath with no bridges**. Sending a large truck over the footpath is impossible — the path will collapse.
>
> **Optimising Web Over Wireless** is like:
> 1. **Repacking goods into small backpacks** (CoAP, 6LoWPAN header compression).
> 2. **Hiring a local guide (gateway/proxy)** who knows the path and translates orders.
> 3. **Combining multiple villagers' orders into one trip** (data aggregation/in-network processing).
> 4. **Posting updates only when something changes** (event-driven / publish-subscribe, not polling).
>
> The result: web services "arrive" at the village efficiently without exhausting the carriers (sensor batteries).

---

## 1.3 Key Technical Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly used in numerical and design problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transceiver electronics energy | **E_elec** | **50 nJ/bit** | Energy to run TX/RX circuitry |
| Amplifier energy (free-space) | **E_fs** | **10 pJ/bit/m²** | For short-range d² loss |
| Amplifier energy (multipath) | **E_mp** | **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** | For long-range d⁴ loss |
| Path-loss exponent (free space) | **n = 2** | — | LOS propagation |
| Path-loss exponent (multipath) | **n = 4** | — | NLOS, cluttered WSN |
| IEEE 802.15.4 max frame | **127 bytes** | — | Physical-layer cap |
| 6LoWPAN header (compressed) | **≈ 2–4 bytes** | — | vs 40 B IPv6 |
| CoAP header (min) | **4 bytes** | | vs 8+ B HTTP request line |
| Energy per useful bit (target) | **≤ 100 nJ/bit** | — | Battery-life constraint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the *energy-vs-payload* trade-off — i.e., the choice of header compression versus application-layer retransmission. Memorise the values above.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Optimisation Problem — Structured Logic

Optimising web traffic over a wireless sensor network is a **multi-layer optimisation problem**. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames it as a four-layer transformation pipeline:

### Step 1 — Application Layer Slimming
- **HTTP (≈ 200–800 bytes headers)** is replaced with **CoAP (4-byte binary header)** or **MQTT-SN (2-byte topic ID)**.
- **HTML/XML payloads** are replaced with **CBOR, SenML, or EXI (Efficient XML Interchange)** — binary serialisation formats.
- **Polling** (HTTP GET every T seconds) is replaced with **event-driven notification** (CoAP Observe, MQTT publish-on-change).

### Step 2 — Transport Layer Adaptation
- **TCP's 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK)** is expensive over multi-hop WSNs because each SYN costs ≈ 3 full radio transmissions.
- Solutions:
  - **TCP splitting** at the gateway: sensors use UDP/CoAP internally; the gateway runs full TCP to the external web client.
  - **TinyTCP / TcpPax** for embedded motes.
  - **Hop-by-hop reliability** using 802.15.4 MAC ACKs instead of end-to-end TCP retransmissions.

### Step 3 — Network Layer Compression (6LoWPAN)
- **IPv6 header (40 bytes)** is compressed to **2–4 bytes** using **IPHC (IP Header Compression)** and **NHC (Next-Header Compression)**.
- **ND (Neighbour Discovery)** is replaced with **6LoWPAN-ND** to avoid flooding.
- **Fragmentation**: 6LoWPAN supports *mesh-under* fragmentation to fit IPv6 packets into 127-byte 802.15.4 frames.

### Step 4 — MAC / Radio Duty Cycling
- The radio is the **dominant energy consumer (≈ 60–80 % of total node energy)**.
- **Duty cycling** (e.g., B-MAC, S-MAC, ContikiMAC) keeps the radio OFF for > 99 % of the time.
- Web requests are queued in RAM and transmitted only during the **periodic wake-up window**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Without these four transformations, a single HTTP request to a sensor node can consume **> 1 mJ of energy**, draining a 2 Ah, 3 V coin cell in days instead of years.

---

## 2.2 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Optimisation Used | Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Agriculture** | 6LoWPAN + CoAP | Soil-moisture sensors accessible via RESTful API |
| **Industrial IoT** | WirelessHART + HTTP proxy | Factory floor monitoring on existing IP infrastructure |
| **Healthcare WSN** | MQTT-SN + duty cycling | Wearable ECG monitors with multi-year battery life |
| **Smart Cities** | LoRaWAN + Web gateway | Air-quality data published to city dashboards |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | HTTP-coap proxy + SenML | Forest-fire sensors accessible to global researchers |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the table below for all numerical/derivation questions. **Remember:** in markdown tables, use `\vert` for any modulus bar to avoid table breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmit energy (free space, d²) | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$ | k = bits, d ≤ d₀ (crossover distance) |
| 2 | Transmit energy (multipath, d⁴) | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$ | d > d₀ |
| 3 | Receive energy | $E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Listening cost |
| 4 | Crossover distance | $d_{0} = \sqrt{E_{fs} / E_{mp}}$ | Boundary between d² and d⁴ models |
| 5 | Total energy per round (LEACH-style) | $E_{round} = k \left( 2N E_{elec} + N E_{fs} d_{toBS}^{2} + N E_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} \right)$ | N nodes, k bits to BS |
| 6 | Data compression ratio | $CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Higher CR = better |
| 7 | Header-compression gain | $G_{hc} = \dfrac{H_{full} - H_{comp}}{H_{full}} \times 100\%$ | % reduction in header bytes |
| 8 | Energy saved per packet | $\Delta E = E_{tx}(k_{orig},d) - E_{tx}(k_{comp},d)$ | Compressed vs original |
| 9 | TCP throughput over lossy link | $T_{TCP} \approx \dfrac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ | p = packet-loss probability |
| 10 | CoAP-overhead reduction vs HTTP | $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{H_{CoAP}}{H_{HTTP}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Typical $\eta \approx 70 \text{–} 85\%$ |
| 11 | Radio duty-cycle energy | $E_{duty} = DC \cdot P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + (1-DC) \cdot P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}$ | DC = duty cycle fraction |
| 12 | Network lifetime (rounds) | $L_{net} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{round}}$ | Until first node dies |
| 13 | Aggregation gain | $G_{agg} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Bytes-after-agg}}{\text{Bytes-before-agg}}$ | Fraction of bytes eliminated |
| 14 | Latency over n hops | $D = n \cdot (t_{proc} + t_{queue} + t_{prop} + t_{tx})$ | End-to-end delay |
| 15 | Energy per useful bit (EPUB) | $EPUB = \dfrac{E_{tx} + E_{rx}}{k_{payload}}$ | Minimise this to extend lifetime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units reminder:** E in **Joules (J)**, k in **bits**, d in **metres (m)**, t in **seconds (s)**, RTT in **seconds (s)**, DC is **dimensionless (0 < DC < 1)**, power P in **Watts (W)**.

---

## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Each Optimisation

- **Why compress headers?** — Because a sensor packet of 30 bytes carrying 10 bytes of temperature data wastes **67 %** of radio energy on header bytes.
- **Why split TCP at the gateway?** — Because end-to-end TCP retransmissions in a 10-hop WSN can multiply by 30× the energy cost of a single lost packet.
- **Why aggregate?** — Because spatial correlation means neighbouring temperature sensors report almost-identical values; aggregation exploits this redundancy.
- **Why duty-cycle?** — The CC2420 radio (used in TelosB/Tmote Sky motes) draws **≈ 18.8 mA** in RX mode but only **≈ 1 µA** in sleep; duty cycling therefore yields 4–5 orders of magnitude energy reduction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Header-Compression Energy Savings

**Problem Setup:** A sensor node transmits a temperature reading every 60 s. The packet is:
- HTTP version: 200 B header + 20 B payload = 220 B
- CoAP version: 4 B header + 20 B payload = 24 B
- Distance to sink: d = 10 m (free-space, d² model)
- $E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit, $E_{fs}$ = 10 pJ/bit/m²

**Find:** Energy saved per packet and percentage reduction.

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert bytes to bits.**
For HTTP:
$$ k_{HTTP} = 220 \text{ B} \times 8 = 1760 \text{ bits} $$
For CoAP:
$$ k_{CoAP} = 24 \text{ B} \times 8 = 192 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 2 — Apply free-space transmit energy formula.**
$$ E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} $$
Factor out k:
$$ E_{tx}(k,d) = k \left( E_{elec} + E_{fs} \cdot d^{2} \right) $$

**Step 3 — Compute the constant term.**
$$ E_{fs} \cdot d^{2} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{2} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 = 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J/bit} = 1 \text{ nJ/bit} $$

$$ E_{elec} + E_{fs} \cdot d^{2} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit} + 1 \text{ nJ/bit} = 51 \text{ nJ/bit} $$

**Step 4 — Compute energy for HTTP packet.**
$$ E_{tx}^{HTTP} = 1760 \times 51 \times 10^{-9} = 89{,}760 \text{ nJ} = 89.76 \text{ µJ} $$

**Step 5 — Compute energy for CoAP packet.**
$$ E_{tx}^{CoAP} = 192 \times 51 \times 10^{-9} = 9{,}792 \text{ nJ} = 9.792 \text{ µJ} $$

**Step 6 — Compute energy saved per packet.**
$$ \Delta E = E_{tx}^{HTTP} - E_{tx}^{CoAP} = 89.76 - 9.792 = 79.968 \text{ µJ} $$

**Step 7 — Compute percentage reduction.**
$$ \eta = \left(1 - \frac{E_{tx}^{CoAP}}{E_{tx}^{HTTP}}\right) \times 100\% $$

$$ \eta = \left(1 - \frac{9.792}{89.76}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.1091) \times 100\% = 89.09\% $$

**Final Result:**
$$ \boxed{\Delta E \approx 79.97 \text{ µJ/packet}, \quad \eta \approx 89.1\%} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Switching from HTTP to CoAP reduces transmission energy by **≈ 89 %** for a 20-byte payload. Over 1 million packets (a day's data from 1 node polling every 60 s), this saves **≈ 80 J** — a significant fraction of a coin-cell's capacity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crossover Distance for Energy Model Selection

**Given:** $E_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$, $E_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$.

**Find:** The crossover distance d₀ where the d² model equals the d⁴ model in energy cost.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Equate the amplifier-energy terms** (the $E_{elec} \cdot k$ terms cancel since both have the same k):
$$ E_{fs} \cdot d_{0}^{2} = E_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{4} $$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $d_{0}^{2}$:**
$$ E_{fs} = E_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{2} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d_{0}^{2}$:**
$$ d_{0}^{2} = \frac{E_{fs}}{E_{mp}} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values:**
$$ d_{0}^{2} = \frac{10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}}{0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}} = \frac{10}{0.0013} \text{ m}^{2} \approx 7692.31 \text{ m}^{2} $$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**
$$ d_{0} = \sqrt{7692.31} \approx 87.7 \text{ m} $$

**Final Result:**
$$ \boxed{d_{0} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a typical indoor WSN with node-to-sink distances of 5–20 m, the **free-space (d²) model is always valid**. The multipath d⁴ model only matters for outdoor deployments or industrial sites with metal obstructions > 87.7 m apart.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TCP Throughput Degradation over Lossy Wireless Link

**Given:** MSS = 250 bytes, RTT = 0.5 s, wireless loss probability p = 0.01 (1 %).

**Find:** TCP throughput and compare with the loss-free case.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute loss-free throughput** (limited by RTT, assuming infinite window):
$$ T_{no\_loss} = \frac{MSS}{RTT} = \frac{250 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{0.5 \text{ s}} = \frac{2000}{0.5} = 4000 \text{ bps} = 4 \text{ kbps} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the lossy-wireless TCP throughput model:**
$$ T_{lossy} \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{p}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sqrt{p}$:**
$$ \sqrt{p} = \sqrt{0.01} = 0.1 $$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$ T_{lossy} = \frac{2000}{0.5 \times 0.1} = \frac{2000}{0.05} = 40{,}000 \text{ bps} = 40 \text{ kbps} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the throughput improvement factor** (note: this model shows that even small loss causes cwnd to shrink; the simplistic formula above gives the *capacity upper-bound* under Reno-style AIMD):
$$ \text{Factor} = \frac{T_{lossy}}{T_{no\_loss}} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \times $$

**Final Result (using a more accurate model with cwnd collapse):**
$$ T_{actual} \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2p/3}} = 4000 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2(0.01)/3}} \approx 4000 \times \frac{1}{0.0816} \approx 49 \text{ kbps} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students use the bandwidth-delay product (BDP = BW × RTT) formula, but over **lossy wireless** links, the cwnd is collapsed by each loss event, and the correct model is the *square-root* (or AIMD-based) throughput formula. Full marks only if you explicitly state the underlying assumption (TCP-Reno, loss-based cwnd reduction).

---

## 3.4 Numerical Practice Quick-Fire Table

| S.No | Question | Setup | Quick Answer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | E_tx for k=2000 bits, d=5 m, free-space | $E_{elec}=50$ nJ/bit, $E_{fs}=10$ pJ/bit/m² | $E = 2000(50+0.25)$ nJ = 100.5 µJ |
| Q2 | E_rx for k=2000 bits | $E_{elec}=50$ nJ/bit | $E_{rx} = 100$ µJ |
| Q3 | Compression ratio: 220 B → 30 B | — | CR = 220/30 = 7.33 |
| Q4 | % header saved by 6LoWPAN: 40 B → 3 B | — | G_hc = (37/40)×100% = 92.5 % |
| Q5 | Network lifetime, 100 nodes, 1 J/node, 0.05 J/round | — | L = 1/0.05 = 20 rounds |
| Q6 | Duty cycle, t_on=10 ms, T=1 s | — | DC = 10/1000 = 0.01 (1 %) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture: Web Client ↔ WSN

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** for accessing web services over a wireless sensor network. The flow follows the KTU 2024 Module-3 layered model.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Web Client Browser or Mobile App] --> B[External Web Server REST API or CoAP endpoint]
    B --> C[WSN Gateway or Edge Proxy 6LoWPAN Border Router]
    C --> D[6LoWPAN Network IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4]
    D --> E1[Sensor Node 1 Temperature]
    D --> E2[Sensor Node 2 Humidity]
    D --> E3[Sensor Node 3 Motion]
    D --> E4[Sensor Node 4 Gas]

    subgraph OPT[Optimisation Layer]
        F1[Header Compression 6LoWPAN IPHC NHC]
        F2[Protocol Translation HTTP to CoAP]
        F3[Data Aggregation In network processing]
        F4[Radio Duty Cycling MAC layer sleep wake]
    end

    C --- F1
    C --- F2
    E1 --- F3
    E4 --- F4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The WSN Gateway (also called 6LoWPAN Border Router) is the *single point of intelligence* that performs **protocol translation** (HTTP ↔ CoAP) and **header compression** (IPv6 ↔ 6LoWPAN). Sensor nodes perform **in-network aggregation** and **duty cycling** internally.

---

## 4.2 Protocol-Stack Comparison — Standard Internet vs Optimised WSN

This Mermaid block compares the **OSI-style protocol stacks** of the traditional Internet and the optimised WSN, layer by layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INT[Standard Internet Stack]
        i7[Application: HTTP, HTML, XML]
        i6[Presentation: TLS, JSON]
        i5[Session: SDP, RPC]
        i4[Transport: TCP, 3 way handshake]
        i3[Network: IPv4 or IPv6, 40 byte header]
        i2[Data Link: Ethernet 802.3]
        i1[Physical: Copper or Fiber]
    end

    subgraph WSN[Optimised WSN Stack]
        w7[Application: CoAP, MQTT SN, SenML]
        w6[Presentation: CBOR, EXI, DTLS]
        w5[Session: CoAP Observe, Pub Sub]
        w4[Transport: UDP, TinyTCP, Split TCP at gateway]
        w3[Network: 6LoWPAN, RPL, 2 to 4 byte header]
        w2[Data Link: IEEE 802.15.4, 127 byte frame]
        w1[Physical: Radio 2.4 GHz OOK or CSS]
    end

    INT -.Replaced by.-> WSN
```

---

## 4.3 Energy-Flow Diagram of a Web-Over-WSN Packet

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[Total Energy Budget per Node 2 Joules] --> P1[Radio Energy 70 percent]
    P0 --> P2[CPU Energy 20 percent]
    P0 --> P3[Sensor and ADC Energy 8 percent]
    P0 --> P4[Idle Leakage 2 percent]

    P1 --> P1a[TX Energy]
    P1 --> P1b[RX Energy]
    P1 --> P1c[Idle Listening WASTE]
    P1 --> P1d[Sleep Energy 0.1 percent]

    P1c -. Removed by .- P1d
    P1a -. Minimised by .- P1d
    P1b -. Minimised by .- P1d
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Idle listening (keeping the radio ON while no packet is expected) is the **single largest waste** in WSNs. Optimising web-over-wireless specifically targets this waste via duty cycling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** (Part A: 3 marks each, Part B: 14 marks with module-internal choice). Two full Part-B alternatives (Question A and Question B) are provided below.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**"Why is HTTP considered inefficient for direct use in Wireless Sensor Networks? List any two protocol-level optimisations used in the web-over-wireless stack."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

HTTP is inefficient for direct WSN use for the following reasons:
1. **Large header overhead** — HTTP request/response headers typically occupy 200–800 bytes, which is a major fraction of the 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frame; this wastes radio energy on non-payload bytes.
2. **Verbose text-based encoding** — HTTP uses human-readable ASCII, which is unsuitable for CPU- and memory-constrained motes (8-bit MCUs, < 10 kB RAM).
3. **TCP-based transport** — The 3-way handshake and end-to-end retransmissions of TCP are energy-expensive over multi-hop, lossy wireless paths.
4. **Polling-based model** — HTTP's request-response paradigm requires the client to repeatedly poll the server, increasing radio-on time.

**Two protocol-level optimisations (2 marks):**
- **6LoWPAN** — Compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to 2–4 bytes.
- **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — Provides an HTTP-like REST model with a 4-byte binary header running over UDP.
- *(Acceptable alternatives: MQTT-SN, DTLS, RPL routing, duty cycling, in-network aggregation.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
**"Explain the role of a 6LoWPAN Border Router in optimising web access for a WSN."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **6LoWPAN Border Router (6LBR)** is the gateway that bridges an IEEE 802.15.4-based WSN to an external IP network (typically IPv6 / Internet). Its role in optimising web access includes:
1. **Header compression and decompression** — Performs IPHC/NHC compression on packets leaving the WSN and decompression on packets entering.
2. **Protocol translation** — Converts between CoAP (used inside the WSN) and HTTP (used by external web clients), enabling RESTful web access to sensor data.
3. **Fragmentation and reassembly** — Splits large IPv6 packets into multiple 802.15.4 frames and reassembles them on the return path.
4. **Routing and Neighbour Discovery** — Acts as the RPL root and runs 6LoWPAN-ND, allowing external clients to discover sensor nodes as standard IPv6 hosts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme uses **module-internal choice** for Part B. Question A and Question B below are **independent alternatives**; examiners may set either.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Module-3 Choice A)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-layer architecture** for optimising web services over a wireless sensor network. Discuss the role of **6LoWPAN, CoAP, RPL, and duty cycling** in this architecture. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WSN node transmits a packet of 100 bytes every 30 seconds to a sink located 8 m away. The free-space amplifier parameter is $E_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$ and the electronics energy is $E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$. Compute the **total energy consumed in 1 hour** and compare it with the energy consumed if the same data were sent using **HTTP (header = 200 B)** instead of **CoAP (header = 4 B)**. State your conclusion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) Four-Layer Architecture (7 marks):**

The four-layer architecture for optimising web over wireless is:

1. **Application Layer — CoAP / MQTT-SN / SenML**
   - Replaces HTTP/HTML with binary protocols.
   - CoAP provides REST methods (GET, PUT, POST, DELETE) over UDP with only a 4-byte header.
   - **SenML** (Sensor Markup Language) is a lightweight media-type for sensor measurements.

2. **Transport Layer — UDP + CoAP / Split TCP**
   - UDP is preferred for energy efficiency (no handshake, no ack).
   - For reliability, CoAP's own confirmable (CON) messages provide light-weight retransmission.
   - Split TCP at the gateway avoids end-to-end TCP over multi-hop WSNs.

3. **Network Layer — 6LoWPAN + RPL**
   - **6LoWPAN** compresses IPv6 headers (40 B → 2–4 B) to fit 802.15.4 frames.
   - **RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)** builds a DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) for upward (sensor-to-sink) traffic.

4. **MAC / Radio Layer — Duty Cycling + IEEE 802.15.4**
   - The radio is duty-cycled (e.g., ContikiMAC with 1 % duty cycle).
   - CSMA/CA from 802.15.4 handles channel access.
   - ACK frames at the MAC layer replace expensive end-to-end retransmissions.

**Block Diagram (already provided in Section 4.1).**

---

**(b) Energy Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- CoAP packet: 4 B header + 100 B payload = 104 B = 832 bits
- HTTP packet: 200 B header + 100 B payload = 300 B = 2400 bits
- d = 8 m (free space, use d² model)
- $E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$, $E_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$
- Transmit interval: 30 s, duration: 1 hour = 3600 s

**Step 1 — Compute amplifier-energy term:**
$$ E_{fs} \cdot d^{2} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 8^{2} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 64 = 640 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J/bit} = 0.64 \text{ nJ/bit} $$

**Step 2 — Total per-bit cost:**
$$ E_{elec} + E_{fs} \cdot d^{2} = 50 + 0.64 = 50.64 \text{ nJ/bit} $$

**Step 3 — Energy per CoAP packet:**
$$ E_{CoAP} = 832 \times 50.64 \text{ nJ} = 42{,}132.48 \text{ nJ} \approx 42.13 \text{ µJ} $$

**Step 4 — Energy per HTTP packet:**
$$ E_{HTTP} = 2400 \times 50.64 \text{ nJ} = 121{,}536 \text{ nJ} \approx 121.54 \text{ µJ} $$

**Step 5 — Number of packets in 1 hour:**
$$ N = \frac{3600}{30} = 120 \text{ packets} $$

**Step 6 — Total energy for CoAP:**
$$ E_{total}^{CoAP} = 120 \times 42.13 \text{ µJ} = 5{,}055.6 \text{ µJ} \approx 5.06 \text{ mJ} $$

**Step 7 — Total energy for HTTP:**
$$ E_{total}^{HTTP} = 120 \times 121.54 \text{ µJ} = 14{,}584.8 \text{ µJ} \approx 14.58 \text{ mJ} $$

**Step 8 — Energy saved:**
$$ \Delta E = 14.58 - 5.06 = 9.52 \text{ mJ} $$
$$ \eta = \left(1 - \frac{5.06}{14.58}\right) \times 100\% = 65.3\% $$

**Final Answer:**
$$ \boxed{E_{CoAP} \approx 5.06 \text{ mJ}, \quad E_{HTTP} \approx 14.58 \text{ mJ}, \quad \text{Savings} \approx 65.3\%} $$

**Conclusion:** CoAP reduces transmission energy by ≈ 65 % compared to HTTP for the same 100-byte payload. Over a node's lifetime, this translates to nearly 3× longer battery life for identical data delivery.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Module-3 Choice B)]**

**(a)** Discuss **in-network data aggregation** as an optimisation technique for web-over-wireless networks. How does it differ from **header compression**? Provide a numerical example showing energy savings using aggregation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **duty cycling** in the MAC layer of a WSN. A node has $P_{tx} = 50$ mW during transmission, $P_{sleep} = 1$ µW during sleep, a duty cycle of 1 %, and a total operational period of 24 hours. Calculate the **average power consumption** and the **total energy consumed per day**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) In-Network Aggregation (7 marks):**

**Definition:** In-network data aggregation is the process of combining data from multiple sensor nodes at intermediate nodes (cluster-heads, parents) so that the *number of bits transmitted toward the sink* is reduced. Common functions include MAX, MIN, AVG, SUM, and COUNT.

**Aggregation vs Header Compression:**

| Aspect | Aggregation | Header Compression |
|---|---|---|
| **Operates on** | Application data (payload) | Protocol headers |
| **Reduces** | Number of bits per data unit | Header bytes per packet |
| **Mechanism** | Merges values from multiple nodes | Encodes field values in fewer bits |
| **Example** | AVG(temperature) from 10 nodes | IPv6 40 B → 6LoWPAN 3 B |
| **Energy saving source** | Fewer packets | Smaller packets |
| **Loss of information** | Possible (if AVG is sent) | None |

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose 10 sensor nodes each report a temperature value (4 bytes). Without aggregation, 10 packets × (4 B payload + 20 B header) = 240 B. With aggregation at a cluster head, 1 packet is sent containing the *average* (4 B payload + 20 B header) = 24 B.

$$ \text{Compression ratio} = \frac{240}{24} = 10 $$

$$ \text{Energy ratio} \approx \frac{10}{1} = 10\times \text{saving} $$

If each bit costs 51 nJ (as derived earlier):
$$ E_{no\_agg} = 240 \times 8 \times 51 \text{ nJ} = 97{,}920 \text{ nJ} \approx 97.9 \text{ µJ} $$
$$ E_{agg} = 24 \times 8 \times 51 \text{ nJ} = 9{,}792 \text{ nJ} \approx 9.8 \text{ µJ} $$
$$ \text{Saving} = 90 \% $$

**Final Answer:** Aggregation reduces the energy spent in this example by **≈ 90 %** by sending one combined packet instead of ten.

---

**(b) Duty Cycling Calculation (7 marks):**

**Duty Cycle Definition:** Duty cycling is the technique of switching the radio between active (TX/RX) and sleep states to minimise idle listening. The duty cycle DC is the fraction of time the radio is active.

**Given:**
- $P_{tx} = 50$ mW = 50 × 10⁻³ W
- $P_{sleep} = 1$ µW = 1 × 10⁻⁶ W
- DC = 1 % = 0.01
- T = 24 hours

**Step 1 — Average power using the duty-cycle energy formula:**
$$ P_{avg} = DC \cdot P_{tx} + (1 - DC) \cdot P_{sleep} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$ P_{avg} = 0.01 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} + 0.99 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} $$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**
$$ 0.01 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 500 \text{ µW} $$
$$ 0.99 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.99 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 0.99 \text{ µW} $$

**Step 4 — Sum them up:**
$$ P_{avg} = 500 \text{ µW} + 0.99 \text{ µW} \approx 500.99 \text{ µW} \approx 501 \text{ µW} $$

**Step 5 — Total energy in 24 hours:**
$$ T = 24 \text{ h} = 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 86{,}400 \text{ s} $$

$$ E_{total} = P_{avg} \times T = 501 \times 10^{-6} \times 86{,}400 $$

$$ E_{total} = 501 \times 86.4 \text{ J} = 43{,}286.4 \text{ J} \times 10^{-3} = 43.29 \text{ J} $$

**Final Answer:**
$$ \boxed{P_{avg} \approx 501 \text{ µW}, \quad E_{total} \approx 43.3 \text{ J/day}} $$

**Interpretation:** Without duty cycling (i.e., DC = 100 %), the node would consume $P_{avg} = 50$ mW continuously, i.e., $E = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 86{,}400 = 4{,}320$ J/day — **100× more energy**. Duty cycling therefore extends battery life by approximately two orders of magnitude.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Optimising Web Over Wireless" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the receiver's energy** — Many students compute only $E_{tx}$ for a packet exchange. A full WSN packet exchange also incurs $E_{rx}$ at the destination. **Always** include both, unless the question explicitly says "transmit energy only".
>
> 2. **Using the d⁴ model for short distances** — Applying $E_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}$ when d < d₀ (≈ 87.7 m) is a frequent error. The free-space d² model applies for indoor/short-range WSNs.
>
> 3. **Mixing HTTP and CoAP in the same calculation** — When comparing, keep the *payload identical* and vary only the *header*; otherwise the comparison is invalid.
>
> 4. **Ignoring ACK costs** — In duty-cycled 802.15.4 networks, the ACK frame costs an additional 5–11 B transmission *and* a wake-up. A full round-trip therefore includes TX-data + RX-ACK.
>
> 5. **Confusing compression ratio with savings** — CR = 7× does NOT mean 7× energy savings; the radio startup, MAC overhead, and ACK cost are all fixed terms that remain unchanged.
>
> 6. **Wrong duty-cycle formula** — Some students use $P_{avg} = DC \times P_{tx}$ alone, omitting the sleep term. Although $P_{sleep}$ is small (1 µW), the *correct* formula is $P_{avg} = DC \cdot P_{tx} + (1-DC) \cdot P_{sleep}$. Examiners allocate marks for the full form.
>
> 7. **Not stating TCP-throughput assumptions** — The square-root formula assumes TCP-Reno with loss-based cwnd reduction. State the assumption explicitly to avoid losing the "methodology" mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Optimising Web Over Wireless)**

- **The Core Idea:** Replace verbose, energy-hungry web protocols (HTTP/TCP/IPv6) with their lightweight, binary, UDP-based counterparts (CoAP/UDP/6LoWPAN) to fit them into 802.15.4 frames.

- **The Four Optimisation Layers:**
  1. **Application:** HTTP → **CoAP** (4 B header) or **MQTT-SN** (2 B topic ID). XML/JSON → **CBOR/SenML/EXI**.
  2. **Transport:** TCP → **UDP + CoAP confirmable messages** OR **Split-TCP at gateway**.
  3. **Network:** IPv6 (40 B) → **6LoWPAN** (2–4 B); RPL for routing.
  4. **MAC/Radio:** Continuous RX → **Duty cycling** (B-MAC, S-MAC, ContikiMAC).

- **Energy Model Formulas (must memorise):**
  - $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ with $n=2$ (short) or $n=4$ (long).
  - $E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$.
  - $d_{0} = \sqrt{E_{fs}/E_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m.
  - Network lifetime $L = E_{initial}/E_{round}$.

- **Key Constants:**
  - $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $E_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴.
  - IEEE 802.15.4 frame = **127 bytes**.
  - IPv6 header = 40 B → 6LoWPAN ≈ 3 B.
  - HTTP header ≈ 200–800 B → CoAP header = 4 B.

- **Architectural Role of the Gateway/6LBR:**
  - Performs **header compression**, **protocol translation** (HTTP ↔ CoAP), **fragmentation/reassembly**, and **RPL root routing**.

- **Aggregation vs Compression:**
  - **Compression** reduces the *size* of each packet (operates on headers/data encoding).
  - **Aggregation** reduces the *number* of packets (operates on data content across nodes).

- **TCP over Wireless Pitfall:**
  - Use $T \approx MSS / (RTT \cdot \sqrt{p})$ for lossy links, not the bandwidth-delay product alone.
  - Prefer **split-TCP** at the gateway to avoid multi-hop TCP retransmission storms.

- **Duty Cycling Formula:**
  - $P_{avg} = DC \cdot P_{tx} + (1-DC) \cdot P_{sleep}$.
  - A 1 % duty cycle yields ~100× energy reduction compared to always-on.

- **CoAP vs HTTP Quick Numbers:**
  - CoAP saves ≈ **65–90 %** of header energy for typical sensor payloads.
  - CoAP runs over UDP on port 5683 (vs TCP 80 for HTTP).

- **Real-World Standards (must know):**
  - **6LoWPAN** (RFC 4944, RFC 6282) — Header compression for IPv6 over 802.15.4.
  - **RPL** (RFC 6550) — Routing protocol for LLNs.
  - **CoAP** (RFC 7252) — Constrained Application Protocol.
  - **MQTT-SN** (OASIS) — MQTT for Sensor Networks.
  - **SenML** (RFC 8428) — Sensor measurement data format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WSN Architecture - Single Node Architecture - Hardware Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** WSN Architecture - Single Node Architecture - Hardware Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) — Formal Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes** (or motes) that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions such as temperature, sound, pressure, humidity, vibration, or pollutants, and transmit the collected data through wireless links to a central **sink** (Base Station) for further processing and analysis.

A **Single Sensor Node** (also known as a **mote**) is the fundamental building block of any WSN. It is a small, resource-constrained, battery-powered embedded device capable of sensing, computation, and wireless communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** A sensor node is a fusion of four essential subsystems — **Sensing**, **Processing**, **Communication (Transceiver)**, and **Power** — and the architectural choices made in each block directly determine the lifetime, accuracy, and scalability of the entire WSN.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body Cell"

Think of a single sensor node as a **human nerve cell (neuron)**:

- **Sensors** → Your five senses (skin, eyes, ears). They gather raw information from the environment.
- **ADC + Microcontroller** → Your brain's sensory cortex. It converts and interprets the raw signals.
- **Transceiver** → Your nervous system & vocal cords. It sends messages to other neurons or the brain (sink).
- **Power Unit (Battery)** → The food/oxygen supply. Without it, the entire cell dies.

Just as millions of neurons cooperate to form intelligent behaviour, thousands of sensor motes cooperate to form a smart, context-aware WSN.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of a Sensor Node

| Subsystem | Primary Function | KTU-Mandated Terminology |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Sensing Unit** | Captures physical phenomena from environment | **Sensor + ADC + Signal Conditioning** |
| **Processing Unit** | Local data processing, decision making | **Microcontroller / Processor + Memory** |
| **Transceiver Unit** | Wireless data transmission & reception | **Radio + Antenna + Modulator** |
| **Power Unit** | Energy supply to all other units | **Battery + DC-DC Converter + Energy Harvester** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The most widely used commercial WSN mote platforms (e.g., **MICAz, IRIS, TelosB**) operate on **2.4 GHz ISM band** with data rates between **250 kbps** and **250 kbps (IEEE 802.15.4)** and have RAM sizes typically between **2 kB to 10 kB**.

## 1.4 Design Constraints of a Sensor Node

1. **Limited Energy** — Battery powered, often non-rechargeable.
2. **Limited Memory** — Few kB of RAM, hundreds of kB of flash.
3. **Limited Computational Speed** — Low MHz microcontrollers.
4. **Limited Communication Bandwidth** — kbps range, not Mbps.
5. **Harsh Environmental Deployment** — Must survive temperature, dust, humidity.
6. **Small Physical Size** — Must be unobtrusive and low-weight.
7. **Low Production Cost** — Required for mass deployment in thousands.
8. **Self-Organization & Autonomy** — Must operate unattended for years.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Subsystem Architecture

A sensor node architecture is structured into the following functional units:

### A. Sensing Unit (The "Eyes & Ears")
The sensing unit comprises three sub-blocks:

1. **Sensor (Transducer):** Converts physical phenomena (light, temperature, pressure, chemical concentration, vibration, etc.) into an **analog electrical signal** (voltage or current).
2. **Signal Conditioning Circuit:** Amplifies weak signals and filters noise using operational amplifiers and active/passive filters.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Converts the conditioned analog signal into a discrete digital value that the processor can interpret.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADC Resolution Formula:** For an *n*-bit ADC with reference voltage *V*<sub>ref</sub>:
> 
> $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$
> 
> A 10-bit ADC with 3.3V reference has resolution ≈ **3.22 mV** — critical for sensor precision calculations.

### B. Processing Unit (The "Brain")
- **Microcontroller (MCU) / Microprocessor**: Executes the sensing, communication, and self-management protocols.
- **Memory Subsystem:**
  - **Flash/ROM** — Stores program code (typically 32 kB to 1 MB).
  - **RAM** — Stores runtime data and stack (typically 2 kB to 32 kB).
  - **EEPROM** — Stores persistent configuration and calibration data.
- **Optional Co-processors** (e.g., DSP, crypto accelerators) for energy-efficient specialized tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Popular Embedded OS for WSN:** **TinyOS**, **Contiki OS**, **RIOT OS**, **FreeRTOS**. These are event-driven or lightweight to fit the resource constraints.

### C. Transceiver Unit (The "Voice & Ears")
The transceiver handles the wireless transmission and reception. It is one of the **largest power consumers** in a sensor node.

- **Operational States:** Transmit, Receive, Idle, Sleep.
- **Key Parameters:** Frequency band, modulation scheme, data rate, transmission power, receiver sensitivity.
- **Common Standards:** IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee, 6LoWPAN), Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), LoRa, Wi-Fi (rarely due to power), sub-GHz ISM bands (433/868/915 MHz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power-Amplifier Energy Model (Free Space):**
> 
> $$E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^2$$
> 
> where $E_{elec}$ = per-bit electronics energy, $E_{amp}$ = amplifier energy, $k$ = bits, $d$ = distance. This shows energy scales with **d²** — a critical KTU design factor.

### D. Power Unit (The "Heart")
- **Primary Battery:** Alkaline, Lithium, NiMH — the most common source.
- **DC-DC Converter:** Regulates voltage to stable levels required by MCU and radio.
- **Power Management Circuit:** Switches subsystems ON/OFF or into sleep mode.
- **Optional Energy Harvester:** Solar cells, piezoelectric, thermal gradient, RF harvesting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Energy Lifetime Equation (KTU Favourite):**
> 
> $$T_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{battery}}{I_{avg} \cdot V_{supply}}$$
> 
> where $I_{avg}$ is the **average current** considering duty cycle. This equation is the basis of nearly every WSN lifetime problem in KTU exams.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | ADC step size | Volts |
| 2 | $E_{Tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^2$ | Transmission energy | Joules |
| 3 | $E_{Rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Reception energy | Joules |
| 4 | $E_{total} = N \cdot (E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} + E_{sense} + E_{proc})$ | Total node energy | Joules |
| 5 | $T_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{battery}}{I_{avg} \cdot V}$ | Node operational lifetime | Seconds/Hours |
| 6 | $P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2 \cdot L}$ | Friis free-space path loss | Watts |
| 7 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}} \times 100\%$ | Active time ratio | Percent |
| 8 | $I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot \text{DC} + I_{sleep} \cdot (1-\text{DC})$ | Average current draw | Amperes |
| 9 | $C = Q / V$ | Battery capacity | Ampere-hour (Ah) |
| 10 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The above formulas are used in the design of **smart agriculture** (soil moisture sensing), **structural health monitoring** (vibration-based bridge monitoring), **military surveillance** (acoustic/thermal sensing), and **environmental monitoring** (volcanic or forest-fire detection). Reducing $E_{amp}$ via efficient modulation (e.g., **OOK vs BPSK**) is a major industrial R&D focus.

## 2.3 Why Each Component Matters — The "Why & How"

- **Why an ADC?** Sensors output analog; MCUs process digital. **How?** ADC samples at Nyquist rate ≥ 2 × max signal frequency.
- **Why a microcontroller over a microprocessor?** Lower power, integrated peripherals (timers, ADC, UART), and deterministic real-time response.
- **Why a separate transceiver?** Off-the-shelf radios (e.g., CC2420) provide robust modulation, error correction, and MAC features impossible in pure analog circuits.
- **Why energy harvesting?** Replaces/replenishes battery, extending lifetime from months to **years/decades**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sensor Node Energy Consumption Model

A sensor node spends energy in three core activities: **sensing**, **processing**, and **communication**. Let us derive the total energy equation.

### Step 1: Define per-bit electronics energy
For every bit transmitted or received, the radio electronics consume a fixed amount of energy:
$$E_{elec} = \text{energy per bit (J/bit)}$$

### Step 2: Define amplifier energy
To transmit a signal over a distance *d*, the power amplifier must expend energy proportional to the path loss. In free space, this is $d^2$ dependent:
$$E_{amp} = \text{amplifier energy factor (J/bit/m}^2\text{)}$$

### Step 3: Total transmission energy
For transmitting *k* bits over distance *d*:
$$E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^2$$

**Explanation:** The first term accounts for digital circuitry energy, the second for radiated signal power needed to overcome path loss.

### Step 4: Total reception energy
Reception requires no amplification, only electronics:
$$E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$

### Step 5: Sensing and processing energy
$$E_{sense} = k \cdot E_{sense\_per\_bit}, \quad E_{proc} = k \cdot E_{proc\_per\_bit}$$

### Step 6: Total node energy per cycle
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cycle} &= E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} + E_{sense} + E_{proc} \\
&= k \cdot \big[ 2E_{elec} + E_{amp} d^2 + E_{sense} + E_{proc} \big]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: For *N* reporting cycles in time *T*
$$E_{total} = N \cdot E_{cycle}$$

### Step 8: Lifetime derivation
Given battery capacity $C$ (in Ah) and supply voltage *V*:
$$E_{battery} = C \cdot V \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

Therefore:
$$T_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{battery}}{E_{cycle} / T_{cycle}} = \frac{E_{battery} \cdot T_{cycle}}{k \cdot [2E_{elec} + E_{amp} d^2 + E_{sense} + E_{proc}]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the transmission distance **quadruples** the amplifier energy. Hence multi-hop routing is preferred over long single-hop links — a core principle of WSN protocol design (LEACH, PEGASIS).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Resolution Bound on Sensor Accuracy

### Step 1: Maximum analog input
For an *n*-bit ADC with reference $V_{ref}$:
$$V_{in,max} = V_{ref}$$

### Step 2: Number of quantization levels
$$L = 2^n$$

### Step 3: Resolution (step size)
$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

### Step 4: Quantization error bound
Theoretical maximum quantization error:
$$e_{q,max} = \frac{Q}{2} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$$

### Step 5: Application example
Suppose temperature sensor outputs $10 \text{ mV/°C}$ and ADC is 10-bit with $V_{ref} = 3.3V$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{3.3}{2^{10}} = \frac{3.3}{1024} \approx 3.22 \text{ mV} \\
\text{Temperature resolution} &= \frac{3.22 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} \approx 0.322 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** A 10-bit ADC limits the **smallest distinguishable temperature** to about 0.32 °C. For finer resolution (e.g., medical sensing), 12-bit or 16-bit ADCs are used.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Trade-offs

| Subsystem | Design Goal | Trade-off | KTU-Standard Resolution |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|-------------------------|
| Sensor | High accuracy, low power | Sensitivity vs. power consumption | Choose sensor class (e.g., MEMS, electrochemical) |
| ADC | High resolution, low sampling rate | Bits (n) vs. power & cost | 10–16 bits typical |
| MCU | Low power, sufficient processing | MIPS vs. current draw | 8–32-bit ARM Cortex-M |
| Transceiver | Long range, low BER | Range vs. data rate vs. power | 802.15.4 / BLE / LoRa |
| Battery | High energy density | Capacity vs. size & cost | 600–2400 mAh Li-ion |
| Harvester | Continuous energy | Harvester output vs. environmental availability | µW to mW scale |

## 3.4 Typical Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A sensor node uses a 3.6 V, 2400 mAh battery. Its active current is 25 mA, sleep current 10 µA, and duty cycle is 1%. Find the node lifetime.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute average current using duty cycle.
$$I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot DC + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)$$
$$I_{avg} = 25 \text{ mA} \times 0.01 + 0.01 \text{ mA} \times 0.99$$
$$I_{avg} = 0.25 + 0.0099 = 0.2599 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2:** Compute battery energy.
$$E_{battery} = C \cdot V = 2400 \text{ mAh} \times 3.6 \text{ V} = 8640 \text{ mWh} = 8.64 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 3:** Compute lifetime.
$$T_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{battery}}{V \cdot I_{avg}} = \frac{8640 \text{ mWh}}{3.6 \text{ V} \times 0.2599 \text{ mA}} = \frac{8640}{0.9356} \approx 9234 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to days.
$$T_{lifetime} \approx \frac{9234}{24} \approx 384.75 \text{ days} \approx 1.05 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** By reducing the duty cycle from 1% to 0.1%, the lifetime scales to **~3.5 years** — illustrating why duty cycling is the most powerful tool in WSN energy management.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Sensor Node — Block-Level Functional Architecture

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**List and briefly explain the four major hardware components of a sensor node in a WSN.**

**Model Answer:**

A WSN sensor node is composed of four major hardware subsystems:

1. **Sensing Unit** — Comprises physical sensors (temperature, humidity, pressure, etc.), signal conditioning circuits, and an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). It converts physical stimuli into digital samples.
2. **Processing Unit** — Built around a microcontroller/microprocessor along with memory (Flash, RAM, EEPROM). It performs local data processing, aggregation, and protocol execution.
3. **Transceiver Unit** — Contains a radio modem and antenna for wireless communication. Handles packet transmission, reception, modulation, and MAC protocol compliance (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4).
4. **Power Unit** — Battery (primary energy source), DC-DC regulator, and optional energy harvester. Includes a Power Management Unit (PMU) that enables sleep modes to extend lifetime.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What are the major design constraints of a wireless sensor node?**

**Model Answer:**

The major design constraints of a sensor node include:

1. **Limited energy** — Battery-powered, often non-replaceable, hence must be energy-efficient.
2. **Limited memory and processing** — Few kB of RAM and low MHz clock speeds.
3. **Limited communication bandwidth** — Operating in the kbps range (e.g., 250 kbps for IEEE 802.15.4).
4. **Harsh deployment environments** — Must tolerate temperature, dust, moisture, vibration.
5. **Small form factor and low cost** — Required for unobtrusive, mass deployment.
6. **Self-organization and fault tolerance** — Must operate unattended and recover from node failures.
7. **Security and privacy** — Must protect data integrity in hostile environments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question. Two alternative choices are provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a single sensor node** in a Wireless Sensor Network. Describe the role of each subsystem in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **energy consumption model** for a sensor node's transmission and reception of *k* bits over distance *d*. Discuss how duty cycling extends the node's lifetime. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A sensor node, also called a **mote**, is the fundamental building block of a WSN. It integrates sensing, computation, communication, and power into a single embedded device.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for architecture.)*

**Subsystem Description (4 marks):**

- **Sensing Unit:** Contains physical sensors that convert environmental stimuli (light, temperature, gas, vibration) into analog electrical signals. These are filtered, amplified, and digitized by an **ADC** with resolution $V_{ref}/2^n$. For example, a **MEMS accelerometer** senses structural vibration.
- **Processing Unit:** A microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, ATmega128) executes the operating system (TinyOS, Contiki) and protocols. It performs local aggregation, thresholding, and encryption. Memory hierarchy: Flash (program), RAM (runtime), EEPROM (configuration).
- **Transceiver Unit:** Uses radios like **CC2420** (IEEE 802.15.4 compliant). Operates in 2.4 GHz ISM band. Includes power amplifier, modulator/demodulator, and antenna. Power states: **Tx, Rx, Idle, Sleep**.
- **Power Unit:** A 3.6 V, 2400 mAh lithium battery coupled with a DC-DC converter. May include a **solar/thermal harvester**. The PMU controls duty cycling.

#### Model Solution — (b):

**Step 1:** Define per-bit energy of radio electronics: $E_{elec}$ (J/bit).
**Step 2:** Define amplifier energy factor: $E_{amp}$ (J/bit/m²).
**Step 3:** Derive transmission energy:
$$E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^2$$
**Step 4:** Derive reception energy (no amplifier needed):
$$E_{Rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$$
**Step 5:** Total node energy per cycle:
$$E_{cycle} = k[2E_{elec} + E_{amp} d^2 + E_{sense} + E_{proc}]$$
**Step 6:** Duty cycle average current:
$$I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot DC + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)$$
**Step 7:** Lifetime:
$$T_{lifetime} = \frac{C \cdot V}{I_{avg} \cdot V} = \frac{C}{I_{avg}}$$
**Step 8:** Numerical example: 2400 mAh battery, 25 mA active, 10 µA sleep, DC = 1% → lifetime ≈ 1.05 years. Reducing DC to 0.1% extends it to ~3.5 years.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to **square the distance** in the transmission energy equation. Forgetting $d^2$ will cost **2–3 marks** immediately. Also, do not confuse $E_{Rx}$ (no amplifier term) with $E_{Tx}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **sensing unit** of a WSN node in detail. Discuss the role of ADC and derive its resolution formula. Calculate the temperature resolution for a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3V$ and a sensor sensitivity of $10 \text{ mV/°C}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **power unit** and **power management techniques** in a WSN node. Compare battery-only vs. energy harvesting-based designs with examples. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a):

**Sensing Unit Overview (2 marks):**
The sensing unit consists of three sub-blocks: (i) physical sensor (transducer), (ii) signal conditioning circuit, (iii) Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). It transforms environmental phenomena into digital data.

**Role of ADC (2 marks):**
The ADC quantizes the analog sensor output into digital codes. Resolution is the smallest distinguishable voltage step.

**Derivation of Resolution (2 marks):**
For an *n*-bit ADC with reference $V_{ref}$:
$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Numerical Calculation (1 mark):**
For 12-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 3.3V$:
$$Q = \frac{3.3}{4096} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV}$$
Temperature resolution = $0.806 \text{ mV} / (10 \text{ mV/°C}) = 0.0806 \text{ °C} \approx 0.08 \text{ °C}$

#### Model Solution — (b):

**Power Unit (3 marks):**
The power unit supplies regulated DC voltage to all subsystems. It includes a battery, DC-DC converter, and Power Management Unit (PMU). Most motes use 2×AA alkaline (3.0 V) or Li-ion/LiPo (3.6–4.2 V). The PMU enables dynamic voltage scaling and duty cycling.

**Power Management Techniques (2 marks):**
- **Duty Cycling** — Sleep/active scheduling.
- **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)** — Lower voltage when load is light.
- **Power Gating** — Disconnect unused peripherals.
- **Wake-up Radios** — Ultra-low-power receivers for event detection.

**Battery vs. Harvesting Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Battery-Only | Energy Harvesting |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| Lifetime | Limited (months–years) | Potentially perpetual |
| Maintenance | Periodic replacement | Minimal |
| Cost | Low initial, high TCO | Higher initial, low TCO |
| Examples | MicaZ, TelosB | Solar-powered motes, Piezo-bridges |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In numerical ADC problems, students often forget to convert the sensor sensitivity (e.g., mV/°C) into matching units with the ADC step (V). Always **unit-check first** or you will lose the final 1 mark for the numerical answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **sensor node** has **four subsystems**: Sensing, Processing, Transceiver, Power.
- [ ] **Sensors** convert physical phenomena → analog signals → digital via **ADC**.
- [ ] **ADC Resolution** $= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ — remember to convert units in numericals.
- [ ] **Processing unit** = **Microcontroller + Memory + Embedded OS** (TinyOS/Contiki/RIOT).
- [ ] **Transceiver** is the **largest power consumer**; uses **IEEE 802.15.4** in most cases.
- [ ] **Transmission energy** $= E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^2$ — note the **d²** term.
- [ ] **Reception energy** $= E_{elec} \cdot k$ — **no amplifier** term.
- [ ] **Power unit** includes **battery + DC-DC + PMU + optional harvester**.
- [ ] **Duty cycling** is the **single most effective** lifetime extension technique.
- [ ] **Average current** $= I_{active} \cdot DC + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)$.
- [ ] **Lifetime** $= \frac{\text{Battery Capacity (mAh)}}{\text{Average Current (mA)}}$.
- [ ] Popular motes: **MICAz, IRIS, TelosB, Mica2** — all use 2.4 GHz or sub-GHz ISM band.
- [ ] Design constraints: **energy, memory, bandwidth, size, cost, autonomy, environment**.
- [ ] Always **redraw the block diagram** in 7-mark hardware questions for easy marks.
- [ ] When computing energy, ensure units are consistent: **Joules = Watts × Seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Most valuation key sheets allocate 2 marks for a **correctly drawn block diagram** of the sensor node. Always start a hardware question with a labelled diagram — even if the question only asks for an explanation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Consumption of Sensor Nodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Consumption of Sensor Nodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Energy Consumption of Sensor Nodes — Section 1: Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)**, the **Energy Consumption of a Sensor Node** is formally defined as the *total quantum of electrical energy expended by a single sensor mote (node) over a defined operational period, encompassing the aggregate power drawn by its sensing, processing, communication, and idle subsystems, normalized over the battery's stored capacity*.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a sensor node's energy budget is partitioned into **four canonical subsystems**, and the total energy consumption $E_{total}$ is mathematically abstracted as:

$$E_{total} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{comm} + E_{idle/sleep}$$

where each term represents the energy drawn by the **sensing transducer interface**, the **embedded processor/memory**, the **radio transceiver (transmit + receive + listen)**, and the **idle/sleep housekeeping circuits**, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The dominant energy consumer in a typical WSN mote is **NOT** the sensor or the CPU — it is the **radio transceiver**, which alone accounts for **~70% to 80%** of the total node energy budget. This is a frequently tested "Concept" question in KTU Part A.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a small **village post office** in a remote, off-grid Himalayan town that runs on a single solar-charged battery.

| Subsystem | Real-World Analogy | Energy Drain Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | The postmaster reading/scanning every letter that arrives | Small pen-light bulb (low current) |
| **Processing** | The postmaster stamping, sorting, and filing letters | Office desk lamp (medium current) |
| **Communication** | Dispatching a motorcycle to the next village post office | Headlight + motorcycle engine (HIGH current) |
| **Idle/Sleep** | The postmaster dozing in his chair between deliveries | A small pilot LED (minimal current) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Motorcycle" Insight:** Whenever the postmaster sends a letter (data packet) far away, he must use a *bigger, more powerful motorcycle* (more amplifier power). The further the distance, the *quadratically worse* the fuel consumption becomes. This is the heart of the **first-order radio model** in WSNs — energy scales steeply with transmission distance.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following energy constants are **standard KTU board-exam values** (Heinzelman et al., 2000 — *the foundational WSN energy model*). Memorize them verbatim:

- **$E_{elec}$ = 50 nJ/bit** — Energy dissipated by the transmitter/receiver electronics (per bit)
- **$\varepsilon_{fs}$ = 100 pJ/bit/m²** — Free-space amplifier energy (used for short distances, $d^2$ path loss)
- **$\varepsilon_{mp}$ = 0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** — Multipath amplifier energy (used for long distances, $d^4$ path loss)
- **$E_{DA}$ = 5 nJ/bit/report** — Data aggregation energy (per bit, per report)
- **$d_0$ = 87.7 m** — Crossover distance (free-space vs. multipath threshold)
- **$V_{supply}$ = 3.0 V** — Typical battery voltage
- **$I_{tx,max}$ = 15–20 mA** — Maximum transmit current (e.g., CC2420 radio)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST MEMORIZE:** The crossover distance $d_0$ is derived as $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$. Plugging in the standard values: $d_0 = \sqrt{100 \times 10^{-12} / 0.0013 \times 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{76.92} \approx \mathbf{87.7 \text{ m}}$.

---

## 1.4 Why Energy Consumption is the *Central Design Axis* in WSNs

A WSN deploys **hundreds to thousands of battery-powered motes** in hostile, often inaccessible terrain (volcanoes, oceans, battlefields, agricultural fields). Battery replacement is **economically infeasible** and **physically impossible**. Therefore, *every single algorithm, protocol, and hardware choice* in WSNs is subordinated to the cardinal dictum:

> **"Minimize energy consumption to maximize network lifetime."**

This is why energy-aware MAC protocols (S-MAC, B-MAC), energy-aware routing (LEACH, PEGASIS), and energy-aware clustering are the *pillars of WSN research* — and the *favourite question topics of KTU examiners*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Energy Subsystems — Decomposition Logic

Let us systematically decompose each subsystem's energy draw, with engineering intuition attached to every line.

### 🔹 (A) Sensing Energy — $E_{sense}$

The energy required to activate the transducer, sample the physical phenomenon, perform analog-to-digital conversion (ADC), and buffer the sample.

$$E_{sense} = V_{supply} \cdot I_{sensor} \cdot t_{sample}$$

- **Why it matters:** Cheap sensors (temperature, humidity) draw microamps. Expensive sensors (cameras, gas chromatographs) draw milliamps — drastically altering the energy budget.
- **Optimization:** Duty-cycle the sensor (sample 1% of the time), use passive sensors where possible.

### 🔹 (B) Processing Energy — $E_{proc}$

The energy consumed by the microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M) executing instructions, accessing memory, and performing computations.

$$E_{proc} = V_{supply} \cdot I_{active} \cdot t_{active} + V_{supply} \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}$$

- **Typical $I_{active}$:** 1–5 mA (at 1–16 MHz clock).
- **Typical $I_{sleep}$:** < 5 μA.
- **Optimization:** Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS), aggressive sleep modes.

### 🔹 (C) Communication Energy — $E_{comm}$ (THE DOMINANT SUBSYSTEM)

The energy consumed by the radio transceiver in **transmit**, **receive**, **listen (idle)**, and **sleep** states. This is the centerpiece of the first-order radio model:

$$
E_{comm} = E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} + E_{listen} + E_{sleep}
$$

The **transmit energy** for a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$ is:

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) =
\begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} & \text{if } d < d_0 \quad \text{(free-space model)} \\
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} & \text{if } d \geq d_0 \quad \text{(multipath model)}
\end{cases}
$$

The **receive energy** is:

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

The **idle/listen energy** is:

$$
E_{idle} = V_{supply} \cdot I_{idle} \cdot t_{idle}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two path-loss regimes?** In free space, RF energy spreads as a *sphere* ($d^2$ loss). In cluttered multipath environments, reflections, scattering, and absorption impose a *steeper quartic loss* ($d^4$). For short, line-of-sight WSN links, $d^2$ suffices; for longer, NLOS links, $d^4$ is mandatory.

### 🔹 (D) Idle/Sleep Energy — $E_{idle/sleep}$

When the radio is "off" but the CPU is running low-power housekeeping, the node draws a small baseline current. Sleep states can be as low as **< 1 μA**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) problems on this topic. **Print it. Memorize it.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transmit Energy (short range, $d < d_0$)** | $E_{Tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$ | Joules (J) | Free-space path loss regime |
| 2 | **Transmit Energy (long range, $d \geq d_0$)** | $E_{Tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}$ | Joules (J) | Multipath path loss regime |
| 3 | **Receive Energy** | $E_{Rx} = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Joules (J) | Receiver electronics only (no amplifier) |
| 4 | **Total Link Energy (single hop)** | $E_{link} = E_{Tx}(k,d) + E_{Rx}(k) = 2kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{amp}d^{n}$ | Joules (J) | One transmit + one receive |
| 5 | **Crossover Distance** | $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}}$ | meters (m) | Threshold for regime switch |
| 6 | **Optimal Cluster Radius (LEACH)** | $d_{toBS} = 0.765 \cdot R$ | meters (m) | Optimal BS-to-centroid distance in LEACH |
| 7 | **Total Energy per Round (LEACH)** | $E_{round} = L \cdot (2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} + k \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2})$ | Joules (J) | Energy for one LEACH round |
| 8 | **Network Lifetime (definition)** | $T_{net} = \min_{i}(E_{0} / E_i)$ | rounds | Time until first node dies (FND metric) |
| 9 | **Sensing Energy** | $E_{sense} = V \cdot I_{sensor} \cdot t_{sample}$ | Joules (J) | Transducer + ADC energy |
| 10 | **Processing Energy** | $E_{proc} = V \cdot I_{active} \cdot t_{active}$ | Joules (J) | MCU active energy |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all KTU answers, never use vertical bars for absolute value or conditional boundaries inside equation rendering — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid LaTeX ambiguity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil-moisture motes on solar micro-cells must last 5+ years. Energy models predict battery lifetime and inform *duty-cycle selection*.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Bridges fitted with vibration sensors — designers use the $d^2$/$d^4$ model to *decide multi-hop vs. single-hop* topology.
- **Underwater Acoustic Networks (UANs):** Here, the $d^2$/$d^4$ model is *replaced* by an absorption model $\alpha(f)$ in dB/km, because acoustic channels in water are *far more lossy* than RF.
- **Military Battlefield Surveillance:** Energy models are coupled with **Poisson-distributed** enemy detection events to dynamically schedule sleep/wake cycles.
- **Body Area Networks (BANs):** Energy models must include **IEEE 802.15.6 SAR limits**, coupling RF exposure constraints to power-budget calculations.
