# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reader's Mandate:** Every single algebraic transition below is **fully written out**. No shortcuts. No "..." placeholders. This is KTU-board-exam-grade rigor.

---

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The First-Order Radio Model (Heinzelman, 2000)

### 🔸 Step 1: Define the Power-Amplifier Loss Model

In a free-space RF channel, the transmitted power $P_t$ attenuates as the *inverse square* of the distance $d$ between isotropic antennas. The received power is:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot d^{2}}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the carrier wavelength, $G_t$ and $G_r$ are antenna gains.

### 🔸 Step 2: Convert to Energy per Bit

The transmit amplifier must supply enough power to overcome this path loss. The energy dissipated in the amplifier to transmit **one bit** over distance $d$ (free space) is:

$$
E_{amp,\,bit}^{fs} = \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}
$$

Multiplying by the packet length $k$ bits:

$$
E_{amp,\,packet}^{fs} = k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}
$$

### 🔸 Step 3: Add the Transmitter Electronics Energy

The digital baseband circuitry (modulator, filter, DAC) consumes an additional $E_{elec}$ per bit. For a $k$-bit packet:

$$
E_{elec,\,packet} = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

### 🔸 Step 4: Combine for Total Transmit Energy (Free-Space Regime)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Tx}^{fs}(k, d) &= E_{elec,\,packet} + E_{amp,\,packet}^{fs} \\
&= k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} \\
&= k \left( E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🔸 Step 5: Repeat for Multipath Regime (Long Range, $d \geq d_0$)

For a cluttered, multipath environment, path loss is proportional to $d^{4}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Tx}^{mp}(k, d) &= k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \\
&= k \left( E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🔸 Step 6: Receive Energy (No Amplifier in Receiver)

A receiver does not have a power amplifier. It only consumes $E_{elec}$ per bit in the demodulation/filtering chain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Rx}(k) &= k \cdot E_{elec}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🔸 Step 7: Compact Two-Regime Final Expression

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) =
\begin{cases}
k \left( E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} \right) & \text{if } d < d_0 \\
k \left( E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \right) & \text{if } d \geq d_0
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — The Crossover Distance $d_0$

### 🔸 Step 1: Equate the Two-Regime Transmit Energies

At the crossover distance, both the free-space and multipath models predict *identical* energy consumption (the transition is continuous):

$$
E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}
$$

### 🔸 Step 2: Cancel Common Terms

The $E_{elec}$ term cancels on both sides:

$$
\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}
$$

### 🔸 Step 3: Divide Both Sides by $d_0^{2}$

$$
\varepsilon_{fs} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{2}
$$

### 🔸 Step 4: Solve for $d_0$

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

### 🔸 Step 5: Numerical Evaluation with Standard Constants

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_0 &= \sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-12} \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-12}}{1.3 \times 10^{-15}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{100}{1.3} \times 10^{3}} \\
&= \sqrt{76.923 \times 10^{3}} \\
&= \sqrt{76923.08} \\
&\approx 277.35 \text{ m (in raw pJ form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — let me correct the unit alignment.**

The textbook standard is $E_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² and $E_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴. With these:

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}}{0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}}} = \sqrt{7692.3 \text{ m}^{2}} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact value 87.7 m is **standard** in Heinzelman's LEACH paper. Always quote **87 m** or **87.7 m** in KTU board answers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Total Energy Per Round in LEACH

LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) is the most-tested clustering protocol in KTU. We derive the energy per round.

### 🔸 Step 1: Define LEACH Assumptions

- $N$ nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region.
- $k$ clusters per round; thus $N/k$ nodes per cluster.
- Each cluster head (CH) aggregates $N/k$ frames of $L$ bits each into one $L$-bit frame.

### 🔸 Step 2: Energy for a Non-CH Node to Send to Its CH

Each non-CH transmits $L$ bits to its CH at average distance $d_{toCH}$. Using the free-space model:

$$
E_{nonCH} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}
$$

### 🔸 Step 3: Energy for a CH Node

A CH performs three tasks:
1. **Receives** $N/k - 1$ frames of $L$ bits each from member nodes.
2. **Aggregates** the data (data aggregation energy $E_{DA}$).
3. **Transmits** one aggregated $L$-bit frame to the base station at distance $d_{toBS}$ (uses multipath model).

$$
E_{CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) L E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} L E_{DA} + L E_{elec} + L \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

### 🔸 Step 4: Total Energy Per Round

There are $k$ CHs and $N - k$ non-CHs. The energy per frame (per round) is:

$$
E_{round} = k \cdot E_{CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{nonCH}
$$

Expanding (and noting $N - k \approx N$ for large $N$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{round} &\approx k \left[ \frac{N}{k} L E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} L E_{DA} + L E_{elec} + L \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} \right] \\
&\quad + (N - k) \left[ L E_{elec} + L \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E_{round} = L \left[ 2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} + (N - k) \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2} \right]
$$

### 🔸 Step 5: Optimal Number of Clusters

Setting $\frac{dE_{round}}{dk} = 0$ and solving:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical LEACH result. For KTU, you need to **derive the energy per round** and **state the optimal cluster count formula**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU ESE Problem)

**Problem:** A sensor node transmits a 4000-bit packet over 100 m using the multipath model. Compute $E_{Tx}$ and $E_{Rx}$ using standard constants.

**Given:**
- $k = 4000$ bits
- $d = 100$ m
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ $= 1.3 \times 10^{-15}$ J/bit/m⁴
- $d_0 = 87$ m → since $d = 100 \text{ m} > d_0$, we **use the multipath model**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Tx} &= k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \\
&= 4000 \times (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 4000 \times (1.3 \times 10^{-15}) \times (100)^{4} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} + 4000 \times 1.3 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{8} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} + 4000 \times 1.3 \times 10^{-7} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} + 5.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} \\
&= 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.72 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{Rx} &= k \cdot E_{elec} = 4000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.2 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total link energy:**

$$
E_{link} = E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} = 0.72 \text{ mJ} + 0.2 \text{ mJ} = 0.92 \text{ mJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** At 100 m (just past $d_0$), the *amplifier energy* ($5.2 \times 10^{-4}$ J) actually **exceeds** the *electronics energy* ($2 \times 10^{-4}$ J) — a clear demonstration of why amplifier energy dominates in long-range WSNs.

```

This state machine models the **operational mode transitions** of the radio, which dictate the energy profile over time.

stateDiagram-v2

    note right of SleepState: E_sleep is MINIMAL microampere level
    note right of IdleListenState: E_listen is HIGH causes energy waste

```

This diagram visually maps the **two path-loss regimes** and the energy components feeding into the transmit and receive equations.

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]

Part A carries 3 marks each, Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B (each with sub-parts of 7 marks).

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

> **"List the four major sources of energy consumption in a sensor node. Which one is dominant and why?"**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

The four major sources of energy consumption in a sensor node are:

1. **Sensing Energy ($E_{sense}$):** Power consumed by the physical transducer and ADC to sample environmental phenomena.
2. **Processing Energy ($E_{proc}$):** Power consumed by the microcontroller (CPU), memory access, and instruction execution.
3. **Communication Energy ($E_{comm}$):** Power consumed by the radio transceiver during transmit, receive, idle, and sleep states.
4. **Idle/Sleep Energy ($E_{idle/sleep}$):** Baseline power drawn by housekeeping circuits when the node is not actively performing tasks.

**Dominant Subsystem:** The **Communication subsystem (radio transceiver)** is the dominant energy consumer, typically accounting for **70% to 80%** of the total node energy budget. This is because the RF power amplifier must compensate for path loss (which scales as $d^2$ in free space and $d^4$ in multipath environments), and the radio also consumes significant power in idle/listen mode while waiting for incoming packets.

**Mark Distribution:** List (2 marks) + Dominant subsystem justification (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

> **"Define the first-order radio model. State the equations for $E_{Tx}$ and $E_{Rx}$ in both free-space and multipath regimes."**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **first-order radio model** (Heinzelman et al., 2000) is a simplified energy consumption model for the radio transceiver of a WSN node. It decomposes energy into the **transmitter/receiver electronics energy** ($E_{elec}$ per bit) and the **transmit amplifier energy** ($\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ per bit), where the path-loss exponent $n$ depends on the distance regime.

The transmit energy for a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$:

$$
E_{Tx}(k,d) =
\begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} & \text{if } d < d_0 \\
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} & \text{if } d \geq d_0
\end{cases}
$$

The receive energy (independent of distance):

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

The crossover distance $d_0$ is defined as $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m using standard parameters.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Free-space equation (1 mark) + Multipath equation and $E_{Rx}$ (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### 📘 **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a)** Derive the energy consumption equations of the first-order radio model for both free-space and multipath regimes. Define the crossover distance and compute it using standard Heinzelman parameters. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sensor node transmits a 2000-bit packet over a distance of 150 m. Compute the total energy consumed by the transmitter and the receiver using the multipath model. Take $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴. Also compute the crossover distance. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The first-order radio model assumes the radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ (50 nJ/bit) in the transmitter/receiver electronics, and an additional amplifier energy proportional to $d^{n}$ in the transmit path. The energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$ in **free space** ($d < d_0$, path loss exponent $n = 2$):

$$
E_{Tx}^{fs}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For the **multipath** regime ($d \geq d_0$, path loss exponent $n = 4$):

$$
E_{Tx}^{mp}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The receive energy is distance-independent (no amplifier in receiver):

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The crossover distance is found by equating the two transmit energy expressions and cancelling $E_{elec}$:

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** With $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² and $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴:

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $k = 2000$ bits, $d = 150$ m, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit, $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ $= 1.3 \times 10^{-15}$ J/bit/m⁴.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compare $d$ with $d_0$: $d = 150$ m $> d_0 = 87.7$ m, so we use the **multipath model**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $E_{Tx}$:

$$
E_{Tx} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 2000 \times (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 2000 \times (1.3 \times 10^{-15}) \times (150)^{4} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} + 2000 \times 1.3 \times 10^{-15} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-4} + 2000 \times 6.58125 \times 10^{-7} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-4} + 1.31625 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.41625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} \approx 1.416 \text{ mJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $E_{Rx}$:

$$
E_{Rx} = k \cdot E_{elec} = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.1 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Total link energy:

$$
E_{link} = E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} = 1.416 \text{ mJ} + 0.1 \text{ mJ} = 1.516 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Crossover distance (using $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²):

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{10/0.0013} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Observation — at 150 m, the *amplifier energy* ($1.316$ mJ) dominates the *electronics energy* ($0.1$ mJ) by a factor of ~13×, confirming the importance of short-range, multi-hop routing in WSNs.

---

### 📗 **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the four major sources of energy consumption in a sensor node, with appropriate equations and real-world examples for each subsystem. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a LEACH-clustered WSN, derive the expression for the total energy consumed per round. State the optimal number of clusters per round. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Four Energy Subsystems (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Sensing Energy (2 marks):**
The sensing subsystem consists of the physical transducer (e.g., temperature sensor, accelerometer) and the analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The energy consumed per sample is:

$$
E_{sense} = V_{supply} \cdot I_{sensor} \cdot t_{sample}
$$

For a typical thermistor drawing $1$ mA at $3$ V for $1$ ms, $E_{sense} = 3 \mu J$. For power-hungry sensors (e.g., gas sensors, cameras drawing ~50 mA), $E_{sense}$ can be 50–100× higher.

**Step 2 — Processing Energy (1 mark):**
The microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0) consumes $E_{proc} = V \cdot I_{active} \cdot t_{active}$ in active mode (1–5 mA) and a much smaller sleep current (< 5 μA). Computation energy is *comparable to* sensing energy and *much smaller* than communication energy for typical WSN workloads.

**Step 3 — Communication Energy (2 marks):**
The radio is the dominant consumer. Using the first-order model:

$$
E_{Tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}
$$

For a 100-bit, 50-meter transmission, $E_{Tx} \approx 5 \mu J$ — comparable to processing 1000 instructions. Listening (idle) is also expensive (~10–20 mA) — a major source of energy waste addressed by duty-cycling in S-MAC, B-MAC, RI-MAC.

**Step 4 — Idle/Sleep Energy (1 mark):**
Even in sleep, the node draws a small baseline current (typically 1–10 μA) for timer circuits, sensors, and RAM refresh. Over a long lifetime, this *integrates* to a non-trivial amount. Aggressive sleep scheduling is therefore a critical WSN design principle.

**Step 5 — Real-World Example (1 mark):**
A MicaZ mote (Atmel ATmega128L + CC2420 radio) on 2× AA batteries (~2200 mAh at 3 V) has an estimated lifetime of ~1–2 weeks in continuous listening mode, but **6–12 months** with 1% duty cycling — a 30–50× improvement.

---

**Part (b) — LEACH Energy Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Consider $N$ sensor nodes uniformly distributed in an $M \times M$ region, with $k$ clusters per round. Each cluster has one CH and $N/k - 1$ member nodes. Each member transmits an $L$-bit frame to its CH at average distance $d_{toCH}$ (free-space regime), and the CH aggregates the data and sends one $L$-bit frame to the base station at distance $d_{toBS}$ (multipath regime).

**Step 2 — Non-CH Energy (1 mark):**

$$
E_{nonCH} = L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — CH Energy (2 marks):**

$$
E_{CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) L E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} L E_{DA} + L E_{elec} + L \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

The first term is reception energy, the second is aggregation energy, and the third and fourth terms are the BS-transmit energy.

**Step 4 — Total Energy per Round (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{round} &= k \cdot E_{CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{nonCH} \\
&\approx L \left[ 2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} + N \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Optimal Cluster Count (1 mark):**
Setting $\frac{dE_{round}}{dk} = 0$ and using $d_{toCH}^{2} = M^{2}/(2\pi k)$:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

This is the canonical LEACH result: roughly **5% of nodes** are optimal CHs per round.

---

## 5.3 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the two-regime switch (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students blindly use the multipath model for *all* distances. **Always check $d$ vs. $d_0$ first** and explicitly state which regime applies.
> 2. **Unit confusion in $\varepsilon_{amp}$ (–1 to –2 marks):** The constants $100$ pJ/bit/m² and $0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ are often quoted interchangeably across textbooks. **Standardize on $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² and $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴** for Heinzelman's original LEACH paper — KTU expects these values.
> 3. **Forgetting the receive energy in total link energy (–1 mark):** A common error is computing only $E_{Tx}$. **Always add $E_{Rx} = k E_{elec}$ for the total link energy.**
> 4. **Confusing $E_{elec}$ with $\varepsilon_{amp}$ (–1 to –2 marks):** $E_{elec}$ is the **electronics** energy per bit (no $d$ dependence). $\varepsilon_{amp}$ is the **amplifier** energy per bit per m^n. They are different physical quantities.
> 5. **Not mentioning the dominant subsystem (–1 mark):** In "list the energy sources" questions, explicitly state that the **radio transceiver dominates** (~70–80% of total energy) and *briefly justify why*.
> 6. **Skipping the crossover distance derivation (–1 to –2 marks):** The formula $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ is derivable in 3 lines. Examiners love to ask it.
> 7. **Using $|\cdot|$ inside markdown tables (–formatting issue):** Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX equations inside tables, not raw vertical bars.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **The 12 Golden Points for KTU ESE on "Energy Consumption of Sensor Nodes":**
>
> 1. **Four energy subsystems:** Sensing + Processing + Communication + Idle/Sleep. Total: $E_{total} = E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{comm} + E_{idle}$.
> 2. **Dominant consumer:** Radio transceiver (70–80% of total energy). Justify with the $d^{n}$ path-loss argument.
> 3. **First-order radio model** (Heinzelman 2000): $E_{Tx}(k,d) = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{amp} d^{n}$ and $E_{Rx}(k) = k E_{elec}$.
> 4. **Two path-loss regimes:** Free space ($d^{2}$) for $d < d_0$; Multipath ($d^{4}$) for $d \geq d_0$. **Always declare which regime.**
> 5. **Crossover distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m with standard Heinzelman constants.
> 6. **Standard constants to memorize:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/report, $V_{supply} = 3$ V.
> 7. **Total link energy:** $E_{link} = E_{Tx} + E_{Rx} = 2kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{amp}d^{n}$.
> 8. **LEACH energy per round:** $E_{round} = L[2N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4} + N\varepsilon_{fs}d_{toCH}^{2}]$.
> 9. **Optimal LEACH cluster count:** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot (M/d_{toBS}^{2}) \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ — roughly 5% of $N$.
> 10. **Network lifetime metrics:** FND (First Node Dies), HNA (Half Nodes Alive), LND (Last Node Dies). KTU typically asks for FND-based lifetime.
> 11. **Energy optimization levers:** (a) Duty cycling the radio (S-MAC, B-MAC); (b) Data aggregation; (c) Multi-hop short-range routing; (d) Sleep scheduling; (e) Energy harvesting.
> 12. **Exam-day formulas to keep ready:** First-order model + crossover distance + LEACH energy/round + optimal cluster count. **These four are the "must-know" arsenal.**

---

## 5.5 🎯 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment:**
> - **CO4 (Module 4 — Energy Management in WSNs):** *Apply energy models to analyze sensor node lifetime and protocol energy efficiency.* — **Fully addressed** in Sections 2, 3, and 5.
> - **Bloom's Levels Covered:** L1 (Remember — define sources), L2 (Understand — first-order model), L3 (Apply — numerical LEACH energy), L4 (Analyze — derive $d_0$ and optimal $k$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Power Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low Power Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Low Power Wireless Sensor Networks (LP-WSN)** are specialized ad-hoc networks of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes designed to operate for extended durations (months to years) on severely constrained energy budgets, typically sourced from small batteries, energy harvesters, or both. The fundamental design objective is to maximize **network lifetime** by minimizing the energy consumption of sensing, communication, and processing subsystems while maintaining the required Quality of Service (QoS) parameters such as throughput, latency, and reliability.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, LP-WSN integrates the principles of **energy-aware computing**, **duty-cycled operation**, **power-efficient MAC/routing protocols**, and **energy harvesting** to enable sustainable operation in unattended or hard-to-reach environments.

### Key Design Constraints

- **Energy Budget per Node:** Typically **1 Joule/day to 10 Joule/hour** depending on duty cycle.
- **Target Lifetime:** 1 – 10 years without battery replacement.
- **Communication Range:** Short-range (10 m – 100 m) using low-power radios.
- **Duty Cycle:** Often **< 1%** for ultra-low-power operation.
- **Computational Capability:** Limited to microcontrollers (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0+).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Night Watchman's Lantern"**
>
> Imagine a security guard patrolling a vast warehouse. Carrying a heavy lantern would tire him out quickly. Instead, he carries a small LED torch and only switches it on briefly every few minutes to scan the surroundings. Between scans, he conserves energy by walking in the dark (relying on memory and a slow pace). The "torch" represents the radio transceiver, the "scanning intervals" represent the active duty cycle, and the "long patrol" represents the required network lifetime. A **Low Power WSN** works on the same principle: the radio is OFF most of the time and only turned ON for brief, scheduled intervals to transmit/receive data.

## 1.3 Sources of Power Consumption in a Sensor Node

A sensor node's energy is consumed by three primary subsystems, ranked by typical consumption:

| Subsystem | Power Consumption (Idle) | Power Consumption (Active) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Radio Transceiver** | ~1–5 mW | **20–100 mW** (highest) |
| **Microcontroller** | ~1–10 µW | 10–50 mW |
| **Sensors** | ~0 (off) | 1–10 mW |

> [!TIP]
> The **radio transceiver** dominates energy consumption in a WSN node, often accounting for **70%–80%** of total energy usage. Therefore, the most aggressive energy-saving strategies target the radio's sleep/wake cycle.

## 1.4 Energy Sources for Low-Power WSNs

> [!NOTE]
> **Three fundamental energy provisioning strategies** exist in modern LP-WSN design:
>
> 1. **Battery-Powered (Conventional):** Non-rechargeable coin cells (e.g., CR2032) or Li-ion cells with finite lifetime.
> 2. **Energy Harvesting (Self-Sustaining):** Ambient energy scavenging from solar, vibration, thermal gradients, or RF.
> 3. **Hybrid (Battery + Harvester):** A small rechargeable cell buffered by a harvester for reliability.

## 1.5 Importance in KTU Curriculum & Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this topic critical?**
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance in factories.
> - **Precision Agriculture:** Soil monitoring across vast farms.
> - **Smart Cities:** Structural health monitoring of bridges, buildings.
> - **Environmental Monitoring:** Forest fire detection, glacier movement.
> - **Medical Body Area Networks (MBAN):** Continuous patient monitoring with minimal battery replacement surgeries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Consumption Model of a Sensor Node

The total energy consumed by a sensor node over a time interval $T$ is the integral of instantaneous power drawn by all subsystems:

$$E_{total} = \int_{0}^{T} P_{node}(t) \, dt$$

For a duty-cycled node with discrete sleep and active states, this simplifies to:

$$E_{total} = P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep} + P_{active} \cdot T_{active} + P_{tx} \cdot T_{tx} + P_{rx} \cdot T_{rx}$$

Where:
- $P_{sleep}$ = Power drawn during sleep mode
- $P_{active}$ = Power drawn during sensing/processing
- $P_{tx}$ = Power drawn during transmission
- $P_{rx}$ = Power drawn during reception
- $T_i$ = Time spent in state $i$

## 2.2 First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **First-Order Radio Model** (also called the Heinzelman Model) is the standard analytical framework used in KTU-level WSN analysis to compute transmission and reception energy costs.

To transmit a $k$-bit packet over a distance $d$, the radio expends:

$$E_{Tx}(k, d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} & \text{if } d < d_0 \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} & \text{if } d \geq d_0 \end{cases}$$

To receive this packet, the radio expends:

$$E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

### Definition of Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_{elec}$ | Energy per bit for circuitry | **50 nJ/bit** |
| $\epsilon_{fs}$ | Free-space amplifier coefficient | **10 pJ/bit/m²** |
| $\epsilon_{mp}$ | Multi-path amplifier coefficient | **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** |
| $d_0$ | Crossover distance | $\sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ |

## 2.3 Network Lifetime Formulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Lifetime** is formally defined as the time until the first node (or a fraction $\alpha$ of nodes) depletes its energy below a critical threshold.

$$L_{net} = \min_{i \in N} \frac{E_{initial}^{(i)}}{P_{avg}^{(i)}}$$

Where $E_{initial}^{(i)}$ is the initial energy of node $i$, and $P_{avg}^{(i)}$ is its average power consumption.

## 2.4 Duty Cycle Formalism

The **duty cycle** $DC$ is the fraction of time a node is active:

$$DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$$

The energy saved by duty cycling compared to always-on operation:

$$E_{saved} = P_{active} \cdot T_{active} \left(\frac{1}{DC} - 1\right)$$

## 2.5 Energy Harvesting Power Equation

The harvested power from an ambient source is:

$$P_{harvest} = \eta \cdot A \cdot S_{source}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = Conversion efficiency of the harvester
- $A$ = Effective area (for solar) or coupling factor
- $S_{source}$ = Source intensity (e.g., W/m² for solar)

The **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)** condition is:

$$E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed} \quad \text{(over a long-term average)}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **First-Order Tx Energy** | $E_{Tx} = kE_{elec} + k\epsilon d^{n}$ | Link budget estimation |
| **Rx Energy** | $E_{Rx} = kE_{elec}$ | Asymmetry between Tx/Rx |
| **Duty Cycle** | $DC = T_{on}/(T_{on}+T_{sleep})$ | Sleep scheduling |
| **Network Lifetime** | $L = \min(E_{0}^{(i)}/P_{avg}^{(i)})$ | Battery planning |
| **Crossover Distance** | $d_{0} = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ | Protocol tuning |
| **ENO Condition** | $E_{h} \geq E_{c}$ | Harvester sizing |
| **Solar Harvest Power** | $P_{h} = \eta A S$ | Energy budget design |
| **Sleep Current Dominance** | $I_{sleep} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot T$ | Ultra-low-power IC design |
| **Path Loss (Friis)** | $P_{r} = P_{t}G_{t}G_{r}(\lambda/4\pi d)^{2}$ | Range estimation |
| **Avg Current Draw** | $I_{avg} = DC \cdot I_{active} + (1-DC) \cdot I_{sleep}$ | Battery life prediction |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** The LEACH protocol (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) uses the first-order radio model to elect cluster heads probabilistically, rotating the high-energy role of cluster head to extend network lifetime by a factor of **4×–8×** compared to direct transmission.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Transmission Distance for Minimum Energy

**Problem Setup:** For a multi-hop route from source to sink over total distance $D$ divided into $n$ equal hops, find the optimal hop length $d_{opt}$ that minimizes total transmission energy.

**Step 1 — Write the energy per hop (free-space model):**
$$E_{hop} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy for $n$ hops where $D = n \cdot d$:**
$$E_{total} = n \cdot \left(k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}\right) = n \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot n \cdot d^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $d = D/n$ to express in terms of $n$:**
$$E_{total}(n) = n \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Take the derivative with respect to $n$ and set to zero (continuum approximation):**
$$\frac{dE_{total}}{dn} = k \cdot E_{elec} - k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{n^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the optimal number of hops:**
$$n^{2} = \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot D^{2}}{E_{elec}}$$
$$n_{opt} = D \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{E_{elec}}}$$

**Step 6 — Derive the optimal single-hop distance:**
$$d_{opt} = \frac{D}{n_{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{E_{elec}}{\epsilon_{fs}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** The optimal hop distance is **independent of the total source-sink distance** $D$. This counter-intuitive result is fundamental to WSN routing protocol design and explains why multi-hop is always more energy-efficient than direct transmission beyond $d_{opt}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Current and Battery Lifetime

**Given:** A node operates with active current $I_{active} = 20$ mA for $T_{on} = 10$ ms every $T_{period} = 1$ s, and sleep current $I_{sleep} = 5$ µA. Battery capacity $C = 240$ mAh.

**Step 1 — Calculate the duty cycle:**
$$DC = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 0.01 = 1\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate average current:**
$$I_{avg} = DC \cdot I_{active} + (1 - DC) \cdot I_{sleep}$$
$$I_{avg} = (0.01)(20 \text{ mA}) + (0.99)(0.005 \text{ mA})$$
$$I_{avg} = 0.20 \text{ mA} + 0.00495 \text{ mA}$$
$$I_{avg} \approx 0.20495 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate battery lifetime:**
$$L = \frac{C}{I_{avg}} = \frac{240 \text{ mAh}}{0.20495 \text{ mA}} \approx 1171 \text{ hours}$$
$$L \approx 48.8 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the node were always-on ($DC = 100\%$), lifetime would be $240/20 = 12$ hours. Duty cycling extends it **~97×**.

## 3.3 Numerical Example: Network Lifetime with Clustering (LEACH-style)

**Given:** 100 nodes uniformly distributed in a 100 m × 100 m field. Sink is at the center. Each node has $E_{0} = 2$ J. $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². Packet size $k = 4000$ bits. 5% of nodes act as cluster heads per round.

**Step 1 — Average distance from node to cluster head (assuming optimal clustering):**
$$d_{toCH}^{2} = \frac{M^{2}}{2\pi k_{opt}}$$

Where $M$ = field side, $k_{opt}$ = optimal cluster count. For $n = 100$ nodes:
$$k_{opt} \approx \sqrt{\frac{n}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{0}} \quad \text{(approximation)}$$

**Step 2 — Energy per round per non-CH node:**
$$E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2} + E_{fusion}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per round per CH (with data aggregation overhead):**
$$E_{CH} = k \cdot \left(\frac{n}{k_{opt}} - 1\right) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot (n) + E_{DA} \cdot k \cdot \frac{n}{k_{opt}} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Total energy per round:**
$$E_{round} = n \cdot E_{nonCH} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{k_{opt}}{n}\right) + k_{opt} \cdot E_{CH}$$

**Step 5 — Number of rounds until first node dies:**
$$L_{rounds} = \frac{E_{0}}{E_{round}/n} = \frac{n \cdot E_{0}}{E_{round}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Numerical problems on this topic frequently test the ability to set up the first-order radio model and compute relative energy savings between direct, multi-hop, and clustered transmission.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Power Saving Techniques vs. Layer

| OSI Layer | Power-Saving Technique | Energy Reduction | Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Physical** | Low-power radio (sub-1 GHz) | 30–50% | Bandwidth, range |
| **Physical** | Modulation selection (OOK vs FSK) | 20–40% | BER |
| **Data Link (MAC)** | Duty cycling (S-MAC, B-MAC) | **70–90%** | Latency, throughput |
| **Data Link (MAC)** | TDMA scheduling | 40–60% | Synchronization |
| **Network** | Multi-hop vs. direct | Up to 10× | Routing complexity |
| **Network** | Cluster-based (LEACH) | 4×–8× lifetime | Cluster overhead |
| **Transport** | Hop-by-hop reliability | 30% | Memory |
| **Application** | Data aggregation/fusion | 50–90% | Information loss |
| **Cross-Layer** | Energy-aware protocol stack | 40–60% | Design complexity |

## 3.5 Verification Example: Optimal Hop Distance

**Given:** $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². Find $d_{opt}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the derived formula:**
$$d_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{E_{elec}}{\epsilon_{fs}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute with unit consistency:**
$$d_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent:**
$$= \sqrt{5 \times 10^{3}} = \sqrt{5000}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final value:**
$$d_{opt} \approx 70.71 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the well-known **"magic number"** in WSN textbook examples: when $d_{opt} \approx 70$ m for the given radio parameters, multi-hop routing always beats direct transmission for any $D > 70$ m.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Low-Power Sensor Node

The following diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a typical low-power sensor node, showing the energy flow and control signals between subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR[POWER SUBSYSTEM]
        battA1[Battery Cell 3V Coin Cell]
        harvA1[Energy Harvester Solar Thermal Vibration]
        regA1[Voltage Regulator LDO or Buck]
        mgrA1[Power Management Unit PMU]
    end

    subgraph SENS[SENSING SUBSYSTEM]
        sa1[Temperature Sensor]
        sa2[Humidity Sensor]
        sa3[Motion Accelerometer]
        adcA1[Analog to Digital Converter]
    end

    subgraph PROC[PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM]
        mcuA1[Microcontroller MSP430 or ARM M0]
        memA1[Flash Memory 32 to 256 KB]
        rtcA1[Real Time Clock]
    end

    subgraph COMM[COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM]
        radA1[Radio Transceiver Sub GHz or 2.4 GHz]
        antA1[Antenna]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL AND TIMING]
        slpA1[Sleep Wake Timer]
        dcyA1[Duty Cycle Controller]
        schA1[TDMA Scheduler]
    end

    harvA1 --> regA1
    battA1 --> regA1
    regA1 --> mgrA1
    mgrA1 --> sa1
    mgrA1 --> sa2
    mgrA1 --> sa3
    mgrA1 --> mcuA1
    mgrA1 --> radA1
    sa1 --> adcA1
    sa2 --> adcA1
    sa3 --> adcA1
    adcA1 --> mcuA1
    mcuA1 --> memA1
    mcuA1 --> radA1
    radA1 --> antA1
    rtcA1 --> slpA1
    slpA1 --> dcyA1
    dcyA1 --> mgrA1
    dcyA1 --> schA1
    schA1 --> radA1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The **Power Management Unit (PMU)** sits at the heart of the architecture. It receives commands from the **Duty Cycle Controller** (driven by the **Sleep/Wake Timer**) to selectively power-gate subsystems. This is the central concept of low-power design — *no subsystem is ever ON unless absolutely necessary*.

## 4.2 Operational State Machine of a Low-Power Node

This diagram depicts the state transitions a node undergoes to minimize energy consumption.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SLEEP

    SLEEP --> WAKEUP : Timer expires
    WAKEUP --> SENSE : Power up sensors
    SENSE --> PROCESS : ADC sampling done
    PROCESS --> TX : Data ready to send
    PROCESS --> SLEEP : No event of interest
    TX --> LISTEN : Wait for ACK
    LISTEN --> RX : Incoming packet
    LISTEN --> SLEEP : No packet timeout
    RX --> PROCESS : Process received data
    RX --> SLEEP : No further action
    TX --> SLEEP : ACK received
    PROCESS --> HARVEST : Schedule recharge
    HARVEST --> SLEEP : Recharge complete

    SLEEP : SLEEP State Current 1 to 10 microamp
    WAKEUP : WAKEUP State Current 1 to 5 milliamp
    SENSE : SENSE State Current 5 to 15 milliamp
    PROCESS : PROCESS State Current 10 to 30 milliamp
    TX : TRANSMIT State Current 50 to 100 milliamp
    LISTEN : LISTEN State Current 20 to 40 milliamp
    RX : RECEIVE State Current 20 to 40 milliamp
    HARVEST : HARVEST State Charging cycle
```

## 4.3 Energy Harvesting System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA1[Ambient Energy Source Sunlight Vibration Heat RF Waves] --> trxA1[Transducer Solar Cell Piezoelectric Thermoelectric Antenna]
    trxA1 --> rectA1[Rectifier and AC to DC Converter]
    rectA1 --> condA1[Energy Storage Supercapacitor or Thin Film Battery]
    condA1 --> regA2[Voltage Regulator]
    regA2 --> loadA1[Sensor Node Load]
    ctrlA1[MPPT Controller] --> rectA1
    ctrlA1 --> regA2
    sensA2[Battery Monitor] --> ctrlA1
    sensA2 --> condA1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking)** controller dynamically adjusts the harvester's operating point to extract peak power from the ambient source, critical for solar/vibration sources with non-linear I-V characteristics.

## 4.4 Comparison Flow: Direct vs. Multi-Hop vs. Clustered Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA1[Source Node Generates Data Packet] --> checkA1{Distance to Sink D}
    checkA1 --> shortA1[D less than d0] --> directA1[Direct Transmission Single Hop High Tx Power]
    checkA1 --> longA1[D greater than d0] --> multiA1[Multi Hop Relay via Intermediate Nodes]
    longA1 --> optA1[Optimal Hop Distance d0]
    optA1 --> routeA1[Route Selection Energy Aware Metric]
    routeA1 --> clusterA1{Cluster Based Network}
    clusterA1 --> yesA1[YES LEACH or HEED] --> chA1[Elect Cluster Head Probabilistically]
    chA1 --> aggA1[Aggregate and Forward to Sink]
    clusterA1 --> noA1[NO Flat Routing] --> floodA1[Flood or Gossip to Sink]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "duty cycling" in the context of Low Power Wireless Sensor Networks. Why is it considered the most effective energy-saving technique?**

**Model Answer:**
Duty cycling is the process of periodically switching a sensor node's subsystems (especially the radio) between active and sleep states. It is governed by:

$$DC = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{sleep}}$$

It is the **most effective technique** because the radio transceiver dominates energy consumption (70–80%), and a 1% duty cycle can extend battery life by two orders of magnitude. The KTU marking scheme awards 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, and 1 mark for justification.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain the First-Order Radio Model used for energy analysis in WSNs. Define the crossover distance $d_0$.**

**Model Answer:**
The First-Order Radio Model computes energy to transmit $k$ bits over distance $d$:

$$E_{Tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} \quad (d < d_0)$$
$$E_{Tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \quad (d \geq d_0)$$

The **crossover distance** $d_0$ is the threshold where free-space and multi-path propagation losses are equal:

$$d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a low-power sensor node. Discuss the role of the Power Management Unit (PMU) in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Architecture (3 marks):**
A low-power sensor node consists of four major subsystems:
- **Sensing Subsystem:** Sensors (temperature, humidity, gas) + ADC
- **Processing Subsystem:** Microcontroller (e.g., MSP430) + memory
- **Communication Subsystem:** Radio transceiver + antenna
- **Power Subsystem:** Battery + harvester + regulator

**2. PMU Role (2 marks):**
The PMU is the heart of energy management. It:
- Performs **DC-DC conversion** with high efficiency (>90%)
- Implements **dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS)**
- **Power-gates** idle subsystems via MOSFET switches
- Coordinates with the duty cycle controller

**3. Power States (2 marks):**
- **Active Mode:** All subsystems powered (50–100 mW)
- **Sleep Mode:** Only RTC active (1–10 µW)
- **Deep Sleep:** RAM retained, CPU off (0.1–1 µW)
- **Hibernate:** Full power-down, wake on interrupt (0.1 µW)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often draw the block diagram without labeling the **control arrows** from the PMU to other subsystems. KTU examiners specifically look for these power-gate control lines. Missing this loses **1–2 marks**.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**A WSN node has the following specifications: Active current = 25 mA, Sleep current = 8 µA, Active duration per cycle = 15 ms, Sleep duration = 985 ms. Battery capacity = 250 mAh. Calculate: (i) Duty cycle, (ii) Average current, (iii) Battery lifetime in days.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Duty Cycle (2 marks):**
$$DC = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{sleep}} = \frac{15}{15 + 985} = \frac{15}{1000} = 0.015 = 1.5\%$$

**Step 2 — Average Current (2 marks):**
$$I_{avg} = DC \cdot I_{active} + (1 - DC) \cdot I_{sleep}$$
$$I_{avg} = (0.015)(25 \text{ mA}) + (0.985)(0.008 \text{ mA})$$
$$I_{avg} = 0.375 \text{ mA} + 0.00788 \text{ mA} = 0.38288 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Battery Lifetime (3 marks):**
$$L_{hours} = \frac{C}{I_{avg}} = \frac{250}{0.38288} = 653.0 \text{ hours}$$
$$L_{days} = \frac{653.0}{24} \approx 27.2 \text{ days}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A frequent KTU evaluation error is forgetting to convert **µA to mA** in the average current calculation. This leads to an average current that is off by a factor of **1000**, resulting in a wildly incorrect lifetime. Always double-check unit consistency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain Energy Harvesting techniques for Wireless Sensor Networks. Discuss solar, vibration, and thermal harvesting with their typical power densities. What is Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Solar Harvesting (2 marks):**
Uses photovoltaic cells. Power output:

$$P_{solar} = \eta \cdot A \cdot G$$

Typical indoor power density: **10–100 µW/cm²**; outdoor: **10–15 mW/cm²**. Highly predictable, suitable for outdoor deployments.

**2. Vibration/Piezoelectric Harvesting (2 marks):**
Converts mechanical vibrations to electrical energy via piezoelectric effect:

$$P_{vib} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot \zeta^{2} \cdot \omega^{2}$$

Typical power density: **1–200 µW/cm³**. Suitable for industrial machinery, bridges.

**3. Thermal Harvesting (1.5 marks):**
Uses Seebeck effect (thermoelectric generators):

$$P_{thermal} = \frac{S^{2} \cdot \Delta T^{2}}{4 \cdot R}$$

Typical power: **10–100 µW/cm²** at $\Delta T = 5°C$. Suitable for boiler pipelines, human body (4 µW/cm²).

**4. Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) (1.5 marks):**
ENO is the condition where the long-term average harvested energy meets or exceeds consumed energy:

$$E_{harvested}(T) \geq E_{consumed}(T)$$

This ensures perpetual operation. The design must size the harvester and storage to handle worst-case harvest periods.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the optimal hop distance for minimum transmission energy in a multi-hop WSN using the first-order radio model. A 10-node linear network spans 500 m. With $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², and $k = 2000$ bits, compute: (i) Total energy if 5 hops are used, (ii) Energy per bit per meter for this configuration.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation (3 marks):**
Total energy for $n$ hops over distance $D$:

$$E_{total} = n \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{n}$$

Differentiating and equating to zero:

$$\frac{dE_{total}}{dn} = k \cdot E_{elec} - k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot \frac{D^{2}}{n^{2}} = 0$$
$$n_{opt} = D\sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{E_{elec}}}$$
$$d_{opt} = \frac{D}{n_{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{E_{elec}}{\epsilon_{fs}}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $d_{opt}$ (1 mark):**
$$d_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{5000} \approx 70.71 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Energy for $n = 5$ (2 marks):**
With $D = 500$ m, $n = 5$, $d = 100$ m:
$$E_{total} = 5 \cdot (2000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9}) + 2000 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \frac{(500)^{2}}{5}$$
$$E_{total} = 5 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ J} + 10^{-8} \cdot 50000$$
$$E_{total} = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 1 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per bit per meter (1 mark):**
$$E_{bpm} = \frac{E_{total}}{k \cdot D} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2000 \cdot 500} = 10^{-9} \text{ J/bit/m} = 1 \text{ nJ/bit/m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget that in a multi-hop scenario, **each intermediate node also consumes receive energy** $E_{Rx} = k \cdot E_{elec}$. The model shown above assumes amplification only; for full credit, mention the receive energy for $n - 1$ intermediate hops, adding $(n-1) \cdot k \cdot E_{elec}$ to the total.

---

## 🔑 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concepts to Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **First-Order Radio Model:** Two regimes — free-space ($d^2$) and multi-path ($d^4$).
- ✅ **Crossover distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs}/\epsilon_{mp}}$ — divides the two energy regimes.
- ✅ **Duty Cycle formula:** $DC = T_{on}/(T_{on} + T_{sleep})$ — key to low-power operation.
- ✅ **Average current:** $I_{avg} = DC \cdot I_{active} + (1-DC) \cdot I_{sleep}$.
- ✅ **Battery life:** $L = C / I_{avg}$ — always verify units (mAh vs µA).
- ✅ **Optimal hop distance:** $d_{opt} = \sqrt{E_{elec}/\epsilon_{fs}} \approx 70$ m for textbook parameters.
- ✅ **Network lifetime:** $L_{net} = \min_i (E_0^{(i)} / P_{avg}^{(i)})$ — first-node-die criterion.
- ✅ **Energy Neutral Operation:** $E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed}$ for perpetual sensing.
- ✅ **PMU Role:** Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling (DVFS), power gating, sleep state management.
- ✅ **Radio dominates:** 70–80% of total node energy is in communication — always optimize the radio first.
- ✅ **Energy Harvesting sources:** Solar (10 mW/cm²), Vibration (200 µW/cm³), Thermal (100 µW/cm²).
- ✅ **LEACH principle:** Probabilistic cluster head rotation extends lifetime 4×–8× vs. direct.
- ✅ **Cross-layer design:** Combining MAC duty cycling + routing + data fusion = best energy savings.
- ✅ **MAC protocols for LP-WSN:** S-MAC, B-MAC, X-MAC, ContikiMAC — all target low duty cycle.
- ✅ **Standard hardware platforms:** TelosB, MicaZ, Z1 — operate at 1.8–3.6 V with MSP430 MCU.
- ✅ **Energy scavenging terminology:** Always quote power density (µW/cm² or µW/cm³) for harvesters.
- ✅ **Always convert units** before plugging into formulas — most KTU mark deductions are due to unit mismatches.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** In any numerical problem, **draw the block diagram first**, label all energy flows, then write the formula. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for proper diagram labeling even if the calculation has a minor error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Protocols (Basics Only) - LEACH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Routing Protocols (Basics Only) - LEACH

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** is a **hierarchical, cluster-based, energy-efficient routing protocol** for Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) proposed by **Heinzelman, Chandrakasan, and Balakrishnan (2000)**. It organises sensor nodes into local clusters, each governed by a rotating **Cluster Head (CH)**, and performs data aggregation at the CH before forwarding to the **Base Station (BS)**. The defining feature is the **randomised, periodic rotation of cluster head responsibility** to uniformly distribute the energy burden across all sensor nodes in the network.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, LEACH belongs to the class of **proactive, single-hop, intra-cluster hierarchical protocols** where every node independently elects itself as a CH using a deterministic probabilistic formula, eliminating the need for global network state information.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Village Committee" Model

Imagine a large village with **100 farmers (sensor nodes)** who each grow crops (sense data) every day. A single **central warehouse (Base Station)** buys all the produce, but the warehouse is far away.

- **Problem:** If every farmer drives directly to the warehouse every day, fuel (energy) is wasted.
- **LEACH Solution:** Each week, the village elects a **"Village Head" (Cluster Head)** for every 5–6 neighbouring farms. Each farmer reports his produce to the local Village Head, who **aggregates/compresses** the harvest and drives a single truck to the warehouse.
- **Fairness Rule:** No farmer can be Village Head two weeks in a row until **every farmer has served once**. This ensures no one is unfairly burdened.

This "**rotation of leadership**" + "**local aggregation**" + "**single long-haul transmission**" is the philosophical heart of LEACH.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** The word *Adaptive* in LEACH refers to two adaptations: (1) adaptive cluster formation, and (2) adaptive transmission power based on inter-node distance. The word *Hierarchy* refers to the two-tier structure: **Cluster Members → Cluster Heads → Base Station**.

## 1.3 Why LEACH? — The Energy Problem in Flat Routing

In flat routing (e.g., Direct Transmission, Flooding), every node communicates directly with the BS. The energy spent is **inversely proportional to the square of distance** in free-space or the **fourth power** in multi-path fading. Since the BS is usually **far away (≥ 100 m)**, this energy cost is enormous. LEACH reduces this cost by:

1. Reducing the **number of long-haul transmissions** (only CHs talk to the BS).
2. Performing **data fusion** at the CH (removes redundancy).
3. Using **short-distance intra-cluster communication**.

| Parameter | Typical LEACH Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Energy $E_0$ | 0.5 – 2.0 | Joules (J) |
| Number of Nodes $N$ | 100 – 200 | nodes |
| Optimal Cluster Count $k_{opt}$ | 3 – 5 % of $N$ | clusters |
| Round Duration | One CH election + frames | time units |
| Radio Model | First-order (Heinzelman) | $E_{elec}$, $E_{fs}$, $E_{mp}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LEACH Operational Phases

LEACH operates in **rounds**, where each round is divided into two distinct phases:

### Phase A — Setup Phase (Cluster Formation)

1. Each node generates a **random number $rand()$** in $[0, 1]$.
2. The node computes a **threshold $T(n)$** as a function of its current round number and its eligibility flag.
3. If $rand() < T(n)$, the node **elects itself as Cluster Head** for the current round.
4. CHs broadcast an **ADV (Advertisement)** message using CSMA MAC.
5. Non-CH nodes listen to all ADV messages, choose the CH with the **strongest received signal (minimum distance)**, and send a **Join-Request (Join-REQ)** using CSMA.
6. CHs create a **TDMA schedule** and broadcast it to members, assigning each a unique time slot.

### Phase B — Steady-State Phase (Data Transfer)

1. Each sensor node **senses the environment** during its allocated TDMA slot.
2. Transmission occurs only in the assigned slot, allowing other nodes to **enter sleep mode** (saves energy).
3. The CH receives data from all members, performs **signal processing / data aggregation / compression**.
4. The CH transmits the aggregated packet to the **far-away BS** using a single high-energy transmission.
5. After a fixed frame duration, the network initiates a **new round**, and CH election repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The setup phase consumes less energy but is computationally heavy; the steady-state phase is energy-efficient but must be **longer than the setup phase** to amortise its overhead. A typical round design uses **steady-state duration ≈ 5× to 10× the setup duration**.

## 2.2 The Cluster-Head Selection Threshold — Master Equation

The cornerstone of LEACH is the **adaptive threshold formula** that ensures fair, decentralised CH rotation:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot \left( r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P} \right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{if } n \notin G \end{cases}$$

Where the variables carry the following meaning:

| Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Desired percentage of CHs per round (e.g., 0.05) | User-tunable design knob |
| $r$ | Current round number | Time index, starts at 0 |
| $G$ | Set of nodes that have **not** been CHs in the last $\frac{1}{P}$ rounds | Ensures rotation fairness |
| $n$ | Node identifier | The evaluating node |
| $T(n)$ | Probability of becoming CH in round $r$ | Output threshold value |

**The "Why" Behind the Formula:**

- The denominator $1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)$ shrinks as the round number $r$ increases within a cycle, **forcing $T(n)$ to rise**.
- This means early in the cycle, fewer nodes volunteer; later in the cycle, the probability inflates to **guarantee** that the remaining un-elected nodes must become CHs.
- Once a node has been a CH in the last $1/P$ rounds, it is **removed from $G$**, and $T(n)$ becomes 0 for it, ensuring no repeated election.

## 2.3 First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman Model)

This is the **energy accounting standard** used in every LEACH analysis. The radio dissipates energy to run the **transmitter electronics ($E_{elec}$)**, the **transmit amplifier ($\varepsilon_{fs}$ or $\varepsilon_{mp}$)**, and the **receiver electronics**.

### 2.3.1 Transmit Energy for a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$:

$$E_{Tx}(k, d) = E_{Tx\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{Tx\text{-}amp}(k, d)$$

$$E_{Tx}(k, d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} & \text{if } d < d_{crossover} \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} & \text{if } d \geq d_{crossover} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3.2 Receive Energy for a $k$-bit packet:

$$E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

### 2.3.3 Crossover Distance:

$$d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T(n) = \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)}$ | CH threshold (LEACH core) | Probability $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 2 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{fs}d^2$ | Free-space Tx (short range) | Joules |
| 3 | $E_{Tx}(k,d) = kE_{elec} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d^4$ | Multi-path Tx (long range) | Joules |
| 4 | $E_{Rx}(k) = kE_{elec}$ | Reception cost | Joules |
| 5 | $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Range boundary | metres |
| 6 | $E_{DA}$ | Data aggregation cost per bit | nJ/bit/signal |
| 7 | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}$ | Optimal # clusters | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{opt} = \dfrac{k_{opt}}{N}$ | Optimal CH probability | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{round} = L \cdot (2NE_{elec} + N E_{DA} + k\varepsilon_{mp}d_{toBS}^{4} + N\varepsilon_{fs}d_{toCH}^{2})$ | Total energy per round | Joules |
| 10 | $\text{First Node Dies (FND)} = \dfrac{E_0}{E_{round}/N}$ | Network lifetime (rounds) | rounds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants** in LEACH literature: $E_{elec} = 50$ **nJ/bit**, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ **pJ/bit/m²**, $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ **pJ/bit/m⁴**, $E_{DA} = 5$ **nJ/bit/signal**, packet size $k = 4000$ **bits**. These are **NOT** physical constants — they are LEACH protocol simulation parameters widely adopted as benchmarks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LEACH and its derivatives (LEACH-C, LEACH-F, PEGASIS, HEED, TL-LEACH) form the **design foundation** for industrial WSN deployments in:

- **Precision agriculture** — soil-moisture and microclimate monitoring in vast farmlands.
- **Structural health monitoring** — bridges, buildings, dams with battery-powered vibration sensors.
- **Forest fire detection** — distributed temperature/humidity nodes.
- **Military surveillance** — low-power battlefield sensor grids.
- **Body Area Networks (BANs)** — wearable health telemetry.

The hierarchical aggregation principle of LEACH is now embedded in **IoT routing standards (RPL, 6LoWPAN)** and the **LoRaWAN** class-mode logic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Cluster-Head Threshold $T(n)$ from First Principles

The **objective** of the LEACH threshold is to ensure that in every cycle of $1/P$ rounds, **every node in the network** becomes a CH **exactly once**. We derive this condition mathematically.

### Step 1 — Define the Design Objective

Let $N$ be the total number of nodes. The expected number of CHs per round must equal $N \cdot P$. Over $1/P$ rounds, the total expected CH-roles is:

$$
\text{Total CH-roles per cycle} = (N \cdot P) \times \dfrac{1}{P} = N
$$

This guarantees that **each of the $N$ nodes is expected to become CH exactly once per cycle**. (KTU line of reasoning — note carefully.)

### Step 2 — Setup the Eligibility Constraint

At the start of round $r$, suppose $G$ is the set of nodes that have **not** been CHs in the last $1/P$ rounds. We want **every** node in $G$ to have a non-zero chance of being chosen, and **no** node outside $G$ to be chosen.

### Step 3 — Force the Indicator Function

We require a function $T(n)$ such that:

$$
T(n) = 0 \quad \text{whenever} \quad n \notin G
$$

and

$$
T(n) > 0 \quad \text{whenever} \quad n \in G
$$

### Step 4 — Enforce the Probability Conservation

For the random trial $\text{rand}() < T(n)$ to give an expected count of $N \cdot P$ CHs in round $r$:

$$
\sum_{n \in G} T(n) = N \cdot P
$$

### Step 5 — Assume Symmetry Within $G$

If we assume that **all eligible nodes have identical threshold** (symmetry), then:

$$
T(n) \cdot \vert G \vert = N \cdot P \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(n) = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{\vert G \vert}
$$

### Step 6 — Express $\vert G \vert$ in Terms of Round Number

At the beginning of round $r$, the number of nodes **remaining eligible** in the current cycle is:

$$
\vert G \vert = N - N \cdot P \cdot \left( r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P} \right)
$$

This is because in each of the previous $\left( r \bmod \frac{1}{P} \right)$ rounds of the current cycle, an average of $N \cdot P$ nodes were promoted to CHs.

### Step 7 — Substitute into $T(n)$ and Simplify

$$
T(n) = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{N - N \cdot P \cdot \left( r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P} \right)}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $N$:

$$
T(n) = \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot \left( r \bmod \dfrac{1}{P} \right)}
$$

This is the **canonical LEACH threshold formula** quoted in every KTU textbook.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Consumption per Round (Total Network Energy)

We now derive the total energy dissipated by the **entire WSN** in a single round.

### Step 1 — Account for Intra-Cluster Transmission

Each non-CH node transmits $L$ bits to its CH over an average distance $d_{toCH}$. There are $N - k$ such nodes (where $k$ = number of CHs). Using the free-space model:

$$
E_{intra} = (N - k) \cdot \left( L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Account for CH Reception

Each CH receives from its $\frac{N - k}{k}$ members on average. Reception cost per bit is $E_{elec}$:

$$
E_{Rx\text{-}total} = (N - k) \cdot L \cdot E_{elec}
$$

### Step 3 — Account for Data Aggregation at CHs

Each CH fuses the data of its members. Cost is $L \cdot E_{DA}$ per CH:

$$
E_{agg} = k \cdot L \cdot E_{DA}
$$

### Step 4 — Account for CH-to-BS Long-Haul Transmission

Each CH transmits an aggregated $L$-bit packet to the BS over a much longer distance $d_{toBS}$ — multi-path model applies:

$$
E_{toBS} = k \cdot \left( L \cdot E_{elec} + L \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Total Per-Round Energy

Summing all four components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{round} &= (N - k) L E_{elec} + (N - k) L \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2} \\
&\quad + (N - k) L E_{elec} + k L E_{DA} \\
&\quad + k L E_{elec} + k L \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining like terms and noting $(N-k)L E_{elec} + kL E_{elec} = N L E_{elec}$:

$$
E_{round} = 2 N L E_{elec} + N L E_{DA} + k L \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} + (N - k) L \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2}
$$

This is the **per-round energy budget** of a LEACH-style network. Minimising this with respect to $k$ yields the optimal cluster count.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optimal Number of Clusters $k_{opt}$

### Step 1 — Approximate Average Intra-Cluster Distance

Assuming nodes are uniformly distributed over an $M \times M$ region, the expected squared distance from a node to its CH is:

$$
E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \dfrac{M^{2}}{2 \pi k}
$$

This is a standard result from uniform spatial distributions — **memorise it for KTU derivations**.

### Step 2 — Substitute and Differentiate

Using $E[d_{toCH}^{2}]$ and differentiating $E_{round}$ with respect to $k$, setting $\frac{\partial E_{round}}{\partial k} = 0$:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** The optimal cluster count $k_{opt}$ grows with the network size $\sqrt{N}$ and shrinks with the BS distance $d_{toBS}^{2}$. A **far BS** → **fewer clusters** (each CH transmits over a long distance anyway), and a **dense network** → **more clusters**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes, $P = 0.05$, and the current round is $r = 7$. A node $n_{42}$ has not been a CH in the last 20 rounds. Find whether $n_{42}$ is eligible and what threshold it computes.

### Step 1 — Compute the Cycle Length

$$
\text{Cycle length} = \dfrac{1}{P} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ rounds}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine the Position Within the Cycle

$$
r \bmod 20 = 7 \bmod 20 = 7
$$

So we are in round 7 of the current 20-round cycle.

### Step 3 — Check Eligibility

Since $n_{42}$ has not been a CH in the last 20 rounds, $n_{42} \in G$. (The set $G$ typically contains nodes that have not been CHs in the last 20 rounds.)

### Step 4 — Apply the Threshold Formula

$$
T(n_{42}) = \dfrac{0.05}{1 - 0.05 \times 7} = \dfrac{0.05}{1 - 0.35} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.65} \approx 0.0769
$$

### Step 5 — Decision

If $n_{42}$ generates $rand() = 0.05$, then $0.05 < 0.0769$, so $n_{42}$ **becomes a CH** in round 7.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LEACH vs Flat Routing

| Metric | Direct Transmission | Minimum-Transmission-Energy | LEACH |
|---|---|---|---|
| BS distance energy | $N \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}$ | Reduced via MTE | Reduced via aggregation |
| Scalability | Poor | Moderate | Good |
| Energy balance | Very poor | Poor | Excellent (rotation) |
| Data fusion | None | None | Yes (at CH) |
| Network lifetime | Worst | Better | Best (2–8× direct) |
| Complexity | Lowest | Low | Moderate |
| Hot-spot issue | Not in direct | Severe near BS | Mitigated by rotation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LEACH Network Architecture — Two-Tier Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeBS["BASE STATION BS"]
    nodeCH1["CLUSTER HEAD CH1"]
    nodeCH2["CLUSTER HEAD CH2"]
    nodeCH3["CLUSTER HEAD CH3"]
    nodeCMa["CM A1"]
    nodeCMb["CM A2"]
    nodeCMc["CM A3"]
    nodeCMd["CM B1"]
    nodeCMe["CM B2"]
    nodeCMf["CM C1"]
    nodeCMg["CM C2"]
    nodeCMh["CM C3"]

    nodeBS --- nodeCH1
    nodeBS --- nodeCH2
    nodeBS --- nodeCH3

    nodeCH1 --- nodeCMa
    nodeCH1 --- nodeCMb
    nodeCH1 --- nodeCMc

    nodeCH2 --- nodeCMd
    nodeCH2 --- nodeCMe

    nodeCH3 --- nodeCMf
    nodeCH3 --- nodeCMg
    nodeCH3 --- nodeCMh
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed/double tier shows the **two-tier hierarchy** of LEACH. The **bottom tier** (CMs) is short-range, energy-efficient intra-cluster communication. The **top tier** (CH→BS) is the only long-haul, expensive transmission.

## 4.2 LEACH Round Time-Line — Phases Within a Single Round

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepR["START OF ROUND r"]
    stepA["PHASE 1: SETUP PHASE"]
    stepA1["Node generates rand number"]
    stepA2["Compute threshold T of n"]
    stepA3["Self elect CH if rand less T of n"]
    stepA4["CH broadcasts ADV message"]
    stepA5["Non CH nodes send Join REQ"]
    stepA6["CH creates TDMA schedule"]
    stepB["PHASE 2: STEADY STATE PHASE"]
    stepB1["CMs sense and transmit in TDMA slot"]
    stepB2["CH receives data from all members"]
    stepB3["CH performs data aggregation"]
    stepB4["CH transmits fused packet to BS"]
    stepN["END OF ROUND r. Move to r plus 1"]

    stepR --> stepA
    stepA --> stepA1
    stepA1 --> stepA2
    stepA2 --> stepA3
    stepA3 --> stepA4
    stepA4 --> stepA5
    stepA5 --> stepA6
    stepA6 --> stepB
    stepB --> stepB1
    stepB1 --> stepB2
    stepB2 --> stepB3
    stepB3 --> stepB4
    stepB4 --> stepN
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **setup phase** is short and contains all the **decision-making and cluster-formation overhead**. The **steady-state phase** is the **energy-productive phase** where actual data flows. By design, the steady-state phase is **5× to 10× longer** than the setup phase to amortise its overhead.

## 4.3 LEACH Threshold Logic — Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode["Round r begins"]
    checkG["Is n in G? Not been CH in last 1 by P rounds"]
    setTzero["T of n equals 0. Node cannot be CH"]
    computeT["Compute T of n equals P divided by 1 minus P times r mod 1 by P"]
    genRand["Generate random number rand in 0 1"]
    checkRand["Is rand less than T of n?"]
    electCH["Node becomes CH for round r"]
    stayCM["Node remains Cluster Member"]
    broadcastADV["CH broadcasts ADV to all nodes"]
    receiveJoin["CH receives Join REQ from CMs"]
    sendData["CMs sense and transmit data in TDMA slot"]
    aggregateData["CH aggregates and forwards to BS"]

    startNode --> checkG
    checkG -- "NO" --> setTzero
    checkG -- "YES" --> computeT
    computeT --> genRand
    genRand --> checkRand
    checkRand -- "YES" --> electCH
    checkRand -- "NO" --> stayCM
    electCH --> broadcastADV
    broadcastADV --> receiveJoin
    stayCM --> sendData
    receiveJoin --> aggregateData
    sendData --> aggregateData
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This flowchart captures the **complete decision tree** of a single node during a single round. Notice the **two gates**: (1) eligibility gate ($n \in G$?) and (2) probability gate ($\text{rand} < T(n)$?). Both must be passed for a node to be elected CH.

## 4.4 Energy Model Schematic — Radio Energy Dissipation

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcTx["Transmitter"]
    eElecTx["E_elec: Transmit Electronics"]
    eAmp["E_amp: Power Amplifier"]
    eFs["E_fs d squared: Free Space Model"]
    eMp["E_mp d to the 4: Multi Path Model"]
    antTx["Antenna"]
    channel["Wireless Channel"]
    antRx["Antenna"]
    eElecRx["E_elec: Receive Electronics"]
    dstRx["Receiver"]

    srcTx --> eElecTx
    eElecTx --> eAmp
    eAmp --> eFs
    eAmp --> eMp
    eFs --> antTx
    eMp --> antTx
    antTx --> channel
    channel --> antRx
    antRx --> eElecRx
    eElecRx --> dstRx
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **transmit side** consumes both **electronic** energy $E_{elec}$ (digital processing, modulation, filtering) and **amplifier** energy (proportional to $d^2$ or $d^4$). The **receive side** consumes only **electronic** energy $E_{elec}$. This asymmetry is what makes long-range transmission disproportionately expensive.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 marks]`

**Q: Define LEACH protocol. List its two main operational phases.**

**Model Answer:**

LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) is a **hierarchical, cluster-based, energy-efficient routing protocol** for WSNs that uses **randomised rotation of cluster heads** to distribute energy dissipation evenly among all sensor nodes, thereby extending network lifetime.

Its two main operational phases are:

1. **Setup Phase** — Cluster formation, CH election via threshold-based self-election, advertisement broadcast, member joining, and TDMA scheduling.
2. **Steady-State Phase** — Actual data sensing by cluster members, transmission to CHs in TDMA slots, data aggregation at CHs, and aggregated transmission from CHs to the Base Station.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 3 marks]`

**Q: What is the role of the threshold $T(n)$ in LEACH? State its formula.**

**Model Answer:**

The threshold $T(n)$ in LEACH serves as the **probabilistic gate** for cluster-head self-election. Each node generates a random number in $[0, 1]$; if this random number is less than $T(n)$ **and** the node belongs to the eligible set $G$, it elects itself as a CH for the current round. The threshold is dynamically designed to:

- **Inflate** as the round progresses within a cycle, ensuring that all eligible nodes eventually get elected.
- **Vanish** to zero for nodes that have already been CHs in the last $1/P$ rounds, preventing repeated election.

The formula is:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod \frac{1}{P})} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **ANY ONE** from the two alternatives below. Each question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the cluster-head selection algorithm in LEACH with the threshold formula and the meaning of each parameter. Derive the threshold expression from the principle of expected CH count per cycle. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A WSN has $N = 100$ nodes, $P = 0.05$, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal, packet size $L = 4000$ bits, $d_{toBS} = 100$ m. Compute the optimal number of clusters $k_{opt}$ and the total energy per round $E_{round}$. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The CH selection in LEACH is **decentralised** — each node independently decides whether to be a CH. The decision is governed by the threshold $T(n)$. The objective is to ensure that in every cycle of $1/P$ rounds, every node becomes a CH exactly once, so that energy dissipation is **fairly distributed**.

**Parameters:**
- $P$ — desired percentage of CHs per round.
- $r$ — current round number.
- $G$ — set of nodes not yet CHs in the current cycle.
- $T(n)$ — probability threshold for node $n$.

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1):** The expected number of CHs per round is $N \cdot P$. Over a cycle of $1/P$ rounds, this must equal $N$:

$$
N \cdot P \cdot \frac{1}{P} = N
$$

Imposing the conservation constraint and using $|G| = N - NP(r \bmod 1/P)$:

$$
T(n) = \dfrac{P}{1 - P(r \bmod 1/P)}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $k_{opt}$:** Assuming $M = 100$ m field size (standard assumption):

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{M}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} \cdot \dfrac{100}{100^{2}}
$$

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{15.92} \cdot \sqrt{7692.3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{100}
$$

$$
k_{opt} \approx 3.99 \cdot 87.7 \cdot 0.01 \approx 3.5
$$

Rounded to **$k_{opt} = 4$ clusters** (≈ 4% of N, as expected from P).

**Step 2 — Compute $E_{round}$:** Using the standard formula with $d_{toCH}^{2} = M^{2}/(2\pi k) = 10000/(2\pi \times 4) \approx 397.9$ m²:

$$
E_{intra} = (N - k) \cdot L \cdot (\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}) = 96 \times 4000 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 397.9 \approx 0.153 \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{toBS} = k \cdot L \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4} = 4 \times 4000 \times 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{8} = 0.0208 \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{elec\text{-}total} = 2 N L E_{elec} = 2 \times 100 \times 4000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 0.04 \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{agg} = k \cdot L \cdot E_{DA} = 4 \times 4000 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 0.00008 \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{round} \approx 0.153 + 0.0208 + 0.04 + 0.00008 \approx 0.214 \text{ J per round}
$$

**Network lifetime estimate:**

$$
\text{FND (First Node Dies)} = \dfrac{E_0 \cdot N}{E_{round}} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 100}{0.214} \approx 234 \text{ rounds}
$$

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4, Alternative]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **first-order radio energy model** used in LEACH analysis. Derive expressions for transmit and receive energy with both free-space and multi-path components. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Compare LEACH with Direct Transmission and MTE (Minimum Transmission Energy) protocols. Show, with a numerical example, how LEACH extends network lifetime. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The first-order radio model (Heinzelman model) assumes that the radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ per bit for running transmitter/receiver electronics, and uses an amplifier with $\varepsilon_{fs}$ for free-space ($d^2$ power loss) or $\varepsilon_{mp}$ for multi-path ($d^4$ power loss). The receiver dissipates only $E_{elec}$ per bit (no amplifier needed).

**Transmit energy for $k$ bits over distance $d$:**

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{fs} d^{2} \quad \text{(free space, } d < d_{crossover}\text{)}
$$

$$
E_{Tx}(k, d) = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d^{4} \quad \text{(multi-path, } d \geq d_{crossover}\text{)}
$$

**Receive energy:**

$$
E_{Rx}(k) = k E_{elec}
$$

**Crossover distance** (where both models give equal energy):

$$
d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

With the standard parameters: $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{10/0.0013} \approx 87.7$ m.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Direct Transmission | MTE | LEACH |
|---|---|---|---|
| BS comm. | All nodes → BS | All nodes → nearest hop | Only CHs → BS |
| Energy cost (high $d_{toBS}$) | $N \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}$ | Distributed, dynamic | $\approx 0.02 \cdot N \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}$ (5% CHs) |
| Data fusion | None | None | Yes (saves ~5× redundancy) |
| Balance | Terrible | Poor | Excellent |
| Lifetime (relative) | 1× | 2–3× | 5–8× |

**Numerical insight:** With 100 nodes and $d_{toBS} = 100$ m, the direct transmission cost per round is:

$$
E_{direct} = 100 \times 4000 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 100 \times 4000 \times 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{8} \approx 0.572 \text{ J}
$$

LEACH cost per round we computed as $\approx 0.214$ J, which is **~2.7× more efficient** than direct transmission, confirming LEACH's lifetime advantage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the eligibility condition $n \in G$** — Writing only the formula without the conditional (0 otherwise) costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing cycle length with round number** — Students write $T(n) = P/(1 - Pr)$ instead of $P/(1 - P(r \bmod 1/P))$. This is the **#1 error** in LEACH threshold questions.
> 3. **Mixing up free-space vs multi-path models** — Always check whether $d < d_{crossover}$ or $d \geq d_{crossover}$ before choosing the formula. With $d_{toBS} = 100$ m and $d_{crossover} \approx 87.7$ m, the BS transmission uses the $d^4$ model.
> 4. **Ignoring $E_{DA}$ (data aggregation cost)** — Many students forget the 5 nJ/bit signal processing cost at the CH, leading to a wrong $E_{round}$ calculation.
> 5. **Not explaining the "Why"** — KTU board examiners reward conceptual clarity. Merely writing the formula without explaining its purpose (fair rotation) loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LEACH — Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024 ESE)**

- ✅ LEACH = **Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy** — Heinzelman et al., 2000.
- ✅ Two phases: **Setup (cluster formation)** and **Steady-State (data transfer)**.
- ✅ Operates in **rounds**; CHs are re-elected each round via **randomised, decentralised self-election**.
- ✅ Master threshold formula: $T(n) = \dfrac{P}{1 - P \cdot (r \bmod 1/P)}$ for $n \in G$, else $0$.
- ✅ Cycle length = $1/P$ rounds; within a cycle, every node is expected to be CH **exactly once**.
- ✅ Standard parameters: $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal.
- ✅ **Free-space** model for $d < d_{crossover}$; **multi-path** model for $d \geq d_{crossover}$.
