- ✅ $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m with standard parameters.
- ✅ Optimal # clusters: $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/(2\pi)} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}} \cdot M/d_{toBS}^{2}$.
- ✅ Per-round energy: $E_{round} = 2 N L E_{elec} + N L E_{DA} + k L \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} + (N - k) L \varepsilon_{fs} d_{toCH}^{2}$.
- ✅ Assumes **all CHs can reach BS directly** (single-hop to BS) — a known limitation leading to LEACH-C, LEACH-F, TL-LEACH.
- ✅ Assumes **uniform energy distribution initially** — limitation addressed by SEP, DEEC, etc.
- ✅ Does **not** consider node mobility or BS mobility.
- ✅ Advantages: **2×–8× lifetime extension** over direct transmission; data fusion reduces traffic; load balancing via rotation.
- ✅ Disadvantages: **Unequal cluster sizes**, **CHs near BS die faster** (hot-spot), **no scalability beyond ~1000 nodes** without modification.
- ✅ **TDMA-based MAC** with CSMA only during setup; intra-cluster collisions avoided by slot allocation.
- ✅ LEACH is a **single-hop intra-cluster** protocol; multi-hop variants exist (M-LEACH, TL-LEACH).
- ✅ KTU favourite question types: (1) Derive $T(n)$ from first principles, (2) Compute $k_{opt}$ and $E_{round}$, (3) Compare with direct/MTE, (4) Draw the two-tier architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PEGASIS and RPL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PEGASIS and RPL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 PEGASIS (Power-Efficient Gathering in Sensor Information Systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** PEGASIS is a **chain-based, near-optimal, energy-efficient data gathering protocol** for Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) proposed by Lindsey & Raghavendra (2002). It is an enhancement over **LEACH**, where sensor nodes organize themselves into a **linear chain** using a *greedy algorithm*, and only **one node (the chain leader)** transmits the aggregated data to the Base Station (BS) in each round.

**Core Architectural Pillars of PEGASIS:**
1. **Chain Formation:** All nodes form a single chain (each node connected to its closest neighbor).
2. **Leader Selection:** A designated node transmits fused data to the BS.
3. **Data Fusion / Aggregation:** Each node aggregates its own data with the received data from its neighbor (using *signal processing* techniques like beamforming).
4. **Token Passing:** A control token moves along the chain granting transmission rights.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Line":**
> Imagine 100 students standing in a classroom who must pass a single message to the teacher at the front desk. Instead of everyone shouting at once (which wastes energy and causes collisions), each student whispers the combined message to the *next closest* student, who fuses it with their own note. Only the **student nearest the desk** finally announces the answer aloud. PEGASIS works exactly this way — communication distance is minimized to the closest neighbor, dramatically reducing transmission energy.

**Key Distinction from LEACH:**
| Property | LEACH | PEGASIS |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Dynamic Clusters | Static Linear Chain |
| Cluster Heads | Many per round | **One leader per round** |
| Communication Hops | Multi-hop within cluster | **Multi-hop along chain** |
| Overhead | High (cluster setup) | **Low (no clusters)** |
| Delay | Lower | **Higher (chain traversal)** |

---

### 1.2 RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** RPL is a **proactive distance-vector routing protocol** standardized by the **IETF (RFC 6550)** specifically designed for **Low-Power and Lossy Networks (LLNs)** such as IEEE 802.15.4 (6LoWPAN) sensor networks. It constructs a **Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph (DODAG)** rooted at a sink/border router, using an **Objective Function (OF)** to compute the optimal path.

**Core Conceptual Constructs of RPL:**
1. **DODAG** — A tree-like directed graph with **no cycles**, rooted at the sink.
2. **Rank** — A scalar representing each node's *logical distance* from the DODAG root.
3. **Objective Function (OF)** — Defines routing metrics (e.g., *hop count*, *ETX*, *energy*). Standards: **OF0 (RFC 6552)** and **MRHOF (RFC 6719)**.
4. **Trickle Timer** — Adaptive algorithm controlling DIO broadcast frequency.
5. **Control Messages:** DIO, DIS, DAO (and DAO-ACK).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Gravity Well River System":**
> Picture a mountain landscape where rainwater (sensor data) must flow toward the lowest valley (sink/Border Router). RPL is like a **gravity-driven river network** — each drop of water naturally chooses the steepest, smoothest downhill path. Nodes advertise their "elevation" (Rank) and "slope steepness" (Objective Function metric). New tributary nodes join the river by picking the closest downhill parent. **No loops** exist (acyclic), and the topology adapts automatically when terrain changes (links break/restore) using the Trickle algorithm.

**Why RPL was needed (vs. traditional routing):**
- Traditional protocols (AODV, OLSR) cannot operate on memory/CPU-constrained nodes.
- LLNs have **high packet loss, low data rates, and unstable links**.
- RPL supports **three traffic patterns**: **MP2P** (sensors → sink), **P2MP** (sink → sensors), and **P2P** (sensor ↔ sensor).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PEGASIS — Operational Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 Chain Construction (Greedy Algorithm)

The chain is built starting from the **farthest node from BS** to ensure spatial distribution:

1. Node *i* begins with itself in the chain.
2. Node *i* finds its closest unvisited neighbor *j* using Euclidean distance.
3. Node *j* appends to the chain; *j* becomes the new *i*.
4. Continue until all *N* nodes are exhausted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why greedy?** Greedy construction is *O(N²)* but produces a near-optimal chain with minimal total link distance.

#### 2.1.2 Data Gathering (Token-Based)

Each round operates in **three phases**:
1. **Token Passing (Leader → Both Ends):** Leader broadcasts a token to both ends of the chain.
2. **Data Aggregation Toward Leader:** Each non-leader node fuses its data with neighbor's data and transmits in sequence. The leader fuses all incoming data.
3. **Leader → BS Transmission:** Only the leader transmits the final aggregated packet to the BS.

#### 2.1.3 Leader Rotation

If node *i* is leader at round *r*, then at round *r+1*:
$$i_{\text{leader}}(r+1) = (i_{\text{leader}}(r) + 1) \bmod N$$

This ensures **uniform energy drainage** across all nodes.

---

### 2.2 PEGASIS — KTU High-Yield Energy Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **First-Order Radio Model** (Heinzelman, 2000) is the standard energy model used by KTU examiners.

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Transmit Energy ($E_{TX}$) | $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | Energy to send $k$ bits over distance $d$; $n=2$ (free space) or $n=4$ (multipath) |
| Receive Energy ($E_{RX}$) | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Energy to receive $k$ bits |
| Data Aggregation ($E_{DA}$) | $E_{DA}(k) = E_{DA} \cdot k$ | Energy to fuse $k$ bits |
| Path-Loss Exponent ($n$) | $n = 2$ if $d \leq d_0$; else $n = 4$ | $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ is crossover distance |
| **Single Node Energy per Round (non-leader)** | $E_{node} = 2 E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{chain}^{2} + E_{DA} k$ | Each non-leader receives once + transmits once + fuses |
| **Leader Energy per Round** | $E_{leader} = E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2} + (N-2) E_{DA} k + E_{RX}k(N-2)$ | Leader fuses all $N-2$ incoming messages |
| **Total Per-Round Energy** | $E_{round} = N \cdot E_{elec} k + (N-1) \varepsilon_{amp} k \bar{d}_{chain}^{2} + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2} + (N-1) E_{DA} k$ | Sum over all nodes |

**Standard Constants (Heinzelman LEACH model):**

| Symbol | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_{elec}$ | **50 nJ/bit** |
| $\varepsilon_{fs}$ | **10 pJ/bit/m²** (free space) |
| $\varepsilon_{mp}$ | **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** (multipath) |
| $E_{DA}$ | **5 nJ/bit/signal** |
| $d_0$ | **≈ 87.7 m** |

---

### 2.3 RPL — Operational Deep Dive

#### 2.3.1 The Three Control Messages

| Message | Full Name | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DIO** | DODAG Information Object | Root → downward | Carries DODAG config, Rank, OF; advertises upward routes |
| **DIS** | DODAG Information Solicitation | Node → upward | "Solicitation" — request DIO from neighbors (like a router solicitation) |
| **DAO** | Destination Advertisement Object | Node → root | Propagates destination prefixes **upward** for downward routing |
| **DAO-ACK** | DAO Acknowledgment | Root → node | Confirms DAO reception (optional) |

#### 2.3.2 DODAG Construction Steps

1. **Root initialization:** Border router sends DIO with Rank = *MinHopRankIncrease* (root's base rank).
2. **Neighbor discovery:** Nodes multicast DIS; receiving nodes reply with DIO.
3. **Rank computation:** Each node picks a parent minimizing the OF cost and computes its own Rank:
$$\text{Rank}(\text{node}) = \text{Rank}(\text{parent}) + \text{MinHopRankIncrease}(\text{parent})$$
4. **DIO propagation:** Node re-advertises DIO with its own Rank.
5. **DAO propagation:** Node unicasts DAO to its selected parent, building downward routes.
6. **Steady-state:** Trickle timer suppresses redundant DIOs.

#### 2.3.3 Trickle Algorithm (RFC 6206) — Key for KTU

The Trickle algorithm regulates DIO transmission:

| Parameter | Description |
|---|---|
| $I_{min}$ | Minimum interval size (e.g., $2^{4}$ ms = 16 ms) |
| $I_{max}$ | Maximum interval size (e.g., $2^{20}$ ms) |
| $k$ | Redundancy constant (typically 20) |
| $I$ | Current interval $\in [I_{min}, I_{max}]$, doubled after each "consistent" cycle |

**Operating Rule:**
- If received DIOs are **inconsistent** (loop detected / rank changed) → **reset** $I = I_{min}$.
- If consistent and DIOs received $\geq k$ → **suppress** transmission.
- If consistent and DIOs received $< k$ → **transmit** at scheduled time in second half of interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Trickle?** It maintains **scalability, low overhead, and rapid convergence** when topology changes — essential for energy-constrained LLNs.

#### 2.3.4 Objective Functions (OF)

| OF | Metric | Equation (Conceptual) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OF0 (RFC 6552)** | Hop Count | $\text{Rank} = \text{Parent Rank} + \text{Step} \cdot \text{RankFactor}$ | Quickest routing, minimal CPU |
| **MRHOF (RFC 6719)** | ETX (Expected Transmission Count) | $\text{Path\_Cost} = \sum_{i} ETX_i$ | Reliability-focused |

$$ETX = \frac{1}{d_f \cdot d_r}$$
where $d_f$ = forward delivery ratio, $d_r$ = reverse delivery ratio.

#### 2.3.5 RPL Modes of Operation

| Mode | DAO Stored At | Memory | Traffic Patterns Supported |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storing** | All ancestor nodes | High | MP2P, P2MP, P2P |
| **Non-Storing** | Root only | Low (at nodes) | MP2P, P2MP (P2P inefficient) |

#### 2.3.6 RPL Real-World Utility

RPL is the **de-facto routing standard** for:
- **Smart grid AMI** (Advanced Metering Infrastructure)
- **Industrial IoT** (WirelessHART, ISA100.11a influenced by RPL design)
- **Building automation** (Zigbee IP, Thread protocol)
- **Smart cities** (street lighting, environmental monitoring)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thread** (used in Nest/Google Home) is built directly atop 6LoWPAN + RPL.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 PEGASIS — Derivation of Per-Node Energy Dissipation

We derive the **total energy per round** for a PEGASIS network of *N* nodes.

**Step 1 — Identify per-node activities in a round:**

Each non-leader node performs:
- **Receive** 1 message of $k$ bits from its downstream neighbor.
- **Transmit** 1 aggregated message of $k$ bits to its upstream neighbor.
- **Fuse** received data with its own data.

**Step 2 — Apply the First-Order Radio Model:**

The energy consumed by a non-leader node at distance $d_{chain}$ from its neighbor:

$$E_{node} = E_{TX}(k, d_{chain}) + E_{RX}(k) + E_{DA}(k)$$

Substituting the model equations:

$$E_{node} = (E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{chain}^{2}) + E_{elec} k + E_{DA} k$$

Grouping the $E_{elec} k$ terms (note: *receive* + *transmit electronics* both appear once):

$$\boxed{E_{node} = 2 E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{chain}^{2} + E_{DA} k}$$

**Step 3 — Leader node activities:**

The leader:
- **Receives** messages from **two** neighbors (it sits somewhere in the chain, not necessarily at an end).
- **Aggregates** all incoming messages plus its own.
- **Transmits** once to BS at distance $d_{to\_BS}$.

$$E_{leader} = (N-2) \cdot E_{RX}(k) + (N-2) \cdot E_{DA}(k) + E_{TX}(k, d_{to\_BS})$$

Expanding:

$$E_{leader} = (N-2) E_{elec} k + (N-2) E_{DA} k + E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2}$$

Simplifying (combining $(N-2) E_{elec} k + E_{elec} k = (N-1) E_{elec} k$):

$$\boxed{E_{leader} = (N-1) E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2} + (N-2) E_{DA} k}$$

**Step 4 — Total per-round energy:**

Sum over $N-1$ non-leader nodes plus 1 leader:

$$E_{round} = (N-1) E_{node} + E_{leader}$$

Plugging in:

$$E_{round} = (N-1)[2 E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k \bar{d}_{chain}^{2} + E_{DA} k] + (N-1) E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2} + (N-2) E_{DA} k$$

Distributing:

$$E_{round} = 2(N-1) E_{elec} k + (N-1) \varepsilon_{amp} k \bar{d}_{chain}^{2} + (N-1) E_{DA} k + (N-1) E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2} + (N-2) E_{DA} k$$

Combining like terms (the $2(N-1) + (N-1) = 3(N-1)$ for $E_{elec}$; the $(N-1) + (N-2) = 2N-3$ for $E_{DA}$):

$$\boxed{E_{round} = 3(N-1) E_{elec} k + (N-1) \varepsilon_{amp} k \bar{d}_{chain}^{2} + \varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2} + (2N-3) E_{DA} k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The dominant terms are $\varepsilon_{amp} k d_{to\_BS}^{2}$ (single long-haul) and $(N-1) \varepsilon_{amp} k \bar{d}_{chain}^{2}$ (many short hops). PEGASIS minimizes $\bar{d}_{chain}$ via the greedy chain — *that is why it beats LEACH by ~100–300%* in network lifetime.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Example — PEGASIS Energy Calculation (KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:**
- $N = 100$ nodes
- $k = 2000$ bits per packet
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m²
- $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit
- $\bar{d}_{chain} = 5$ m (average link along greedy chain)
- $d_{to\_BS} = 100$ m

**Solution:**

Since $d_{to\_BS} = 100$ m $> d_0 = 87.7$ m, technically multipath ($n=4$). But for exam simplicity with $d_{to\_BS}$ short, we use **free-space model** ($n=2$).

Step 1 — Compute non-leader node energy:

$$E_{node} = 2(50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(5^2) + (5 \times 10^{-9})(2000)$$

$$E_{node} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 5 \times 10^{-7} + 10^{-5}$$

$$E_{node} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 5 \times 10^{-7} + 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$E_{node} \approx 2.105 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 210.5 \; \mu\text{J}$$

Step 2 — Compute leader energy (assume leader is at one end for simplicity, $N-2=98$ receives, transmits to BS):

$$E_{leader} = (99)(50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(100^2) + (98)(5 \times 10^{-9})(2000)$$

$$E_{leader} = 9.9 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-4} + 9.8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$E_{leader} = 9.9 \times 10^{-3} + 0.2 \times 10^{-3} + 0.98 \times 10^{-3} = 11.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} \approx 11.08 \text{ mJ}$$

Step 3 — Total per-round energy:

$$E_{round} = (99)(2.105 \times 10^{-4}) + 11.08 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$E_{round} = 2.084 \times 10^{-2} + 1.108 \times 10^{-2} = 3.192 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} \approx 31.92 \text{ mJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observations for exam:** The leader consumes $\approx 50\times$ the energy of a non-leader node. This is precisely why **leader rotation** is essential — otherwise the leader drains its battery first, causing network failure (the **"hot-spot" problem**).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PEGASIS vs RPL vs LEACH

| Criterion | LEACH | PEGASIS | RPL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topology Type | Cluster-based | Chain-based | DODAG (tree/graph) |
| Routing Paradigm | Reactive/Hierarchical | Greedy chain + leader | Proactive distance-vector |
| Scalability | Medium | Poor (chain delay) | High (DODAG scales) |
| Energy Efficiency | Moderate | High (~2× LEACH) | OF-dependent |
| Delay per Round | Low | High (chain traversal) | Very Low |
| Mobility Support | Poor | Poor | Moderate (DIO trickle) |
| Application | Periodic monitoring | Periodic monitoring | Event-driven + periodic LLNs |
| Standardization | Research protocol | Research protocol | **IETF RFC 6550** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PEGASIS Chain Topology and Round Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Node 1\nEnd A"] -- "d1" --> node2["Node 2"]
    node2 -- "d2" --> node3["Node 3\nCurrent Leader"]
    node3 -- "d3" --> node4["Node 4"]
    node4 -- "d4" --> node5["Node 5\nEnd B"]
    node3 -. "Aggregated Data\nto BS" .-> bsNode["BS\nBase Station"]

    tokenStart["Token from Leader"] -. "Pass Right" .-> node4
    tokenStart -. "Pass Left" .-> node2
```

**Description of flow:** The current leader (Node 3) issues a token. Node 4 receives, fuses its data, transmits to leader. Node 2 receives, fuses, transmits to leader. Leader fuses all and transmits once to BS.

---

### 4.2 PEGASIS Round Phases (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phaseA["Phase A\nChain Construction\nGreedy Algorithm"] --> phaseB["Phase B\nLeader Selection\nRotational r mod N"]
    phaseB --> phaseC["Phase C\nToken Dispatch\nLeader broadcasts token"]
    phaseC --> phaseD["Phase D\nData Aggregation\nEach node fuses k bits"]
    phaseD --> phaseE["Phase E\nLeader to BS Transmission\nSingle long-haul TX"]
    phaseE --> phaseF["Phase F\nRound Complete\nIncrement r by 1"]
    phaseF -. "Restart" .-> phaseA
```

---

### 4.3 RPL DODAG Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootNode["Border Router / Sink\nRank = 1\nDIO Source"]

    rootNode -- "DIO\nParent" --> l1n1["Node A\nRank = 4"]
    rootNode -- "DIO\nParent" --> l1n2["Node B\nRank = 4"]

    l1n1 -- "DIO" --> l2n1["Node C\nRank = 8"]
    l1n1 -- "DIO" --> l2n2["Node D\nRank = 8"]
    l1n2 -- "DIO" --> l2n3["Node E\nRank = 8"]

    l2n1 -- "DAO\nUpward" --> l1n1
    l2n2 -- "DAO\nUpward" --> l1n1
    l2n3 -- "DAO\nUpward" --> l1n2
    l1n1 -- "DAO\nUpward" --> rootNode
    l1n2 -- "DAO\nUpward" --> rootNode

    l2n1 -. "Application Data\nMP2P" .-> rootNode
    l2n2 -. "App Data" .-> rootNode
    l2n3 -. "App Data" .-> rootNode
    rootNode -. "Config / P2MP" .-> l2n1

    noteNote["DODAG Root is the\ndestination for MP2P\nsource for P2MP"]
```

---

### 4.4 RPL Trickle Timer State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateImin
    StateImin --> Listening: "Start Interval I = Imin"
    Listening --> ConsistentCheck: "Receive DIOs during interval"
    ConsistentCheck --> Suppressed: "DIOs received >= k\n& Consistent"
    ConsistentCheck --> TransmitDIO: "DIOs received < k\nor Inconsistent"
    Suppressed --> DoubleInterval: "I = 2I (if I < Imax)"
    DoubleInterval --> Listening: "New I cycle"
    TransmitDIO --> ResetImin: "I = Imin (inconsistency)"
    ResetImin --> Listening: "Fast re-convergence"
    DoubleInterval --> CapInterval: "I = Imax"
    CapInterval --> Listening: "Cap reached"
```

---

### 4.5 Comparative Protocol Stack — Where PEGASIS and RPL Sit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["Application Layer\nCOAP, MQTT"]
    transportLayer["Transport Layer\nUDP"]
    networkLayer["Network Layer\n6LoWPAN + RPL / PEGASIS"]
    macLayer["MAC Layer\nIEEE 802.15.4 / S-MAC"]
    phyLayer["Physical Layer\nRadio Transceiver"]

    appLayer --> transportLayer --> networkLayer --> macLayer --> phyLayer

    pegasisBox["PEGASIS is an\nApplication/Cross-layer\nData Gathering Protocol\nUsed directly over MAC"]
    pegasisBox -. "Alternative Path" .-> macLayer

    rplBox["RPL is a Network Layer\nRouting Protocol\nStandardized for LLNs"]
    rplBox -. "Native Path" .-> networkLayer
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List the four control message types in RPL. Which one is used for downward route construction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **DIO** (DODAG Information Object) — used by root to advertise DODAG configuration and Rank information downward.
- **DIS** (DODAG Information Solicitation) — sent by a node to solicit DIOs from neighbors (similar to router solicitation in IPv6 NDP).
- **DAO** (Destination Advertisement Object) — used by nodes to advertise their prefixes *upward* so that the root can build downward routes.
- **DAO-ACK** — acknowledgment of DAO from the root.

**Downward route construction is performed using DAO messages** (unicast upward to selected parents and propagated toward the root). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Model]**
*Explain the "token passing" mechanism in PEGASIS. Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The leader in PEGASIS broadcasts a **control token** along the chain in both directions. Only the node currently holding the token is permitted to transmit its data toward the leader. This mechanism is necessary to:
1. **Avoid collisions** when multiple nodes transmit simultaneously.
2. **Provide TDMA-like scheduling** without explicit time-slot allocation overhead.
3. **Enable sequential, in-network data aggregation** so each hop fuses one packet, keeping total transmissions minimal.

This is essential for power efficiency since each non-leader node performs **only one transmission per round**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE

#### Question A (14 Marks) — PEGASIS-Focused

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of the PEGASIS protocol. Discuss chain formation using the greedy algorithm and leader rotation policy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEGASIS network has $N=50$ nodes uniformly distributed in a $100 \times 100$ m² field. The BS is at the field's center. Given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit, $k = 4000$ bits, $\bar{d}_{chain} = 4$ m, $d_{to\_BS} = 70$ m, compute:
- (i) The energy dissipated by a **non-leader** node per round. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The energy dissipated by the **leader** node per round. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The total **per-round energy** of the network. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) PEGASIS Operation (7 marks):**

PEGASIS is a chain-based protocol that improves on LEACH by eliminating cluster formation overhead. The protocol operates in **three phases per round**:

**Phase 1 – Chain Formation (Greedy Algorithm):**
- All nodes know their locations (via GPS or signal-strength estimation).
- Starting from the **farthest node from BS**, each node appends its **nearest unvisited neighbor** to the chain.
- This produces a near-minimal total link length.
- Chain construction is performed **only once at network deployment** and reused (with repairs when nodes die).

**Phase 2 – Leader Selection:**
- A node is designated as leader for round $r$ as: $i(r) = (i(r-1) + 1) \bmod N$.
- **Rotation ensures uniform energy dissipation** across nodes.
- Alternatively, a node may volunteer as leader if its residual energy is above a threshold (energy-aware variant).

**Phase 3 – Data Gathering (per round):**
1. Leader sends a control token to both ends of the chain.
2. End nodes transmit to next neighbor; each node fuses received + own data and transmits toward leader.
3. Leader receives fused data, aggregates, and transmits **one packet** to the BS.

**Advantages:** 100–300% longer lifetime than LEACH; no cluster overhead; minimal transmissions per round.
**Disadvantages:** High delay (chain traversal); single leader = bottleneck; greedy chain assumes global location knowledge. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Since $d_{to\_BS} = 70$ m $< d_0 \approx 87.7$ m, use **free-space model** ($n=2$).

**(i) Non-leader node energy:**

$$E_{node} = 2 E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{fs} k \bar{d}_{chain}^{2} + E_{DA} k$$

Compute each term:
- $2 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 4000 = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- $(10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4000 \cdot 16 = 6.4 \times 10^{-7}$ J
- $(5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 4000 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ J

$$E_{node} = 4 \times 10^{-4} + 6.4 \times 10^{-7} + 2 \times 10^{-5} = 4.206 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 420.6 \; \mu\text{J}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Leader node energy:**

$$E_{leader} = (N-1) E_{elec} k + \varepsilon_{fs} k d_{to\_BS}^{2} + (N-2) E_{DA} k$$

- $(49) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 4000 = 9.8 \times 10^{-3}$ J
- $(10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 4000 \cdot 4900 = 1.96 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- $(48) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 4000 = 9.6 \times 10^{-4}$ J

$$E_{leader} = 9.8 \times 10^{-3} + 1.96 \times 10^{-4} + 9.6 \times 10^{-4} = 1.096 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} \approx 10.96 \text{ mJ}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Total per-round energy:**

$$E_{round} = (N-1) E_{node} + E_{leader} = 49 \times 4.206 \times 10^{-4} + 1.096 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$E_{round} = 2.061 \times 10^{-2} + 1.096 \times 10^{-2} = 3.157 \times 10^{-2} \text{ J} \approx 31.57 \text{ mJ}$$ **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — RPL-Focused

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the RPL routing protocol with reference to:
- (i) DODAG construction and Rank computation. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The three/four control message types. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Objective Functions OF0 and MRHOF. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Trickle algorithm** used in RPL. Explain its state transitions and how it responds to network inconsistencies. Why is Trickle preferred over periodic broadcasting? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) RPL Routing Protocol (7 marks):**

**(i) DODAG Construction & Rank (3 marks):**
A DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) is a logical tree rooted at a **Border Router / Sink**. The root initiates construction by broadcasting DIO messages containing its Rank. Receiving nodes compute their Rank using the **Objective Function**:

$$\text{Rank}(\text{node}) = \text{Rank}(\text{parent}) + \text{parent's MinHopRankIncrease}$$

Each node selects **one or more parents** that minimize the OF cost (default: minimal Rank). The Rank strictly increases along any downward path, which **prevents routing loops** (acyclic property). Multiple parents allow **multipath routing** for reliability.

**(ii) Control Messages (2 marks):**
- **DIO** — Root → downward; carries DODAG config, Rank, OF parameters.
- **DIS** — Node → upward; solicits DIO from neighbors (used when a node joins or detects an inconsistency).
- **DAO** — Node → upward (unicast to parent); advertises node's prefixes for **downward route support**.
- **DAO-ACK** — Optional acknowledgment from root.

**(iii) Objective Functions (2 marks):**
- **OF0 (RFC 6552):** Uses **hop count** as the metric. Simple, low overhead, suitable for CPU-limited nodes.
$$\text{Rank}_{\text{OF0}} = \text{ParentRank} + \text{Step} \cdot \text{RankFactor}$$
- **MRHOF (RFC 6719):** Uses **ETX (Expected Transmission Count)** to find the path with minimum packet retransmissions.
$$\text{ETX}_{\text{link}} = \frac{1}{d_f \cdot d_r}, \quad \text{PathCost} = \sum \text{ETX}_{\text{links}}$$
MRHOF supports metrics like node energy and latency (extensible).

---

**(b) Trickle Algorithm (7 marks):**

The Trickle algorithm (RFC 6206) regulates **how often a node transmits DIO messages** in RPL. It balances **rapid convergence** with **low control overhead**.

**State Transitions:**
1. **Initialize:** Set current interval $I \in [I_{min}, I_{max}]$ and transmission counter $c = 0$.
2. **At interval start:** Reset $c = 0$ and select a random time $t$ in $[I/2, I]$.
3. **Listen phase:** During $[0, I/2]$, count DIOs received:
   - If $c < k$ (redundancy constant, default 20) AND DIOs are **inconsistent** → transmit at $t$.
   - If $c \geq k$ AND DIOs are **consistent** → suppress transmission.
4. **At interval end:** If consistent, double $I$ (capped at $I_{max}$). If inconsistent, reset $I = I_{min}$.

**Response to Inconsistency:**
When a node detects an inconsistency (e.g., loop, rank change, neighbor unreachable), Trickle **resets $I$ to $I_{min}$**, ensuring rapid DIO re-broadcasting to converge quickly.

**Why Trickle over Periodic Broadcasting:**
- **Adaptive:** Doubles interval in stable topology → saves energy; shrinks on instability → fast healing.
- **Scalable:** Suppression mechanism avoids broadcast storms in dense LLNs.
- **Energy-efficient:** Consistent cycles have exponentially fewer transmissions.

For a network with 1000 nodes, periodic broadcast would mean **1000 packets per period**. Trickle reduces this to **roughly $\log_2(I_{max}/I_{min})$ transmissions per node per convergence event**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing DIO direction** — DIO flows **downward** (root → leaves); DAO flows **upward** (leaves → root). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this reversal.
> 2. **Wrong energy formula for leader** — Students often forget that the leader performs **both** TX-to-BS and RX-from-neighbors. Use $E_{leader} = (N-1)E_{elec}k + \varepsilon k d_{BS}^2 + (N-2)E_{DA}k$.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit means $\times 10^{-9}$. Misplaced decimal = full-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing PEGASIS with hierarchical PEGASIS (H-PEGASIS)** — H-PEGASIS uses signal-processing-based *beamforming* aggregation (binary tree). Examiners expect the **linear-chain** version unless explicitly specified.
> 5. **Stating RPL supports reactive routing** — RPL is **proactive** (DIO-driven). Only P2P on-demand uses source routing extensions.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **PEGASIS** = Linear-chain protocol; one leader/round; **greedy chain** built once at deployment.
- ✅ Leader rotation prevents **hot-spot failure** ($i(r) = (i(r-1) + 1) \bmod N$).
- ✅ Energy model uses **Heinzelman First-Order Radio Model** with constants $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m².
- ✅ **Non-leader**: $E_{node} = 2 E_{elec}k + \varepsilon k d_{chain}^2 + E_{DA}k$.
- ✅ **Leader**: $E_{leader} = (N-1) E_{elec}k + \varepsilon k d_{BS}^2 + (N-2) E_{DA}k$.
- ✅ PEGASIS advantages: **100–300% longer lifetime** than LEACH; minimal cluster overhead.
- ✅ PEGASIS disadvantages: **High delay**, **single-leader bottleneck**, **single-point failure risk**.
- ✅ **RPL** = IETF RFC 6550 standard for **LLNs / 6LoWPAN**.
- ✅ DODAG = Acyclic tree rooted at border router; **Rank increases monotonically** downward.
- ✅ Four control messages: **DIO, DIS, DAO, DAO-ACK**.
- ✅ **DIO flows downward**, **DAO flows upward** (this is the most-tested fact).
- ✅ Two main **Objective Functions**: **OF0 (hop count)** and **MRHOF (ETX)**.
- ✅ **Trickle Algorithm** regulates DIOs: $I$ doubles on consistency, resets to $I_{min}$ on inconsistency.
- ✅ **Trickle redundancy constant $k = 20$** by default (suppression if $\geq k$ consistent DIOs received).
- ✅ **Storing vs Non-Storing modes**: storing = DAO cached at all ancestors; non-storing = only at root.
- ✅ RPL supports **MP2P, P2MP, and P2P** traffic patterns.
- ✅ RPL is used in **Zigbee IP, Thread, Smart Grid AMI, Industrial IoT**.
- ✅ Comparison: **PEGASIS = periodic monitoring with aggregation**; **RPL = general-purpose LLN routing with multi-metric flexibility**.
- ✅ Trickle's **suppression mechanism** is the key scalability feature for energy-constrained LLNs.
- ✅ Rank computation: $\text{Rank}(\text{node}) = \text{Rank}(\text{parent}) + \text{parent.MinHopRankIncrease}$.
- ✅ $ETX = \frac{1}{d_f \cdot d_r}$ — always appear in KTU derivations involving MRHOF.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Operating Systems and Execution Environments
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**, the **Operating System (OS)** refers to a highly specialized, resource-constrained system software that manages the hardware resources, scheduling, communication, and application execution on individual sensor nodes (motes). Unlike traditional desktop/server operating systems, a WSN operating system is designed to operate within severe constraints of **energy (typically 1–3 V battery, < 100 mW)**, **memory (typically 10–512 KB RAM, 48–1024 KB ROM)**, **processing capability (8/16/32-bit microcontrollers, 4–80 MHz)**, and **bandwidth**.

An **Execution Environment (EE)** in WSN is the layered software/hardware substrate that provides abstraction between the raw mote hardware and the application logic. It encompasses the operating system, hardware abstraction layer (HAL), middleware services (routing, localization, time synchronization), programming abstractions (components, protothreads, threads), and runtime libraries that collectively enable sensor application programs to be developed, deployed, and executed efficiently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Wireless Sensor Network operating system is a *resource-aware, application-specific, often event-driven or multithreaded kernel* that provides primitives for concurrency, communication, power management, and hardware abstraction while fitting within the tiny footprint of a sensor mote.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a sensor node as a **tiny, battery-powered field worker** assigned to a remote forest to continuously monitor temperature, humidity, and motion. The Operating System is like the **shift manager and rulebook** of this worker:

- It tells the worker **when to sleep** (to save battery) and **when to wake up** to take readings.
- It provides **standard tools** (sensors, radio) and **handles** so the worker doesn't need to manually operate the bare metal.
- It enforces a **discipline** — if a fire alarm (event) is detected, the worker immediately reacts without waiting in a queue for hours.

The **Execution Environment** is the **entire toolkit, training manual, communication radio, and supervisor** that allows the field worker to do the job reliably across many forests (network) and many workers (nodes). Just as a field worker cannot run a heavy "Windows-like" application on a 4-bit battery device, a sensor node cannot run a traditional OS — hence the need for **TinyOS, Contiki, RIOT, LiteOS, and Zephyr**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **OS** = Kernel + Scheduler + Drivers (core software inside the node)
> - **Execution Environment** = OS + Middleware + Programming Model + Toolchain (the whole runtime stack a developer sees)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Hardware Constraints

The following are the standard hardware specifications a KTU student must remember for WSN OS design:

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|--------|--------------|------|
| Microcontroller | 8/16/32-bit (e.g., **Atmel AVR ATmega128**, **TI MSP430**, **ARM Cortex-M**) | bits |
| Clock Frequency | **4 – 80** | MHz |
| RAM | **4 – 512** | KB |
| ROM / Flash | **48 – 1024** | KB |
| Battery | **1.5 – 3.6** | V |
| Transceiver | **CC2420, CC2520, RF231** (IEEE 802.15.4) | — |
| Typical Radio Range | **10 – 100** | m |
| Power (active TX) | **~ 30 – 60** | mA |
| Power (sleep) | **~ 1 – 10** | µA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These severe constraints (especially the **8–80 MHz** CPU and **few hundred KB of memory**) are the **driving force** behind specialized WSN operating systems — general-purpose OS like Linux simply cannot fit.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a WSN Operating System

A typical WSN OS is organized into the following stacked layers (from bottom to top):

1. **Hardware Layer** — Mote hardware (MCU, radio, sensors, memory).
2. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** — Thin software layer that hides hardware-specific registers, GPIO, ADC, and timers from upper layers.
3. **Kernel / Microkernel** — Scheduler, interrupt handlers, power manager, memory allocator, and basic synchronization primitives.
4. **System Services Layer** — Networking stack (e.g., **6LoWPAN, RPL, IEEE 802.15.4 MAC**), timers, file systems (TinyDB, Coffee), and security primitives.
5. **Programming Model / Application Layer** — Component-based, event-driven, protothread-based, or multithreaded abstractions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture?** Because sensor applications are typically simple, single-purpose, and long-lived (months to years), the OS favors **asynchronous, event-driven designs** over heavy preemptive multitasking.

## 2.2 Classification of WSN Operating Systems

WSN operating systems can be broadly classified along two axes:

### Axis 1: Scheduling Model

- **Event-Driven (asynchronous):** *Examples — TinyOS, Mantis OS (early versions), SOS.* The kernel executes small *tasks* in response to *events* (interrupts, timer expiry, message arrival). No preemption; extremely small footprint (~ **400 bytes** RAM).
- **Multithreaded (preemptive):** *Examples — Contiki (with protothreads + library), Zephyr, RIOT, FreeRTOS.* Supports concurrent threads (cooperative or preemptive), with a thread control block (~ **64–256 bytes** per thread).
- **Hybrid:** Combines event-driven kernel with optional multithreading (e.g., **Contiki** offers both *protothreads* and *events*).

### Axis 2: Programming Model

- **Component-Based:** *TinyOS / nesC.* Applications are composed of reusable software components wired together at compile time.
- **Protothread-Based:** *Contiki.* Lightweight stackless threads using `PT_THREAD` macros; saves stack memory.
- **Standard C / C++ API:** *Zephyr, RIOT.* Familiar POSIX-like APIs.

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Major WSN Operating Systems

### 2.3.1 TinyOS

- **Developed by:** UC Berkeley (open-source).
- **Programming Language:** **nesC** (network embedded systems C) — a dialect of C with component-oriented semantics.
- **Execution Model:** **Event-driven, two-level scheduling** (Tasks + Hardware Event Handlers).
- **Concurrency Model:** *Split-phase operations* — long-running operations are split into a request and a completion event.
- **Memory Footprint:** ~ **400 bytes** RAM, ~ **4 KB** ROM (minimum configuration).
- **Key Feature:** **Compile-time component composition** — all wiring is done statically, yielding highly optimized code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tasks** in TinyOS are non-preemptive, run-to-completion, FIFO-scheduled. **Hardware Event Handlers** can preempt tasks and other events at the same priority.

### 2.3.2 Contiki

- **Developed by:** Adam Dunkels (SICS, Sweden).
- **Programming Language:** Standard C with **protothreads** and **events**.
- **Execution Model:** Event-driven kernel with optional multithreading via **protothreads** (stackless threads, ~ 2 bytes per thread).
- **Networking Stack:** Built-in **uIP** (TCP/IP for 8-bit) or **uIPv6** (6LoWPAN) stack.
- **Memory Footprint:** ~ **2 KB** RAM for the kernel.
- **Key Feature:** **Loadable modules** at runtime via the *dynamic loader* (uncommon in WSN).

### 2.3.3 RIOT

- **Developed by:** RIOT OS community (FU Berlin, INRIA).
- **Programming Language:** C and C++.
- **Execution Model:** **Preemptive, priority-based, multithreaded** with full POSIX API support.
- **Memory Footprint:** ~ **1.5 KB** RAM (minimal kernel).
- **Key Feature:** Real-time guarantees, **tickless scheduler**, and **developer-friendly** Linux-like API.

### 2.3.4 Zephyr

- **Developed by:** Linux Foundation (with Intel, Nordic, NXP).
- **Programming Language:** C with minimal C++ support.
- **Execution Model:** **Preemptive, priority-based, multithreaded** with fibers and work queues.
- **Memory Footprint:** ~ **8 KB** RAM (minimum).
- **Key Feature:** Strong industry backing, **BLE + IEEE 802.15.4** support, LTS releases.

### 2.3.5 LiteOS

- **Developed by:** Huawei (LiteOS Foundation).
- **Programming Model:** Component-based, hierarchical.
- **Key Feature:** LiteOS Studio IDE, file system, network stack for IoT/WSN.

### 2.3.6 Comparison Snapshot

| Feature | TinyOS | Contiki | RIOT | Zephyr |
|---------|--------|---------|------|--------|
| Scheduling | Event-driven | Event + Protothread | Preemptive | Preemptive |
| Language | nesC | C | C / C++ | C |
| Min RAM | ~ **400 B** | ~ **2 KB** | ~ **1.5 KB** | ~ **8 KB** |
| Min ROM | ~ **4 KB** | ~ **30 KB** | ~ **5 KB** | ~ **32 KB** |
| API Style | Component | Protothread | POSIX-like | POSIX-like |
| Real-Time | No | Soft | Yes (hard) | Yes (hard) |
| Network | Active Messages | uIP / uIPv6 / RPL | 6LoWPAN / RPL | 802.15.4 / BLE |

## 2.4 Execution Environment (EE) — The Broader Stack

The **execution environment** is broader than the OS alone. It defines the *contract* between the application and the underlying hardware/OS. Major layers include:

1. **Hardware Platform** (Tmote Sky, MicaZ, IRIS, Waspmote, Arduino-based motes).
2. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL).**
3. **Kernel** (scheduler + memory manager + interrupt controller).
4. **System Services** (file system, energy manager, radio stack, time sync).
5. **Middleware / Programming Model** (TinyDB, Agilla, Mate, Regimen, SOS API).
6. **Application Layer.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Middleware in WSN** provides network-wide services like *data aggregation, query processing (TinyDB), mobile code injection (Maté), and replication.* A **Programming Model** provides the abstraction (events, components, protothreads) used by the application developer.

## 2.5 Design Goals Driving WSN OS / EE Choices

- **Energy Efficiency:** Scheduler must prefer sleep states (Duty Cycle < 1%).
- **Small Footprint:** Code must fit in < 100 KB ROM.
- **Reactive Concurrency:** Must respond to radio/ADC events within microseconds.
- **Robustness:** Must survive years unattended without reboot.
- **Flexibility:** Must allow reprogramming (e.g., Deluge, Trickle) over the air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Constraint:** A typical WSN OS aims for interrupt latency in the range of **1–10 microseconds** and task scheduling latency in the range of **100 microseconds – 10 milliseconds**, depending on whether soft or hard real-time guarantees are needed.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Memory & Energy Quantitative Relationships

The following equations are routinely tested in KTU examinations to relate duty cycling, current draw, and battery lifetime:

$$
T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}
$$

where $C_{\text{bat}}$ is the battery capacity in **mAh** and $I_{\text{avg}}$ is the average current consumption in **mA**.

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \alpha \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **duty cycle fraction** (active time / total time), $I_{\text{active}}$ is the active current, and $I_{\text{sleep}}$ is the sleep current.

$$
\alpha = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}
$$

where $T_{\text{on}}$ and $T_{\text{off}}$ are the active and sleep durations in seconds.

### Real-Time Scheduling Latency

$$
T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{interrupt}} + T_{\text{schedule}} + T_{\text{preempt}}
$$

where $T_{\text{interrupt}}$ is the hardware interrupt latency, $T_{\text{schedule}}$ is the dispatcher delay, and $T_{\text{preempt}}$ is the preemption overhead.

### Memory Footprint Estimation

$$
M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{kernel}} + N_{\text{threads}} \cdot M_{\text{TCB}} + N_{\text{queues}} \cdot M_{\text{queue}} + M_{\text{stack}}
$$

where $M_{\text{TCB}}$ is the per-thread control block size (~ 64–256 bytes), $M_{\text{queue}}$ is the per-queue overhead, and $M_{\text{stack}}$ is the stack memory.

| Formula / Concept | Symbol | Units | Typical WSN Value |
|-------------------|--------|-------|-------------------|
| Battery Lifetime | $T_{\text{lifetime}}$ | hours / days / years | **1–5 years** |
| Average Current | $I_{\text{avg}}$ | mA | **0.1 – 5** |
| Active Current | $I_{\text{active}}$ | mA | **20 – 60** |
| Sleep Current | $I_{\text{sleep}}$ | µA | **1 – 10** |
| Duty Cycle | $\alpha$ | dimensionless | **0.001 – 0.1** |
| Interrupt Latency | $T_{\text{interrupt}}$ | µs | **1 – 10** |
| Per-Thread Memory | $M_{\text{TCB}}$ | bytes | **64 – 256** |
| Protothread Memory | $M_{\text{PT}}$ | bytes | **~ 2** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Battery Lifetime vs Duty Cycle

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A sensor node draws $I_{\text{active}} = 30\ \text{mA}$ when awake and $I_{\text{sleep}} = 10\ \mu\text{A}$ when asleep. It operates on two AA alkaline batteries with total capacity $C_{\text{bat}} = 2500\ \text{mAh}$. The node wakes up for $T_{\text{on}} = 10\ \text{ms}$ every $T_{\text{period}} = 1\ \text{s}$. Compute the average current, the duty cycle, and the total battery lifetime in days.

### Step 1 — Compute the Duty Cycle Fraction

$$
\alpha = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}} = \frac{10\ \text{ms}}{10\ \text{ms} + (1\ \text{s} - 10\ \text{ms})}
$$

Convert units consistently — both numerator and denominator to milliseconds:

$$
\alpha = \frac{10}{10 + 990} = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01
$$

So the duty cycle is **1%**.

### Step 2 — Compute the Average Current

Convert $I_{\text{sleep}}$ from µA to mA for consistency:

$$
I_{\text{sleep}} = 10\ \mu\text{A} = 0.010\ \text{mA}
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \alpha \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (0.01)(30) + (0.99)(0.010)
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 0.30 + 0.0099 = 0.3099\ \text{mA}
$$

So $I_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.31\ \text{mA}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Battery Lifetime

$$
T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{2500\ \text{mAh}}{0.31\ \text{mA}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{lifetime}} = 8064.5\ \text{hours}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Days

$$
T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{8064.5}{24} = 336.0\ \text{days}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Years

$$
T_{\text{lifetime}} = \frac{336.0}{365.25} = 0.92\ \text{years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** By sleeping **99% of the time**, the node's lifetime extends from a few days (if always on) to nearly **one full year** on two AA cells. This is precisely why WSN OSes aggressively duty-cycle the CPU and radio.

## 3.2 Derivation: Memory Footprint for a Multithreaded WSN Kernel

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A WSN OS uses a multithreaded kernel. Each thread requires:
- Thread Control Block (TCB): **128 bytes**
- Stack space per thread: **256 bytes**
- Queue overhead per queue: **32 bytes**

The system runs **8 threads** and uses **3 queues**. The kernel itself uses **1.5 KB RAM**. Find the total RAM usage.

### Step 1 — Compute Memory for Threads

$$
M_{\text{threads}} = N_{\text{threads}} \cdot (M_{\text{TCB}} + M_{\text{stack}})
$$

$$
M_{\text{threads}} = 8 \cdot (128 + 256) = 8 \cdot 384 = 3072\ \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Memory for Queues

$$
M_{\text{queues}} = N_{\text{queues}} \cdot M_{\text{queue}} = 3 \cdot 32 = 96\ \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Memory

$$
M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{kernel}} + M_{\text{threads}} + M_{\text{queues}}
$$

$$
M_{\text{total}} = 1536 + 3072 + 96 = 4704\ \text{bytes}
$$

So the total memory footprint is **4.7 KB** of RAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** This is why **protothreads** (only 2 bytes per thread) are favored in deeply constrained nodes — for 8 threads, protothreads save $(384 - 2) \cdot 8 = 3056$ bytes — a **huge** saving in a 10 KB RAM mote.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: WSN OS Selection Decision

| Application Requirement | Best OS Choice | Justification |
|-------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Lowest memory footprint (< 1 KB RAM) | **TinyOS** | Event-driven, no per-thread overhead |
| Need TCP/IP over 802.15.4 (6LoWPAN) | **Contiki** | Built-in uIPv6 stack |
| Hard real-time deadlines | **RIOT / Zephyr** | Preemptive priority scheduling |
| Industrial IoT, long-term support | **Zephyr** | LTS, industry backing |
| Easy programming in C++ with POSIX | **RIOT** | POSIX-like API, full C++ |
| Compile-time component reuse | **TinyOS / nesC** | Static component wiring |
| Programming flexibility (loadable modules) | **Contiki** | Dynamic loader support |
| BLE + 802.15.4 dual-stack | **Zephyr** | Native multi-protocol support |

## 3.4 Comparative Numerical Example: Event vs Threaded Kernel

**Scenario:** Two nodes — Node A runs TinyOS (event-driven), Node B runs RIOT (preemptive multithreaded). Both process 5 sensor events per second; each event takes 2 ms of CPU work.

### Node A (TinyOS):
Each event spawns a *task* that runs to completion. Worst-case scheduling latency is the sum of all pending tasks:

$$
L_{\text{A,max}} = N_{\text{events}} \cdot t_{\text{task}} = 5 \cdot 2 = 10\ \text{ms}
$$

If a higher-priority event arrives during task execution, it must **wait** until the current task finishes — this is the *head-of-line blocking* problem.

### Node B (RIOT):
Each event is handled by a separate thread with priority. Worst-case latency for the highest-priority event is:

$$
L_{\text{B,max}} = t_{\text{preempt}} \approx 10\ \mu\text{s}
$$

But Node B uses more memory: $5 \cdot 384 = 1920$ bytes for TCBs + stacks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Event-driven designs minimize memory but introduce **head-of-line blocking**. Preemptive designs minimize latency but consume more memory. The choice is a **fundamental engineering trade-off**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Layered Execution Environment of a WSN Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HW1["HARDWARE LAYER - MCU - RADIO - SENSORS - MEMORY"]
    HAL1["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER - GPIO - ADC - TIMERS - SPI I2C DRIVERS"]
    KER1["KERNEL - SCHEDULER - INTERRUPT HANDLER - MEMORY MANAGER - POWER MANAGER"]
    SVC1["SYSTEM SERVICES - NETWORK STACK 802.15.4 - 6LOWPAN - RPL - TIMERS - FILE SYSTEM"]
    MID1["MIDDLEWARE - DATA AGGREGATION - QUERY ENGINE - REPLICATION - TIME SYNC"]
    APP1["APPLICATION LAYER - SENSING LOGIC - ACTUATION - USER CODE"]
    HW1 --> HAL1
    HAL1 --> KER1
    KER1 --> SVC1
    SVC1 --> MID1
    MID1 --> APP1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram illustrates the **stack of execution environment** inside a single sensor node. The OS (kernel + HAL) sits between raw hardware and the application, while middleware provides network-wide abstractions above the OS.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: TinyOS Two-Level Scheduling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INT1["HARDWARE INTERRUPT - RADIO PACKET ARRIVAL - ADC DONE - TIMER TICK"]
    HEH1["HARDWARE EVENT HANDLER - PRIORITY HIGHEST - PREEMPTS TASKS - RUNS TO COMPLETION"]
    TQ1["TASK QUEUE - FIFO"]
    TSK1["TASK 1 - LIGHTWEIGHT JOB"]
    TSK2["TASK 2 - LIGHTWEIGHT JOB"]
    TSKN["TASK N - LIGHTWEIGHT JOB"]
    SCH1["SCHEDULER DISPATCHES NEXT TASK"]
    INT1 --> HEH1
    HEH1 --> TQ1
    TQ1 --> TSK1
    TQ1 --> TSK2
    TQ1 --> TSKN
    TSK1 --> SCH1
    TSK2 --> SCH1
    TSKN --> SCH1
```

> [!NOTE]
> TinyOS uses a **strict two-level priority**: Hardware Event Handlers preempt everything; Tasks run cooperatively in FIFO order.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Contiki Dual-Execution Model (Events + Protothreads)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EVT1["EVENT SOURCES - TIMER - RADIO - SENSOR INTERRUPT"]
    EVK1["EVENT KERNEL - ASYNCHRONOUS - NON PREEMPTIVE"]
    PT1["PROTOTHREAD LIBRARY - STACKLESS - 2 BYTES PER THREAD"]
    PT2["PROTOTHREAD A - BLOCKED OR RUNNING"]
    PT3["PROTOTHREAD B - BLOCKED OR RUNNING"]
    PTN["PROTOTHREAD N - BLOCKED OR RUNNING"]
    APP1["APPLICATION CODE - C WITH PT THREAD MACROS"]
    EVT1 --> EVK1
    EVK1 --> PT1
    PT1 --> PT2
    PT1 --> PT3
    PT1 --> PTN
    PT2 --> APP1
    PT3 --> APP1
    PTN --> APP1
```

> [!NOTE]
> Contiki elegantly combines **events** (asynchronous, no blocking) with **protothreads** (synchronous-looking, but stackless and lightweight). This dual model is one of the most tested concepts in KTU exams.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow: WSN OS Selection Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ1["APPLICATION REQUIREMENT"] --> DEC1{"REAL TIME NEEDED"}
    DEC1 -- YES --> RIO1["CHOOSE RIOT OR ZEPHYR"]
    DEC1 -- NO --> DEC2{"MEMORY LESS THAN 2 KB"}
    DEC2 -- YES --> TIN1["CHOOSE TINYOS"]
    DEC2 -- NO --> DEC3{"NEED TCP IP STACK"}
    DEC3 -- YES --> CON1["CHOOSE CONTIKI"]
    DEC3 -- NO --> DEC4{"PREFER C PLUS PLUS API"}
    DEC4 -- YES --> RIO2["CHOOSE RIOT"]
    DEC4 -- NO --> GEN1["CHOOSE ZEPHYR"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Wireless Sensor Network Operating System. List any four design goals. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Wireless Sensor Network Operating System (WSN OS)** is a lightweight, resource-constrained system software that manages hardware resources, scheduling, communication, and application execution on individual sensor nodes (motes). It is specifically engineered to operate within tight constraints of energy, memory, processing power, and bandwidth.

**Four Design Goals:**

1. **Energy Efficiency** — Maximize battery lifetime through aggressive duty cycling.
2. **Small Memory Footprint** — Fit kernel and applications within KB-level RAM and ROM.
3. **Reactive Concurrency** — Provide low-latency response to interrupts/events.
4. **Reliability** — Operate unattended for months/years without reboot.

---

### Question 2: What are protothreads? Mention their key advantages. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Protothreads** are *stackless, lightweight threads* introduced in the **Contiki** operating system. Each protothread consumes only **~ 2 bytes** of memory (a single 16-bit `pt_t` structure storing the line number to resume from), compared to hundreds of bytes for traditional stackful threads.

**Key Advantages:**
1. Drastically reduced memory footprint per thread.
2. Blocking-style programming without the cost of full context switching.
3. Suitable for deeply memory-constrained sensor motes.
4. Compatible with standard C compilers (no special toolchain needed).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain the architecture and execution model of TinyOS in detail. Discuss its scheduling, programming model, and suitability for WSN. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` [14 Marks]

#### Part (a) — Architecture of TinyOS [7 Marks]

TinyOS follows a **component-based architecture** built with the **nesC** language. The system has three main architectural elements:

1. **Hardware Components** — Direct interface to physical devices like ADC, radio, LEDs, timers.
2. **Software Components** — Application logic modules.
3. **Wiring / Configuration Files** — Static component composition declared at compile time.

Each component declares **commands** (callable functions) and **events** (callbacks). Components are connected via *interfaces* and *wiring* declarations.

The architecture has **two types of execution contexts:**

- **Tasks (Software Context):** Non-preemptive, FIFO-scheduled, run-to-completion jobs posted via `post task()`. Maximum one task runs at a time.
- **Hardware Event Handlers:** Atomic, preempt tasks and other same-priority events, but **cannot be preempted by tasks or events at the same priority**. They call commands, post tasks, and signal events.

#### Part (b) — Programming Model, Scheduling & Suitability [7 Marks]

**Programming Model — nesC:**
- Static, compile-time component composition (no dynamic linking).
- **Split-phase operations** for non-blocking I/O — a request returns immediately, completion is delivered via an event.
- Eliminates dynamic memory allocation (no `malloc`), improving predictability.

**Scheduling:**
- Tasks follow **FIFO + run-to-completion**.
- Events preempt tasks but are themselves non-preemptive.
- Power management integrated into scheduler — if no tasks are pending, the CPU is sent to sleep until the next hardware interrupt.

**Suitability for WSN:**
- Extremely small footprint (kernel ~ **400 bytes**).
- Energy efficient — natural sleep during idle.
- Well-suited for **event-driven sensing workloads** like periodic temperature monitoring.
- **Limitation:** No preemptive multithreading → head-of-line blocking; difficult to express complex control flows; steep learning curve for nesC.

---

### Question B: Compare event-driven and multithreaded operating systems for WSN. Discuss the role of execution environment with a suitable layered architecture diagram. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` [14 Marks]

#### Part (a) — Event-Driven vs Multithreaded WSN OS [7 Marks]

| Parameter | Event-Driven (e.g., TinyOS) | Multithreaded (e.g., RIOT, Zephyr) |
|-----------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Concurrency | Tasks + Events | Threads (preemptive) |
| Memory per unit | ~ 4–32 bytes (task struct) | ~ 64–384 bytes (TCB + stack) |
| Context switch cost | Very low (no full save) | Higher (register + stack save) |
| Blocking calls | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Real-time guarantees | Soft only | Soft / Hard |
| Programming model | Event callbacks (complex) | Threads (familiar) |
| Energy efficiency | Excellent (idle = sleep) | Good (tickless schedulers) |
| Example suitability | Periodic sensing, fire alarm | Industrial control, robotics |

**Key Trade-off:**
- Event-driven designs **save memory** but **sacrifice expressiveness** and can suffer **head-of-line blocking**.
- Multithreaded designs **ease programming** and provide **real-time guarantees** but **consume more RAM**.

#### Part (b) — Execution Environment & Layered Architecture [7 Marks]

The **Execution Environment** is the complete software stack that sits between the sensor hardware and the application developer. It includes:

1. **Hardware Platform** — Mote (Tmote Sky, MicaZ, etc.).
2. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** — Drivers and register mappings.
3. **Kernel / OS** — Scheduler, memory manager, power manager.
4. **System Services** — Networking (802.15.4, 6LoWPAN, RPL), timers, file system.
5. **Middleware** — Query processing (TinyDB), aggregation, replication.
6. **Application Layer** — Sensing, actuation, user code.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HWLAYER["LAYER 6 HARDWARE MOTE MCU RADIO SENSORS"]
    HALLAYER["LAYER 5 HARDWARE ABSTRACTION GPIO ADC DRIVERS"]
    KERLAYER["LAYER 4 KERNEL SCHEDULER MEMORY POWER MANAGER"]
    SVCLAYER["LAYER 3 SYSTEM SERVICES NETWORK STACK 6LOWPAN RPL TIMERS"]
    MIDLAYER["LAYER 2 MIDDLEWARE TINYDB AGGREGATION REPLICATION"]
    APPLAYER["LAYER 1 APPLICATION SENSING ACTUATION USER CODE"]
    HWLAYER --> HALLAYER
    HALLAYER --> KERLAYER
    KERLAYER --> SVCLAYER
    SVCLAYER --> MIDLAYER
    MIDLAYER --> APPLAYER
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **confuse OS with Execution Environment**. Remember: OS is just *one layer*; EE is the *entire stack*.
> 2. Students frequently **forget split-phase operations** when describing TinyOS — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 3. In comparison questions, students write **advantages only** — always include **limitations**.
> 4. Numerical duty-cycle problems are often solved with **unit inconsistencies** (mixing µA and mA) — always normalize units first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **WSN OS** = lightweight, resource-aware software for sensor motes (TinyOS, Contiki, RIOT, Zephyr, LiteOS).
- [ ] **Execution Environment** = OS + HAL + Middleware + Programming Model + Application Stack.
- [ ] **TinyOS** uses **nesC** + **two-level scheduling** (Tasks FIFO + Hardware Event Handlers).
- [ ] **Contiki** uses **C** + **events** + **protothreads** (2 bytes each).
- [ ] **RIOT** offers **POSIX-like C/C++ API** with **preemptive scheduling**.
- [ ] **Zephyr** is the **Linux Foundation-backed** RTOS with **LTS releases**.
- [ ] **Protothreads** save memory by avoiding stack allocation — only a single `pt_t` variable is stored.
- [ ] **Hardware constraints:** 4–80 MHz MCU, 4–512 KB RAM, 1.5–3.6 V battery.
- [ ] **Duty cycle formula:** $\alpha = \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}$.
- [ ] **Average current:** $I_{\text{avg}} = \alpha I_{\text{active}} + (1 - \alpha) I_{\text{sleep}}$.
- [ ] **Battery lifetime:** $T_{\text{lifetime}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$.
- [ ] **TinyOS footprint:** ~ 400 bytes RAM / ~ 4 KB ROM.
- [ ] **Event-driven vs Multithreaded trade-off:** Memory vs Latency / Expressiveness.
- [ ] **Split-phase operations** in TinyOS avoid blocking — request and completion are decoupled.
- [ ] **Middleware** services include TinyDB, Maté, Agilla, query processing, replication.
- [ ] **Always normalize units** (µA ↔ mA ↔ A) in numerical WSN OS problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study (Basics Only) - TinyOS and nesC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET756 - WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Case Study (Basics Only) - TinyOS and nesC ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is TinyOS? **TinyOS** is an open-source, component-based, event-driven operating system specifically designed for **wireless sensor networks (WSN)** running on resource-constrained **mote** hardware platforms. It was developed primarily at the **University of California, Berkeley** in collaboration with Intel Research, and it is written in a specialized dialect of the C language called **nesC (networked embedded systems C)**. The system is engineered to operate within the strict memory and energy budgets of tiny sensor nodes — typically **few kilobytes of RAM** and **few tens of kilobytes of ROM/Flash**, and to deliver event responsiveness in the order of **sub-millisecond** to **few milliseconds**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** TinyOS and nesC are introduced as a *Case Study (Basics Only)*. Students are expected to understand the architectural philosophy, the component model, the execution model, and the programming style — **not** to write production-grade code or implement complex protocols. ### 1.2 What is nesC? **nesC** is a **statically-typed**, component-oriented programming language derived from the C language. It is the **canonical language** for programming applications on top of TinyOS. It directly supports the TinyOS concurrency model (tasks and hardware event handlers) and the **component / interface / wiring** abstraction that TinyOS is built on. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction to Remember** > - **TinyOS** = the *operating system / runtime* and scheduling kernel. > - **nesC** = the *programming language* used to write TinyOS applications and the OS components themselves. > TinyOS is **what runs**; nesC is **what you write**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Building Analogy Imagine a **large, highly automated smart building**: - The **building infrastructure** (electrical wiring, plumbing network, HVAC ducts) is fixed and pre-planned. You do not invent new plumbing every day. This is like the **TinyOS kernel and scheduler** — the fixed, pre-compiled runtime substrate. - The building is composed of **standardized units** (rooms, switches, sensors, controllers). Each unit has a clear set of **plugs and sockets** (interfaces) — a light switch can only fit into a "switch socket", not a "water socket". This is the **component / interface model of nesC**. - When a motion sensor fires, it sends a "signal" to the alarm controller. The controller schedules a response — but it does *not* block the building. Other events keep flowing. This is the **event-driven, non-blocking execution** of TinyOS. - The **blueprint** that wires up which room has which switch, drawn at design time, is the **nesC Configuration file**. In short: *TinyOS gives you a tiny, ultra-efficient building infrastructure, and nesC gives you the language to design, wire, and snap together the rooms (components) that make up your sensor application.* ### 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Numbers (Bolded) | Metric | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **RAM footprint** | **< 1 KB to 4 KB** | Forces static allocation | | **Code size (core)** | **~ 3.5 KB** | Ultra-compact kernel | | **Scheduler type** | **Non-preemptive FIFO** | Single thread of execution | | **Power goal** | **Years of life on 2× AA** | Energy-first design | | **Event latency** | **Few µs to ms** | Real-time-ish responsiveness | | **Target platforms** | **Mica2, MicaZ, TelosB, BTnode, eyesIFXv2** | Berkeley/Crossbow motes | > [!TIP] > Whenever the KTU examiner frames a question like *"Why does TinyOS use such a small kernel?"* — the answer is **energy, memory, and predictability of the event-driven model on sensor motes**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Design Goals of TinyOS (Why it exists) TinyOS was not built to run on laptops or phones. Every design decision is filtered through the WSN lens: - **Energy Efficiency First:** Sensor motes are battery-powered, often deployed in inaccessible locations. Idle listening and busy CPU cycles directly drain the battery. TinyOS aggressively puts the CPU to **sleep** when no events are pending. - **Small Memory Footprint:** RAM is the most expensive resource on a mote. TinyOS avoids dynamic memory (`malloc`/`free`) and uses a **statically allocated, compile-time known memory budget**. - **Concurrency without Threads:** A full thread abstraction is too heavy. TinyOS instead offers **two lightweight concurrency primitives** — *tasks* and *hardware event handlers (HETs)* — both of which run to completion (run-to-completion, RTC). - **Reactive / Event-Driven:** The system is structured as a pipeline of *event → handler → task → deferred work*. There is no busy-wait polling in the core. - **Modularity / Reuse:** A sensor application is built by **wiring pre-built components** (radio, ADC, LED, timer, etc.). This encourages reuse across research groups and projects. ### 2.2 TinyOS Architecture (The Layered View) TinyOS is best visualized as a layered architecture: | Layer | Contents | Function | |---|---|---| | **Application Layer** | User-written nesC application components | Sensing, routing, app logic | | **System Services** | Active Message (AM), networking stack, sensors drivers | Reusable services (radio, ADC, timer, flash) | | **TinyOS Component Framework** | Components, interfaces, wiring | The structural model of the OS | | **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HALE)** | Platform-specific HPL (Hardware Presentation Layer) + HIL (Hardware Interface Layer) | Clean, portable mapping to mote hardware | | **Hardware** | MCU, radio chip, sensors | Physical mote | ### 2.3 The nesC Component Model — The Heart of the System nesC organizes all software (both OS and apps) as **components** connected by **interfaces**. **2.3.1 Two Kinds of Components** 1. **Module** — Contains the *implementation* of one or more interfaces. Equivalent to a "black box" with code inside. Written in `.nc` files using the keyword `module`. 2. **Configuration** — *Wires* modules together so that the `uses` of one component is bound to the `provides` of another. Has no executable code of its own. Written in `.nc` files using the keyword `configuration`. **2.3.2 Interfaces** An **interface** is a **bidirectional contract** that declares: - A set of **commands** (analogous to function calls *the component must implement* if it *provides* the interface). - A set of **events** (signals *the component must signal* if it *provides* the interface). A component that *uses* an interface calls its commands; the component that *provides* the interface implements the commands and signals the events. This is the **provide/use symmetry**. > [!IMPORTANT] > A single component can both **provide** interfaces (be the server) and **use** interfaces (be the client of another component). This is the cornerstone of nesC's flexibility. **2.3.3 Wiring** Wiring is **static** and **complete** at compile time. The compiler verifies that every `uses` is matched by exactly one `provides` of the same interface. There is **no dynamic binding at runtime**, which keeps the system small and predictable. ### 2.4 Execution Model — Tasks and Hardware Event Handlers The TinyOS scheduler is **non-preemptive** and maintains **two distinct queues** of runnable units: - **Tasks (Logical Concurrency):** Deferred work; posted via `post taskName()`. Scheduled FIFO. Can be **interrupted by hardware events** but cannot interrupt each other. - **Hardware Event Handlers (HETs):** Triggered by hardware interrupts (e.g., timer overflow, radio packet arrival, ADC ready). They preempt the currently running task. They themselves are **non-preemptible** by other HETs of the same priority. The atomicity primitive is the `atomic` statement, which disables interrupts over a critical section to prevent race conditions on shared state. ### 2.5 The "Split-Phase Operation" Pattern Because TinyOS has no blocking calls, long-latency operations (e.g., sending a packet over the radio) are split into two halves: - **Start phase:** A `command` is called to *initiate* the operation (returns immediately). - **Completion phase:** An `event` is signaled when the operation finishes. This is conceptually similar to **asynchronous programming** or **futures/promises** in higher-level software. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | One-Line Definition | KTU-Exam Importance | |---|---|---| | **TinyOS** | Component-based, event-driven OS for WSN motes | High — definition, features | | **nesC** | Static, component-oriented dialect of C for WSN | High — features, role | | **Module** | Component containing executable code | High | | **Configuration** | Component that wires modules together | High | | **Interface** | Declares commands + events (bidirectional) | Very High | | **Provide / Use** | A component provides or uses an interface | Very High | | **Task** | Deferred, non-preemptible work unit | High | | **HET (Hardware Event Handler)** | Interrupt-level handler, preempts tasks | High | | **Scheduler** | Non-preemptive FIFO (single thread of execution) | Medium | | **`atomic` block** | Disables interrupts to protect shared state | Medium | | **Split-phase** | Non-blocking operation in start + event pair | High | | **HPL / HIL** | Hardware Presentation / Interface Layer (portability) | Medium | | **Active Message (AM)** | TinyOS default messaging abstraction | Medium | > [!NOTE] > **Why a Table, not an Equation Sheet?** The topic is qualitative-architectural. KTU tests it via short answer/essay style. Memorize the *names*, the *two-component types*, the *bidirectional interface*, the *two concurrency primitives*, and the *split-phase idea*. These five items alone cover ~80% of past paper marks. ### 2.7 Real-World Utility and Engineering Relevance TinyOS and nesC were the **de-facto standard** for academic WSN research from roughly 2002 to 2015. They powered thousands of published research papers in routing, MAC, localization, and data aggregation. Modern descendant systems — **Contiki, RIOT, Zephyr, OpenThread** — share many of its design philosophies: componentization, event-driven kernels, static allocation, and a small footprint. Understanding TinyOS gives a student a strong conceptual foundation for evaluating any modern IoT RTOS. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Reasoning Because the topic is a *qualitative case study*, the "derivations" here are **logical walkthroughs** of how TinyOS and nesC concepts are constructed and how they interact. We treat each concept as a "theorem" and prove it step-by-step in plain reasoning. ### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1 — From Requirement to TinyOS Architecture **Premise (Why TinyOS exists):** A sensor mote has *X* KB RAM, *Y* KB Flash, runs on 2× AA batteries for months/years, and must react to radio, timer, and sensor events in real time. **Step 1 — Eliminate a full process/thread model.** Threads require per-thread stacks (≥ 256 B each) and a context switch (saves/restores registers ≈ tens of cycles). On a mote with 4 KB RAM, even 4 threads consume > 1 KB of precious memory just for stacks. This is wasteful. **Step 2 — Substitute a single-thread, two-queue scheduler.** A single thread of execution with two work queues (tasks and HETs) consumes a single stack frame. Concurrency is *logical*, not *physical*. This saves memory and reduces context-switch overhead. **Step 3 — Use static allocation.** Since the whole application's components and buffers are known at compile time, nesC and the TinyOS build system (using `nesC` compiler and `gcc` cross-compiler) produce a single statically linked image. No heap, no `malloc`, no fragmentation. **Step 4 — Make blocking impossible.** A blocking call inside a non-preemptive kernel would freeze the whole system. Therefore, all long operations (radio, I²C, ADC read) are expressed as **split-phase** — the call returns immediately and an event fires on completion. **Step 5 — Componentize for reuse.** Researchers should not re-write the radio driver for every project. So TinyOS ships a library of components (radio, ADC, timer, LEDs, AM layer, routing). Applications are written as **thin configurations** that wire these components together. **Conclusion:** TinyOS = (static memory) + (event-driven, single-thread scheduler) + (component model) + (split-phase I/O). Each design decision follows directly from the constraints of the mote. ### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2 — Anatomy of an nesC Component Pair We illustrate a minimal pair: a *Blink* application and a *Timer* service it uses. **Step 1 — Declare the interface in a separate header file (e.g., `Timer.nc`):** ``` interface Timer { // command: starts a periodic timer command void start(uint32_t interval, bool isPeriodic); // command: stops the timer command void stop(); // event: signaled when the timer fires event void fired(); } ``` **Step 2 — Implement the interface in a *Module* file (`TimerMilliC.nc` or user module):** The module writes the bodies of `start` and `stop` commands and signals the `fired` event. For instance, it might configure a hardware timer compare register inside `start`, and from the timer's interrupt service routine (the HET) call `signal Timer.fired()`. **Step 3 — A user module (`BlinkM.nc`) provides `Boot` and `Leds` interfaces, and `uses Timer`:** - It calls `call Timer.start(500, TRUE)` inside `Boot.booted()`. - Inside `event void Timer.fired()`, it toggles the LED and re-posts itself. **Step 4 — A configuration (`AppC.nc`) wires them:** ``` AppC.nc: components MainC, BlinkM, LedsC, TimerMilliC; BlinkM.Timer -> TimerMilliC.Timer; BlinkM.Leds -> LedsC; } ``` Here, `BlinkM.Timer -> TimerMilliC.Timer` says: "the `Timer` interface that `BlinkM` *uses* is bound to the `Timer` interface that `TimerMilliC` *provides*." **Step 5 — Compile.** The nesC compiler performs a **whole-program analysis**: - It checks that every `uses` is bound (no dangling references). - It inlines interfaces where possible and generates C code. - It calls out any cross-component data-race hazards if shared state is touched outside `atomic`. **Outcome:** A single statically linked ELF/Intel-HEX image ready to flash onto a mote. ### 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 3 — Concurrency Math & Worst-Case Latency Although TinyOS is non-preemptive, we can still bound event latency. Let: - $T_{\text{exec}}$ = worst-case execution time of a single task (in CPU cycles) - $T_{\text{HET}}$ = worst-case execution time of a single HET - $f_{\text{CPU}}$ = CPU clock frequency in Hz **Worst-case response time of a task to a hardware event:** $$ T_{\text{response}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{HET, current}} + T_{\text{task, queue}} + T_{\text{HET, pending}}}{f_{\text{CPU}}} $$ where $T_{\text{task, queue}}$ is the worst-case sum of all tasks queued ahead of it. **Step-by-step interpretation:** - When a hardware event fires, the current HET (if any) finishes (term $T_{\text{HET, current}}$). - The scheduler then drains all tasks queued ahead of our task (term $T_{\text{task, queue}}$). - Higher-priority HETs that arrive during task execution preempt it (term $T_{\text{HET, pending}}$). - Dividing by $f_{\text{CPU}}$ converts cycles into seconds. **Conversion logic (worked numbers):** Let $T_{\text{HET, current}} = 200$ cycles, $T_{\text{task, queue}} = 1500$ cycles, $T_{\text{HET, pending}} = 300$ cycles, $f_{\text{CPU}} = 8 \times 10^{6}$ Hz. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{response}} &= \frac{200 + 1500 + 300}{8 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= \frac{2000}{8 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} \\ &= 250\ \mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** TinyOS can offer bounded, sub-millisecond event response *provided* the application keeps tasks short — a discipline that is enforced by convention and the `atomic` keyword. ### 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough 4 — Memory-Budget Reasoning Suppose a MicaZ mote has: - **4 KB** RAM, **128 KB** program flash. TinyOS reserves approximately: - Kernel + scheduler: **≈ 400 B** of code. - Per-component state: typically **few hundred bytes**. - Active Message buffer: **≈ 30–60 B per packet**. A typical application (e.g., a periodic light sensor that broadcasts readings) might use: - 1 task buffer - 1 timer component - 1 radio component - 1 sensor (ADC) component - 1 LED component **Total:** still well under 2 KB, leaving 2 KB for application-specific state and queues. This is precisely the regime for which TinyOS was engineered. ### 3.5 Analytical Walkthrough 5 — The `atomic` Keyword and Race Conditions A classic race: the HET for the radio modifies a buffer pointer; a task reads the same pointer. Without protection: - Task starts reading pointer (value = X). - HET fires, updates pointer to Y. - Task continues with stale X — corrupted state. **Solution:** Wrap the read *and* the HET's update inside `atomic { ... }`. The compiler emits code that: - Disables interrupts (via `__nesc_atomic_start`). - Executes the block. - Restores the previous interrupt state. Because TinyOS has no thread preemption among tasks, `atomic` only needs to block HETs, not other tasks. This is a much simpler atomicity model than in a full preemptive OS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark-winning remark (KTU):** "Because TinyOS uses a non-preemptive single-thread scheduler, atomicity is needed only against hardware event handlers — not against other tasks." ### 3.6 Summary Matrix of nesC vs Standard C | Feature | Standard C | nesC (for TinyOS) | |---|---|---| | Memory model | Heap + stack | **Static only** | | Linking | Often dynamic | **Statically linked** | | Components | None | **Module + Configuration** | | Interfaces | None | **Bidirectional command/event** | | Concurrency | Threads / processes | **Tasks + HETs** | | Blocking I/O | Allowed | **Forbidden — split-phase** | | Atomicity | OS-dependent | **`atomic` keyword built-in** | | Compilation | per-file | **whole-program compile** | --- ## 4.
