Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TinyOS Layered Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB APP["APPLICATION LAYER
User Written nesC Application"] SVC["SYSTEM SERVICES
Active Message Layer, Routing, Sensors"] CF["COMPONENT FRAMEWORK
Components, Interfaces, Wiring"] HIL["HARDWARE INTERFACE LAYER HIL
Platform Independent Abstractions"] HPL["HARDWARE PRESENTATION LAYER HPL
Platform Specific Drivers"] HW["PHYSICAL HARDWARE
MCU Radio ADC Sensors LEDs"] APP --> SVC SVC --> CF CF --> HIL HIL --> HPL HPL --> HW ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each layer is built entirely from nesC components; the lowest layer (HPL) is the *only* place where vendor-specific register names appear. Porting TinyOS to a new mote means re-writing only the HPL. ### 4.2 The nesC Component Wiring Model ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USERA["USER MODULE BlinkM"] UM1["provides interface Boot"] UM2["provides interface Leds"] UM3["uses interface Timer"] end subgraph TIM["TIMER MODULE TimerMilliC"] TM1["provides interface Timer"] TM2["uses interface HPLTimer"] end subgraph LED["LED MODULE LedsC"] LM1["provides interface Leds"] end subgraph CFG["CONFIGURATION AppC"] CFG1["BlinkM.Timer to TimerMilliC.Timer"] CFG2["BlinkM.Leds to LedsC.Leds"] end BootReq["Boot Signal from Power On"] --> UM1 UM3 -. "wire" .-> TM1 UM2 -. "wire" .-> LM1 TimerSignal["Hardware Timer Tick"] --> TM2 UM1 -->|"post task to toggle LED"| UM2 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The dashed edges are **wires** set in the configuration. The solid edge labelled *"post task to toggle LED"* shows the *runtime* flow (Boot fires → BlinkM calls Timer.start → on Timer.fired event → BlinkM posts a task that toggles Leds). ### 4.3 Split-Phase Operation (Radio Send Example) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant App as Application Module participant Radio as Radio Component participant HW as Radio Hardware Note over App: Step 1 START phase App->>Radio: call AMSend.send(amId, buf, len) Radio-->>App: command returns immediately Radio->>HW: write buffer to TX FIFO HW-->>Radio: TX_DONE interrupt Note over App: Step 2 COMPLETE phase Radio->>App: signal AMSend.sendDone(buf, err) App->>App: post next task (e.g., schedule next sample) ``` **Reading the diagram:** The call returns immediately at *Step 1*. The application is free to do other work (or sleep). When the radio finishes transmitting, it signals `sendDone` at *Step 2*. This is the textbook TinyOS split-phase pattern. ### 4.4 Scheduler State Machine (Tasks + HETs) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> RunningTask: scheduler picks next task from FIFO queue RunningTask --> RunningTask: task runs to completion run to completion RTC RunningTask --> Idle: task finishes Idle --> RunningHET: hardware interrupt arrives RunningHET --> RunningHET: HET runs to completion RunningHET --> Idle: HET returns and clears flag RunningHET --> RunningTask: HET posts a task before returning RunningTask --> RunningHET: hardware interrupt preempts task RunningHET --> RunningHET: HET signals event or modifies state via atomic ``` **Reading the diagram:** There is no preemption *between tasks* — only *hardware events* can interrupt a running task. The HET itself cannot be interrupted by another HET of equal priority; this is the "single thread of execution" property. ### 4.5 Component Provide/Use Symmetry (Conceptual Block) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Component A
provides interface X"] -- "wires" --> B["Component B
uses interface X"] B -- "calls commands on A" --> A A -- "signals events to B" --> B style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e style B fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#075985 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The interface `X` is the *contract*. Commands flow upward (caller → callee), events flow downward (callee → caller). The wire is established at compile time, not runtime. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]** *Define TinyOS. List any **four** key features of TinyOS that make it suitable for wireless sensor networks.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** TinyOS is an open-source, component-based, event-driven operating system designed specifically for resource-constrained wireless sensor motes. **Four key features:** 1. **Tiny footprint** — kernel fits in about 3.5 KB of code, allowing it to run on motes with only a few KB of RAM. 2. **Event-driven execution** — uses a non-preemptive scheduler with tasks and hardware event handlers, avoiding the overhead of a full thread model. 3. **Component-based architecture** — applications are built by *wiring* pre-existing nesC components, promoting reuse. 4. **Energy-aware design** — aggressively puts the CPU to sleep when idle, enabling years of battery life. 5. *(Optional 5th point for safety margin)* **Static memory allocation** — no dynamic memory, no fragmentation, predictable memory usage. *(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four features — ½ mark each.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** *What is nesC? Explain the concept of **interface** in nesC with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **nesC** is a statically-typed, component-oriented extension of the C programming language, designed to program applications for TinyOS-based wireless sensor networks. **Interface in nesC:** An *interface* in nesC is a **bidirectional named contract** that declares a set of **commands** (functions that the provider must implement) and a set of **events** (signals that the provider must fire). A component that *uses* an interface calls its commands, while the component that *provides* the interface implements those commands and signals the events. **Example:** ``` interface Timer { command void start(char type, uint32_t interval); command void stop(); event void fired(); } ``` Here, any component providing `Timer` must implement `start` and `stop` and signal `fired`. Any component using `Timer` can call `start` and `stop` and must handle the `fired` event. *(Allocation: 1 mark for nesC definition, 2 marks for interface explanation + example.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of TinyOS**. Describe each layer and its function. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **nesC component model** in detail. Differentiate between a *module* and a *configuration* with examples. --- #### **Model Solution to Question A** **Answer to (a) — TinyOS Architecture (7 marks):** TinyOS follows a **layered, component-based architecture** designed to deliver high modularity and platform portability. The layers, from top to bottom, are: 1. **Application Layer (1 mark):** Contains the user-written application components (e.g., a periodic sensing task, a routing protocol). These are typically thin nesC configurations that wire together reusable system services. 2. **System Services Layer (1 mark):** Houses the higher-level reusable services — the *Active Message (AM)* layer for packet-based radio communication, the *sensor drivers*, the *LED* and *timer* abstractions, the *routing* components. These services are pre-compiled libraries. 3. **Component Framework (1 mark):** The structural model — the notion of components, interfaces, configurations, modules, wiring. This is the conceptual heart of TinyOS and the place where nesC semantics are enforced. 4. **Hardware Interface Layer (HIL) (1 mark):** Provides *platform-independent* abstractions such as `HplAtm128Timer` or `GpioInterrupt`. Code at this layer does not touch raw register addresses. 5. **Hardware Presentation Layer (HPL) (1 mark):** *Platform-specific* thin layer that maps HIL abstractions to actual hardware registers on the target MCU (e.g., ATmega128 on MicaZ, MSP430 on TelosB). 6. **Hardware (1 mark):** The physical mote: microcontroller, radio transceiver, sensors, LEDs, and battery. 7. **Diagrammatic representation (1 mark):** (Use the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 as a guide; a hand-drawn layered stack in the answer booklet is acceptable.) --- **Answer to (b) — nesC Component Model (7 marks):** The nesC component model is the programming abstraction for TinyOS. Its key ideas are: 1. **Component (1 mark):** A component is a self-contained software unit with well-defined interfaces. All software in TinyOS — both the OS and the application — is a component. 2. **Two types of components (2 marks):** - **Module:** Contains the *implementation* (the actual C-like code) of one or more interfaces. Defined with the keyword `module`. Example: a `BlinkM.nc` module that implements a periodic LED-blinking logic. - **Configuration:** Contains *no* executable code. It *wires* modules together, binding the `uses` of one component to the `provides` of another. Defined with the keyword `configuration`. Example: `AppC.nc` that wires `BlinkM` to `TimerMilliC` and `LedsC`. 3. **Interface (1 mark):** A bidirectional contract that declares a set of *commands* (caller → callee) and *events* (callee → caller). Example interface `Timer` with `start` and `stop` commands and a `fired` event. 4. **Provide/Use Symmetry (1 mark):** A component can *provide* interfaces (acting as a server) and/or *use* interfaces (acting as a client). Wiring is performed in a configuration using the `->` operator: `BlinkM.Timer -> TimerMilliC.Timer;` 5. **Static Linking (1 mark):** All wiring is resolved at compile time. The nesC compiler performs a *whole-program* check that every `uses` is bound to exactly one `provides`. No dynamic binding exists at runtime. 6. **Example summary (1 mark):** `BlinkM.nc` (module) provides `Boot` and `Leds`; uses `Timer`. `AppC.nc` (configuration) wires `BlinkM` to `TimerMilliC` and `LedsC`. The compiler produces a single statically-linked image. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **concurrency model of TinyOS**. Differentiate between *tasks* and *hardware event handlers*. Discuss the role of the `atomic` keyword. **(b)** *(7 marks)* What is a **split-phase operation**? Illustrate with the example of sending a packet using the *Active Message* layer. Why are blocking calls not allowed in TinyOS? --- #### **Model Solution to Question B** **Answer to (a) — Concurrency Model of TinyOS (7 marks):** TinyOS is built around a **single-thread, non-preemptive scheduler** that provides two forms of *logical* concurrency: 1. **Tasks (3 marks):** - Posted using `post taskName()`. - Scheduled by a **FIFO scheduler** in a queue. - Run to completion (RTC); a task cannot be preempted by another task. - Can be **interrupted** by a hardware event handler. - Used for *deferred work* (work that does not need to finish inside an interrupt). - They form the "background" processing of the system. 2. **Hardware Event Handlers / HETs (3 marks):** - Triggered directly by hardware interrupts (radio packet arrival, timer overflow, ADC complete). - Preempt the currently running task. - They themselves are **non-preemptible** by other HETs of the same priority — they run to completion. - Used for *time-critical* work that must be acknowledged promptly. - Should be kept *short* because they block all other HETs and tasks. 3. **The `atomic` keyword (1 mark):** Because tasks and HETs share state, race conditions can occur. The `atomic { ... }` statement disables interrupts over its block, guaranteeing mutual exclusion between the enclosed code and any HET. It is the only atomicity primitive in nesC. **Key insight:** *Concurrency in TinyOS is logical, not physical. There is a single thread of execution. Tasks do not preempt each other; only HETs preempt tasks. This keeps the kernel small and predictable.* --- **Answer to (b) — Split-Phase Operation (7 marks):** 1. **Definition (2 marks):** A *split-phase operation* is a non-blocking interaction that is split into two parts: - A **command** that *initiates* the operation and returns immediately. - An **event** that is *signaled* when the operation completes. It is the TinyOS equivalent of asynchronous programming. There is no thread of execution that blocks waiting for the operation to finish. 2. **Active Message send example (3 marks):** The `AMSend` interface in TinyOS is split-phase: ``` interface AMSend { command error_t send(am_addr_t addr, message_t* msg, uint8_t len); event void sendDone(message_t* msg, error_t error); } ``` - The application calls `AMSend.send(addr, msg, len)`. The command **returns immediately** with an error code (e.g., SUCCESS or FAIL — busy). It does *not* wait for the radio to finish transmitting. - When the radio hardware signals "TX complete" (an interrupt), the radio component signals `sendDone` back to the application. The application can then process the result — for example, freeing the buffer, toggling a status LED, or scheduling the next sample. 3. **Why blocking calls are not allowed (2 marks):** - TinyOS is **non-preemptive**: a single task that blocks would freeze the *entire* system — no other event could be processed, no other HET could be served. - On a sensor mote, this would mean missing radio packets, losing timer events, and draining the battery on useless active waiting. - Split-phase solves this by returning control to the scheduler immediately; the application can do other work (or post a task and let the CPU sleep) while the slow operation proceeds in the background. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks on TinyOS/nesC questions:** > 1. **Confusing TinyOS and nesC.** TinyOS is the OS/runtime; nesC is the language. The examiner will *deduct marks* if you write "TinyOS is a programming language" or "nesC is an OS". > 2. **Forgetting the bidirectionality of interfaces.** Many students describe an interface as "a set of functions to call". Always mention *both* commands and events, and the provide/use split. > 3. **Mixing up module and configuration.** A *module* contains code; a *configuration* contains wiring. If you swap these, you lose at least 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. > 4. **Stating that TinyOS supports threads/preemption.** It does **not**. TinyOS has a *single thread of execution* with two queues (tasks + HETs). Preemption is *only* by HETs over tasks, never by tasks over each other. > 5. **Forgetting `atomic` when discussing race conditions.** Any answer that mentions a shared state between a task and a HET must reference the `atomic` block. > 6. **Omitting split-phase.** Whenever a question asks "how does TinyOS handle long operations", the answer must contain the words *split-phase*, *command*, and *event*. > 7. **Writing a C program.** The syllabus is *Basics Only*. Do not dump large code blocks. A 4–6 line interface declaration is more than enough. No marks are awarded for a working blink program in C. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **TinyOS = OS**, **nesC = language**. Never confuse them. - Designed for **motes** (Mica2, MicaZ, TelosB); tiny footprint (~3.5 KB kernel). - **Event-driven**, **non-preemptive**, **single-threaded** scheduler. - Two concurrency units: **Tasks** (FIFO, RTC, deferred) and **HETs** (hardware interrupt handlers, preempt tasks, RTC). - **Component model** of nesC: every piece of code is a component. - **Two component types:** **Module** (implementation) and **Configuration** (wiring). - **Interface** = bidirectional; **commands** (callee side) + **events** (callee signals back). - **Provide/Use** symmetry; wiring is **static** and **complete** at compile time. - **Split-phase operations:** long calls return immediately; completion is signaled by an event. - **`atomic` keyword** disables interrupts to protect shared state from HET races. - **HPL (Hardware Presentation Layer)** + **HIL (Hardware Interface Layer)** provide portability across motes. - **Active Message (AM)** is the standard messaging abstraction. - **No dynamic memory** — everything is statically allocated. No `malloc`, no `free`, no fragmentation. - **Energy-first design philosophy** — CPU sleeps when no events are pending. - TinyOS influenced modern IoT OSes: **Contiki, RIOT, Zephyr** (conceptual lineage). - **KTU exam focus:** definition, architecture diagram, component model, concurrency model, split-phase, and the role of `atomic`. Code is not required. ---
